<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0019 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Preparation of the Candidate

This section details the initial preparation and entry of a candidate for the First Degree in Masonry. It begins with an interrogation to confirm the candidate's motivations and willingness to conform to Masonic usages. Following assent, the candidate undergoes specific physical preparation, including partial disrobing and the application of a hoodwink, slipper, and cable-tow. The narrative then describes the ritualistic knocking at the Lodge door, the dialogue between the Deacons regarding the candidate's qualifications and desire for light, and the formal reception into the Lodge. This reception involves the symbolic presentation of the compasses to the candidate's naked breast, followed by a prayer led by the Worshipful Master and the candidate's declaration of trust in God.

This section details the final preparatory steps for an Entered Apprentice candidate. It includes the candidate kneeling for a prayer recited by the Worshipful Master, followed by the Master asking the candidate in whom they put their trust. Upon declaring trust in God, the candidate is assisted to rise and begins a perambulation around the Lodge room, guided by the Senior Deacon. During this circuit, the Worshipful Master recites a passage on unity, and the Master, Junior Warden, and Senior Warden each give a single gavel rap as the candidate passes their stations. The perambulation concludes at the Junior Warden's station, where a challenge is issued.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0019 -->
practical_elements: Candidate interrogated on motivations, desire for knowledge, service, and conformity to Order's usages.
Candidate requested to strip: coat, shoes, stockings, vest, cravat, pantaloons.
Candidate provided with a pair of drawers.
Left arm slipped out of shirt-sleeve and put through bosom, baring arm and breast.
Handkerchief or hoodwink tied over candidate's eyes.
Slipper placed on candidate's right foot.
Rope, called a cable-tow, placed once around candidate's neck, dragging behind.
Junior Deacon leads candidate to Lodge door and gives three distinct knocks.
Senior Deacon makes the sign of an Entered Apprentice to the Master.
Dialogue at the door between Senior Deacon and Junior Deacon (responding for candidate).
Senior Deacon takes compasses from the altar.
Senior Deacon presents one point of the compasses to the candidate's naked left breast.
Junior Deacon leaves candidate with Senior Deacon and takes his seat.
Senior Deacon, followed by two Stewards, travels once regularly around the Lodge-room with the candidate.
Worshipful Master gives one rap to stop the procession.
Candidate conducted to the center of the Lodge and kneels for prayer.
Worshipful Master leaves his seat, kneels by candidate, and recites a prayer.
All respond with "So mote it be."
Worshipful Master rises, takes candidate by the right hand, places left hand on candidate's head.
Candidate prompted to declare trust in God.
Candidate kneels for prayer.
Worshipful Master leaves his seat, kneels by the candidate, and recites a prayer.
Worshipful Master takes the candidate by the right hand and places his left on the candidate's head.
Candidate declares trust in God.
Worshipful Master assists the candidate to rise.
Senior Deacon conducts the candidate once around the Lodge room.
Worshipful Master recites the passage 'Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!' during the perambulation.
The Master, Junior Warden, and Senior Warden each give one gavel rap as the candidate passes their respective stations.
The candidate halts at the Junior Warden's station in the south.
Junior Warden gives one rap, followed by the conductor giving one rap.
Junior Warden asks, 'Who comes here?'
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The physical preparation, including partial nudity, hoodwink, slipper, and cable-tow, symbolizes the candidate's vulnerable state, humility, and journey from darkness (ignorance) to light (knowledge). The hoodwink specifically represents the darkness from which the candidate seeks enlightenment. The cable-tow signifies the candidate's bond and obligation to the Craft. The presentation of the point of the compasses to the naked left breast serves as a powerful symbol and a physical reminder of the severe consequences and internal 'torture' to one's conscience should Masonic secrets be unlawfully revealed. The invocation of the Deity and the candidate's declaration of trust in God underscore the spiritual foundation of Freemasonry and the necessity of faith in a Supreme Being.

The act of kneeling for prayer signifies humility and a plea for divine guidance and aid in the candidate's journey into Masonry. The declaration of trust in God establishes a foundational principle of faith and reliance on a higher power. The perambulation around the Lodge room symbolizes the candidate's initial journey and introduction to the sacred space and its officers, while the accompanying passage emphasizes the core Masonic value of unity and brotherly love. The gavel raps serve as a ritualistic acknowledgment of the candidate's passage and the officers' presence.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate is taught the importance of sincere and honorable motivations for seeking admission, including a desire for knowledge and a commitment to serving fellow creatures. The ritualistic reception, particularly the point of the compasses, impresses upon the candidate the solemnity of the obligation and the grave consequences of violating Masonic trust and secrecy. The prayer and the declaration of trust in God instill the fundamental lesson of reliance on a divine power and the moral principles of brotherly love, relief, and truth that are central to the Masonic way of life.

The candidate learns the importance of seeking divine aid and dedicating their life to service. They are taught that their faith in God is a well-founded trust. The perambulation and accompanying passage instill the lesson of unity and brotherly love as essential tenets of Freemasonry, encouraging them to become a true and faithful brother.
tradition_notes: The text notes that the Worshipful Master sometimes addresses the candidate as 'Stranger!' instead of by name during the declaration of trust in God.
The Junior Deacon 'always responds for the candidate' during the initial questioning at the door.
The phrase 'as all brothers and fellows have done before' emphasizes the continuity of Masonic tradition.
Masters sometimes use 'Stranger!' instead of the candidate's name when asking about trust.
The passage 'Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!' is noted as being from 'opening, or Monitor's.'
caution_notes: The point of a sharp instrument pressing the naked left breast is to teach the candidate that 'as it is a torture to your flesh, so should the recollection of it ever be to your mind and conscience, should you attempt to reveal the secrets of Masonry unlawfully.'

### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Tiler’s Obligation

This section details the Tiler's Obligation, a solemn oath taken by the Tiler confirming his Masonic degrees and good standing. It then outlines a series of declarations required from every candidate prior to their reception into the Entered Apprentice Degree, covering their motives, belief in God, immortality of the soul, resurrection of the body, and willingness to conform to ancient Masonic customs and usages. The section also notes the ancient custom of invoking a blessing from Deity before important undertakings.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0016 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0009 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mason.

This section details the duegard and sign of a Master Mason, which alludes to the penalty of the obligation, and the grand hailing sign of distress. It describes the physical actions for each sign, including the accompanying words for the grand hailing sign of distress, "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?" This sign is given by the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The text also outlines the specific gavel raps used by the Master and Wardens to open a Lodge on the First, Second, and Third Degrees, concluding with the Master reciting a passage of Scripture (Psalm 133) about brethren dwelling in unity, followed by the brethren's response.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0009 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0020 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Reception and Perambulation

This section details the reception and perambulation of an Entered Apprentice candidate, Mr. Peter Gabe, within the Lodge. It covers the initial questioning by the Junior Warden regarding his free will, qualifications, and right of admission, followed by similar inquiries from the Senior Warden and Worshipful Master. The candidate's journey from west to east in search of light is emphasized. The ritual then proceeds to instruct the candidate on the proper approach to the east and the altar, culminating in the preparation for taking the solemn oath or obligation of an Entered Apprentice Mason, including the specific 'due form' posture involving kneeling on the naked left knee with the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0020 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0021 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Obligation.

This section details the Entered Apprentice obligation, emphasizing secrecy and the severe penalties for its violation. It describes the ritualistic process of the candidate receiving 'light,' which involves the removal of the hoodwink and cable-tow, and the explanation of the three great lights (Holy Bible, square, and compasses) and three lesser lights (sun, moon, and Master of the Lodge). The section concludes with the Master presenting the grip and word of an Entered Apprentice, reinforcing the commitment to Masonic principles.

This section details the ritualistic process for identifying an Entered Apprentice Mason through a specific grip and the word "Boaz," which is revealed through a letter-by-letter exchange. Following this, the newly obligated candidate is assisted to rise from the altar and presented to the Junior and Senior Wardens. The text describes variations in lodge procedures for this presentation, either a single circuit around the room with signs or a more formal halt at the Wardens' stations involving a dialogue about the nature of Masonic signs (right angles, horizontals, perpendiculars) and tokens (brotherly grips for recognition).
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0021 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0022 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Bringing to Light: Entered Apprentice Sign and Word

This section details the Entered Apprentice signs, token, and the word 'Boaz,' explaining their allusions to the obligation and secrecy. It describes the ritualistic examination by the Junior and Senior Wardens. The Worshipful Master then presents the candidate with a lambskin apron, an emblem of innocence and a badge of a Mason, instructing how it should be worn. The section concludes with the ancient custom of requesting a metallic deposit from the candidate as a memento for the Lodge archives.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0022 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0025 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: First Section.

This section presents a catechism for an Entered Apprentice Mason, covering the candidate's origin, purpose in Masonry (to subdue passions and improve oneself), and methods of recognition. It details how a Mason is known by signs, tokens, a word, and the perfect points of entrance, including specific descriptions of signs as right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, and tokens as friendly grips. The symbolic meaning of the common gavel is explained as a tool for divesting the mind of vices to become a 'living stone' for a 'spiritual building'. The structure of the degree's lecture is outlined, consisting of three sections: the manner of initiation, the reasons for it, and the form, furniture, and lights of the Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0025 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0089 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master Reception and Obligation

This section details the reception and obligation ceremony for the Mark Master Degree. It begins with a severe reprimand given to the candidate for presenting unfit work and claiming undeserved wages, a practice noted as often omitted in modern lodges. The candidate is then prepared by being divested of valuables, blindfolded, and having a cable-tow placed around his body. After an alarm at the door, the candidate, identified as a Master Mason seeking further light, is admitted using the pass-word JOPPA. The Senior Deacon then symbolically receives the candidate upon the edge of an indenting chisel and under the pressure of a mallet, explaining their use for 'indenting the mind.' The ceremony concludes with a perambulation around the Lodge, during which the Master reads scripture passages concerning 'the stone which the builders refused/rejected' becoming 'the head stone of the corner.'

This section details the reception and obligation of a Mark Master Mason. The candidate undergoes a ritualistic perambulation, answering questions at the posts of the Junior and Senior Wardens, and the Right Worshipful Master. The core of the section is the extensive obligation, which includes promises of secrecy, adherence to Masonic law, obedience to summonses, and specific rules regarding the use, exchange, and redemption of a personal 'mark.' It also outlines severe penalties for violating the oath and concludes with a practical demonstration of how a brother's mark can be used to request financial aid.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0089 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Royal Arch: Exaltation Ceremony

The Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony details the admission process for candidates seeking further light in Masonry. After an alarm at the Chapter door, candidates, who have previously attained the degrees of Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master, confirm their free will, preparation, and proficiency. They present a pass word, RABBONI, before being admitted under a 'Living Arch' formed by companions. This arch involves physical prostration and difficulty in passage. Candidates then kneel at the altar for their seventh obligation, a solemn oath covering secrecy, protection of companions, non-revelation of ineffable characters or the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word (except under specific conditions), avoidance of clandestine chapters, and strict adherence to procedural requirements such as exalting exactly three candidates at a time and requiring nine Royal Arch Masons to open a Chapter.

This section details the extensive obligation of a Royal Arch Mason, covering secrecy, prerequisites for candidates (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master), rules for exaltation (three candidates at a time, nine Masons for opening a Chapter), conduct towards fellow Companions (no speaking evil, apprising of danger, preference in employment, assistance in difficulty, keeping secrets, aiding the indigent), and adherence to constitutional laws and by-laws. The obligation concludes with a severe penalty for violation. Following the obligation, the ritual describes the candidate kissing the book seven times and the Principal Sojourner's address, emphasizing the importance of the Royal Arch secrets and the necessity of trials through 'rough and rugged ways' to test fidelity. The candidates are then led around the Chapter and kneel for a prayer to the 'Supreme and inscrutable Architect of universal Nature'.

This section describes a prayer offered during the Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony, emphasizing gratitude for human reason, capacity for improvement, and the institution of Freemasonry. It highlights new obligations of virtue and holiness, reverence for God's name, and the diligent search for divine authority in nature. The prayer concludes with a plea for devotion, love, and charity, and for heavenly grace to assist in rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' after the 'first temple of purity and innocence' was destroyed by sin. Following the prayer, the Principal Sojourner leads candidates around the Chapter, reading the first six verses of Exodus chapter 3, which describe Moses encountering the burning bush on Mount Horeb. The candidates are then brought before a representation of the burning bush, and their blindfolds are removed.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->
practical_elements: The Tiler's Obligation: 'I, A. B., do hereby and hereon solemnly and sincerely swear that I have been regularly initiated as an Entered Apprentice, passed to the degree of Fellow Craft, and raised to the sublime degree of Master Mason, in a just and legally constituted Lodge of such, and that I am not suspended or expelled, but am now a Master Mason in good standing. So help me God.'

Candidate declaration: State if previously rejected by another Lodge, including which Lodge and when.

Grand Hailing Sign of Distress: Raise the hands as represented in Fig. 7 and drop them with spirit, repeated three times.
Words accompanying the Grand Hailing Sign of Distress (in night/dark): "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?"

Gavel raps for opening a Second Degree Lodge: Two raps only.
Gavel raps for opening a First Degree Lodge: One rap each, first by Master, then Senior Warden, lastly Junior Warden.

Master removes hat and recites Psalm 133: "Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity! It is like the precious ointment upon the head, that ran down upon the beard, even Aaron's beard; that went down to the skirts of his garments; as the dew of Hermon, and as the dew that descended upon the mountains of Zion: for there the Lord commanded the blessing, even life forever more." Amen!
Brethren respond: "Amen! So mote it be!"

The Senior Deacon confirms the candidate's qualifications, including being a man, free born, of good repute, and well recommended.

The Worshipful Master asks the candidate about his origin ('From whence come you') and destination ('whither are you travelling'), to which the Senior Deacon replies, 'From the west, and travelling toward the east.'
The Worshipful Master asks the reason for traveling from west to east, and the Senior Deacon replies, 'In search of light.'
The candidate is instructed to approach the east by advancing with one upright, regular step, placing the heel of his right foot in the hollow of his left, with his body erect at the altar.

entries.symbolic_meaning:
The sign of a Master Mason alludes to the penalty of the obligation of a Master Mason. The grand hailing sign of distress, accompanied by the words "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?", is given by the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, symbolizing a plea for aid in dire circumstances and a connection to the central legend of the degree. The scripture passage (Psalm 133) emphasizes the importance of unity among brethren.

The journey from the west to the east symbolizes the candidate's transition from a state of darkness or ignorance to a search for light, knowledge, and enlightenment within Masonry. The 'due form' for the obligation, involving kneeling on the naked left knee and forming a square with the right, signifies humility, readiness to receive instruction, and adherence to moral principles. The presence of the Holy Bible, square, and compasses during the obligation represents the Volume of Sacred Law as a guide for faith and morals, and the tools for circumscribing passions and squaring actions.

The compasses being hid by the square signifies the candidate's initial darkness or lack of full understanding in Masonry, having only received the Entered Apprentice degree. The three great lights (Holy Bible, square, and compasses) symbolize the rule and guide of faith and practice, the squaring of actions, and the circumscribing of passions to keep within moral bounds. The three lesser lights (sun, moon, and Master of the Lodge) represent the regular governance of the Lodge, mirroring the celestial bodies' rule over day and night. The candidate's desire for 'Light' symbolizes the pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment within Freemasonry. The removal of the hoodwink signifies the transition from ignorance to understanding, or darkness to light.

The exchange of the grip and the word "Boaz" serves as a fundamental means of recognition and confirmation of an Entered Apprentice's obligated status. The definition of signs as "right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars" symbolically represents principles of rectitude, balance, and uprightness in conduct. Tokens, as "friendly or brotherly grips," symbolize the fraternal bond and the ability to recognize fellow Masons under any circumstances.

The duegard and the sign of an Entered Apprentice symbolize the candidate's obligation and its associated penalty. The token and grip signify the commitment to conceal Masonic secrets. The word 'Boaz' serves as a password and a means to detect impostors. The lambskin or white apron is presented as an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason, described as more ancient and honorable than other prestigious orders. The metallic deposit, though of little intrinsic value, symbolizes a personal memento of the candidate's initiation into the Lodge.

The common gavel, typically used by operative Masons to remove superfluous corners from rough stones, is symbolically used by Free and Accepted Masons to divest their minds and consciences of all vices and superfluities of life. This process prepares them as 'living stones' for a 'spiritual building, that house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens'. The sign of an Entered Apprentice alludes to the penalty of the obligation.

The reprimand symbolizes the importance of diligence, skill, and integrity in one's work and the consequences of attempting to deceive or impose upon the Craft. The chisel and mallet, used by operative masons to shape stone, are symbolically employed by Free and Accepted Masons to 'hew, cut, carve, and indent the mind,' signifying the moral and intellectual refinement of the candidate. The reception upon the chisel and mallet represents the enduring impression of the Mark Master's lessons. The scripture passages about 'the stone which the builders refused' becoming 'the head stone of the corner' allude to the value of that which is initially rejected or overlooked, and its ultimate importance, a central theme of the Mark Master degree.

The four upright, regular steps forming a square symbolize precision, rectitude, and the candidate's commitment to moral and Masonic principles in their approach to sacred duties. The repeated questioning at various posts signifies the importance of consistency, truthfulness, and the candidate's unwavering affirmation of their commitment. The 'mark' itself is a profound symbol of a personal pledge, a bond of trust, and a form of security or credit within the fraternity, representing a brother's honor and commitment. Its specified value (half a shekel of silver or quarter of a dollar) gives it a tangible, albeit symbolic, economic weight. The severe penalties of having the right ear smitten off and the right hand chopped off symbolize the complete forfeiture of the ability to hear the sacred word and to perform Masonic work, representing the ultimate consequence for an impostor or oath-breaker.

The 'Living Arch' symbolizes the difficult and humbling path to exaltation, requiring perseverance and submission. The physical prostration and kneading represent the trials and purification necessary for advancement. Kneeling for the seventh time at the altar signifies the increasing solemnity and binding nature of the obligations as one progresses through Masonic degrees. The phrase 'he that humbleth himself shall be exalted' directly links the physical act of stooping low under the arch to the spiritual lesson of humility leading to higher understanding. The journey through darkness and crooked paths made straight ('I will bring the blind by a way they know not...') suggests divine guidance and enlightenment provided through the Masonic path.

The 'rough and rugged ways' and 'many trials' symbolize the challenges and tests of fidelity a candidate must undergo to prove their worthiness for the deeper secrets of the Royal Arch Degree. The 'stupendous arch of heaven' mentioned in the prayer symbolizes the divine architectural power of the Supreme Architect of universal Nature.

The prayer symbolizes a deep adoration for divine perfection, gratitude for the gifts of reason and improvement, and a commitment to virtue and holiness. The 'second temple of reformation' represents the spiritual rebuilding of the individual's character after the loss of original purity. The burning bush, where God appeared to Moses, symbolizes divine presence, revelation, and the sacred nature of the encounter, signifying a moment of profound spiritual awakening and instruction for the candidate.
entries.candidate_lesson:
Candidates are taught the importance of sincere and voluntary commitment to Masonry, free from ulterior motives. They must possess a genuine desire for knowledge and a commitment to service to humanity. Adherence to the ancient customs and usages of the fraternity is emphasized. Fundamental beliefs in God, the immortality of the soul, and the resurrection of the body are presented as prerequisites for membership. The tradition of seeking divine blessing before significant endeavors is also highlighted.

The candidate learns the specific signs and words associated with the Master Mason degree, particularly the solemnity of the obligation and the importance of recognizing and responding to a brother's distress. The ritual of opening the Lodge and the recitation of Psalm 133 reinforce the core Masonic value of unity and brotherly love, reminding members of the blessings that come from harmonious fellowship.

The candidate learns that entry into Masonry requires free will and a sincere desire for moral and intellectual improvement, symbolized by the search for light. They are taught the importance of the solemn obligation, which is presented as a commitment that aligns with, rather than conflicts with, their existing duties to God, family, country, and neighbor. The ritualistic posture for the oath emphasizes humility, reverence, and the foundational principles of morality and rectitude.

The candidate learns the profound importance of secrecy and fidelity to the Masonic obligation, understanding the severe consequences of its violation. They are introduced to fundamental Masonic symbols, such as the great and lesser lights and working tools, and their moral applications. The ritual emphasizes the pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment, symbolized by the request for 'Light,' and the concept of being bound by a 'stronger tie' than physical restraints, referring to the moral commitment of the obligation. The candidate also learns about brotherly love and favor through the Master's actions.

The candidate learns the specific grip, the method of revealing the word "Boaz," the duegard, and the definitions of Masonic signs and tokens. These elements are crucial for demonstrating their obligated status and for mutual recognition within the fraternity.

The candidate learns the solemnity of the Entered Apprentice obligation and the importance of maintaining secrecy regarding Masonic knowledge. They are taught the significance of the lambskin apron as a symbol of purity and honor within the fraternity. The ritual also imparts the tradition of contributing a personal item as a lasting reminder of their initiation.

The primary lesson for the candidate is to learn to subdue their passions and improve themselves in Masonry. This is achieved by symbolically using the common gavel to cleanse the mind and conscience of vices, thereby becoming a suitable 'living stone' for a spiritual edifice.

The candidate is taught the critical importance of performing work with skill and industry, and the ethical principle of not claiming rewards or wages that are not rightfully earned. The reprimand serves as a 'striking lesson' against imposition upon the Craft. The symbolic use of the chisel and mallet emphasizes the continuous process of self-improvement and the shaping of one's character and mind. The scripture readings impart a lesson on the potential for the rejected to become essential, encouraging perseverance and recognizing inherent worth.

The candidate learns the paramount importance of strict secrecy regarding the Mark Master Mason's secrets, disclosing them only to duly qualified brethren after rigorous examination. They are taught the duty to uphold the Constitutions of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter and State Grand Royal Arch Chapter, as well as the by-laws of any Mark Master Lodge. The obligation instills obedience to all regular signs and summonses from fellow Mark Master Masons or Lodges, within the bounds of their cable-tow. A strong ethical lesson is imparted regarding not wronging a brother or the Lodge, even to the smallest value, and preventing such wrongs if possible. The unique rules surrounding the 'mark' teach about personal integrity, the sanctity of a pledge, and the responsibility to honor commitments related to one's mark, including its redemption. The lesson of mutual aid is emphasized through the promise to receive a brother's mark when a favor is requested and to grant it if possible, or to return its specified value if the favor cannot be granted.

Candidates learn the importance of humility and perseverance in their Masonic journey, symbolized by the 'Living Arch.' They are taught the paramount importance of secrecy regarding the Royal Arch degree's secrets, including its ineffable characters and the Grand Omnific Word. The obligation reinforces loyalty to the Chapter and fellow companions, prohibiting wronging them or speaking ill of them, and requiring warning of danger. Candidates are instructed on the strict procedural requirements for the Royal Arch degree, including prerequisites, the number of candidates for exaltation, and the quorum for opening a Chapter. The repeated obligations emphasize the cumulative and increasingly binding nature of Masonic commitments, always subordinate to duties to country and Maker.

The candidate is taught the profound importance of fidelity, secrecy, and mutual support within the Royal Arch. They learn the strict procedural requirements for the degree and the extensive moral and fraternal duties expected of a Royal Arch Mason, including charity, assistance, and adherence to Masonic law. The journey through 'rough and rugged ways' emphasizes that true understanding and higher secrets are earned through perseverance and proven commitment.

Candidates are taught the importance of gratitude, humility, and adherence to new and stronger obligations of virtue and holiness. They are encouraged to seek divine wisdom in the 'book of nature' and to allow the solemnity of the ceremonies to profoundly impact their lives. The narrative of Moses and the burning bush instructs them in reverence for God's 'great, mighty, and terrible Name,' inspiring devotion, love, and charity, and emphasizing the continuous process of personal reformation and spiritual rebuilding.
tradition_notes: It has been a custom from time immemorial, among good men, before engaging in any great or important undertaking, first to invoke a blessing of Deity.
Candidates are required to cheerfully conform to all the ancient established usages and customs of the fraternity.
The grand hailing sign of distress is specifically linked to the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff.
The specific sequence of gavel raps by the Master and Wardens is a traditional method for opening Lodges on different degrees.
The recitation of Psalm 133 and the response "Amen! So mote it be!" are traditional elements of the Lodge opening ceremony.
The Lodge is described as 'erected to God, and dedicated to the holy St. John,' reflecting a traditional Masonic dedication.
The phrase 'as all brothers and fellows have done before' emphasizes the continuity and tradition of the Masonic initiation process.
Some Masters use the phrase 'As we now hold the brother by a stronger tie' when the cable-tow is removed, indicating a symbolic shift from physical restraint to moral commitment.
The text notes that the old way of spelling the word "Boaz," as represented by Morgan, Craft, Allyn, Richardson, and Barnard, was by syllabling it.
Lodges differ in their post-obligation procedure: some pass the candidate once around the room, while others require a halt at the Wardens' stations for a specific ceremony.
The lambskin apron is described as 'more ancient than the Golden Fleece or Roman Eagle' and 'more honorable than the Star and Garter'.
Entered Apprentices wore their aprons with the flap turned up 'at the building of King Solomon's Temple'.
The request for a metallic deposit is an 'ancient custom, adopted among Masons'.
The origin of the Lodge is stated as 'From a Lodge of the Sts. John of Jerusalem', a traditional Masonic reference.
The severe reprimand ordered to be given to the candidate is omitted in most Lodges at the present day.
The Lodge is erected to Almighty God and dedicated to Hiram the Builder.
The obligation is stated to be 'in addition to my former obligations,' indicating a progressive system of degrees.

The obligation explicitly mentions supporting the 'Constitution of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America' and the 'Grand Royal Arch Chapter of this State,' placing the Mark Master degree within the American Royal Arch system.
The 'mark' chosen by a brother must be recorded in a 'book of marks.'
The value of a brother's mark, when a favor cannot be granted, is specified as 'half a shekel of silver, or quarter of a dollar,' linking ancient and modern monetary equivalents.
The Royal Arch degree is referred to as the 'august sublime Degree of the Holy Royal Arch'.
Candidates must have received the degrees of Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master as prerequisites.
The Chapter is dedicated to Zerubbabel.
The obligation taken is the seventh in the candidate's Masonic journey.
The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken by three companions, by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.
Exaltations must involve exactly three candidates at a time.
A minimum of nine Royal Arch Masons are required to form or open a Chapter.
The Royal Arch secrets are considered 'infinitely more importance than any that precede it'.
The obligation is taken 'under a Living Arch, and at low breath'.
The General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America and State Grand Chapters are recognized authorities.
The concept of 'cable-tow' is applied to the Royal Arch degree for answering summonses.
The reading from Exodus chapter 3, verses 1-6, is a specific part of the ritual, timed to coincide with the candidates' arrival at the burning bush representation.
The concept of rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' is a significant allegorical theme within this degree, contrasting with a 'first temple of purity and innocence' lost through sin.
caution_notes: Candidates are cautioned to declare if they have been previously rejected by another Lodge.
The declarations serve to ensure the candidate's motives are pure and not influenced by improper solicitation or mercenary reasons.

The practice of brethren stamping their feet and clapping their hands once at the word 'light' is noted as 'nearly done away with now-a-days' due to being 'Too much "Morganry" about it, as it is styled by Masons.'
The spelling of 'Boaz' (always beginning with 'A') is noted as a way Masons detect impostors, such as 'Morgan or book Masons'.

I will not give the secrets of a Mark Master Mason to any one of an inferior degree, nor to any other person in the known world, except it be a true and lawful brother, or brethren, of this degree; and not unto him nor unto them whom I shall hear so to be, but unto him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Lodge, or a brother of this Degree, to the value of his wages (or one penny), myself, knowingly, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.

I will not sell, swap, barter, or exchange my mark, which I shall hereafter choose, after it has been recorded in the book of marks, for any other one, unless it be a dead mark, or one of an older date, nor will I pledge it a second time until it is lawfully redeemed from the first pledge.

Binding myself under no less penalty than to have my right ear smitten off, that I may forever be unable to hear the word, and my right hand chopped off, as the penalty of an impostor, if I should ever prove wilfully guilty of violating any part of this my solemn oath, or obligation, of a Mark Master Mason.

I will not reveal the secrets of this Degree to any of an inferior Degree, nor to any being in the known world, except it be to a true and lawful Companion Royal Arch Mason, or within the body of a just and legally constituted Chapter of such and never unto him, or them, whom I shall hear so to be, but to him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Chapter of Royal Arch Masons, or a companion of this Degree, out of the value of any thing, myself, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.
I will not reveal the key to the ineffable characters of this Degree, nor retain it in my possession, but will destroy it whenever it comes to my sight.

I will not speak the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word, which I shall hereafter receive, in any manner, except in that in which I shall receive it, which will be in the presence of three Companions Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of the number; and then by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.

I will not be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter, nor converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or with one who has been expelled or suspended, while under that sentence.

I will not assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate to this Degree, who has not received the Degrees of: Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master.
I will not be at the exaltation of more nor less than three candidates at one and the same time.
I will not be at the forming or opening of a Chapter of Royal Arch Masons unless there be present nine Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of that number.
I will not speak evil of a Companion Royal Arch Mason, behind his back nor before his face, but will apprise him of all approaching danger, if in my power.
Penalty for violating the obligation: 'skull smote off, and my brains exposed to the scorching rays of the meridian sun'.
Not to be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter.
Not to converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or one who has been expelled or suspended.
Not to assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate who has not received the prerequisite degrees.
Not to strike a Companion Royal Arch Mason so as to draw his blood, in anger.

entries.title: Signs, Tokens and Words in Masonry
entries.short_summary: This section introduces the fundamental Masonic elements of signs, tokens, and words, explaining their dual purpose as modes of recognition among Masons and as carriers of symbolic meaning intended to enrich the mind and improve their lives.
entries.full_summary:
This section introduces the fundamental Masonic elements of signs, tokens, and words, explaining their dual purpose as modes of recognition among Masons and as carriers of symbolic meaning intended to enrich the mind and improve their lives.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0045 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brother A. B.

This section, addressed to a newly initiated Brother A. B., congratulates him on joining the ancient and honorable fraternity of Freemasonry. It outlines the fundamental duties of a Mason towards God, his neighbor, and himself, emphasizing reverence, acting upon the square, and avoiding intemperance. It also details civic responsibilities, proper outward demeanor, the importance of lodge attendance without neglecting vocations, and the caution against arguing with those who ridicule the Institution. The text encourages seeking further Masonic knowledge from well-informed brethren and stresses the importance of carefully vetting potential candidates. Finally, it charges the new Mason to keep the mysteries of the Institution sacred and inviolable.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0045 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0082 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Guarded by the Tiler’s Sword

The Tiler's Sword serves as a reminder for Masons to maintain vigilance and discretion in their thoughts, words, and actions, especially when in the presence of the uninitiated, upholding the Masonic virtues of silence and circumspection.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0082 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0128 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Senior Warden

This section details the installation ceremony of the Senior Warden, including the Marshal's presentation, the oath of office, and the charge delivered by the Installing Officer. The charge emphasizes the symbolic meaning of the Level, representing equality and the transient nature of worldly distinctions, and outlines the Senior Warden's duties, such as regular attendance and governing the Lodge in the Master's absence.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0128 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0168 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: V. Organization of Institution

The Masonic institution is fundamentally organized into three Symbolic Degrees. These degrees are characterized by unique modes of recognition, specific ceremonial forms, and notably, the Legend of the Third Degree. All these elements are preserved through the inherent secrecy of the Institution.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0168 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0027 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fellow Craft: Winding Stairs, Middle Chamber, Shibboleth

This section reviews key elements of the Entered Apprentice degree, including the candidate's return to the Lodge, placement in the northeast corner, and the presentation of the working tools: the twenty-four-inch gauge and the common gavel, along with their operative and speculative meanings. It also introduces the new name 'Caution', the 'Three Precious Jewels' (a listening ear, a silent tongue, and a faithful heart), and the Grand Master's check-word 'Truth', explaining the moral lessons associated with each. The text notes that this content generally concludes the first section of the lecture, distinguishing between contemporary and older lecture formats.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0027 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0031 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Charge and Commentary

This section presents an Entered Apprentice Charge and commentary, focusing on the 'points of profession' (Brotherly Love, Relief, and Truth) and the 'points of entrance' (Guttural, Pectoral, Manual, and Pedestal). Each point of entrance is explicitly linked to one of the four cardinal virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice, with detailed explanations of their Masonic application and the importance of concealing secrets. It also touches upon how Entered Apprentices served their Master in ancient and modern times, represented by Chalk, Charcoal, and Clay.

This section details the Entered Apprentice's oath, emphasizing the importance of remembering it while kneeling at the altar with the Holy Bible, square, and compasses. It highlights the virtue of Justice as a fundamental Masonic principle, essential for civil society and the conduct of a good man. The charge received in the northeast corner of the Lodge is mentioned, alluding to the Pedestal. The text also explains how Entered Apprentices served their Master in ancient and modern times, characterized by freedom, fervency, and zeal, symbolically represented by chalk, charcoal, and clay.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0031 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0033 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Rough Ashler

This section introduces the concepts of the Rough Ashler and Perfect Ashler, fundamental symbols in Freemasonry. It is accompanied by footnotes that elaborate on specific Masonic traditions and terminology. These include the practice of the Master wearing a hat in certain lodges, a detailed etymological and symbolic explanation of the term 'cowan' as an unqualified or profane intruder, and a description of the solemn Lodge Opening ceremony, emphasizing duties, obligations, and precautions against the profane.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0033 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0034 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Rough Ashler

This section discusses fundamental aspects of Masonic practice, including the concept of Masonry as a cohesive whole where each Mason plays a part. It details the solemn procedure of opening a Lodge, emphasizing officer duties, and the protocol for a Mason entering an opened Lodge, which involves approaching the altar, facing the Worshipful Master, and giving the duegard and sign of the degree. The text also highlights a mandatory emblematical feature that every initiated Mason must undergo as an indispensable pledge of fidelity, regardless of personal objections, noting its ancient and universal nature.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0034 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Historical Account.

This section provides a detailed historical account of the murder of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, a pivotal event in the Master Mason degree. It describes a conspiracy by fifteen, then three, Fellow Crafts (Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum) to extort the Master Mason's secrets for wages and travel. Hiram Abiff's daily ritual of offering adorations and drawing designs in the Sanctum Sanctorum is detailed, as are his attempts to escape through the south, west, and east gates, where he was successively accosted and struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul, leading to his death. The ruffians buried his body west of Mount Moriah, marking the grave with an acacia. King Solomon's subsequent discovery of Hiram's absence, the confession of the twelve recanting Fellow Crafts, and the organized search for the ruffians are recounted. The ruffians were eventually found near Joppa after their self-imprecations were overheard, apprehended, and executed. The narrative concludes with the discovery of Hiram's putrefied body by the twelve Fellow Crafts, noting the specific gesture of the Master Mason's duegard.

This section details the discovery and raising of Hiram Abiff's body after his murder. It describes the failed attempts to raise the body using the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips due to decomposition, and the successful raising by King Solomon using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, on the five points of fellowship. The narrative includes the agreement between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, to use the first sigh and word spoken at the grave as a temporary Master's word until the true one is found. The text also describes the three burials of Hiram Abiff and the traditional Masonic monument erected to his memory, detailing its symbolic elements: a weeping virgin, a broken column, an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Father Time.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: The Beehive

This section describes several Masonic emblems and their moral and philosophical lessons. It begins with the Beehive, symbolizing industry, social dependence, and the importance of contributing to the common stock of knowledge. It then discusses the Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, which emphasizes watchfulness, silence, and circumspection, especially against enemies of Masonry. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart and the All-Seeing Eye are presented as symbols of divine justice and omnipresence, reminding Masons that all thoughts, words, and actions are observed and will be judged. Finally, the Anchor and Ark are explained as emblems of well-grounded hope and a well-spent life, guiding one through life's troubles to a peaceful harbor. The section also briefly mentions the Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid and its connection to Pythagoras.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0130 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Appendix: Notes and Authorities

This section provides an appendix of notes and authorities, covering various aspects of the Master Mason degree. It details the strong grip of a Master Mason, the three Grand Masonic Pillars (Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty) and their representation by Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff. It also describes the construction of the Temple, including the number of columns, pilasters, and workers by degree. The text explains the symbolism of the three steps on the Master's carpet and the nine classes of Master's emblems, specifically focusing on the setting maul, spade, coffin, and sprig of acacia as emblems of morality and immortality. Finally, it includes a historical note on the transmission and abridgment of Masonic lectures by figures like Webb, Preston, Gleason, and J. Browne.

This section, an address by M. W. P. Al. Tucker, Grand Master of Vermont, defends the authenticity and lineage of Masonic lectures, specifically those transmitted through T. S. Webb and Benjamin Gleason, asserting their connection to Preston's original lectures. Tucker addresses criticisms regarding his previous statements, clarifying that Webb abridged and rearranged Preston's lectures but retained their substance. He cites his possession of 'key' copies of both Webb's abridgment and Preston's full lectures, believed to be true manuscripts as taught by Gleason. Tucker also defends the claim that Gleason's lectures were approved by the Grand Lodge of England, referencing Gleason's own published letter from 1843, where Gleason states he acquired complete knowledge of the three degrees' lectures from Webb and successfully communicated them across the United States and Europe, 'according to the ancient landmarks,' without objection. He further supports Gleason's knowledge of Preston by quoting Gleason's 1812 oration, which praises Preston's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and his 'estimable system of improvements'. The discussion also mentions J. Browne's 1802 'Master Key' as a presumed source for Prestonian lectures.

This section discusses the historical presence and influence of Preston's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and the 'Prestonian system of work and lectures' in the United States, particularly in New England. It highlights Benjamin Gleason's crucial role, alongside Thomas Smith Webb, in introducing and promulgating this system. The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts adopted the Preston ritual as its standard in 1804, employing Gleason to communicate it to lodges under its jurisdiction. Gleason continued as a dedicated lecturer until his death in 1847, remembered as a master teacher of ancient Craft Masonry. The author defends the existence of Prestonian lectures in the US against a critic, emphasizing the importance of factual investigation over mere negation.

This section discusses the history and transmission of the Prestonian system of Masonic ritual. It notes that the system, as revived by Preston and sanctioned by the Grand Lodge of England, underwent a revision in 1813 for reconciliation purposes, which led to some incongruities. The original Prestonian ritual was brought to the United States around 1803 by two English brethren, one of whom was a pupil of Preston. It was communicated to Thomas Smith Webb and subsequently imparted to Brother Gleason, who then taught it to lodges in Massachusetts. The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts approved and adopted this system, which has since been the recognized Masonic work in the Commonwealth. Despite some modifications and innovations, the ritual has been largely preserved in its original purity, especially in older jurisdictions. The text also highlights the ongoing struggle against corrupting influences from ignorant itinerant lecturers and spurious publications, emphasizing the importance of preserving the ritual's integrity.

This section discusses the nature and importance of Masonic lectures, specifically comparing Preston's lengthy lectures with Webb's abridgment. The author, through the Grand Lecturer's opinion, suggests that Webb's version retains practical value and is more manageable. The text criticizes those who undervalue lectures, labeling proponents of their preservation as 'parrot Masons' and advocating for a 'new era' focused solely on the philosophy, history, and symbolism of Masonry, arguing that these elements are already embedded within the traditional lectures.
