practical_elements: Modes of recognition
Implore God's aid in all laudable undertakings.
Act upon the square and do unto your neighbor as you wish them to do unto you.
Avoid all irregularity and intemperance.
Be a quiet and peaceful citizen, true to your government, and just to your country.
Patiently submit to legal authority and conform to the government of your country.
Be particularly careful to avoid censure or reproach in your outward demeanor.
Attend regular lodge meetings frequently.
Do not let Masonry interfere with necessary vocations.
Do not suffer zeal for the Institution to lead into argument with those who ridicule it.
Converse with well-informed brethren to improve in Masonic knowledge.
Do not recommend a person for initiation unless convinced they will conform to Masonic rules.
Keep sacred and inviolable the mysteries of the Institution.
The Tiler's Sword is an implement that guards the entrance to the Lodge.
The Marshal presents the Senior Warden-elect to the Installing Officer.
The Senior Warden-elect takes a solemn promise upon the honor of a Mason to serve the Lodge and comply with Grand Lodge laws and ancient Masonic usages.
The Senior Warden is invested with the badge of office.
The Installing Officer delivers a charge to the Senior Warden.
The Senior Warden is instructed to attend stated meetings regularly.
The Senior Warden is to govern the Lodge in the Master's absence and assist him in his presence.
The Senior Warden is conducted to his appointed station while a song is sung.
Peculiar modes of recognition are used within the degrees.
Certain forms of ceremonies are conducted as part of the degrees.
Candidate returned to the Lodge and reinvested.
Candidate placed in the northeast corner, feet forming a right angle, body erect, at the right hand of the Worshipful Master.
Presentation of the twenty-four-inch gauge and common gavel as working tools.
Presentation of a new name: Caution.
Presentation of three precious jewels: a listening ear, a silent tongue, and a faithful heart.
Presentation of the Grand Master's check-word: Truth.
The four points of entrance are Guttural, Pectoral, Manual, and Pedestal.
The Guttural alludes to the Mason's obligation.
The Pectoral is emblematically represented by a sharp instrument pressing the naked left breast upon first admission.
The Manual alludes to the position during the oath: kneeling on the naked left knee at the altar, left hand supporting the Holy Bible, square, and compasses, and the right hand resting thereon.
The Pedestal alludes to the charge received while standing in the northeast corner of the Lodge, with feet forming a right angle.
Kneeling at the altar on the naked left knee
Left hand supporting the Holy Bible, square, and compasses
Right hand resting on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses
Standing in the northeast corner of the Lodge with feet forming a right angle
In most foreign Lodges, the Master wears his hat while the rest of the brethren remain uncovered, a practice followed by MacKenzie Beverly Esq., D. P. G. M. for the East Riding of York.
The ceremony of OPENING THE LODGE is solemn and impressive, reminding every brother of his duties and obligations.
Necessary precautions are employed during the Lodge Opening to avoid the intrusion of the profane.
Every member is compelled to assume a share of responsibility during the Lodge Opening ceremony.
Opening the Lodge is a solemn and imposing rite.
Every officer is made acquainted with his duty and impressed with its importance.
When a Mason enters an opened Lodge, he proceeds to the center of the Lodge, at the altar.
The Mason must face the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Mason must give the duegard and sign of the degree in which the Lodge is working.
The duegard is never omitted when the Master is addressed.
Every initiated person is bound to pass through an emblematical feature as an unmistakable pledge of fidelity.
This emblematical feature is an indispensable condition of Masonic tenure and cannot be avoided.
The Master Mason candidate represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff during the exemplification of the degree.
Hiram Abiff's daily practice involved entering the unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The three ruffians positioned themselves at the south, west, and east gates of the inner courts of the Temple.
Hiram Abiff was struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge across his throat, a square across his breast, and a setting-maul on his forehead.
The ruffians buried Hiram's body in the rubbish of the Temple until low-twelve, then moved it to a grave dug due east and west, six feet perpendicular, west of Mount Moriah.
An acacia was planted at the head of Hiram's grave to conceal it and mark the place.
The twelve recanting Fellow Crafts appeared before King Solomon clothed in white gloves and aprons as a token of their innocence.
Search parties were divided into groups of three, traveling east, west, north, and south.
The Master places his hands in the form of a duegard of a Master Mason, alluding to the manner in which Hiram's body was found.
Attempted raising of the body using the Entered Apprentice grip
Attempted raising of the body using the Fellow Craft grip
Successful raising of the body using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw
Raising on the five points of fellowship
Explanation of the several signs and tokens of the Master Mason Degree, commencing with the first and ending with the grips (referencing Figs. 5, 6, 7, 16, 17 and Note L, Appendix)
The strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, is mentioned.
The name associated with the strong grip cannot be given except on the five (5) points of fellowship and heard in a low breath.
The instruction to 'Advance and give it' refers to the word associated with the grip.
The word is 'right'.
Promulgating the Prestonian system of work and lectures.
Communicating the Preston ritual to lodges under Grand Lodge jurisdiction.
Teaching Masonic ritual as a lecturer.
Adoption of a ritual standard by a Grand Lodge.
The Prestonian system of ritual was brought to the United States around 1803.
The ritual was communicated to Webb and then imparted to Gleason.
Brother Gleason was employed to teach the ritual to lodges in Massachusetts.
The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts approved and adopted this system.
The ritual has been preserved in a remarkable degree of purity in Massachusetts lodges, particularly in the lodge where Gleason was Master in 1809.
The system underwent modifications in its general arrangement and adaptation after its introduction to the US.
Grand Lecturer compared Preston and Gleason Lectures
Preston Lectures are lengthy, estimated to cover nearly one hundred pages of foolscap paper if written out in full
Requirement for Masons to commit lectures in extenso is considered a difficult task
Webb's abridgment and new arrangement of lectures are considered to preserve substantial practical value and use preferable language
entries.symbolic_meaning:
Each sign, token, and word in Masonry possesses a symbolic meaning that serves to enrich the mind and improve the lives of Masons.

The phrase "acting upon the square" symbolically represents living a life of integrity, fairness, and moral rectitude in dealings with others, aligning with the Masonic principle of upright conduct.

The Tiler's Sword symbolizes the constant need for Masons to be watchful and guarded in their conduct, particularly concerning Masonic secrets and practices when outside the Lodge or before those not initiated.

The Level symbolizes that all men are descended from the same stock, share the same nature and hope, and that despite necessary distinctions for subordination, all are brethren. It also represents death as the 'grand leveler of human greatness,' reducing all to the same state where only goodness distinguishes individuals.

The twenty-four-inch gauge symbolizes the division of time into portions for the service of God and relief of a distressed brother, usual avocations, and refreshment/sleep. The common gavel symbolizes divesting the mind and conscience of vices and superfluities, fitting one as a living stone for a spiritual building. The new name 'Caution' teaches carefulness in words and actions, especially before enemies of Masonry. The listening ear symbolizes heeding instructions and the cries of a distressed brother. The silent tongue symbolizes maintaining peace and harmony in the Lodge and discretion before enemies. The faithful heart symbolizes keeping and concealing Masonic secrets and those of a brother. Truth is explained as a divine attribute, the foundation of virtue, and a principle guiding conduct, promoting sincerity and plain-dealing.

The Guttural, Pectoral, Manual, and Pedestal points of entrance symbolize the four cardinal virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice. Temperance signifies due restraint on passions to avoid excess and protect secrets. Fortitude represents a steady purpose to endure pain or danger, safeguarding entrusted secrets against illegal attacks. Prudence teaches regulation of life by reason, ensuring wise judgment and careful conduct, especially in mixed company, to prevent unlawful disclosure of Masonic secrets. Justice is the standard of right, rendering to every man his due, and is the cement of civil society, requiring Masons to never deviate from its principles. Chalk, Charcoal, and Clay represent freedom, fervency, and zeal, respectively, in how Entered Apprentices serve their Master.

The position of the hands on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses alludes to the Manual. The charge in the northeast corner of the Lodge, with feet forming a right angle, is an allusion to the Pedestal. Chalk symbolizes freedom, leaving a trace with the slightest touch. Charcoal symbolizes fervency, capable of melting obdurate metals when well lit. Clay, or mother earth, symbolizes zeal, in its ability to bring forth.

The Rough Ashler symbolizes man in his rude and unpolished state, while the Perfect Ashler represents man in a state of moral and intellectual refinement achieved through education and discipline. The term 'cowan' is symbolically linked to 'dogs' (κυνες) or 'evil workers' in biblical contexts, representing unqualified or profane listeners and intruders in a Masonic setting, emphasizing the need for vigilance and purity within the Lodge.

Masonry is presented as a whole, with each Mason forming an integral part. The 'emblematical feature' serves as a profound pledge of fidelity, drawing an analogy to the customs of primitive nations and suggesting an ancient, unfathomable origin.

The narrative exemplifies virtue, fortitude, and integrity through Hiram Abiff's unwavering refusal to betray his trust and the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death. The acacia planted at the grave symbolizes remembrance and potentially immortality. The entire account serves as a profound allegory for fidelity, the consequences of treachery, and the eventual triumph of justice.

The beautiful virgin weeping over the broken column symbolizes the unfinished state of the Temple and the untimely death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The open book before her signifies that his virtues are perpetually recorded. The sprig of acacia in her right hand represents the divinity of the body. The urn in her left hand indicates that his ashes were safely deposited near the Sanctum Sanctorum. Time, unfolding the ringlets of her hair, denotes that time, patience, and perseverance accomplish all things. The failure of the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips to raise the body, and the success of the Master Mason grip, symbolizes the progression of knowledge and the ultimate power of the Master's degree.

The Beehive symbolizes industry, mutual dependence, and the necessity for all rational beings to contribute to society and knowledge, avoiding idleness. The Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, represents the need for vigilance, discretion, and protection of Masonic principles against external threats. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart signifies that divine justice will ultimately prevail, revealing all hidden thoughts and actions. The All-Seeing Eye embodies the omnipresence and omniscience of the Great Creator, who observes the innermost recesses of the human heart and rewards according to one's deeds. The Anchor and Ark are emblems of a steadfast hope and a virtuous life, symbolizing safe passage through life's challenges to a state of eternal peace and rest.

The three grand masonic pillars, Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty, are represented by Solomon, King of Israel (wisdom to contrive), Hiram, King of Tyre (strength to support), and Hiram Abiff (beauty to adorn). The three steps on the Master's carpet are emblematic of the three principal stages of human life: youth, manhood, and age. The setting maul, spade, and coffin are striking emblems of mortality. The sprig of acacia symbolizes the imperishable part of man that survives the grave, bearing affinity to the Supreme Intelligence that pervades all nature and never dies.

The text implies that Masonic lectures are a primary vehicle for conveying Masonic symbolism, arguing against the idea that symbolism exists independently or is better understood by dismissing the lectures.
entries.candidate_lesson:
Candidates should understand that Masonic signs, tokens, and words are not only for identification but also convey profound symbolic lessons designed to foster personal growth and moral improvement.

The candidate is taught the foundational principles of Masonic conduct, encompassing moral, ethical, civic, and fraternal duties. Key lessons include reverence for God, justice and charity towards others, self-control, loyalty to one's country, discretion in public and private life, and the importance of preserving the integrity and reputation of the Craft through personal conduct and careful selection of new members. The inviolability of Masonic mysteries is also presented as a distinguishing characteristic.

Masons are taught to exercise silence and circumspection in all their dealings, especially regarding Masonic matters, to protect the integrity of the Craft and its teachings from the uninitiated.

The Senior Warden is reminded that no station of eminence should make one forget the brotherhood of man, as all are equal in the eyes of a Mason and ultimately in death. The importance of duty, knowledge of Masonry, and attachment to the Lodge for the faithful discharge of trust are highlighted. The lesson also includes the transient nature of worldly distinctions and the ultimate equality brought by death.

Candidates are taught to divide their time wisely, to purify their minds of vices, to be cautious in their conduct and speech, especially regarding Masonic matters, to listen attentively to instruction and the needs of others, to maintain silence and harmony, and to be faithful in guarding Masonic secrets and a brother's trust. The overarching lesson is to be good and true, regulating conduct by the dictates of truth, fostering sincerity and mutual welfare.

An Entered Apprentice must practice Temperance by restraining affections and passions, avoiding excess and vicious habits to protect Masonic secrets. Fortitude is essential to withstand any attempts to extort secrets. Prudence guides conduct in and out of the Lodge, ensuring no sign, token, or word is revealed unlawfully. Justice is a fundamental principle, requiring Masons to render to every man his just due and never deviate from its minutest principles. The candidate is taught to serve with freedom, fervency, and zeal.

The Entered Apprentice must always remember their oath. The virtue of Justice is paramount, serving as the standard of right, ensuring fairness to all, and acting as the foundation of civil society. Masons should never deviate from the principles of justice. Apprentices are taught to serve with freedom, fervency, and zeal, embodying these qualities in their Masonic and daily lives.

Candidates are taught the importance of continuous moral and intellectual self-improvement, striving to transform from a 'Rough Ashler' to a 'Perfect Ashler'. They learn the necessity of upholding their duties and obligations within the Lodge and the critical importance of guarding the sanctity and privacy of Masonic proceedings from the profane and unqualified.

Candidates are taught that Masonry is a serious and cohesive institution requiring adherence to its forms and procedures. They must understand the importance of officer duties and the solemnity of Lodge operations. Furthermore, they learn that certain 'emblematical features' are mandatory and indispensable conditions for being a Mason, serving as a pledge of fidelity, and must be endured with patience, regardless of personal feelings.

The candidate is taught the paramount importance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity, as demonstrated by Grand Master Hiram Abiff's ultimate sacrifice. It underscores the solemnity of Masonic obligations and the sacredness of its secrets, emphasizing that these principles must be upheld at all costs. The narrative also conveys lessons on the inevitable consequences of betrayal and the pursuit of justice.

The candidate learns about the profound loss of the Master's word and the temporary substitute established. The narrative emphasizes the importance of the Master Mason's strong grip and the five points of fellowship in restoring what was lost. The monument's symbolism teaches lessons of virtue, remembrance, the transient nature of life, and the enduring power of patience and perseverance.

Candidates are taught the importance of industry, social responsibility, and mutual support, emphasizing that they should not be 'drones' but actively contribute to the common good and knowledge. They are instructed to be watchful, guarded in their conduct, and to practice silence and circumspection, particularly when facing those hostile to Masonry. The emblems also impart the lesson of divine justice and accountability, urging Masons to live righteously, knowing that all their actions and thoughts are observed by a higher power. Finally, they are encouraged to cultivate a well-grounded hope and lead a well-spent life, anticipating a peaceful end to their earthly journey.

Candidates are encouraged to imitate Grand Master Hiram Abiff in his virtuous conduct, unfeigned piety to God, and inflexible fidelity to his trust. This imitation prepares them to welcome death as a kind messenger from the Supreme Grand Master, translating them from an imperfect lodge to a perfect, glorious, and celestial lodge above, presided over by the Supreme Architect of the universe.

The candidate should understand that Masonic lectures are a vital source for learning the history, philosophy, and symbolism of Freemasonry, and that their preservation and study are crucial for a complete understanding of the Craft, rather than being dismissed as mere rote memorization.
tradition_notes: Freemasonry is described as an ancient fraternity, having existed from time immemorial.
The Institution is founded on excellent rules and useful maxims inculcated in Masonic lectures.
The greatest and best of men in all ages have been encouragers and promoters of the art, extending privileges and patronizing assemblies.
The Senior Warden's promise includes strictly complying with 'ancient Masonic usages'.
The organization of Freemasonry into three Symbolic Degrees is a foundational tradition.
The use of peculiar modes of recognition and specific ceremonial forms are traditional aspects of the degrees.
The preservation of the institution's elements through secrecy is a long-standing tradition.
This content generally ends the first section of the lecture as given in Lodges at the present day.
Some Lodges still persist in keeping up the old lecture as revealed by William Morgan in 1826, and by Bernard, Allyn, Richardson, and others.
The interrogations and answers regarding metallic kind, reinvestment, and northeast corner are embodied in the 'new-fangled lecture'.
Some Masters qualify for office by purchasing and amending Masonic exposures like Richardson's or Avery Allyn's.
The lecture presented is used in some Lodges, especially in the Northwestern States.
The text references 'Masonic Monitors, on "Brotherly Love, Relief, and Truth."' and 'Webb's Monitor' for further information.
Reference to how Entered Apprentices served their Master in ancient times and how they should in modern times.
The representation of apprentices by Chalk, Charcoal, and Clay is attributed to Webb's Monitor.
In most foreign Lodges, the Master wears his hat while the rest of the brethren remain uncovered, a practice historically followed by figures like MacKenzie Beverly Esq.
The term 'cowan' originated from the affair of Jephthah, where an Ephraimite was termed a cowan.
Historically, 'cohen' in Egypt was a title of honor for a priest or prince, but also meant 'dog,' leading to symbolic associations with 'dogs of Jove' and later, unqualified intruders in Masonry.
Opening the Lodge is described as a 'solemn and imposing rite.'
The 'emblematical feature' bears a 'beautiful analogy to the customs of all primitive nations' and its origin 'may be reasonably ascribed to some unfathomable antiquity.'
It was Hiram Abiff's usual practice to enter the unfinished 'Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies,' at high twelve to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, were believed to be brothers and men from Tyre.
The Master Mason's secrets were traditionally believed to allow travel in foreign countries and the reception of Master's wages.
Hiram Abiff's body was buried 'in a grave dug due east and west, six feet, perpendicular, at the head of which they planted an acacia'.
The twelve Fellow Crafts who recanted their murderous designs appeared before King Solomon 'clothed in white gloves and aprons, in token of their innocence'.
The specific imprecations uttered by the ruffians (e.g., 'Oh! that my throat had been cut across') are part of the traditional narrative.
The 'duegard of a Master Mason' gesture is explicitly linked to the discovery of Hiram Abiff's body.

It was agreed between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, that the first sigh given at the grave and the first word spoken after the body was raised would be used as the Master's word until the right one is found.
Masonic tradition informs us of a monument erected to Hiram Abiff's memory, consisting of a beautiful virgin weeping over a broken column, with an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Time.
The Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid is attributed to our ancient friend and brother, the great Pythagoras, who was initiated into several orders during his travels through Asia, Africa, and Europe.

The text includes an extract from an Annual Address by M. W. P. Al. Tucker, G. M. of Vermont, discussing the history and transmission of Masonic lectures, specifically noting Webb's abridgment and rearrangement of Preston's lectures.
It mentions that Webb learned and taught Preston's lectures in full, as well as his own abridgment.
Reference is made to a 'copy in key' of both Webb's abridgment and Preston's full lectures, believed to be true manuscripts as taught by Gleason.
A 'very rare' book by J. Browne, published in London in 1802, called the 'Master Key,' containing lectures in an 'abstruse cypher,' is also noted.
The text discusses the historical transmission and authenticity of Masonic lectures, specifically the lineage from Preston to Webb and Gleason.
It highlights the practice of Grand Lecturers, such as Benjamin Gleason, being commissioned to instruct lodges.
The importance of 'ancient landmarks' in the communication of lectures is emphasized.
Reference is made to historical Masonic publications like 'Free Mason's Monitor' by T. S. Webb, 'Masonic Trestleboard', and J. Browne's 'Master Key'.
The role of Grand Lodges (England, Massachusetts and Maine) in overseeing and potentially authenticating lecture content is noted.
Preston of England's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and his 'estimable system of improvements' are noted for defining the 'immovable landmarks' of the ancient Society.
Brother Benjamin Gleason, in association with Thomas Smith Webb, introduced the Prestonian system of work and lectures into the Lodges of New England and other sections of the country.
In 1804, the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts adopted the Preston ritual as its standard of work.
Brother Gleason was exclusively employed by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts during 1804 to communicate the ritual to lodges under its jurisdiction, including what is now the State of Maine.
Benjamin Gleason continued his labors as a lecturer until his death in 1847.
Charles W. Moore, Editor of the Freemasons' Monthly Magazine, acknowledged Benjamin Gleason as a master and teacher in the purest and most perfect Masonic ritual of ancient Craft Masonry.
The 'work' of Masonry, as revived by Preston, was approved and sanctioned by the Grand Lodge of England near the close of the last century.
The 'union' in 1813 led to a revision of the ritual for reconciliation purposes.
The verbal ritual, as revised by Preston, was introduced to the US around 1803.
The system was received and preserved, especially in older jurisdictions, essentially as it came from the original source of Craft Masonry.
Discussion of the historical lectures by Preston and their abridgment by Webb, highlighting their role in transmitting Masonic knowledge.
Emphasis on the importance of preserving Masonic lectures intact as a repository of history, philosophy, and symbolism.
Reference to the role of a Grand Lecturer in comparing and evaluating different versions of Masonic lectures.
caution_notes: Avoid all irregularity and intemperance which may impair your faculties or debase the dignity of your profession.
Do not countenance disloyalty or rebellion.
Be particularly careful to avoid censure or reproach in your outward demeanor.
Do not suffer your zeal for the Institution to lead you into argument with those who, through ignorance, may ridicule it.
Be particularly attentive not to recommend a person for initiation unless you are convinced that he will conform to our rules.
Masons must be particularly cautious and guarded in their thoughts, words, and actions when in the presence of the uninitiated.
The candidate is taught to be cautious over all words and actions, especially when before the enemies of Masonry.
A silent tongue teaches to be silent in the Lodge to maintain peace and harmony, and especially before the enemies of Masonry.
A faithful heart teaches to keep and conceal the secrets of Masonry and those of a brother, ensuring they remain secure and inviolable.
Masons are taught to avoid excess or contracting any licentious or vicious habit, the indulgence of which might lead to disclosing valuable secrets.
Fortitude serves as a safeguard against any illegal attack to extort secrets.
Prudence dictates never to let fall the least sign, token, or word in strange and mixed companies, whereby the secrets of Masonry might be unlawfully obtained.
Always remember your oath as an Entered Apprentice to conceal and never reveal secrets.
Secrets of Masonry might be unlawfully obtained.
Always remember your oath as an Entered Apprentice.
St. John cautions Christian brethren that 'without are dogs' (κυνες), referring to cowans or listeners (Rev. xxii. 15).
St. Paul exhorts Christians to 'beware of dogs, because they are evil workers' (Phil. W. 2).
Necessary precautions are employed during the Lodge Opening to avoid the intrusion of the profane.
The text implies a caution against resisting the 'emblematical feature,' stating that a Mason 'has no option' and 'cannot avoid it,' even if they 'may not like it' or 'think it degrading.'
The text describes a 'horrid conspiracy' and 'murderous designs' by Fellow Crafts, serving as a strong caution against treachery and the violation of Masonic trust.
The execution of the ruffians 'according to the several imprecations upon their own heads' highlights the severe and just consequences for such heinous crimes within the Masonic tradition.
Masons should be ever watchful and guarded in their thoughts, words, and actions, particularly when before the enemies of Masonry.
Justice will sooner or later overtake us, and our hidden thoughts, words, and actions are beheld by the All-Seeing Eye.
The name associated with the strong grip cannot be given except on the five points of fellowship and heard in a low breath, emphasizing secrecy and proper ritualistic context.
Do not 'pin their faith' upon the mere negations of any writer, whatever may be the strength of his masonic reputation, but rather examine and investigate matters of fact and principle.
The revised Prestonian system after the 1813 union had many incongruities and departures from the original.
Many unwarrantable liberties have been taken with the ritual, and innovations have crept in.
The ritual has had to contend against the corrupting influences of ignorant itinerant lecturers and spurious publications.
Caution against undervaluing Masonic lectures and dismissing them as mere rote memorization.
Warning against attempts to inaugurate a 'new era' that seeks to change or disregard established Masonic history, philosophy, and symbolism as contained in the lectures.
