entries.source_heading: Signs, Tokens and Words in Masonry

entries.title: Examining Visitors and Vouching for Petitioners
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the responsibilities associated with examining visitors and vouching for petitioners in a Masonic Lodge. It emphasizes that examining visitors should be done with kindness and courtesy, aiming to confirm their Masonic affiliation. For petitioners, it details the sponsor's duty to discuss Masonry with the applicant, understand their motivations and expectations, and ensure their good moral character. It also highlights the significant responsibility of sponsoring a petition, which creates a strong bond and commitment to mentor the new Mason. Finally, it describes the Investigating Committee's role as a position of special trust, requiring impartial and thorough inquiry into a petitioner's worthiness.
entries.full_summary:
This section outlines the responsibilities associated with examining visitors and vouching for petitioners in a Masonic Lodge. It emphasizes that examining visitors should be done with kindness and courtesy, aiming to confirm their Masonic affiliation. For petitioners, it details the sponsor's duty to discuss Masonry with the applicant, understand their motivations and expectations, and ensure their good moral character. It also highlights the significant responsibility of sponsoring a petition, which creates a strong bond and commitment to mentor the new Mason. Finally, it describes the Investigating Committee's role as a position of special trust, requiring impartial and thorough inquiry into a petitioner's worthiness.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0045 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brother A. B.

This section, addressed to a newly initiated Brother A. B., congratulates him on joining the ancient and honorable fraternity of Freemasonry. It outlines the fundamental duties of a Mason towards God, his neighbor, and himself, emphasizing reverence, acting upon the square, and avoiding intemperance. It also details civic responsibilities, proper outward demeanor, the importance of lodge attendance without neglecting vocations, and the caution against arguing with those who ridicule the Institution. The text encourages seeking further Masonic knowledge from well-informed brethren and stresses the importance of carefully vetting potential candidates. Finally, it charges the new Mason to keep the mysteries of the Institution sacred and inviolable.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0045 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0126 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Worshipful Master

This section details the installation ceremony of a Worshipful Master, beginning with the Master's assent to fifteen Ancient Charges and Regulations that outline moral conduct, civic duties, obedience to Masonic law, and lodge governance. The ceremony proceeds with the investiture of the Master with the collar and the presentation of key Masonic implements and documents: the Square, Compasses, Rule, Line, Book of Constitutions, Lodge Charter, Grand Lodge Regulations, By-Laws, and the Holy Writings. Each item is accompanied by its symbolic meaning and practical instructions for the Master's responsibilities in governing the Lodge.

This section describes a procedural sequence during a lodge installation, where Grand Honors are given, the Worshipful Master seats the Lodge, and an appropriate ode is sung, with an exception noted for public installations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0126 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0198 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Senior & Junior Deacons

The Senior and Junior Deacons are lodge officers responsible for introducing visitors and candidates, attending the Master and Wardens, and acting as their proxies in active lodge duties and rites. The Master-elect and Senior Warden-elect are responsible for appointing their respective Deacons. The Senior Deacon specifically introduces guests, while the Junior Deacon receives candidates and assists with Masonic regalia storage.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0198 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0205 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Committee on Investigation

The Committee on Investigation holds a critical duty to thoroughly vet candidates for Freemasonry. This involves inquiring into all aspects of a candidate's qualifications, including their mental and physical soundness, character, family life, and standing as a responsible member of the community. The purpose is to ensure that only worthy individuals are admitted, thereby preserving the unstained reputation of Freemasonry.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0205 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0207 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Committee on Investigation

The Committee on Investigation is a critical duty for Masons, tasked with thoroughly vetting a candidate's qualifications. This includes assessing their mental and physical soundness, reputation ('tongue of good report'), character, and family life. The process ensures that candidates meet the high standards of responsibility expected of a Freemason, emphasizing the importance of diligent inquiry and family involvement in the discussion.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0207 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0006 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Masons.

This section outlines the physical layout of a Masonic Lodge, including ritualistic stops, rooms, doors, and the stations of various officers. It details the procedural aspects of conducting lodge business, emphasizing that all significant Masonic business, such as balloting for candidates and receiving petitions, is conducted in the Master Mason Degree. It describes the process of character inquiry by a committee and the balloting procedure. The text also clarifies the progression through the degrees, using terms like 'entered,' 'passed,' and 'raised,' and specifies attendance restrictions based on degree. It defines a 'Blue Lodge' and describes the symbolic designations of the three Craft degrees as the Ground Floor, Middle Chamber, and Sanctum Sanctorum, relating them to King Solomon's Temple. Finally, it provides a practical guide for brethren entering a Lodge already at work, including the Tyler's role and the specific knocking signals for each degree.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0006 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0007 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Most Worshipful Master in the East

This section outlines the detailed procedures for brethren entering a Masonic Lodge, including the alarm system, the roles of the Junior Deacon and Tyler in admitting visitors, and the mandatory salutation of the Worshipful Master with the duegard and sign of the degree. It also describes the process for visitors to be vouched for or examined, particularly in cities with multiple lodges. Furthermore, it details the preparation for opening a Lodge, specifying the Master's attire and command, and the precise stations, attire, and tools of various officers within the Lodge.

This section details the ritualistic procedure for ensuring the Lodge is properly tyled before opening. It describes the interaction between the Worshipful Master, the Tyler, and the Junior Deacon, outlining the Tyler's station, duties, and the implement of his office (the sword), as well as the Junior Deacon's role in confirming the Lodge's security. The process ensures that only qualified Masons are present and that the Lodge is prepared for the specific degree (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, or Master Mason).
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0007 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0011 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Visiting Brothers.

This section details the proceedings of a St. John's Lodge communication, listing officers, members, and visiting brothers. It describes the opening and closing of the lodge in the Third, Entered Apprentice, and Fellow Craft degrees, along with the processing of candidate petitions, including balloting, rejections, and elections for initiation and advancement. It also records the initiation of an Entered Apprentice, the passing of a Fellow Craft, and the raising of a Master Mason, noting the associated fees and their payment to the Treasurer.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0011 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0013 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Form of Petition.

This section outlines the formal process for submitting a petition for membership into a Masonic lodge. It details the required information on the petition, the need for recommendations from existing Brothers, the accompanying fee, and the procedural steps taken by the Worshipful Master and Brethren, including a motion to receive petitions, a vote by the usual sign, and the appointment of a committee of investigation for each applicant.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0013 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0014 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Third Order of Business

This section details the 'Third Order of Business' in a Masonic lodge, focusing on the process of receiving and acting upon committee reports concerning candidate applications. It illustrates the roles of the Worshipful Master, Secretary, and committee chairmen in presenting findings on a candidate's character. The text also highlights potential discussions and delays that can arise if further inquiry into a candidate's background is deemed necessary, concluding with the motion and vote to proceed with balloting.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0014 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0018 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Seventh Order of Business

This section details the seventh order of business, which involves the procedural transition of a Lodge from the Master Mason degree to an Entered Apprentice Lodge for the purpose of initiation. It outlines the specific commands, ritual actions, and officer duties, including the Senior Warden's verification, the use of gavel raps and signs, the altar setup with compasses and square, and the Junior Deacon's role in preparing the candidate. It also introduces the Secretary's duty to conduct initial interrogations with the candidate in the ante-room, emphasizing free will and voluntary participation.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0018 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0025 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: First Section.

This section presents a catechism for an Entered Apprentice Mason, covering the candidate's origin, purpose in Masonry (to subdue passions and improve oneself), and methods of recognition. It details how a Mason is known by signs, tokens, a word, and the perfect points of entrance, including specific descriptions of signs as right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, and tokens as friendly grips. The symbolic meaning of the common gavel is explained as a tool for divesting the mind of vices to become a 'living stone' for a 'spiritual building'. The structure of the degree's lecture is outlined, consisting of three sections: the manner of initiation, the reasons for it, and the form, furniture, and lights of the Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0025 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0032 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Charge at Initiation Into the First Degree

This section presents the Charge given to a newly initiated Entered Apprentice, congratulating them on joining an ancient and honorable order. It outlines three fundamental duties: to God (reverence, imploring aid), to one's neighbor (acting upon the square, reciprocity), and to oneself (avoiding intemperance, maintaining dignity). The charge also details civic responsibilities, proper outward demeanor, the balance between Masonic duties and personal avocations, the importance of secrecy, and the careful recommendation of new candidates. A special instruction is included for clergymen, emphasizing Freemasonry's alignment with universal benevolence and charity. Footnotes provide historical context on the Master's hat in foreign lodges and the etymology of 'cowan'.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0032 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Degree: Historical Notes

This section provides historical notes and explanations for various elements of the Entered Apprentice Degree. It discusses the reverence associated with removing footwear in the East, linking it to the purity of the Mosaic pavement. It emphasizes that a candidate's admission is solely based on their avowal of belief and trust in God. Practical details are given regarding the number of candidates that can be initiated, passed, or raised in each degree. The text debunks claims that biblical figures like Adam, Moses, or Solomon were Freemasons. It clarifies the dual meaning of the word 'Hail' or 'Hale' in Masonic context. Specific ritual actions, such as drawing a hand or sword across the candidate's throat, are mentioned. The story of Hipparchus, a Pythagorean, is recounted as a cautionary tale about the severe consequences of violating societal engagements. The concept of 'Light' in initiation is explained as progressing from material to intellectual illumination. Finally, the symbolic meaning of the first sign, related to the palate and throat, is detailed as a lesson in temperance and restraint of passions, and the role of the Master and Senior Warden in laying the corner-stone is noted, along with the practice of fostering emulation and discussion in Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->
practical_elements: Lodge is responsible for examining visitors.
Purpose of examination is to prove a visitor is a Mason.
Show kindness and courtesy to all visitors.
Sponsor should discuss Masonry with an applicant before endorsing their petition.
Sponsor should understand the applicant's reasons, expectations, and what is expected of them.
Sponsor must be satisfied with the applicant's understanding and good moral character.
Investigating Committee should explain aspects of Masonry to the applicant.
Investigating Committee members must conduct a complete and impartial inquiry into the petitioner's character and worthiness.
Implore God's aid in all laudable undertakings.
Act upon the square and do unto your neighbor as you wish them to do unto you.
Avoid all irregularity and intemperance.
Be a quiet and peaceful citizen, true to your government, and just to your country.
Patiently submit to legal authority and conform to the government of your country.
Be particularly careful to avoid censure or reproach in your outward demeanor.
Attend regular lodge meetings frequently.
Do not let Masonry interfere with necessary vocations.
Do not suffer zeal for the Institution to lead into argument with those who ridicule it.
Converse with well-informed brethren to improve in Masonic knowledge.
Do not recommend a person for initiation unless convinced they will conform to Masonic rules.
Keep sacred and inviolable the mysteries of the Institution.
The new Master faces the East.
The Installing Officer addresses the Ancient Charges and Regulations to the Master.
The Master signifies assent to each charge by answering "I do".
The Master is invested with the insignia of office (the collar is placed upon his shoulders).
The Square is presented with its teaching.
The Compasses are presented with their teaching.
The Rule is presented with its teaching.
The Line is presented with its teaching.
The Book of Constitutions is given in charge, with instruction to search and read it in the Lodge.
The Lodge Charter is given in charge, with instruction for careful preservation and transmission to the successor.
The Regulations of the Grand Lodge relating to Subordinate Lodges and the By-Laws for the government of the same are given in charge, with instruction for careful and punctual execution.
The Holy Writings are placed in the Master's hands as a Great Light in Masonry.
The Installing Officer calls up the brethren.
The Installing Officer takes the Master by the hand and seats him in the Oriental Chair.
The Installing Officer declares the Master installed.
Grand Honors are given (unless it is a public installation).
The Master seats the Lodge.
A suitable ode is sung.
Introduce visitors to the Lodge.
Attend on the Master and Wardens.
Act as proxies for the Master and Wardens in active Lodge duties.
Receive candidates into the different degrees of Masonry.
Engage in the immediate practice of Masonic rites.
Senior Deacon: Receive and introduce all guests of the Lodge, ensuring they feel welcomed and comfortable.
Junior Deacon: Receive all candidates of the Lodge, ensuring they feel welcomed and comfortable.
Junior Deacon: Assist in ensuring all Masonic regalia is properly stored and cared for.
Inquire into everything affecting the qualifications of a candidate.
Verify the candidate's sound mind and members.
Ascertain if the candidate comes under the tongue of good report.
Investigate the candidate's character and family life.
Confirm the candidate is a responsible member of their community, engaged with domestic, social, vocational, and civic obligations.
Prevent the admission of unworthy individuals to protect the reputation of Freemasonry.
Take assignment on an Investigation Committee seriously.
Inquire into all qualifications of a candidate.
Assess candidate's sound mind and members (physical and mental fitness).
Verify candidate comes under the 'tongue of good report' (reputation).
Evaluate candidate's character and family life.
Do not neglect questions during the investigation.
Include the candidate's family in the discussion.
Candidate prays.
First stop, Second stop, Third stop in the ritual.
Room where candidates are prepared.
Ante-room where members enter the lodge.
Hall.
Doors.
Door through which candidates are admitted into the lodge.
Door through which members enter.
Altar.
Officers: Treasurer, Secretary, Senior Deacon, Worshipful Master, Junior Warden, Stewards (17 and 18), Senior Warden, Junior Deacon, Tyler.
All business relative to Masonry is done at a 'regular' meeting, specifically in the Third, or Master Mason Degree.
Only Master Masons are allowed to be present at business meetings.
Balloting for candidates, receiving petitions, and committee reports are generally done on a 'regular' meeting.
A petition for degrees is received at a 'regular' meeting.
The Master appoints a committee of three to inquire into the applicant's character.
The committee reports good or bad at the next regular meeting.
A ballot is held after the committee's report.
No black balls mean the candidate is elected; one or more black balls mean rejection.
No business is done in an Entered Apprentices' Lodge except to initiate a candidate to the First Degree.
No business is done in a Fellow Crafts' Lodge except to pass a Fellow Craft from the first to the second degree.
A candidate initiated to the First Degree is styled 'entered'.
A candidate who has taken the Second Degree is styled 'passed'.
A candidate who has taken the Third Degree is styled 'raised' to the sublime Degree of a Master Mason.
No one is allowed to be present in any degree of Masonry unless they are of that same degree or higher.
The Master always wears his hat when presiding in a 'Blue Lodge'.
No other officer wears a hat in a 'Blue Lodge'.
A 'Blue Lodge' is a Lodge of Master Masons where only three degrees are conferred: Entered Apprentice (1st), Fellow Craft (2d), Master Mason (3d).
A Lodge of Fellow Craft Masons consists of five officers (Worshipful Master, Senior and Junior Wardens, Senior and Junior Deacons), with seven generally assisting besides the Tyler.
Three Master Masons are the requisite number to constitute a Masters' Lodge, though generally seven assist besides the Tyler.
All Lodges meet in one room, alike furnished, for conferring different degrees.
The Tyler's duty is to inform brethren on what degree the Lodge is at work, especially late arrivals.
Brethren arriving late come to the ante-room, where the Tyler is stationed with a sword.
Late brethren inquire of the Tyler on what degree the Lodge is opened, put on an apron, and request entry.
The Tyler gives one rap (•) if the Lodge is opened on the First Degree.
The Tyler gives two raps (• •) if the Lodge is opened on the Second Degree.
The Tyler gives three raps (• • •) if the Lodge is opened on the Third Degree.
The Junior Deacon reports the alarm at the inner door to the Worshipful Master.
The Junior Deacon attends to alarms from the outside, inquires of the Tyler, and reports the cause to the Master.
The Tyler reports the names of brethren seeking admission.
The Master grants permission for brethren to be admitted.
Brethren advance to the center of the Lodge, at the altar, make the duegard and sign of the degree on which the Lodge is opened.
No brother is allowed to take his seat until he has saluted the Worshipful Master upon entering a Lodge.
The Tyler generally cautions brethren about giving the sign before they enter the Lodge.
The Junior Deacon cautions brethren about giving the sign as soon as they are inside the Lodge.
The Junior Deacon's station is at the inner door, and it is his duty to attend to all alarms, report to the Master, and get permission before admitting anyone.
Visitors not personally known to the Master or brethren must be vouched for or pass an examination.
Upon admission, the Tyler informs visitors of the degree the Lodge is opened on and furnishes them with aprons.
Brethren generally gather at an early hour at the Lodge-room, which has been opened and cleaned by the Tyler.
Upon the Master's arrival and the hour of meeting, the Master repairs to his seat in the East, puts on his hat, sash, yoke, and apron, with gavel in hand.
The Master commands: "Brethren will be properly clothed and in order; officers repair to their stations for the purpose of opening."
Brethren put on their aprons and seat themselves around the Lodge-room.
Officers invest themselves with their yokes and aprons and take their specific stations.

Officer stations include: Senior Warden in the West; Junior Warden in the South; Senior Deacon in front of the Worshipful Master in the East, a little to his right, with a long rod; Junior Deacon at the right hand of the Senior Warden in the West, guarding the inner door, with rod; Secretary at the left of the Worshipful Master; Treasurer at the right; two Stewards on the right and left of the Junior Warden in the South, with rods.
The Worshipful Master calls the Tyler to approach the East.
The Tyler approaches the Worshipful Master's seat with yoke and apron on.
The Tyler states his place: 'Without the inner door.'
The Tyler states his duty: 'To keep off all cowans and eavesdroppers, and not to pass or repass any but such as are duly qualified and have the Worshipful Master's permission.'
The Worshipful Master hands the Tyler a sword as the implement of his office.
The Tyler retires to the inside of the outer door of the ante-room.
All Lodge-doors are closed after the Tyler retires.
The Worshipful Master gives one rap with his gavel.
The Junior Deacon rises upon the gavel rap.
The Junior Deacon states the first and constant care of Masons: 'To see that the Lodge is duly tyled.'
The Junior Deacon opens the door to communicate with the Tyler.
The Junior Deacon informs the Tyler to tyle the Lodge as an Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, or Master Mason, as appropriate.
The Junior Deacon closes the door after giving instructions to the Tyler.
Listing of officers present: Worshipful Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Treasurer, Secretary, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon, Stewards (two), Tyler.
Listing of lodge members present.
Listing of visiting brothers, including their lodge name, number, city, and state.
Lodge opened in due form on the Third Degree of Masonry.
Minutes of the last communication of St. John's Lodge were read and confirmed.
Committee reported favorably on a petition for initiation (John B. Crockerberry), followed by ballot and election.
Committee reported favorably on another petition for initiation (D. C. Woolevert), followed by ballot, but the 'box appearing foul,' he was rejected.
Committee reported unfavorably on a petition for initiation (William S. Anderson), leading to rejection without a ballot.
Petition for initiation from Robert Chase, accompanied by a $10 fee, recommended by Brothers Hart, Lewis, and Onion, and referred to a committee of investigation (Brothers Slick, Wise, and Swift).
Entered Apprentice Samuel Brevoort applied for advancement and was elected to the Second Degree.
Fellow Craft Thomas Jansen applied for advancement and was elected to the Third Degree.
Lodge of Master Masons closed.
Lodge of Entered Apprentices opened in due form.
Charles Fronde, a candidate, was prepared, brought forward, and initiated as an Entered Apprentice Mason in due and ancient form, paying a $5 fee.
Lodge of Entered Apprentices closed.
Lodge of Fellow Crafts opened in due form.
Stephen Currie, an Entered Apprentice, was prepared, brought forward, and passed to the degree of a Fellow Craft, paying a $5 fee.
Lodge of Fellow Crafts closed.
Lodge of Master Masons opened in due form.
John Smith, a Fellow Craft, was prepared, brought forward, and raised to the sublime degree of a Master Mason, paying a $5 fee.

Detailed accounting of fees received: $10.00 for Robert Chase's petition, $5.00 for Charles Fronde (Fellow Craft), $5.00 for Stephen Currie (Fellow Craft), $5.00 for John Smith (Master Mason), totaling $25.00.
All funds paid over to the Treasurer.
Lodge closed in due form and harmony.
Secretary Samuel Slick noted and approved.
A petition for membership must include the applicant's residence (e.g., New York City), age (e.g., thirty-eight years), and occupation (e.g., bookseller).
Petitions require recommendations from existing Brothers (e.g., Brothers Jones, Carson, Fox, Derby, Jackson).
Petitions must be accompanied by a fee (e.g., ten dollars).
The Worshipful Master presents petitions to the Wardens and Brethren for their consideration.
A motion is made and seconded to receive petitions and appoint a committee of investigation.
Brethren vote on motions using the 'usual sign'.
The Worshipful Master appoints specific Brothers to serve on committees of investigation for each petitioner.
Worshipful Master asks the Secretary for committee reports.
Secretary announces pending reports on candidate applications.
Worshipful Master asks if committee chairmen are present.
Committee chairmen present their reports on candidate character, stating whether they are favorable or unfavorable.
Lodge members may engage in discussion regarding a candidate's character or history.
A motion is made to receive the report, discharge the committee, and ballot for the candidate.
The motion is seconded.
Worshipful Master calls for a vote using the 'usual sign'.
Worshipful Master disposes of other lawful business.
Worshipful Master declares intent to close Master Mason Lodge and open an Entered Apprentice Lodge for initiation.
Senior Warden verifies all present are Entered Apprentice Masons.
Senior Warden gives three gavel raps to bring members to order as Entered Apprentice Masons.
Members place hands in the duegard position of an Entered Apprentice.
Master makes the sign by drawing his hand across his throat, followed by all members.
Worshipful Master, Senior Warden, and Junior Warden each give one gavel rap in sequence.
Worshipful Master declares the Master Mason Lodge closed and an Entered Apprentice Lodge open.
Junior Deacon is instructed to inform the Tyler.
Senior Deacon is instructed to attend at the altar, placing both points of the compasses under the square.
Worshipful Master gives one rap to seat the entire Lodge.
Junior Deacon, with two Stewards as assistants, repairs to the ante-room to prepare the candidate (Mr. Gabe) for the First Degree.
Junior Deacon and assistants make the sign of the First Degree to the Master before leaving the Lodge-room.
Secretary accompanies the Junior Deacon and assistants to the ante-room.
Secretary obtains the candidate's assent to interrogations before the candidate is required to strip.
The usual sign (one rap) is to be used to make known when the candidate is duly prepared.
Catechism format (Q&A)
Giving the sign of an Entered Apprentice
Giving the token of an Entered Apprentice
Verbal exchange: 'I hail.' 'I conceal.'
Recognition by signs, a token, a word, and the perfect points of entrance
Description of signs as right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars
Description of tokens as certain friendly or brotherly grips
Strictly observe and inculcate duties to God, your neighbor, and yourself.
Never mention God's name but with reverential awe, implore His aid, and esteem Him as your chief good.
Act upon the square with your neighbor, doing unto him as you would have him do unto you.
Avoid all irregularity and intemperance that may impair faculties or debase your profession's dignity.
Be a quiet and peaceable citizen, true to your government, and just to your country.
Do not countenance disloyalty or rebellion; patiently submit to legal authority and conform to the government.
Be particularly careful in outward demeanor to avoid censure or reproach.
Do not let interest, favor, or prejudice bias your integrity or lead to dishonorable actions.
Ensure Masonry does not interfere with necessary avocations, as these are not to be neglected.
Do not let zeal for the institution lead to arguments with those who ridicule it through ignorance.
At leisure hours, converse with well-informed brethren to improve in Masonic knowledge.
Keep sacred and inviolable the mysteries of the Order.
Be particularly careful not to recommend a person for initiation unless convinced they will conform to rules.
If the candidate is a clergyman, add the specific charge emphasizing benevolence and charity.
Putting off the covering of the feet as a sign of reverence, paralleling Eastern customs.
Candidate avows belief and trust in God as the sole basis for admission; refusal leads to rejection.
Chief officer expresses satisfaction upon a candidate's declaration of trust in God.
If more than one candidate is initiated at once, they are required to take hold of each other's arms.
Up to five candidates can be initiated as Entered Apprentices at once.
Up to five candidates can be 'passed' to the Second Degree at once.
Only one candidate at a time can be 'raised' to the Master's Degree.
Small Lodges may only manage one candidate at a time conveniently.
In some Lodges, at the words 'throat cut across,' a member or conductor draws their hand across the candidate's throat, or sometimes draws a sword.
The Master, assisted by the Senior Warden, lays the chief corner-stone of a fabric.
Lodge labors are conducted to produce an exciting spirit of emulation.
The Worshipful Master personally requests each individual to give an opinion on a specific doctrine or ceremony from the Chair.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The phrase "acting upon the square" symbolically represents living a life of integrity, fairness, and moral rectitude in dealings with others, aligning with the Masonic principle of upright conduct.

The Square teaches to regulate actions by rule and line, harmonizing conduct with principles of morality and virtue. The Compasses teach to limit desires, enabling one to rise to eminence by merit, live respected, and die regretted. The Rule directs punctual observance of duty, pressing forward in virtue, and having eternity in view. The Line teaches moral rectitude, avoiding dissimulation, and directing steps towards immortality. The Holy Writings are presented as an inestimable gift from God, the Great Light in Masonry, guiding to truth, happiness, and the whole duty of man.

The Fellow Craft Lodge is symbolically styled 'the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple,' representing a stage of intellectual and moral advancement. The Master Masons' Lodge is symbolically called 'the Sanctum Sanctorum, or, Holy of Holies of King Solomon's Temple,' signifying the most sacred and complete state of Masonic knowledge and authority. The three degrees are masonically styled by the Craft as the Ground Floor (Entered Apprentice), Middle Chamber (Fellow Craft), and Sanctum Sanctorum (Master Mason), representing a progression through different levels of understanding and spiritual significance within the Temple.

The strict adherence to protocol, including the duegard and sign, and the mandatory salutation of the Worshipful Master upon entering, symbolizes respect for authority, order, and the sanctity of the Masonic space. The structured placement of officers and their specific attire and tools during the opening ceremony further emphasizes the organized and purposeful nature of the Lodge's work.

The act of tyling symbolizes the protection of Masonic secrets and the sanctity of the Lodge from unqualified intrusion. The Tyler's sword represents vigilance and defense against external threats, while the Junior Deacon's role emphasizes internal security and order, ensuring the integrity of the Craft.

The placement of both points of the compasses under the square at the altar symbolizes the Entered Apprentice degree, indicating that the candidate is still learning to control their passions and desires within the bounds of morality and duty.

The common gavel, typically used by operative Masons to remove superfluous corners from rough stones, is symbolically used by Free and Accepted Masons to divest their minds and consciences of all vices and superfluities of life. This process prepares them as 'living stones' for a 'spiritual building, that house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens'. The sign of an Entered Apprentice alludes to the penalty of the obligation.

The phrase 'acting upon the square' symbolizes fairness, honesty, and integrity in dealings with others. Freemasonry is presented as a 'centre of union' for mankind, symbolizing its power to bring together individuals who might otherwise remain distant. Virtue is described as 'luminous as the meridian sun,' shining refulgent on the mind, symbolizing its illuminating and enlivening effect on the human spirit and its capacity to convert mere approval into deep affection.

The Mosaic pavement is accounted pure and immaculate, symbolizing that no pollution can be tolerated on that sacred floor. The word 'Hail' (from Saxon 'Hael') is used as a salutation, inquiring 'Whence do you hail?' The word 'Hale' (from Saxon 'helan') signifies to conceal, referring to the 'tye.' Light is the first demand of a candidate at initiation, with material light succeeded by intellectual illumination. The palate and throat are considered the chief seat of irregular appetites, and the first sign instructs Masons to avoid temptation through proper restraint on passions, promoting temperance, decency, and decorum.
entries.candidate_lesson:
For those considering becoming Masons, it is important to understand that the process involves thorough vetting by a sponsor and an Investigating Committee, who will assess your character and understanding of Masonic principles. For Masons, the lesson is about the profound responsibility of sponsoring a new member and the importance of upholding the integrity of the Craft through diligent examination of visitors and petitioners.

The candidate is taught the foundational principles of Masonic conduct, encompassing moral, ethical, civic, and fraternal duties. Key lessons include reverence for God, justice and charity towards others, self-control, loyalty to one's country, discretion in public and private life, and the importance of preserving the integrity and reputation of the Craft through personal conduct and careful selection of new members. The inviolability of Masonic mysteries is also presented as a distinguishing characteristic.

The Worshipful Master is charged with upholding moral law, being a peaceful and law-abiding citizen, respecting civil authorities, working diligently, living creditably, and acting honorably. They must venerate the Order's rulers, submit to Lodge resolutions, avoid quarrels and excess, be cautious, courteous, and faithful to the Lodge. The Master is to respect true brethren, discountenance imposters, promote the good of society, cultivate social virtues, and propagate Masonic knowledge. They must pay homage to the Grand Master, conform to Grand Lodge edicts, and preserve the Ancient Landmarks. Regular attendance at Grand Lodge committees and communications is required, along with ensuring proper Lodge formation, initiation, and visitor examination. The Master is taught to regulate actions by morality, limit desires, observe duty, pursue virtue, avoid dissimulation, and be guided by divine truth.

Candidates for Freemasonry are expected to possess sound mind and body, demonstrate good character, maintain a positive family life, and be responsible, engaged members of their community. The thorough investigation process underscores the high standards and integrity required of those who wish to join the fraternity.

The thorough investigation process underscores the high standards of character, reputation, and family responsibility expected of individuals seeking to become Freemasons, demonstrating the fraternity's commitment to maintaining the integrity of its membership.

Candidates learn the importance of character and integrity through the rigorous inquiry process for petitioners. The progression through the degrees, marked by being 'entered,' 'passed,' and 'raised,' teaches a journey of continuous self-improvement and increasing knowledge. The caution regarding giving the correct sign emphasizes the importance of attentiveness, respect for the degree at which the Lodge is working, and adherence to proper protocol.

A candidate learns the critical importance of proper decorum, respect for the Master and the Lodge, and strict adherence to established ritualistic procedures, including the correct use of signs and duegards. The anecdote about the Master Mason's error highlights the need for attentiveness to the degree on which the Lodge is opened. The process of admitting visitors underscores the fraternal bonds and the system of recognition and trust within Masonry.

The importance of secrecy, vigilance, and the proper order and security within a Masonic Lodge. It teaches that the integrity of the Craft depends on preventing unauthorized access and maintaining the sanctity of its proceedings.

The importance of proper procedure and adherence to rules in lodge operations, including the rigorous vetting of candidates through committees and balloting. It also demonstrates the structured progression through the degrees, each requiring an application and fee. The rejection of candidates highlights the selective nature of Masonic membership.

The process emphasizes the importance of a candidate's good character and reputation within society, as well as the thoroughness with which a lodge investigates potential members. It demonstrates that entry into Freemasonry is not taken lightly and requires the approval of the brethren based on a careful assessment of one's moral standing.

The initial interrogation of the candidate emphasizes the importance of free will, voluntary choice, and unbiassed, non-mercenary motives for seeking the mysteries of Freemasonry, instilling principles of integrity and sincerity from the outset.

The primary lesson for the candidate is to learn to subdue their passions and improve themselves in Masonry. This is achieved by symbolically using the common gavel to cleanse the mind and conscience of vices, thereby becoming a suitable 'living stone' for a spiritual edifice.

The candidate is taught the foundational moral and ethical principles of Freemasonry, encompassing duties to God, humanity, and self. They learn the importance of reverence, integrity, temperance, and civic responsibility. The lesson emphasizes balancing Masonic commitments with personal and professional life, maintaining the secrecy of the Order, and exercising discernment when recommending new members. For clergymen, it highlights the profound alignment between Masonic tenets and the virtues of universal benevolence and charity, encouraging a life of virtue and continuous self-improvement.

Admission into the fraternity is based on a sincere belief and trust in God, and where God's name is invoked, no danger can ensue. It is unwise to assert more than can be proven or argue against probability regarding historical claims. The story of Hipparchus serves as a severe caution against violating engagements, emphasizing the importance of truth and fidelity, and the dire consequences of their breach. Candidates are taught that the pursuit of 'Light' extends beyond the material to intellectual understanding. The first sign teaches the importance of temperance in all indulgences, self-restraint, and never exceeding the boundary of decency and decorum, under penalty of violating Masonic engagements.
tradition_notes: The practice of examining visitors is a long-standing tradition to ensure only Masons enter a Lodge.
The role of a sponsor in vouching for a petitioner is a fundamental aspect of Masonic entry, emphasizing personal responsibility and mentorship.
The Investigating Committee serves as a traditional safeguard to maintain the quality and character of Lodge membership.
Freemasonry is described as an ancient fraternity, having existed from time immemorial.
The Institution is founded on excellent rules and useful maxims inculcated in Masonic lectures.
The greatest and best of men in all ages have been encouragers and promoters of the art, extending privileges and patronizing assemblies.
The Master must assent to Ancient Charges and Regulations which point out the duty of the Master of a Lodge.
The Master must hold in veneration the original rulers and patrons of the Order of Freemasonry and their regular successors.
The Master must submit to the awards and resolutions of brethren in Lodge convened, consistent with the constitutions of the Order.
The Master must respect genuine and true brethren and discountenance dissenters from the Ancient Landmarks and Constitutions of Masonry.
The Master must conform to every edict of the Grand Lodge or General Assembly of Masons that is not subversive of the principles and groundwork of Masonry.
It is admitted that it is not in the power of any man, or body of men, to make innovations in the Landmarks of Masonry.
It is admitted that no new Lodge can be formed without permission of the Grand Lodge.
It is admitted that no countenance ought to be given to any irregular Lodge, or to any person clandestinely initiated therein, as being contrary to the ancient charges of the Order.
It is admitted that no person can be regularly made a Freemason in, or admitted a member of, any regular Lodge, without previous notice, and due inquiry into his character.
It is agreed that no visitors shall be received into your Lodge without due examination, and producing proper vouchers of their having been initiated in a regular Lodge.
Masters have supported these regulations in all ages before.
The practice of giving Grand Honors and singing an ode is part of the lodge installation ceremony.
The Master-elect and Senior Warden-elect shall appoint their respective Deacons.
The phrase 'comes under the tongue of good report' refers to a candidate's reputation and standing within the community.
All business relative to Masonry is traditionally conducted at a 'regular' meeting, specifically within the Third, or Master Mason Degree.
Only Master Masons are permitted to be present at such business meetings.
Grand Lodges typically make arrangements for the regulation of their subordinate Lodges.
The Master always wears his hat when presiding in a 'Blue Lodge', a privilege not extended to other officers.
A 'Blue Lodge' is defined as a Lodge of Master Masons where only the three Craft degrees (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason) are conferred.
A Lodge of Fellow Craft Masons traditionally consists of five officers, with seven generally assisting besides the Tyler.
A Masters' Lodge requires three Master Masons to constitute it by Masonic Law, though generally seven assist besides the Tyler.

The three degrees are symbolically styled by the Craft as the Ground Floor (Entered Apprentice), Middle Chamber (Fellow Craft), and Sanctum Sanctorum (Master Mason), referencing King Solomon's Temple.
In large cities, the presence of numerous lodges leads to frequent visiting among brethren.
The tradition of visitors not personally known to the Master or brethren being vouched for or undergoing an examination.
The established practice of officers investing themselves with specific attire (yokes, aprons) and taking their designated stations for the formal opening of the Lodge.

The dialogue and actions described are part of the traditional opening ceremony of a Masonic Lodge, emphasizing the historical roles of the Tyler and Junior Deacon in maintaining the integrity and privacy of the Craft.
Lodge opened 'in due form'.
Initiation performed 'in due and ancient form'.
Lodge closed 'in due form and harmony'.
The institution is referred to as an 'ancient institution'.
It is not uncommon for discussions to lead to a delay in balloting for a candidate if more thorough inquiry is deemed necessary.
Lodges differ in the mode of lowering from a Master's to an Entered Apprentice's Lodge; some close entirely and open on the First, while others adopt a 'short way'.
The origin of the Lodge is stated as 'From a Lodge of the Sts. John of Jerusalem', a traditional Masonic reference.
Freemasonry is described as an 'ancient and honorable order,' existing from time immemorial.
In most foreign Lodges, the Master wears his hat while brethren remain uncovered, a practice exemplified by MacKenzie Beverly Esq. as D. P. G. M. for the East Riding of York.

The term 'cowan' is historically linked to Jephthah and Ephraimites, and etymologically to the Egyptian 'cohen' (priest/prince/dog), with biblical references from St. John and St. Paul equating 'dogs' with 'cowans' or 'listeners' (profane intruders).

The reverence indicated by putting off the covering of the feet is still prevalent in the East among Christians, Moslems, or pagans, where it is considered highly irreverent to enter a sacred place with feet covered.
Masonic traditions do not support the belief that figures like Adam, Nimrod, Moses, Joshua, David, Solomon, Hiram, St. John the Evangelist, or St. John the Baptist belonged to the Masonic Order.
caution_notes: The purpose of examining visitors is to prove they are Masons, not to prove they are not. Approach with kindness and courtesy.
Signing a petition is a significant responsibility, committing the sponsor to assist the new Mason's growth.
Serving on an Investigating Committee is a solemn responsibility requiring complete and impartial inquiry.
Avoid all irregularity and intemperance which may impair your faculties or debase the dignity of your profession.
Do not countenance disloyalty or rebellion.
Be particularly careful to avoid censure or reproach in your outward demeanor.
Do not suffer your zeal for the Institution to lead you into argument with those who, through ignorance, may ridicule it.
Be particularly attentive not to recommend a person for initiation unless you are convinced that he will conform to our rules.
Do not be concerned in plots and conspiracies against the government; patiently submit to law and authorities.
Avoid private piques and quarrels, and guard against intemperance and excess.
Discountenance imposters and all dissenters from the Ancient Landmarks and Constitutions of Masonry.
It is not in the power of any man or body of men to make innovations in the Landmarks of Masonry.
No new Lodge can be formed without Grand Lodge permission; do not countenance irregular Lodges or clandestinely initiated persons.
No person can be regularly made a Freemason or admitted to a regular Lodge without previous notice and due inquiry into character.
No visitors shall be received without due examination and proper vouchers of regular initiation.
The Lodge Charter should never be out of the Master's immediate control and must be duly transmitted to the successor.
Grand Honors are not given during a public installation.
The committee's duty is to prevent the admission of individuals whose misconduct could tarnish the reputation of Freemasonry.
Do not neglect the questions during the investigation.
Include the family in the discussion to ensure a comprehensive assessment.

Brethren arriving late must be informed by the Tyler on what degree the Lodge is at work to prevent them from giving the wrong sign to the Worshipful Master, especially if members of a lower degree are present.
No brother is allowed to take his seat until he has saluted the Worshipful Master on entering a Lodge; omission results in immediate reminder.
The Tyler and Junior Deacon caution brethren about giving the correct sign before/upon entering the Lodge.
It is a common error among Masons to carelessly give the duegard and sign of a higher degree when the Lodge is actually open on a lower one.
A candidate can be rejected even with a favorable committee report if the ballot box appears 'foul'.
A candidate can be rejected without a ballot if the committee reports unfavorably.
Lengthy discussions can arise regarding a candidate's early history or character, potentially delaying the ballot until a subsequent meeting.
Never mention God's name but with reverential awe.
Avoid all irregularity and intemperance.
Be particularly careful to avoid censure or reproach in outward demeanor.
Do not let interest, favor, or prejudice bias your integrity or lead to dishonorable actions.
Masonry should not interfere with necessary avocations; these are on no account to be neglected.
Do not suffer zeal for the institution to lead into arguments with those who ridicule it through ignorance.
Keep sacred and inviolable the mysteries of the Order.
Be particularly careful not to recommend a person for initiation unless convinced they will conform to our rules.
It is unwise to assert more than can be proven and to argue against probability regarding historical figures being Freemasons.

The story of Hipparchus illustrates the terrible consequences of violating engagements, leading to expulsion, social death, and denial of burial rites, serving as a warning against breaches of truth and fidelity.
Disobedience or violation of Masonic engagements, particularly regarding temperance and decorum, carries a penalty.

entries.title: Lodge Membership and Financial Responsibilities
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the conditions for lodge membership, noting that an Entered Apprentice becomes a full member only after being raised. It details four ways membership can terminate: demit, suspension, expulsion, or death. A Mason can apply for a demit or transfer if dues are current and they are in good standing, and plural or dual membership is possible. Suspension can occur for nonpayment of dues or 'unMasonic conduct,' with reinstatement possible upon payment of current dues. Suspension or expulsion from a lodge results in denial of membership in all Masonic organizations. Financial responsibilities include mandatory annual dues and voluntary contributions to charities and distressed Brothers. Failure to pay dues for over twelve months can lead to suspension, and distressed Brothers should inform the Master or Secretary.
entries.full_summary:
This section outlines the conditions for lodge membership, noting that an Entered Apprentice becomes a full member only after being raised. It details four ways membership can terminate: demit, suspension, expulsion, or death. A Mason can apply for a demit or transfer if dues are current and they are in good standing, and plural or dual membership is possible. Suspension can occur for nonpayment of dues or 'unMasonic conduct,' with reinstatement possible upon payment of current dues. Suspension or expulsion from a lodge results in denial of membership in all Masonic organizations. Financial responsibilities include mandatory annual dues and voluntary contributions to charities and distressed Brothers. Failure to pay dues for over twelve months can lead to suspension, and distressed Brothers should inform the Master or Secretary.

### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Order of Business

This section outlines the standard order of business for a Masonic Lodge after it has been opened, detailing the sequence of activities from reading minutes and committee reports to handling petitions, motions, accounts, and new business, concluding with Grand Lodge communications and closing the Lodge. It also includes rules for brothers desiring to speak, limiting debate, and determining the Lodge's sense on questions.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0014 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0035 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Relief

The text defines relief as a universal duty, particularly incumbent upon Masons who are bound by an indissoluble chain of sincere affection. It highlights the Masonic aim to soothe, sympathize with, compassionate, and restore peace to the troubled minds of the unhappy, forming the foundation of their friendships and connections.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0035 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0036 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Truth

Truth is presented as a divine attribute and the fundamental basis for all virtues. It is identified as the first lesson taught in Masonry, guiding members to regulate their conduct. Adherence to truth fosters sincerity, plain dealing, and mutual prosperity, eliminating hypocrisy and deceit. The text also alludes to the four Cardinal Virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0036 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0039 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Prudence

Prudence is defined as the habit of regulating one's life and actions according to reason, enabling wise judgment and determination for present and future happiness. It is a characteristic every Mason should possess, guiding their conduct both within the Lodge and in the wider world. Masons are specifically cautioned to exercise prudence in mixed company to prevent the unlawful disclosure of Masonic secrets, signs, tokens, or words.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0039 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0041 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Prudence

Prudence is defined as the habit of wisely judging and prudentially determining actions for present and future happiness, guided by reason. It is a characteristic virtue for every Mason, governing conduct both within the Lodge and in the outside world. Masons are specifically cautioned to exercise prudence in all companies, especially mixed ones, to prevent the unlawful disclosure of Masonic secrets through any sign, token, or word.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0041 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0047 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: First Section

The passage describes a ritualistic teaching moment where the candidate is instructed that the 'Square of Virtue' should serve as the fundamental rule and guide for their conduct in all future interactions with humanity.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0047 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0051 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: First Section

This section introduces the concept of the Square of Virtue, emphasizing its role as a fundamental principle for guiding a candidate's conduct in all interactions with mankind.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0051 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Second Section

The Second Section of the ritual recounts the Institution's historical traditions and presents an exemplary instance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity. It emphasizes the importance of spreading brotherly love and affection, which serves as the cement uniting Masons into a sacred society where the only acceptable contention is a noble emulation to excel in work and agreement.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0126 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Worshipful Master

This section details the installation ceremony of a Worshipful Master, beginning with the Master's assent to fifteen Ancient Charges and Regulations that outline moral conduct, civic duties, obedience to Masonic law, and lodge governance. The ceremony proceeds with the investiture of the Master with the collar and the presentation of key Masonic implements and documents: the Square, Compasses, Rule, Line, Book of Constitutions, Lodge Charter, Grand Lodge Regulations, By-Laws, and the Holy Writings. Each item is accompanied by its symbolic meaning and practical instructions for the Master's responsibilities in governing the Lodge.

This section describes a procedural sequence during a lodge installation, where Grand Honors are given, the Worshipful Master seats the Lodge, and an appropriate ode is sung, with an exception noted for public installations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0126 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0142 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brothers Senior and Junior Wardens

This section addresses the Senior and Junior Wardens, emphasizing their deep understanding of Masonic principles and their expected exemplary conduct. They are tasked with assisting the Master, diffusing light, and imparting knowledge to those under their care. The text highlights their crucial role in succession, ensuring the Craft's continuous instruction, and anticipates their future conduct will earn the applause of their brethren and a good conscience.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0142 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0166 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Iii. Essentials in Conduct

This section outlines the fundamental principles of conduct for a Mason, emphasizing the necessity of obedience to the Moral Law, the Civil Law of the country, and the specific Masonic jurisdiction where the Mason resides. It also notes that a Mason remains amenable to that jurisdiction even if they are not a member of any particular Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0166 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0175 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Proclamation

This section describes a formal proclamation made by the Grand Master to an assembly of brethren and friends during a ceremony, likely a cornerstone laying. The proclamation affirms the Masons' adherence to the laws of their country, their fear of God as the Great Architect, their commitment to benefiting brethren, and practicing universal benevolence. Following this, the Grand Master delivers the Square, Level, Plumb, and the building plan to the Architect, entrusting them with the superintendence and direction of the work, expressing confidence in their skill to ensure the building rises in order, harmony, and beauty, fulfilling its purpose to the credit and honor of all involved.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0175 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0185 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: General Duties & Responsibilities

This section introduces the general duties and responsibilities expected within Freemasonry.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0185 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0194 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Treasurer

The Treasurer is responsible for safeguarding the Lodge's funds, receiving money from the Secretary, and disbursing it according to the Lodge's By-Laws. This officer must maintain accurate financial records, ensure deposits match receipts, and provide regular financial reports to the membership. The Treasurer also acts as the Lodge's accountant, collaborating with the Senior Warden and the Lodge Finance Committee, but is explicitly prohibited from serving on the auditing committee.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0194 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0208 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Special Committees

This section details the role and types of special committees within a Masonic Lodge, which operate under the authority of the Worshipful Master. It outlines the composition and responsibilities of several key committees, including those for By-Laws, Finance, Education, Membership Satisfaction, Lodge Involvement, Property and Maintenance, and Lodge Planning, emphasizing their importance to the Lodge's successful functioning.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0208 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0214 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Unanimous Ballot

A unanimous ballot is a critical procedural requirement in Freemasonry, necessary for various actions related to membership and degree advancement. It ensures full consent from the lodge members for significant decisions.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0214 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0215 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: 2/3 Majority Ballot

This section outlines specific procedural actions within a Masonic lodge that require a two-thirds majority ballot for approval. These actions include the withdrawal of a petition, indefinite suspension, expulsion, consolidation of lodges, substitution of a lesser penalty, and determining guilt or innocence on charges.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0215 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0008 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Opening the Lodge: Officer Stations and Duties

This section details the ritualistic opening of a Masonic Lodge, focusing on the stations and duties of various officers, including the Tyler, Junior Deacon, Senior Warden, Senior Deacon, Secretary, Treasurer, Junior Warden, and Worshipful Master. It outlines the procedure for ensuring the Lodge is properly tyled, confirming the qualification of members, and the symbolic significance of officer placements and responsibilities, particularly for an Entered Apprentice Lodge.

This section details the stations and duties of the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Worshipful Master during the opening of a Masonic Lodge. It explains their symbolic positions relative to the sun and their responsibilities, such as calling the craft to refreshment, paying wages, and governing the Lodge. The text also outlines the procedure for opening the Lodge on the First, Second, or Third Degree, including the communication of orders and the performance of the duegards and signs for the Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, and Master Mason degrees, with specific instructions on their execution and symbolic allusions to their respective obligations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0008 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0011 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Visiting Brothers.

This section details the proceedings of a St. John's Lodge communication, listing officers, members, and visiting brothers. It describes the opening and closing of the lodge in the Third, Entered Apprentice, and Fellow Craft degrees, along with the processing of candidate petitions, including balloting, rejections, and elections for initiation and advancement. It also records the initiation of an Entered Apprentice, the passing of a Fellow Craft, and the raising of a Master Mason, noting the associated fees and their payment to the Treasurer.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0011 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0013 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Form of Petition.

This section outlines the formal process for submitting a petition for membership into a Masonic lodge. It details the required information on the petition, the need for recommendations from existing Brothers, the accompanying fee, and the procedural steps taken by the Worshipful Master and Brethren, including a motion to receive petitions, a vote by the usual sign, and the appointment of a committee of investigation for each applicant.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0013 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0025 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: First Section.

This section presents a catechism for an Entered Apprentice Mason, covering the candidate's origin, purpose in Masonry (to subdue passions and improve oneself), and methods of recognition. It details how a Mason is known by signs, tokens, a word, and the perfect points of entrance, including specific descriptions of signs as right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, and tokens as friendly grips. The symbolic meaning of the common gavel is explained as a tool for divesting the mind of vices to become a 'living stone' for a 'spiritual building'. The structure of the degree's lecture is outlined, consisting of three sections: the manner of initiation, the reasons for it, and the form, furniture, and lights of the Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0025 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0088 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master's Song.

This section details the 'Mark Master's Song' and a significant ritualistic drama involving a candidate attempting to receive wages. The song calls Mark Masters to present their work for inspection by the Chief Overseer and Chief Architect, emphasizing the importance of approved work and the right to wages. It references Hiram, the widow's son, and the keystone. The ritual reenacts a scenario where a candidate, after the second verse of the song, attempts to receive a penny from the Senior Warden through a lattice-window, using a specific hand gesture. The candidate is accused of being an 'impostor' for not being able to give the proper token, leading to a dramatic threat of having his hand struck off. The Senior Deacon intercedes, vouching for the candidate as a craftsman and promising to take him to the Right Worshipful Master. The candidate then proves himself a Fellow Craft Mason. The Right Worshipful Master sternly reprimands the candidate for attempting to receive wages without the token, but remits the penalty due to youth and inexperience, ordering the Senior Deacon to teach him how to bring up a regularly wrought stone in the quarries. The reprimand itself is often omitted in modern lodges but is included for historical context. The section concludes with the preparation of the candidate for the degree, including divesting of apparel, money, and valuables, baring the breast, a cable-tow four times around the body, and blindfolding with a hoodwink, followed by four distinct knocks at the door.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0088 -->

Regulate our conduct by the dictates of truth

Regulating one's life and actions according to reason

Governing conduct when abroad in the world

Wisely judge and prudentially determine on all things relative to present and future happiness

The 'Square of Virtue' is presented as a guiding principle for conduct.

The Square of Virtue is presented as a rule and guide for conduct.

The Master signifies assent to each charge by answering "I do".

The Installing Officer calls up the brethren.
The Installing Officer takes the Master by the hand and seats him in the Oriental Chair.

Grand Honors are given (unless it is a public installation).
The Master seats the Lodge.
A suitable ode is sung.
Wardens are to assist the Master in his trust.
Wardens are to diffuse light and impart knowledge to those under their care.
Wardens succeed to higher duties in the Master's absence.
The Installing Officer delivers this charge to the Wardens with the Lodge members standing.
Obedience to the Moral Law.
Obedience to the Civil Law of the country.
Obedience to the Masonic jurisdiction where the Mason resides.
Amenability of the Mason to their Masonic jurisdiction, even if not a member of any Lodge.

The Grand Master delivers the Square, Level, Plumb, and the plan of the building to the Architect.

Receive all money from the hands of the Secretary.
Pay out funds on proper authority in accordance with the By-Laws of the Lodge.

The Worshipful Master has the authority to create and dissolve special committees.

Lodge By-Laws Committee: Composed of three well-studied Master Masons, tasked with reviewing and reporting on Lodge By-Laws, ensuring they adhere to The Williams Digest without alteration except for filling blanks.

Finance Committee: Composed of three to five well-educated Master Masons (Senior Warden and Treasurer suggested), responsible for managing income, expenses, investments, fundraising, budgeting, formulating the annual budget, and contacting members regarding unpaid dues.

Lodge Education Committee: Composed of three to five Past Masters (or other knowledgeable members), responsible for presenting Masonic programs, ensuring member proficiency in work and lectures, informing new members on history and traditions, and reviewing Grand Lodge educational resources.

Membership Satisfaction Committee: Composed of three to five Master Masons, tasked with ensuring a positive member experience, communicating feedback to the Worshipful Master, addressing Lodge challenges (e.g., unproductive meetings, conflicts, membership issues, fraternal culture), and contacting suspended members for reinstatement.
Lodge Involvement Committee: Composed of three to five Master Masons, tasked with developing opportunities for membership and family fellowship, outreach, public relations, and community service.

Property and Maintenance Committee: Composed of three to five Master Masons, charged with ensuring the security and maintenance of Lodge property, regalia, and materials, in cooperation with the Lodge Steward.

Lodge Planning Committee: Composed of the Worshipful Master, Lodge officers, committee chairmen, or other members as deemed appropriate by the Worshipful Master, tasked with developing an active and engaging annual Lodge program.
