entries.title: Third Circuit
entries.short_summary: This section describes the 'Third Circuit' within a Masonic ritual, featuring a call and response among the Master, Senior Warden, and Junior Warden. Each officer recites a scriptural passage emphasizing the Lord's attentiveness to the cries and prayers of the poor and righteous, promising deliverance from troubles.
entries.full_summary:
This section describes the 'Third Circuit' within a Masonic ritual, featuring a call and response among the Master, Senior Warden, and Junior Warden. Each officer recites a scriptural passage emphasizing the Lord's attentiveness to the cries and prayers of the poor and righteous, promising deliverance from troubles.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0008 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Opening the Lodge: Officer Stations and Duties

This section details the ritualistic opening of a Masonic Lodge, focusing on the stations and duties of various officers, including the Tyler, Junior Deacon, Senior Warden, Senior Deacon, Secretary, Treasurer, Junior Warden, and Worshipful Master. It outlines the procedure for ensuring the Lodge is properly tyled, confirming the qualification of members, and the symbolic significance of officer placements and responsibilities, particularly for an Entered Apprentice Lodge.

This section details the stations and duties of the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Worshipful Master during the opening of a Masonic Lodge. It explains their symbolic positions relative to the sun and their responsibilities, such as calling the craft to refreshment, paying wages, and governing the Lodge. The text also outlines the procedure for opening the Lodge on the First, Second, or Third Degree, including the communication of orders and the performance of the duegards and signs for the Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, and Master Mason degrees, with specific instructions on their execution and symbolic allusions to their respective obligations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0008 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0016 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Balloting and Petitions

This section details the precise procedure for balloting on a candidate's petition for the First Degree in Masonry. It outlines the sequence of voting, starting with the Master, followed by the Secretary calling names, and each Mason casting a white or black ball. The ballot box is then systematically examined by the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and finally the Worshipful Master, who declares the outcome of the election.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0016 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0022 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Bringing to Light: Entered Apprentice Sign and Word

This section details the Entered Apprentice signs, token, and the word 'Boaz,' explaining their allusions to the obligation and secrecy. It describes the ritualistic examination by the Junior and Senior Wardens. The Worshipful Master then presents the candidate with a lambskin apron, an emblem of innocence and a badge of a Mason, instructing how it should be worn. The section concludes with the ancient custom of requesting a metallic deposit from the candidate as a memento for the Lodge archives.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0022 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0026 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Lecture: First Section

This section presents the First Section of the Entered Apprentice Lecture in a question-and-answer format, detailing the candidate's journey through the initiation ritual. It covers the secrecy of Masonic knowledge, the preparation of the candidate, the process of admission including knocks and questions, the solemn obligation, the discovery and meaning of the three great and three lesser lights in Masonry, the presentation of the lambskin apron as an emblem of innocence, and the ancient custom of depositing a metallic memorial.

This section details the Entered Apprentice's return to the Lodge after examination, his placement in the northeast corner, and the presentation of the working tools: the twenty-four-inch gauge and common gavel. It explains the symbolic use of the twenty-four-inch gauge for dividing one's time into portions for the service of God and relief of a distressed brother, usual avocations, and refreshment and sleep.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0026 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0049 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fellow Craft Degree: Closing Notes

This section concludes the Fellow Craft Degree, offering detailed explanations and historical context through footnotes. It explores the historical assertion of St. John's connection to the Essenes as a secret society conveying moral truths through symbols. The text describes a ritualistic gesture involving a rake across the breast, emphasizing the penalty of the obligation and symbolizing fortitude against adversity and the importance of fidelity to secrets. It introduces the Jewish Cabala's rule of Transposition, applying it to Hebrew words like ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) to signify moral and physical light. A comprehensive explanation of 'Shibboleth' is provided, detailing its dual meanings (ear of grain, stream of water) and its historical use by the Gileadites to identify Ephraimites based on speech peculiarities. Finally, it outlines the Fellow Craft Lecture, covering the passing ceremony, the distinction between operative and speculative Masonry, the study of liberal arts and sciences, and the legend of wages paid in the Middle Chamber, guarded by the Junior and Senior Wardens.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0049 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: The Beehive

This section describes several Masonic emblems and their moral and philosophical lessons. It begins with the Beehive, symbolizing industry, social dependence, and the importance of contributing to the common stock of knowledge. It then discusses the Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, which emphasizes watchfulness, silence, and circumspection, especially against enemies of Masonry. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart and the All-Seeing Eye are presented as symbols of divine justice and omnipresence, reminding Masons that all thoughts, words, and actions are observed and will be judged. Finally, the Anchor and Ark are explained as emblems of well-grounded hope and a well-spent life, guiding one through life's troubles to a peaceful harbor. The section also briefly mentions the Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid and its connection to Pythagoras.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->
practical_elements: Call and response ritualistic exchange between the Master, Senior Warden, and Junior Warden.
Recitation of specific scriptural passages by each officer.
One rap for an Entered Apprentice Lodge, two for a Fellow Craft Lodge, and three for a Master Mason's Lodge.
The Tyler responds to the raps.
The Junior Deacon confirms the Lodge is tyled.
The Tyler is stationed without the inner door, invested with a sword.
The Senior Warden confirms all present are of the correct degree.
The Senior Warden's station is in the west.
The Junior Deacon's station is at the right hand of the Senior Warden in the west.
The Senior Deacon's station is at the right hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Secretary's station is at the left hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Treasurer's station is at the right hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Junior Warden's station is in the south.
The Worshipful Master's station is in the east.
The Junior Deacon makes the sign of an Entered Apprentice Mason.
The Worshipful Master uses two raps with his gavel to prompt all officers to rise.
The Secretary records Lodge proceedings, transmits copies to the Grand Lodge if required, receives money from brethren, and passes it to the Treasurer, taking a receipt.
The Treasurer receives money from the Secretary, keeps accounts, and pays out funds by order of the Worshipful Master and consent of the Lodge.
The Senior Deacon carries orders from the Worshipful Master to the Senior Warden and elsewhere, introduces and clothes visiting brethren, and receives and conducts candidates.
The Junior Deacon carries orders from the Senior Warden to the Junior Warden and elsewhere, and ensures the Lodge is tyled.
Junior Warden's station is in the south.
Senior Warden's station is in the west.
Worshipful Master's station is in the east.
Worshipful Master gives three raps with his gavel to signal brethren to rise.
Orders for opening the Lodge are communicated from Worshipful Master to Senior Warden, then to Junior Warden, and finally to the brethren.
Brethren perform signs together, led by the Master.
Duegard of an Entered Apprentice: Left hand supporting the Bible, right hand resting thereon.
Sign of an Entered Apprentice: Draw the right hand rapidly across the neck and drop the arm to the side.
Duegard of a Fellow Craft: Left arm held horizontally to the elbow, rest of the arm vertically, forming a square; right hand detached from the stomach, fingers extending outward.
Sign of a Fellow Craft is given after its duegard.
Duegard of a Master Mason: Both hands resting on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
Duegards and signs are made with right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, with slight, marked pauses.
For Fellow Craft duegard and sign, the left arm must be dropped suddenly and with spirit.
The Master initiates the balloting process.
The Secretary calls the names of Masons, from the Senior Warden down to the Tyler.
Each Mason approaches the altar, makes the sign of a Master Mason to the Master.
Each Mason takes a ball (white or black) from the lower drawer of the ballot-box.
Each Mason deposits the ball in the hopper above and returns to their seat.
The Senior Deacon closes the ballot drawer after all have voted.
The Senior Deacon carries the ballot box to the Junior Warden in the south.
The Junior Warden examines the top drawer to determine if the ballot is 'clear' or 'not clear' and reports to the Worshipful Master.
The Deacon then carries the ballot box to the Senior Warden in the west for examination.
The Senior Warden examines the ballot and reports its status ('clear' or 'not clear') to the Worshipful Master.
The Deacon delivers the ballot box to the Worshipful Master's station in the east.
The Worshipful Master examines the box and declares whether the candidate (e.g., Mr. Joseph Locker) has been elected or not to the First Degree in Masonry.
The same balloting process is followed for any other candidates.
Candidate performs a duegard, alluding to the hand placement during the Entered Apprentice obligation.
Candidate makes the sign of an Entered Apprentice, alluding to the penalty of the obligation.
Senior Deacon guides the candidate to take the Junior Warden by the right hand for the token/grip.
The word 'Boaz' is lettered and then pronounced during the examination.
Candidate undergoes examination by the Junior Warden and Senior Warden.
Worshipful Master presents a white linen or lambskin apron to the candidate.
Senior Warden ties the apron on the candidate with the flap turned up, explaining how Entered Apprentices wore them at King Solomon's Temple.
Worshipful Master requests a metallic deposit from the candidate as a memento for the Lodge archives.
Concealing secrets of Masons, alluded to by pressing the thumb-nail on the first joint.
Being divested of all metals, neither naked nor clothed, barefoot nor shod, hoodwinked, with a cable-tow around the neck during preparation.
Gaining admission by three distinct knocks.
Being received on the point of a sharp instrument pressing the naked left breast.
Being conducted to the center of the Lodge and caused to kneel for prayer.
Being taken by the right hand and ordered to arise, follow the conductor, and fear no danger.
Following the conductor once around the Lodge to the Junior Warden's station in the south, then to the Senior Warden in the west, and finally to the Worshipful Master in the east.
Approaching the east by one upright, regular step, with feet forming an angle of an oblong square and body erect, at the altar.
Kneeling on the naked left knee, with the right forming a square, left hand supporting the Holy Bible, square, and compasses, and right hand resting thereon, to take the solemn oath.
Receiving light by order of the Worshipful Master and assistance of the brethren.
Worshipful Master approaching under the duegard and sign of an Entered Apprentice, presenting the right hand, grip, and word.
Saluting the Junior and Senior Wardens as an Entered Apprentice.
Receiving a lambskin or white linen apron and being taught to wear it with the flap turned up.
Being requested to deposit something of a metallic kind as a memorial.
Candidate is returned to the place from whence he came, reinvested, and returned to the Lodge for further instructions.
Candidate is placed in the northeast corner, with feet forming a right angle and body erect, at the right hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Worshipful Master presents the working-tools of an Entered Apprentice Mason: the twenty-four-inch gauge and common gavel.
Operative masons use the twenty-four-inch gauge to measure and lay out their work.
Gives candidate a rake across his breast with the hand to draw attention to the penalty.
Workmen's wages were paid daily, weekly, monthly, and quarterly in their respective Lodges.
Wages were paid in the Middle Chamber when the Temple was nearly completed.
The foot of the winding staircase to the Middle Chamber was guarded by the Junior Warden, and the summit by the Senior Warden of a Fellow Crafts' Lodge.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The exchange symbolizes divine providence, the Lord's constant attention to the righteous, and the assurance of salvation and deliverance from troubles for those who cry out to Him. It reinforces themes of faith, divine support, and the power of prayer.

The Lodge is symbolically prepared in a room adjacent to a legally constituted Lodge, representing the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple. The Junior Warden's station in the south symbolizes the sun at high meridian, representing the beauty and glory of the day, and his role in observing time and managing periods of labor and refreshment. The Worshipful Master's station in the east symbolizes the rising sun, indicating his role in opening the Lodge. Harmony is presented as the strength of all institutions, especially Freemasonry.

The Junior Warden's station in the south symbolizes the sun at high meridian, representing the beauty and glory of the day, and his duty to regulate the balance between labor and refreshment. The Worshipful Master's station in the east symbolizes the rising sun, signifying his role in opening and governing the Lodge, setting the craft to work, and providing instruction. The signs of each degree (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason) allude to the penalty of their respective obligations, emphasizing the solemnity and consequences of their vows.

The duegard and the sign of an Entered Apprentice symbolize the candidate's obligation and its associated penalty. The token and grip signify the commitment to conceal Masonic secrets. The word 'Boaz' serves as a password and a means to detect impostors. The lambskin or white apron is presented as an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason, described as more ancient and honorable than other prestigious orders. The metallic deposit, though of little intrinsic value, symbolizes a personal memento of the candidate's initiation into the Lodge.

The lambskin or white linen apron is an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason, signifying purity and honor. The Holy Bible is presented as the rule and guide to faith and practice, the square to square actions, and the compasses to circumscribe and keep Masons within bounds with all mankind, especially brother Masons. The three lesser lights (sun, moon, and Master of the Lodge) represent the principle of regularity in governance, as the sun rules the day and the moon governs the night, so the Worshipful Master should rule and govern the Lodge with equal regularity. Being brought from darkness to light symbolizes the candidate's transition from ignorance to Masonic knowledge.

The placement in the northeast corner symbolizes the candidate becoming an upright man and Mason. The twenty-four-inch gauge, divided into twenty-four equal parts, is emblematic of the twenty-four hours of the day. Its symbolic use teaches the division of time into three parts: for the service of God and relief of a distressed worthy brother, for usual avocations, and for refreshment and sleep, representing a balanced life of duty, labor, and rest.

Secret societies like the Essenes conveyed moral truths under symbolical figures. The breast, as the abode of fortitude, teaches the candidate to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity with patience rather than betraying secrets. Hebrew words ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) are symbolically interpreted to mean fortifying oneself with higher sciences and preparing to receive moral light. Shibboleth signifies both an ear of grain and a stream of water, with its symbolic interpretation increasing interest as one advances through the 'field of corn by the river-side'. The payment of wages without fear or scruple symbolizes the employers' entitlement to unlimited confidence.

The Beehive symbolizes industry, mutual dependence, and the necessity for all rational beings to contribute to society and knowledge, avoiding idleness. The Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, represents the need for vigilance, discretion, and protection of Masonic principles against external threats. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart signifies that divine justice will ultimately prevail, revealing all hidden thoughts and actions. The All-Seeing Eye embodies the omnipresence and omniscience of the Great Creator, who observes the innermost recesses of the human heart and rewards according to one's deeds. The Anchor and Ark are emblems of a steadfast hope and a virtuous life, symbolizing safe passage through life's challenges to a state of eternal peace and rest.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate is taught about the importance of faith and prayer, understanding that the Lord hears the cries of the righteous and will deliver them from their troubles. It instills a sense of divine protection and the moral imperative to live righteously.

The candidate learns that their obligation is what makes them an Entered Apprentice Mason. They are taught the importance of temperance and avoiding excess during periods of refreshment. The concept of harmony as the strength of the institution is also emphasized.

Candidates are taught the importance of temperance and moderation during refreshment, the value of harmony and satisfaction among brethren, and the necessity of proper conduct within the Lodge. The performance of the duegards and signs, which allude to the penalties of the obligations, serves as a constant reminder of the seriousness of their vows and the duties they have undertaken.

The candidate learns the solemnity of the Entered Apprentice obligation and the importance of maintaining secrecy regarding Masonic knowledge. They are taught the significance of the lambskin apron as a symbol of purity and honor within the fraternity. The ritual also imparts the tradition of contributing a personal item as a lasting reminder of their initiation.

The candidate is taught the importance of secrecy regarding Masonic knowledge and the solemnity of the Entered Apprentice obligation. Trust in God is emphasized as a well-founded faith. The candidate learns the moral application of the working tools: to square their actions and to circumscribe their passions and desires, keeping them within due bounds. The apron teaches the value of innocence and purity, and the overall ritual emphasizes the importance of free will, worthiness, and good repute for admission into the fraternity.

The candidate is strictly charged to ever walk and act as an upright man and Mason. He is taught the importance of wisely dividing his time to fulfill his duties to God and his brethren, attend to his daily work, and allow for necessary rest.

Candidates are taught to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity, distress, pain, or disappointment with patience. This fortitude prevents the bitter stings of remorse that result from betraying secrets entrusted under solemn obligation. The neophyte should prepare to receive moral light through higher sciences. Candidates are directed to an attentive study of the liberal arts and sciences. The legend of wages paid without fear or scruple teaches the importance of unlimited confidence in employers.

Candidates are taught the importance of industry, social responsibility, and mutual support, emphasizing that they should not be 'drones' but actively contribute to the common good and knowledge. They are instructed to be watchful, guarded in their conduct, and to practice silence and circumspection, particularly when facing those hostile to Masonry. The emblems also impart the lesson of divine justice and accountability, urging Masons to live righteously, knowing that all their actions and thoughts are observed by a higher power. Finally, they are encouraged to cultivate a well-grounded hope and lead a well-spent life, anticipating a peaceful end to their earthly journey.
tradition_notes: This segment is a specific ritualistic 'circuit' involving the principal officers of the lodge.
The scriptural quotes are from Psalm 34.
The number of raps given by the Worshipful Master varies by degree: one for Entered Apprentice, two for Fellow Craft, and three for Master Mason.
The Lodge is symbolically represented as the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple.
The traditional response to 'Are you an Entered Apprentice Mason?' is 'I am so taken and accepted among all brothers and fellows.'
The stations of the officers in the Lodge (East, West, South) are symbolically aligned with the course of the sun.
The Master always leads in giving the sign or signs during the opening ceremony.
The duegard represents the position of the hands when taking the oath of a degree.
The specific sequence of officers examining the ballot box (Junior Warden, Senior Warden, Worshipful Master) is a traditional procedural element.
The use of white and black balls for voting is a traditional method of secret ballot in Freemasonry.
The requirement for each Mason to make the sign of a Master Mason to the Master before voting is a traditional ritualistic detail.
The lambskin apron is described as 'more ancient than the Golden Fleece or Roman Eagle' and 'more honorable than the Star and Garter'.
Entered Apprentices wore their aprons with the flap turned up 'at the building of King Solomon's Temple'.
The request for a metallic deposit is an 'ancient custom, adopted among Masons'.
The lambskin or white linen apron is described as 'more ancient than the Golden Fleece or Roman Eagle; more honorable than the Star or Garter, or any other order'.
The request to deposit something of a metallic kind is noted as 'agreeably to an ancient custom, adopted in every regulated and well-governed Lodge'.
Calmet asserts that St. John was an Essene, a secret society of his day that conveyed moral truths under symbolical figures, which may be termed Freemasonry under another name.
The Jewish Cabala includes a rule called Transposition, used to find meaning in anagrammatically formed words.
Brother Rosenberg, a Jewish Mason, used Transposition to interpret ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) in Hebrew, relating them to receiving higher sciences and moral light.
SHIBBOLETH in Hebrew has two significations: an ear of grain and a stream of water.
The historical account of the Ephraimites and Gileadites illustrates how the word 'Shibboleth' was used as a test, exploiting a peculiarity in the Ephraimites' speech organs.

The Fellow Crafts' Degree Lecture's first section recapitulates the ceremonies of passing a candidate, and the second section discusses the ancient division into operative and speculative Masons and the liberal arts and sciences.

According to Masonic legends, workmen's wages were paid in their respective Lodges, and later in the Middle Chamber, which was accessible by a winding staircase guarded by the Junior and Senior Wardens.
The Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid is attributed to our ancient friend and brother, the great Pythagoras, who was initiated into several orders during his travels through Asia, Africa, and Europe.
caution_notes: The Tyler's duty is to keep off all cowans and eavesdroppers.
The Junior Warden must ensure that the means of refreshment are not converted into intemperance or excess.
Means of refreshment should not be converted into intemperance or excess.
Private committees and improper, unmasonic conduct are strictly forbidden during Lodge business, under penalty determined by a majority of brethren acting under by-laws.
Duegards and signs must be made with right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, with very slight, but marked pauses between each motion or part of the sign.
When making the duegard and sign of the Fellow Craft, the left arm must be dropped suddenly and with spirit as soon as the two motions are accomplished.
The spelling of 'Boaz' (always beginning with 'A') is noted as a way Masons detect impostors, such as 'Morgan or book Masons'.
The candidate is obligated to conceal 'All the secrets of Masons, in Masons'.
A rake across the breast is given to draw the candidate's attention to the penalty.
Betrayal of secrets entrusted on the faith of a solemn obligation will inevitably result in the bitter stings of remorse.
Masons should be ever watchful and guarded in their thoughts, words, and actions, particularly when before the enemies of Masonry.
Justice will sooner or later overtake us, and our hidden thoughts, words, and actions are beheld by the All-Seeing Eye.

entries.title: Second Circuit
entries.short_summary: This section details the 'Second Circuit', a ritualistic sequence where various lodge officers recite scriptural verses emphasizing divine trust and protection. The Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon, and Chaplain each deliver a line from scripture, collectively conveying a message of divine guardianship and preservation from evil.

The Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon, and Chaplain each deliver a line from scripture, collectively conveying a message of divine guardianship and preservation from evil.

This section details the stations and duties of the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Worshipful Master during the opening of a Masonic Lodge. ### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Preparation of the Candidate

This section details the initial preparation and entry of a candidate for the First Degree in Masonry. It begins with an interrogation to confirm the candidate's motivations and willingness to conform to Masonic usages. Following assent, the candidate undergoes specific physical preparation, including partial disrobing and the application of a hoodwink, slipper, and cable-tow. The narrative then describes the ritualistic knocking at the Lodge door, the dialogue between the Deacons regarding the candidate's qualifications and desire for light, and the formal reception into the Lodge. This reception involves the symbolic presentation of the compasses to the candidate's naked breast, followed by a prayer led by the Worshipful Master and the candidate's declaration of trust in God.

This section details the final preparatory steps for an Entered Apprentice candidate. It includes the candidate kneeling for a prayer recited by the Worshipful Master, followed by the Master asking the candidate in whom they put their trust. Upon declaring trust in God, the candidate is assisted to rise and begins a perambulation around the Lodge room, guided by the Senior Deacon. During this circuit, the Worshipful Master recites a passage on unity, and the Master, Junior Warden, and Senior Warden each give a single gavel rap as the candidate passes their stations. The perambulation concludes at the Junior Warden's station, where a challenge is issued.

### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fellow Craft Degree: Closing Notes

This section concludes the Fellow Craft Degree, offering detailed explanations and historical context through footnotes. It explores the historical assertion of St. John's connection to the Essenes as a secret society conveying moral truths through symbols. The text describes a ritualistic gesture involving a rake across the breast, emphasizing the penalty of the obligation and symbolizing fortitude against adversity and the importance of fidelity to secrets. It introduces the Jewish Cabala's rule of Transposition, applying it to Hebrew words like ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) to signify moral and physical light. A comprehensive explanation of 'Shibboleth' is provided, detailing its dual meanings (ear of grain, stream of water) and its historical use by the Gileadites to identify Ephraimites based on speech peculiarities. Finally, it outlines the Fellow Craft Lecture, covering the passing ceremony, the distinction between operative and speculative Masonry, the study of liberal arts and sciences, and the legend of wages paid in the Middle Chamber, guarded by the Junior and Senior Wardens.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0049 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0086 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master.

This section details the opening of a Mark Master Masons Lodge, including the specific gavel raps and declaration by the Right Worshipful Master. It clarifies that the Mark Master Lodge's sole business is candidate initiation, with other administrative tasks reserved for the Royal Arch Degree. The process of preparing a candidate for advancement is described, involving specific attire adjustments and the carrying of symbolic blocks (square, plumb, and a keystone with initials H. T. W. S. S. T. K. S.). The candidate, along with two officers, enters the Lodge as "Workmen from the quarries" and undergoes a ritualistic challenge at the Junior Overseer's station, presenting their work for inspection.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0086 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0087 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master: the Rejected Keystone

This section details a ritualistic sequence within the Mark Master degree where a candidate presents a keystone for inspection by the Junior, Senior, and Master Overseers. Despite its unique appearance, the keystone is repeatedly rejected for not being 'square work' and lacking a craftsman's mark. The Overseers ultimately agree to cast it aside as rubbish. The scene concludes with the workmen becoming impatient for their wages, leading to a song and a procession to receive payment.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0087 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0088 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master's Song.

This section details the 'Mark Master's Song' and a significant ritualistic drama involving a candidate attempting to receive wages. The song calls Mark Masters to present their work for inspection by the Chief Overseer and Chief Architect, emphasizing the importance of approved work and the right to wages. It references Hiram, the widow's son, and the keystone. The ritual reenacts a scenario where a candidate, after the second verse of the song, attempts to receive a penny from the Senior Warden through a lattice-window, using a specific hand gesture. The candidate is accused of being an 'impostor' for not being able to give the proper token, leading to a dramatic threat of having his hand struck off. The Senior Deacon intercedes, vouching for the candidate as a craftsman and promising to take him to the Right Worshipful Master. The candidate then proves himself a Fellow Craft Mason. The Right Worshipful Master sternly reprimands the candidate for attempting to receive wages without the token, but remits the penalty due to youth and inexperience, ordering the Senior Deacon to teach him how to bring up a regularly wrought stone in the quarries. The reprimand itself is often omitted in modern lodges but is included for historical context. The section concludes with the preparation of the candidate for the degree, including divesting of apparel, money, and valuables, baring the breast, a cable-tow four times around the body, and blindfolding with a hoodwink, followed by four distinct knocks at the door.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0088 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0089 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master Reception and Obligation

This section details the reception and obligation ceremony for the Mark Master Degree. It begins with a severe reprimand given to the candidate for presenting unfit work and claiming undeserved wages, a practice noted as often omitted in modern lodges. The candidate is then prepared by being divested of valuables, blindfolded, and having a cable-tow placed around his body. After an alarm at the door, the candidate, identified as a Master Mason seeking further light, is admitted using the pass-word JOPPA. The Senior Deacon then symbolically receives the candidate upon the edge of an indenting chisel and under the pressure of a mallet, explaining their use for 'indenting the mind.' The ceremony concludes with a perambulation around the Lodge, during which the Master reads scripture passages concerning 'the stone which the builders refused/rejected' becoming 'the head stone of the corner.'

This section details the reception and obligation of a Mark Master Mason. The candidate undergoes a ritualistic perambulation, answering questions at the posts of the Junior and Senior Wardens, and the Right Worshipful Master. The core of the section is the extensive obligation, which includes promises of secrecy, adherence to Masonic law, obedience to summonses, and specific rules regarding the use, exchange, and redemption of a personal 'mark.' It also outlines severe penalties for violating the oath and concludes with a practical demonstration of how a brother's mark can be used to request financial aid.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0089 -->
practical_elements: The Master recites a verse about trusting in the Lord for protection.
The Senior Warden recites a verse about angels being given charge to keep one in all ways.
The Junior Warden recites a verse about being borne in hands to prevent injury.
The Senior Deacon recites a verse about the Lord, the keeper of Israel, neither slumbering nor sleeping.
The Junior Deacon recites a verse about the Lord being one's keeper and defense.
The Chaplain recites a verse about the Lord preserving from all evil and keeping one's soul.
The sequence of recitations constitutes a 'Second Circuit' within the ritual.
One rap for an Entered Apprentice Lodge, two for a Fellow Craft Lodge, and three for a Master Mason's Lodge.
The Tyler responds to the raps.
The Junior Deacon confirms the Lodge is tyled.
The Tyler is stationed without the inner door, invested with a sword.
The Senior Warden confirms all present are of the correct degree.
The Senior Warden's station is in the west.
The Junior Deacon's station is at the right hand of the Senior Warden in the west.
The Senior Deacon's station is at the right hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Secretary's station is at the left hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Treasurer's station is at the right hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Junior Warden's station is in the south.
The Worshipful Master's station is in the east.
The Junior Deacon makes the sign of an Entered Apprentice Mason.
The Worshipful Master uses two raps with his gavel to prompt all officers to rise.
The Secretary records Lodge proceedings, transmits copies to the Grand Lodge if required, receives money from brethren, and passes it to the Treasurer, taking a receipt.
The Treasurer receives money from the Secretary, keeps accounts, and pays out funds by order of the Worshipful Master and consent of the Lodge.
The Senior Deacon carries orders from the Worshipful Master to the Senior Warden and elsewhere, introduces and clothes visiting brethren, and receives and conducts candidates.
The Junior Deacon carries orders from the Senior Warden to the Junior Warden and elsewhere, and ensures the Lodge is tyled.
Junior Warden's station is in the south.
Senior Warden's station is in the west.
Worshipful Master's station is in the east.
Worshipful Master gives three raps with his gavel to signal brethren to rise.
Orders for opening the Lodge are communicated from Worshipful Master to Senior Warden, then to Junior Warden, and finally to the brethren.
Brethren perform signs together, led by the Master.
Duegard of an Entered Apprentice: Left hand supporting the Bible, right hand resting thereon.
Sign of an Entered Apprentice: Draw the right hand rapidly across the neck and drop the arm to the side.
Duegard of a Fellow Craft: Left arm held horizontally to the elbow, rest of the arm vertically, forming a square; right hand detached from the stomach, fingers extending outward.
Sign of a Fellow Craft is given after its duegard.
Duegard of a Master Mason: Both hands resting on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
Duegards and signs are made with right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, with slight, marked pauses.
For Fellow Craft duegard and sign, the left arm must be dropped suddenly and with spirit.
The Worshipful Master initiates the balloting process for candidates.
The Secretary identifies candidates to be balloted for.
Brethren are given an opportunity to speak for or against a candidate before balloting.
The Senior Deacon is instructed to prepare the ballot-box.
The ballot-box is described as a small box, five or six inches square, with two drawers and a small hopper on top.
The hopper's hole leads to the first drawer, which is empty and shoved in.
The lower drawer is drawn out and contains both black and white balls.
The Senior Deacon places the ballot-box on the altar in the middle of the Lodge.
The Worshipful Master instructs the brethren to proceed with balloting.
The Master initiates the balloting process.
The Secretary calls the names of Masons, from the Senior Warden down to the Tyler.
Each Mason approaches the altar, makes the sign of a Master Mason to the Master.
Each Mason takes a ball (white or black) from the lower drawer of the ballot-box.
Each Mason deposits the ball in the hopper above and returns to their seat.
The Senior Deacon closes the ballot drawer after all have voted.
The Senior Deacon carries the ballot box to the Junior Warden in the south.
The Junior Warden examines the top drawer to determine if the ballot is 'clear' or 'not clear' and reports to the Worshipful Master.
The Deacon then carries the ballot box to the Senior Warden in the west for examination.
The Senior Warden examines the ballot and reports its status ('clear' or 'not clear') to the Worshipful Master.
The Deacon delivers the ballot box to the Worshipful Master's station in the east.
The Worshipful Master examines the box and declares whether the candidate (e.g., Mr. Joseph Locker) has been elected or not to the First Degree in Masonry.
The same balloting process is followed for any other candidates.
Candidate interrogated on motivations, desire for knowledge, service, and conformity to Order's usages.
Candidate requested to strip: coat, shoes, stockings, vest, cravat, pantaloons.
Candidate provided with a pair of drawers.
Left arm slipped out of shirt-sleeve and put through bosom, baring arm and breast.
Handkerchief or hoodwink tied over candidate's eyes.
Slipper placed on candidate's right foot.
Rope, called a cable-tow, placed once around candidate's neck, dragging behind.
Junior Deacon leads candidate to Lodge door and gives three distinct knocks.
Senior Deacon makes the sign of an Entered Apprentice to the Master.
Dialogue at the door between Senior Deacon and Junior Deacon (responding for candidate).
Senior Deacon takes compasses from the altar.
Senior Deacon presents one point of the compasses to the candidate's naked left breast.
Junior Deacon leaves candidate with Senior Deacon and takes his seat.
Senior Deacon, followed by two Stewards, travels once regularly around the Lodge-room with the candidate.
Worshipful Master gives one rap to stop the procession.
Candidate conducted to the center of the Lodge and kneels for prayer.
Worshipful Master leaves his seat, kneels by candidate, and recites a prayer.
All respond with "So mote it be."
Worshipful Master rises, takes candidate by the right hand, places left hand on candidate's head.
Candidate prompted to declare trust in God.
Candidate kneels for prayer.
Worshipful Master leaves his seat, kneels by the candidate, and recites a prayer.
Worshipful Master takes the candidate by the right hand and places his left on the candidate's head.
Candidate declares trust in God.
Worshipful Master assists the candidate to rise.
Senior Deacon conducts the candidate once around the Lodge room.
Worshipful Master recites the passage 'Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!' during the perambulation.
The Master, Junior Warden, and Senior Warden each give one gavel rap as the candidate passes their respective stations.
The candidate halts at the Junior Warden's station in the south.
Junior Warden gives one rap, followed by the conductor giving one rap.
Junior Warden asks, 'Who comes here?'
Candidate performs a duegard, alluding to the hand placement during the Entered Apprentice obligation.
Candidate makes the sign of an Entered Apprentice, alluding to the penalty of the obligation.
Senior Deacon guides the candidate to take the Junior Warden by the right hand for the token/grip.
The word 'Boaz' is lettered and then pronounced during the examination.
Candidate undergoes examination by the Junior Warden and Senior Warden.
Worshipful Master presents a white linen or lambskin apron to the candidate.
Senior Warden ties the apron on the candidate with the flap turned up, explaining how Entered Apprentices wore them at King Solomon's Temple.
Worshipful Master requests a metallic deposit from the candidate as a memento for the Lodge archives.
Concealing secrets of Masons, alluded to by pressing the thumb-nail on the first joint.
Being divested of all metals, neither naked nor clothed, barefoot nor shod, hoodwinked, with a cable-tow around the neck during preparation.
Gaining admission by three distinct knocks.
Being received on the point of a sharp instrument pressing the naked left breast.
Being conducted to the center of the Lodge and caused to kneel for prayer.
Being taken by the right hand and ordered to arise, follow the conductor, and fear no danger.
Following the conductor once around the Lodge to the Junior Warden's station in the south, then to the Senior Warden in the west, and finally to the Worshipful Master in the east.
Approaching the east by one upright, regular step, with feet forming an angle of an oblong square and body erect, at the altar.
Kneeling on the naked left knee, with the right forming a square, left hand supporting the Holy Bible, square, and compasses, and right hand resting thereon, to take the solemn oath.
Receiving light by order of the Worshipful Master and assistance of the brethren.
Worshipful Master approaching under the duegard and sign of an Entered Apprentice, presenting the right hand, grip, and word.
Saluting the Junior and Senior Wardens as an Entered Apprentice.
Receiving a lambskin or white linen apron and being taught to wear it with the flap turned up.
Being requested to deposit something of a metallic kind as a memorial.
Candidate is returned to the place from whence he came, reinvested, and returned to the Lodge for further instructions.
Candidate is placed in the northeast corner, with feet forming a right angle and body erect, at the right hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Worshipful Master presents the working-tools of an Entered Apprentice Mason: the twenty-four-inch gauge and common gavel.
Operative masons use the twenty-four-inch gauge to measure and lay out their work.
Gives candidate a rake across his breast with the hand to draw attention to the penalty.
Workmen's wages were paid daily, weekly, monthly, and quarterly in their respective Lodges.
Wages were paid in the Middle Chamber when the Temple was nearly completed.
The foot of the winding staircase to the Middle Chamber was guarded by the Junior Warden, and the summit by the Senior Warden of a Fellow Crafts' Lodge.
The Right Worshipful Master gives two raps with his gavel.
The Senior Grand Warden gives two raps.
The Junior Grand Warden gives two raps.
The raps are then repeated.
The Junior Deacon informs the Tyler that the Lodge is open.
The candidate divests himself of his coat and rolls up his shirt-sleeves to the shoulder.
The Senior Deacon and his associate also divest their coats and roll up their shirt-sleeves.
The Deacon takes a small block of marble or painted wood, about the size of a brick and weighing five or six pounds, with a square engraved upon it.
The Deacon's associate takes a similar block with a plumb engraved upon it.

The candidate is furnished with a block representing a keystone, which he carries between the thumb and two first fingers of the right hand, with other fingers clinched and the arm extended down perpendicularly at the side.
The three "Workmen from the quarries" form in a line about three feet distant from each other, with the candidate being last.
The door is opened without ceremony.
The Junior Deacon, as conductor, together with his associate and the candidate, enter the Lodge.
They march four times around the room.
They halt the last time at the Junior Overseer's station, at the south gate.
The conductor gives four raps (in couplets) on the floor with his heel.
The Senior Deacon presents his stone to the Junior Overseer.
The Junior Overseer applies his small trying square to the different angles of the stone.
Returning the block to the Senior Deacon after inspection.
Workmen moving six paces to position the candidate.
Junior Deacon instructing the candidate on how to make the alarm and present work.
Candidate presenting the keystone for inspection.
Junior Overseer applying a square to the keystone.
Junior Overseer pointing to letters on the keystone.
Candidate stating 'It is not' regarding the mark.
Passing on to the Senior Overseer at the west gate for further inspection.
Repeating the inspection scene at the Senior Overseer's station.
Proceeding to the Master Overseer at the east gate.
Senior Deacon presenting his block or stone to the Master Overseer.
Master Overseer applying his square to the Senior Deacon's work.
Master Overseer applying his square to the candidate's keystone.
Master Overseer stamping on the floor four times to summon other Overseers.
Master and Senior Overseers taking up the keystone.
Swinging the keystone four times back and forth.
Junior Overseer catching the keystone over the left shoulder of the Master Overseer (imitating the 'heave-over' sign).
Throwing the keystone aside as rubbish.
Brethren shuffling around the room, leaving their seats.
R. W. M. giving one rap with his gavel to restore order.
Lodge rising to their feet and singing a song.
Members forming two by two (candidate behind) and marching around the Lodge, singing.
Singing the 'Mark Master's Song' to the tune of 'America'.
Brothers walking up to the Senior Warden after the second verse.
Senior Warden standing behind a lattice-window.
Candidate thrusting right hand, with thumb and two first fingers open, third and little fingers clinched, palm up, through a hole in the window.
Receiving a penny as wages.
Senior Grand Warden seizing the candidate's hand and drawing his arm through to the shoulder.
Exclaiming 'An impostor! an impostor!'
Another person running up with a drawn sword, exclaiming 'Strike off his hand! strike off his hand!'
Senior Deacon interceding for the candidate, stating 'Spare him! spare him! he is not an impostor; I know him to be a craftsman; I have wrought with him in the quarries.'
Candidate withdrawing arm and giving the sign of a Fellow Craft Mason.
Senior Deacon taking candidate to the Master and stating the case.
Right Worshipful Master questioning the candidate about being a Fellow Craft Mason and asking for the sign.
Right Worshipful Master reprimanding the candidate.
Senior Deacon taking candidate by the collar and delivering a severe reprimand (often omitted).
Senior Deacon shaking the candidate severely and hurrying him to the preparation-room.
Junior Deacon divesting candidate of outward apparel, money, and valuables.
Baring the candidate's breast.
Placing a cable-tow four times around the candidate's body.
Blindfolding the candidate with a hoodwink.
Junior Deacon conducting the candidate to the door and giving four distinct knocks (• • • •).
Candidate receives a severe reprimand for presenting unfit work and claiming undeserved wages.
Candidate is taken to the quarries to learn how to bring up a regularly wrought stone.
Candidate is divested of outward apparel, money, and valuables.
Candidate's breast is made bare.
A cable-tow is placed four times around the candidate's body.
Candidate is securely blindfolded with a hoodwink.
Junior Deacon conducts candidate to the door and gives four distinct knocks (• • • •).
Senior Deacon answers the alarm with four knocks, responded to by one knock from outside, then returned by Senior Deacon.
Junior Deacon whispers the pass-word JOPPA to the Senior Deacon.
Candidate enters the Lodge.
Senior Deacon approaches candidate with a mallet and engraving chisel.
Senior Deacon places the edge of the chisel against the candidate's breast.
Senior Deacon raises mallet, makes false motions, and gives a violent blow upon the head of the chisel.
Senior Deacon throws down mallet and chisel, takes candidate's left arm, and instructs him to 'Follow me.'
Candidate and Senior Deacon walk four times around the Lodge.
Master, Senior, and Junior Grand Wardens each give one loud rap with their mallet as the candidate passes their stations.
Master reads scripture passages from Psalm cxviii. 22, Matthew xxi. 42, Mark xii. 10, and Luke xx. 17.
Candidate arrives at the Junior Warden's post, where four knocks are given, and questions are asked and answered.
Candidate proceeds to the Senior Warden, who gives four raps, and the same questions and answers are exchanged.
Candidate is directed to the Right Worshipful Master in the East, where the same questions are asked and answered.
The Master orders the candidate to be conducted back to the Senior Warden in the West.
The candidate is taught to approach the East by four upright, regular steps, with feet forming a square and body erect at the altar.
The candidate kneels to receive the obligation.
After the obligation, the Right Worshipful Master instructs the candidate to detach their hand and kiss the book four times.
An alarm is made on the outside of the door immediately after the obligation.
The Junior Deacon attends the alarm and returns with a letter for the Right Worshipful Master.
The letter requests a loan of twenty-five dollars, offering the sender's mark as security.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The recited verses symbolize the omnipresent and unwavering divine protection afforded to those who place their trust in a higher power. They convey the assurance that a divine guardian watches over and preserves individuals from harm, both physical and spiritual, through constant vigilance and angelic charge.

The Lodge is symbolically prepared in a room adjacent to a legally constituted Lodge, representing the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple. The Junior Warden's station in the south symbolizes the sun at high meridian, representing the beauty and glory of the day, and his role in observing time and managing periods of labor and refreshment. The Worshipful Master's station in the east symbolizes the rising sun, indicating his role in opening the Lodge. Harmony is presented as the strength of all institutions, especially Freemasonry.

The Junior Warden's station in the south symbolizes the sun at high meridian, representing the beauty and glory of the day, and his duty to regulate the balance between labor and refreshment. The Worshipful Master's station in the east symbolizes the rising sun, signifying his role in opening and governing the Lodge, setting the craft to work, and providing instruction. The signs of each degree (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason) allude to the penalty of their respective obligations, emphasizing the solemnity and consequences of their vows.

The physical preparation, including partial nudity, hoodwink, slipper, and cable-tow, symbolizes the candidate's vulnerable state, humility, and journey from darkness (ignorance) to light (knowledge). The hoodwink specifically represents the darkness from which the candidate seeks enlightenment. The cable-tow signifies the candidate's bond and obligation to the Craft. The presentation of the point of the compasses to the naked left breast serves as a powerful symbol and a physical reminder of the severe consequences and internal 'torture' to one's conscience should Masonic secrets be unlawfully revealed. The invocation of the Deity and the candidate's declaration of trust in God underscore the spiritual foundation of Freemasonry and the necessity of faith in a Supreme Being.

The act of kneeling for prayer signifies humility and a plea for divine guidance and aid in the candidate's journey into Masonry. The declaration of trust in God establishes a foundational principle of faith and reliance on a higher power. The perambulation around the Lodge room symbolizes the candidate's initial journey and introduction to the sacred space and its officers, while the accompanying passage emphasizes the core Masonic value of unity and brotherly love. The gavel raps serve as a ritualistic acknowledgment of the candidate's passage and the officers' presence.

The duegard and the sign of an Entered Apprentice symbolize the candidate's obligation and its associated penalty. The token and grip signify the commitment to conceal Masonic secrets. The word 'Boaz' serves as a password and a means to detect impostors. The lambskin or white apron is presented as an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason, described as more ancient and honorable than other prestigious orders. The metallic deposit, though of little intrinsic value, symbolizes a personal memento of the candidate's initiation into the Lodge.

The lambskin or white linen apron is an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason, signifying purity and honor. The Holy Bible is presented as the rule and guide to faith and practice, the square to square actions, and the compasses to circumscribe and keep Masons within bounds with all mankind, especially brother Masons. The three lesser lights (sun, moon, and Master of the Lodge) represent the principle of regularity in governance, as the sun rules the day and the moon governs the night, so the Worshipful Master should rule and govern the Lodge with equal regularity. Being brought from darkness to light symbolizes the candidate's transition from ignorance to Masonic knowledge.

The placement in the northeast corner symbolizes the candidate becoming an upright man and Mason. The twenty-four-inch gauge, divided into twenty-four equal parts, is emblematic of the twenty-four hours of the day. Its symbolic use teaches the division of time into three parts: for the service of God and relief of a distressed worthy brother, for usual avocations, and for refreshment and sleep, representing a balanced life of duty, labor, and rest.

Secret societies like the Essenes conveyed moral truths under symbolical figures. The breast, as the abode of fortitude, teaches the candidate to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity with patience rather than betraying secrets. Hebrew words ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) are symbolically interpreted to mean fortifying oneself with higher sciences and preparing to receive moral light. Shibboleth signifies both an ear of grain and a stream of water, with its symbolic interpretation increasing interest as one advances through the 'field of corn by the river-side'. The payment of wages without fear or scruple symbolizes the employers' entitlement to unlimited confidence.

The biblical quote emphasizes the candidate as a "living stone" to be built into a "spiritual house" and an "holy priesthood," highlighting the transformation from raw material to a refined, useful element in a sacred structure. The keystone, carried by the candidate, symbolizes the essential, crowning element of an arch, representing perfection and completion in the work. The square and plumb carried by the deacons represent accuracy, rectitude, and uprightness in their work.

The rejected keystone, despite its 'singular form and beauty,' symbolizes work that does not conform to established Masonic standards or lacks proper identification. Its rejection and casting aside as rubbish emphasize the importance of precision, adherence to rules, and accountability for one's craft. The subsequent impatience for wages and the song about the 'Sabbath' highlight the completion of labor and the expectation of just reward, contrasting with the fate of the non-conforming work.

The ritual symbolizes the importance of honest labor and the proper acquisition of wages, emphasizing that one must be duly qualified and recognized to receive the rewards of their craft. The 'impostor' scenario highlights the consequences of attempting to claim benefits or status without legitimate entitlement or skill, representing a test of integrity and knowledge. The threat to the right hand signifies the potential loss of one's ability to work and contribute, a severe penalty for deception in the craft. The intercession and subsequent instruction symbolize the mercy, guidance, and educational role of the fraternity in correcting errors and teaching proper conduct and skill. The keystone mentioned in the song represents a crucial element of the work and a symbol of recognition and fame for those who truly understand its significance.

The reprimand symbolizes the importance of diligence, skill, and integrity in one's work and the consequences of attempting to deceive or impose upon the Craft. The chisel and mallet, used by operative masons to shape stone, are symbolically employed by Free and Accepted Masons to 'hew, cut, carve, and indent the mind,' signifying the moral and intellectual refinement of the candidate. The reception upon the chisel and mallet represents the enduring impression of the Mark Master's lessons. The scripture passages about 'the stone which the builders refused' becoming 'the head stone of the corner' allude to the value of that which is initially rejected or overlooked, and its ultimate importance, a central theme of the Mark Master degree.

The four upright, regular steps forming a square symbolize precision, rectitude, and the candidate's commitment to moral and Masonic principles in their approach to sacred duties. The repeated questioning at various posts signifies the importance of consistency, truthfulness, and the candidate's unwavering affirmation of their commitment. The 'mark' itself is a profound symbol of a personal pledge, a bond of trust, and a form of security or credit within the fraternity, representing a brother's honor and commitment. Its specified value (half a shekel of silver or quarter of a dollar) gives it a tangible, albeit symbolic, economic weight. The severe penalties of having the right ear smitten off and the right hand chopped off symbolize the complete forfeiture of the ability to hear the sacred word and to perform Masonic work, representing the ultimate consequence for an impostor or oath-breaker.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate is taught the profound importance of faith and unwavering trust in the divine. This segment instills the lesson that with such trust comes the promise of divine protection, guidance, and preservation from evil, reinforcing a sense of security and spiritual guardianship.

The candidate learns that their obligation is what makes them an Entered Apprentice Mason. They are taught the importance of temperance and avoiding excess during periods of refreshment. The concept of harmony as the strength of the institution is also emphasized.

Candidates are taught the importance of temperance and moderation during refreshment, the value of harmony and satisfaction among brethren, and the necessity of proper conduct within the Lodge. The performance of the duegards and signs, which allude to the penalties of the obligations, serves as a constant reminder of the seriousness of their vows and the duties they have undertaken.

The candidate is taught the importance of sincere and honorable motivations for seeking admission, including a desire for knowledge and a commitment to serving fellow creatures. The ritualistic reception, particularly the point of the compasses, impresses upon the candidate the solemnity of the obligation and the grave consequences of violating Masonic trust and secrecy. The prayer and the declaration of trust in God instill the fundamental lesson of reliance on a divine power and the moral principles of brotherly love, relief, and truth that are central to the Masonic way of life.

The candidate learns the importance of seeking divine aid and dedicating their life to service. They are taught that their faith in God is a well-founded trust. The perambulation and accompanying passage instill the lesson of unity and brotherly love as essential tenets of Freemasonry, encouraging them to become a true and faithful brother.

The candidate learns the solemnity of the Entered Apprentice obligation and the importance of maintaining secrecy regarding Masonic knowledge. They are taught the significance of the lambskin apron as a symbol of purity and honor within the fraternity. The ritual also imparts the tradition of contributing a personal item as a lasting reminder of their initiation.

The candidate is taught the importance of secrecy regarding Masonic knowledge and the solemnity of the Entered Apprentice obligation. Trust in God is emphasized as a well-founded faith. The candidate learns the moral application of the working tools: to square their actions and to circumscribe their passions and desires, keeping them within due bounds. The apron teaches the value of innocence and purity, and the overall ritual emphasizes the importance of free will, worthiness, and good repute for admission into the fraternity.

The candidate is strictly charged to ever walk and act as an upright man and Mason. He is taught the importance of wisely dividing his time to fulfill his duties to God and his brethren, attend to his daily work, and allow for necessary rest.

Candidates are taught to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity, distress, pain, or disappointment with patience. This fortitude prevents the bitter stings of remorse that result from betraying secrets entrusted under solemn obligation. The neophyte should prepare to receive moral light through higher sciences. Candidates are directed to an attentive study of the liberal arts and sciences. The legend of wages paid without fear or scruple teaches the importance of unlimited confidence in employers.

The candidate is taught to be a "living stone," chosen and precious, contributing to a "spiritual house" through "well-doing." They are encouraged to act as free servants of God, honoring all men, loving the brotherhood, and fearing God, putting to silence the ignorance of foolish men. The act of carrying the keystone and presenting work for inspection teaches the importance of diligent, accurate, and upright labor, and the necessity of having one's work proven worthy.

The candidate learns the critical importance of presenting work that is 'good work, true work, and square work,' adhering strictly to the craft's authorized standards and bearing the proper mark of a craftsman. It teaches about the consequences of attempting to pass off unauthorized or improperly marked work, and the necessity of personal integrity and accountability in all endeavors.

The candidate learns the critical importance of integrity, honesty, and proper qualification in all endeavors, especially concerning work and reward. It teaches that one must earn their wages through approved work and legitimate means, and that attempting to deceive or claim what is not due will lead to severe consequences. The lesson also emphasizes the value of humility, acknowledging one's inexperience, and being open to instruction from more experienced brethren to improve one's craft and conduct.

The candidate is taught the critical importance of performing work with skill and industry, and the ethical principle of not claiming rewards or wages that are not rightfully earned. The reprimand serves as a 'striking lesson' against imposition upon the Craft. The symbolic use of the chisel and mallet emphasizes the continuous process of self-improvement and the shaping of one's character and mind. The scripture readings impart a lesson on the potential for the rejected to become essential, encouraging perseverance and recognizing inherent worth.

The candidate learns the paramount importance of strict secrecy regarding the Mark Master Mason's secrets, disclosing them only to duly qualified brethren after rigorous examination. They are taught the duty to uphold the Constitutions of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter and State Grand Royal Arch Chapter, as well as the by-laws of any Mark Master Lodge. The obligation instills obedience to all regular signs and summonses from fellow Mark Master Masons or Lodges, within the bounds of their cable-tow. A strong ethical lesson is imparted regarding not wronging a brother or the Lodge, even to the smallest value, and preventing such wrongs if possible. The unique rules surrounding the 'mark' teach about personal integrity, the sanctity of a pledge, and the responsibility to honor commitments related to one's mark, including its redemption. The lesson of mutual aid is emphasized through the promise to receive a brother's mark when a favor is requested and to grant it if possible, or to return its specified value if the favor cannot be granted.
tradition_notes: The number of raps given by the Worshipful Master varies by degree: one for Entered Apprentice, two for Fellow Craft, and three for Master Mason.
The Lodge is symbolically represented as the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple.
The traditional response to 'Are you an Entered Apprentice Mason?' is 'I am so taken and accepted among all brothers and fellows.'
The stations of the officers in the Lodge (East, West, South) are symbolically aligned with the course of the sun.
The Master always leads in giving the sign or signs during the opening ceremony.
The duegard represents the position of the hands when taking the oath of a degree.
The process of balloting for candidates, including the opportunity for discussion and the use of a specific ballot-box, is a traditional Masonic procedure for admission to the First Degree.
The specific sequence of officers examining the ballot box (Junior Warden, Senior Warden, Worshipful Master) is a traditional procedural element.
The use of white and black balls for voting is a traditional method of secret ballot in Freemasonry.
The requirement for each Mason to make the sign of a Master Mason to the Master before voting is a traditional ritualistic detail.
The text notes that the Worshipful Master sometimes addresses the candidate as 'Stranger!' instead of by name during the declaration of trust in God.
The Junior Deacon 'always responds for the candidate' during the initial questioning at the door.
The phrase 'as all brothers and fellows have done before' emphasizes the continuity of Masonic tradition.
Masters sometimes use 'Stranger!' instead of the candidate's name when asking about trust.
The passage 'Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!' is noted as being from 'opening, or Monitor's.'
The lambskin apron is described as 'more ancient than the Golden Fleece or Roman Eagle' and 'more honorable than the Star and Garter'.
Entered Apprentices wore their aprons with the flap turned up 'at the building of King Solomon's Temple'.
The request for a metallic deposit is an 'ancient custom, adopted among Masons'.
The lambskin or white linen apron is described as 'more ancient than the Golden Fleece or Roman Eagle; more honorable than the Star or Garter, or any other order'.
The request to deposit something of a metallic kind is noted as 'agreeably to an ancient custom, adopted in every regulated and well-governed Lodge'.
Calmet asserts that St. John was an Essene, a secret society of his day that conveyed moral truths under symbolical figures, which may be termed Freemasonry under another name.
The Jewish Cabala includes a rule called Transposition, used to find meaning in anagrammatically formed words.
Brother Rosenberg, a Jewish Mason, used Transposition to interpret ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) in Hebrew, relating them to receiving higher sciences and moral light.
SHIBBOLETH in Hebrew has two significations: an ear of grain and a stream of water.
The historical account of the Ephraimites and Gileadites illustrates how the word 'Shibboleth' was used as a test, exploiting a peculiarity in the Ephraimites' speech organs.

The Fellow Crafts' Degree Lecture's first section recapitulates the ceremonies of passing a candidate, and the second section discusses the ancient division into operative and speculative Masons and the liberal arts and sciences.

According to Masonic legends, workmen's wages were paid in their respective Lodges, and later in the Middle Chamber, which was accessible by a winding staircase guarded by the Junior and Senior Wardens.
No business other than candidate initiation is done in a Lodge of Mark Master Masons.
All other business, such as balloting for candidates and committee reports, is done in the Seventh, or Royal Arch Degree.
The Mark Master Degree is under the sanction of the Royal Arch Chapter.
The Lodge is declared opened in "due and ancient form."
The ritual involves a specific sequence of inspection by three Overseers (Junior, Senior, Master) at different 'gates' (stations).
The 'heave-over' sign is a specific ritualistic gesture associated with casting aside the keystone.
The timing of the disturbance and the call for wages is traditionally set at 'the sixth hour of the sixth day of the week,' signifying the end of the work week.
The singing of a hymn and a procession for wages are traditional elements concluding this part of the ritual.
The severe reprimand ordered to be given to the candidate is omitted in most Lodges at the present day, but is inserted in this text for the satisfaction of young Masons and the curious.
The severe reprimand ordered to be given to the candidate is omitted in most Lodges at the present day.
The Lodge is erected to Almighty God and dedicated to Hiram the Builder.
The obligation is stated to be 'in addition to my former obligations,' indicating a progressive system of degrees.

The obligation explicitly mentions supporting the 'Constitution of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America' and the 'Grand Royal Arch Chapter of this State,' placing the Mark Master degree within the American Royal Arch system.
The 'mark' chosen by a brother must be recorded in a 'book of marks.'
The value of a brother's mark, when a favor cannot be granted, is specified as 'half a shekel of silver, or quarter of a dollar,' linking ancient and modern monetary equivalents.
caution_notes: The Tyler's duty is to keep off all cowans and eavesdroppers.
The Junior Warden must ensure that the means of refreshment are not converted into intemperance or excess.
Means of refreshment should not be converted into intemperance or excess.
Private committees and improper, unmasonic conduct are strictly forbidden during Lodge business, under penalty determined by a majority of brethren acting under by-laws.
Duegards and signs must be made with right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, with very slight, but marked pauses between each motion or part of the sign.
When making the duegard and sign of the Fellow Craft, the left arm must be dropped suddenly and with spirit as soon as the two motions are accomplished.

The point of a sharp instrument pressing the naked left breast is to teach the candidate that 'as it is a torture to your flesh, so should the recollection of it ever be to your mind and conscience, should you attempt to reveal the secrets of Masonry unlawfully.'
The spelling of 'Boaz' (always beginning with 'A') is noted as a way Masons detect impostors, such as 'Morgan or book Masons'.
The candidate is obligated to conceal 'All the secrets of Masons, in Masons'.
A rake across the breast is given to draw the candidate's attention to the penalty.
Betrayal of secrets entrusted on the faith of a solemn obligation will inevitably result in the bitter stings of remorse.
The Right Worshipful Master forbids "all improper conduct whereby this Lodge may be disturbed, under no less penalty than the by-laws of a majority of the Lodge may see fit to inflict."

The Master Overseer's stern look and questioning ('Why do you bring another man's work to impose upon the Overseers?') serve as a caution against deception, presenting work that is not one's own, or attempting to circumvent established inspection processes.
Caution against attempting to receive wages without being able to give the token.
Warning that such conduct requires severe punishment, with the penalty being to have the right hand struck off.
The ritual includes a dramatic threat of striking off the hand and even life, emphasizing the seriousness of the offense.

I will not give the secrets of a Mark Master Mason to any one of an inferior degree, nor to any other person in the known world, except it be a true and lawful brother, or brethren, of this degree; and not unto him nor unto them whom I shall hear so to be, but unto him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Lodge, or a brother of this Degree, to the value of his wages (or one penny), myself, knowingly, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.

I will not sell, swap, barter, or exchange my mark, which I shall hereafter choose, after it has been recorded in the book of marks, for any other one, unless it be a dead mark, or one of an older date, nor will I pledge it a second time until it is lawfully redeemed from the first pledge.

Binding myself under no less penalty than to have my right ear smitten off, that I may forever be unable to hear the word, and my right hand chopped off, as the penalty of an impostor, if I should ever prove wilfully guilty of violating any part of this my solemn oath, or obligation, of a Mark Master Mason.
