entries.title: Opening
entries.short_summary: The opening of the Lodge involves a formal processional entry, typically accompanied by music, after which all members take their seats before the formal exercises of the meeting begin.
entries.full_summary:
The opening of the Lodge involves a formal processional entry, typically accompanied by music, after which all members take their seats before the formal exercises of the meeting begin.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0033 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Rough Ashler

This section introduces the concepts of the Rough Ashler and Perfect Ashler, fundamental symbols in Freemasonry. It is accompanied by footnotes that elaborate on specific Masonic traditions and terminology. These include the practice of the Master wearing a hat in certain lodges, a detailed etymological and symbolic explanation of the term 'cowan' as an unqualified or profane intruder, and a description of the solemn Lodge Opening ceremony, emphasizing duties, obligations, and precautions against the profane.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0033 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0034 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Rough Ashler

This section discusses fundamental aspects of Masonic practice, including the concept of Masonry as a cohesive whole where each Mason plays a part. It details the solemn procedure of opening a Lodge, emphasizing officer duties, and the protocol for a Mason entering an opened Lodge, which involves approaching the altar, facing the Worshipful Master, and giving the duegard and sign of the degree. The text also highlights a mandatory emblematical feature that every initiated Mason must undergo as an indispensable pledge of fidelity, regardless of personal objections, noting its ancient and universal nature.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0034 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0051 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Master Mason: Opening, Obligation, Overview

This section introduces the Master Mason degree, focusing on the traditional narrative of Hiram Abiff's death, multiple burials, and resurrections. It highlights the absence of Hiram Abiff's death in external historical records like the Bible or Josephus, emphasizing his significant role as an arbiter and a distinguished figure. The text also describes a Master Mason's Lodge as the 'Sanctum Sanctorum' of King Solomon's Temple and details the ritualistic opening where both points of the compasses are elevated above the square. Candidate preparation for this sublime degree is typically performed by the Junior Deacon and Stewards.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0051 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0127 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Appendix: Original Points and Commentary

This appendix section details several foundational Masonic concepts, including the twelve 'Original Points' essential for legal initiation, the symbolic meaning of the penal sign linked to Adam's fall and divine judgment, and the specific phrases used to describe a candidate's progression through various degrees (initiated, passed, raised, etc.). It also explains the 'Sign of Distress' as a call for fraternal assistance and discusses the traditional Masonic stance against written work to maintain uniformity, emphasizing oral transmission. Finally, it touches upon the historical use of vaults for secret purposes and questions the historical accuracy of the tradition regarding workmen's wages at Solomon's Temple.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0127 -->
practical_elements: Lodge members enter in procession
Music should be played during the procession
All members are to be seated
Exercises will be opened
In most foreign Lodges, the Master wears his hat while the rest of the brethren remain uncovered, a practice followed by MacKenzie Beverly Esq., D. P. G. M. for the East Riding of York.
The ceremony of OPENING THE LODGE is solemn and impressive, reminding every brother of his duties and obligations.
Necessary precautions are employed during the Lodge Opening to avoid the intrusion of the profane.
Every member is compelled to assume a share of responsibility during the Lodge Opening ceremony.
Opening the Lodge is a solemn and imposing rite.
Every officer is made acquainted with his duty and impressed with its importance.
When a Mason enters an opened Lodge, he proceeds to the center of the Lodge, at the altar.
The Mason must face the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Mason must give the duegard and sign of the degree in which the Lodge is working.
The duegard is never omitted when the Master is addressed.
Every initiated person is bound to pass through an emblematical feature as an unmistakable pledge of fidelity.
This emblematical feature is an indispensable condition of Masonic tenure and cannot be avoided.
A Master Mason's Lodge is opened with both points of the compasses elevated above the square.
A candidate for the Master Mason Degree is generally prepared by the Junior Deacon and the two Stewards, or other brethren acting as such.

The twelve 'Original Points' are essential forms and ceremonies for legal reception into the Order: opening, preparing, reporting, entering, prayer, circumambulation, advancing, obligation, intrusted, invested, placed, closing.
The penal sign marks the obligation.
A candidate is 'initiated' for the first Degree, 'passed' for the second, 'raised' for the third.
A candidate is 'congratulated' (advanced) for the Mark Degree.
One who has 'passed the chair' is said to have 'presided'.
A Most Excellent Master is 'acknowledged and received'.
A Royal Arch Mason is 'exalted'.
The sign of distress, in conjunction with a few words, is used by a brother in difficulty or danger to claim assistance and protection from other brethren.
Masonic mysteries are communicated orally to candidates, not written.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The Rough Ashler symbolizes man in his rude and unpolished state, while the Perfect Ashler represents man in a state of moral and intellectual refinement achieved through education and discipline. The term 'cowan' is symbolically linked to 'dogs' (κυνες) or 'evil workers' in biblical contexts, representing unqualified or profane listeners and intruders in a Masonic setting, emphasizing the need for vigilance and purity within the Lodge.

Masonry is presented as a whole, with each Mason forming an integral part. The 'emblematical feature' serves as a profound pledge of fidelity, drawing an analogy to the customs of primitive nations and suggesting an ancient, unfathomable origin.

A Master Mason's Lodge is symbolically styled the 'Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies, of King Solomon's Temple'. The elevation of both points of the compasses above the square during the opening of the Lodge signifies the advanced knowledge and moral principles of the Master Mason degree.

The penal sign symbolizes the obligation and serves as a reminder of Adam's fall, the penalty of death, and the judgment of God upon the disobedient, likening it to the severing of the head from the body by human justice. Vaults are noted as being used for secret purposes.
entries.candidate_lesson:
Candidates are taught the importance of continuous moral and intellectual self-improvement, striving to transform from a 'Rough Ashler' to a 'Perfect Ashler'. They learn the necessity of upholding their duties and obligations within the Lodge and the critical importance of guarding the sanctity and privacy of Masonic proceedings from the profane and unqualified.

Candidates are taught that Masonry is a serious and cohesive institution requiring adherence to its forms and procedures. They must understand the importance of officer duties and the solemnity of Lodge operations. Furthermore, they learn that certain 'emblematical features' are mandatory and indispensable conditions for being a Mason, serving as a pledge of fidelity, and must be endured with patience, regardless of personal feelings.

The Hiramic legend, central to the Master Mason degree, conveys lessons through the account of Hiram Abiff's death, burials, and resurrections. It emphasizes the importance of fidelity and the pursuit of truth, even in the face of adversity, and introduces themes of mortality and immortality.

Every candidate must pass through the twelve 'Original Points' for their initiation to be considered legal. A true and faithful brother is obligated to respond to the sign of distress, offering assistance and protection to a brother in need. Masons are taught to uphold their obligations by not writing down the work, preserving the oral tradition.
tradition_notes: The tradition of a processional entry for the Lodge opening.
The customary use of music during the opening procession.
In most foreign Lodges, the Master wears his hat while the rest of the brethren remain uncovered, a practice historically followed by figures like MacKenzie Beverly Esq.
The term 'cowan' originated from the affair of Jephthah, where an Ephraimite was termed a cowan.
Historically, 'cohen' in Egypt was a title of honor for a priest or prince, but also meant 'dog,' leading to symbolic associations with 'dogs of Jove' and later, unqualified intruders in Masonry.
Opening the Lodge is described as a 'solemn and imposing rite.'
The 'emblematical feature' bears a 'beautiful analogy to the customs of all primitive nations' and its origin 'may be reasonably ascribed to some unfathomable antiquity.'
The traditional account of Hiram Abiff's death, several burials, and resurrections is central to the Master Mason Degree.
Hiram Abiff is described as one of the head workmen at King Solomon's Temple.
Ancient writings inform us that Hiram Abiff was an arbiter between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre.
The tragical death of Hiram Abiff is not recorded in the Bible, the writings of Josephus, or any other ancient writings known outside of Freemasonry.
Hiram Abiff was universally acknowledged as the third most distinguished man then living, and in many respects, the greatest man in the world.
Ancient Masonry recognized twelve original points as the basis of the entire system.
The penal sign applies to both the Entered Apprentice and Royal Arch Mason's 'Duegard'.

The tradition regarding the payment of workmen's wages at the building of Solomon's Temple may not be historically accurate and could have been fabricated in a subsequent age without authenticating documents.
caution_notes: St. John cautions Christian brethren that 'without are dogs' (κυνες), referring to cowans or listeners (Rev. xxii. 15).
St. Paul exhorts Christians to 'beware of dogs, because they are evil workers' (Phil. W. 2).
Necessary precautions are employed during the Lodge Opening to avoid the intrusion of the profane.
The text implies a caution against resisting the 'emblematical feature,' stating that a Mason 'has no option' and 'cannot avoid it,' even if they 'may not like it' or 'think it degrading.'
Writing the Masonic work is considered a violation of obligations and would lead to a general uprising of the craft.
A General Grand Lodge attempting to authorize written work for uniformity would be a foolish sanction by the fraternity.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0008 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Opening the Lodge: Officer Stations and Duties

This section details the ritualistic opening of a Masonic Lodge, focusing on the stations and duties of various officers, including the Tyler, Junior Deacon, Senior Warden, Senior Deacon, Secretary, Treasurer, Junior Warden, and Worshipful Master. It outlines the procedure for ensuring the Lodge is properly tyled, confirming the qualification of members, and the symbolic significance of officer placements and responsibilities, particularly for an Entered Apprentice Lodge.

This section details the stations and duties of the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Worshipful Master during the opening of a Masonic Lodge. It explains their symbolic positions relative to the sun and their responsibilities, such as calling the craft to refreshment, paying wages, and governing the Lodge. The text also outlines the procedure for opening the Lodge on the First, Second, or Third Degree, including the communication of orders and the performance of the duegards and signs for the Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, and Master Mason degrees, with specific instructions on their execution and symbolic allusions to their respective obligations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0008 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0019 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Preparation of the Candidate

This section details the initial preparation and entry of a candidate for the First Degree in Masonry. It begins with an interrogation to confirm the candidate's motivations and willingness to conform to Masonic usages. Following assent, the candidate undergoes specific physical preparation, including partial disrobing and the application of a hoodwink, slipper, and cable-tow. The narrative then describes the ritualistic knocking at the Lodge door, the dialogue between the Deacons regarding the candidate's qualifications and desire for light, and the formal reception into the Lodge. This reception involves the symbolic presentation of the compasses to the candidate's naked breast, followed by a prayer led by the Worshipful Master and the candidate's declaration of trust in God.

This section details the final preparatory steps for an Entered Apprentice candidate. It includes the candidate kneeling for a prayer recited by the Worshipful Master, followed by the Master asking the candidate in whom they put their trust. Upon declaring trust in God, the candidate is assisted to rise and begins a perambulation around the Lodge room, guided by the Senior Deacon. During this circuit, the Worshipful Master recites a passage on unity, and the Master, Junior Warden, and Senior Warden each give a single gavel rap as the candidate passes their stations. The perambulation concludes at the Junior Warden's station, where a challenge is issued.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0019 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Degree: Historical Notes

This section provides historical notes and explanations for various elements of the Entered Apprentice Degree. It discusses the reverence associated with removing footwear in the East, linking it to the purity of the Mosaic pavement. It emphasizes that a candidate's admission is solely based on their avowal of belief and trust in God. Practical details are given regarding the number of candidates that can be initiated, passed, or raised in each degree. The text debunks claims that biblical figures like Adam, Moses, or Solomon were Freemasons. It clarifies the dual meaning of the word 'Hail' or 'Hale' in Masonic context. Specific ritual actions, such as drawing a hand or sword across the candidate's throat, are mentioned. The story of Hipparchus, a Pythagorean, is recounted as a cautionary tale about the severe consequences of violating societal engagements. The concept of 'Light' in initiation is explained as progressing from material to intellectual illumination. Finally, the symbolic meaning of the first sign, related to the palate and throat, is detailed as a lesson in temperance and restraint of passions, and the role of the Master and Senior Warden in laying the corner-stone is noted, along with the practice of fostering emulation and discussion in Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0083 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Critical Notes on the Third Degree

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing the historical variations in Masonic 'work' and lectures across different U.S. states and Europe. It highlights the influence of Brother John Barney's lectures, derived from the Preston Lectures, and their adoption in the Northwestern states, noting how Barney adapted his teachings to different systems in the Western and Southwestern states. The text also mentions the impact of 'Morganry' in Minnesota and New York, where rituals closely resemble Morgan's disclosures. It asserts that while minor differences exist, the essential points of Masonry are universal. Footnotes offer critical commentary on the Third Degree's nature, describing it as traditionary, historical, and legendary, but also hyperbolical, apocryphal, and fabulous. They include practical details of a ritualistic element involving a sword, discuss the symbolism of worldly possession and the historical connections of Tubal Cain, Jubal, and Vulcan. Detailed descriptions of Master's Lodge regalia and officer jewels are provided, alongside the historical evolution of the 'fatal weapons' used in the Third Degree. The section also references Professor Stuart's critique regarding the linguistic origins of names in the legend and notes variations in the narrative concerning the number of Fellow Crafts searching for Hiram and the placement of the sprig of acacia.

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing variations in the Hiramic legend, including the disputed location of the lost body and the names of the ruffians. It clarifies the symbolic use of acacia, distinguishing it from cassia, and explains its significance in Jewish law for marking graves. The text also connects Masonic penalties to ancient Hebrew covenant ceremonies, describing their ritualistic elements. Furthermore, it discusses the allegorical nature of the Master Mason degree, particularly regarding the lost word and the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, cautioning against literal historical interpretations of the legend.

This section critically examines the historical and internal consistency of the Hiramic legend within the Third Degree. It challenges the historical accuracy of Hiram Abiff's death and burial, citing Dr. Anderson and Josephus, who assert Hiram lived to complete the Temple and returned to Tyre. The text also highlights inconsistencies in ritual narratives, such as Hiram drawing plans in the Holy of Holies before its completion or accessibility, and the division of operatives and payment of wages before the pillars and Middle Chamber were built. Despite these critiques, the section reaffirms the symbolic importance of the legend in teaching the doctrine of resurrection, fidelity, and a future state, representing a Master Mason as one saved from iniquity and raised to faith.

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing historical variations in ritual descriptions of the "three principal entrances" to the Temple across different jurisdictions and time periods. It outlines the structure of the Master Mason lecture, covering the mode of raising, historical traditions, and emblem explanations. A significant portion is dedicated to an extensive Christian allegorical interpretation of the Third Degree, linking Hiram Abiff's death and resurrection to the Christian dispensation, the state of religion before Christ, and its restoration by the Saviour. This allegory is further elaborated with specific parallels drawn from the 32nd Degree, connecting Hiram's fate to Christ's condemnation, suffering, and the symbolism of the lost Word, false brethren, and the sprig of cassia. The text also includes a description of the solemn closing ceremony of a Mason's Lodge and an author's note emphasizing the allegorical nature of the Hiramic legend over its literal historical accuracy, citing scriptural evidence. Finally, it briefly defines the role of a Grand Lecturer.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0083 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0088 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master's Song.

This section details the 'Mark Master's Song' and a significant ritualistic drama involving a candidate attempting to receive wages. The song calls Mark Masters to present their work for inspection by the Chief Overseer and Chief Architect, emphasizing the importance of approved work and the right to wages. It references Hiram, the widow's son, and the keystone. The ritual reenacts a scenario where a candidate, after the second verse of the song, attempts to receive a penny from the Senior Warden through a lattice-window, using a specific hand gesture. The candidate is accused of being an 'impostor' for not being able to give the proper token, leading to a dramatic threat of having his hand struck off. The Senior Deacon intercedes, vouching for the candidate as a craftsman and promising to take him to the Right Worshipful Master. The candidate then proves himself a Fellow Craft Mason. The Right Worshipful Master sternly reprimands the candidate for attempting to receive wages without the token, but remits the penalty due to youth and inexperience, ordering the Senior Deacon to teach him how to bring up a regularly wrought stone in the quarries. The reprimand itself is often omitted in modern lodges but is included for historical context. The section concludes with the preparation of the candidate for the degree, including divesting of apparel, money, and valuables, baring the breast, a cable-tow four times around the body, and blindfolding with a hoodwink, followed by four distinct knocks at the door.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0088 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0089 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master Reception and Obligation

This section details the reception and obligation ceremony for the Mark Master Degree. It begins with a severe reprimand given to the candidate for presenting unfit work and claiming undeserved wages, a practice noted as often omitted in modern lodges. The candidate is then prepared by being divested of valuables, blindfolded, and having a cable-tow placed around his body. After an alarm at the door, the candidate, identified as a Master Mason seeking further light, is admitted using the pass-word JOPPA. The Senior Deacon then symbolically receives the candidate upon the edge of an indenting chisel and under the pressure of a mallet, explaining their use for 'indenting the mind.' The ceremony concludes with a perambulation around the Lodge, during which the Master reads scripture passages concerning 'the stone which the builders refused/rejected' becoming 'the head stone of the corner.'

This section details the reception and obligation of a Mark Master Mason. The candidate undergoes a ritualistic perambulation, answering questions at the posts of the Junior and Senior Wardens, and the Right Worshipful Master. The core of the section is the extensive obligation, which includes promises of secrecy, adherence to Masonic law, obedience to summonses, and specific rules regarding the use, exchange, and redemption of a personal 'mark.' It also outlines severe penalties for violating the oath and concludes with a practical demonstration of how a brother's mark can be used to request financial aid.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0089 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0091 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master: Grips and the Wages Parable

This section details the grips and passwords of a Mark Master Mason, including the pass-grip (JOPPA) and the real grip (SIROC or MARK WELL). It then describes a ritualistic parable concerning a keystone ordered by Hiram Abiff, which was initially rejected by the Overseers but later found in the rubbish. The Right Worshipful Master presents this keystone, reads several scriptural passages about the 'stone which the builders refused,' and invests the candidate with a new name, represented by a circle of letters on the keystone.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0091 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0102 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Page 344

This section details the ceremonies of the Past Master Degree, which often involves a mock lodge closing and business session designed to test the candidate. The candidate, placed in the Master's chair, is expected to close the Lodge but is reminded of the obligation to deliver a lecture. The experience is intended to teach the necessity of being qualified for any office accepted. The lecture for this degree covers constituting a Lodge, installation ceremonies, laying foundation stones, dedicating Masonic Halls, and funeral ceremonies. The text also provides historical context for the Past Master Degree, explaining its origin as a prerequisite for Royal Arch Masonry when it was governed by symbolic Lodges, and the practice of granting dispensations to 'pass the chair' for Master Masons not actually elected to preside.

This section explains the Past Master's Degree, distinguishing between 'Actual Past Masters' who are elected to preside over a symbolic Lodge and 'Virtual Past Masters' who receive the degree in a Chapter as a prerequisite for the Royal Arch. It clarifies that the Virtual Past Master status does not confer official rank or privileges in a symbolic Lodge. The text includes a closing prayer for the Lodge and a detailed Q&A lecture on the Past Master's Degree, covering the obligation, specific signs (the duegard and 'the sign'), the grip, the word 'Stone Squarer,' the jewel of office (a square), the three great lights (Holy Bible, square, and compasses) and their varying positions across the Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, and Master Mason degrees, and the charter or warrant.

This section details the various items presented to a Master, likely during an installation, and the extensive duties associated with the office. These include the constitution, by-laws, records, the privilege of being covered, the Great Lights (Bible, Square, and Compasses) with their specific positions for each degree, the charter or warrant, and the gavel. The Master's responsibilities encompass opening, instructing, and closing Lodges, initiating, crafting, and raising Masons, and presiding over numerous Masonic ceremonies.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0102 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Royal Arch: Exaltation Ceremony

The Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony details the admission process for candidates seeking further light in Masonry. After an alarm at the Chapter door, candidates, who have previously attained the degrees of Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master, confirm their free will, preparation, and proficiency. They present a pass word, RABBONI, before being admitted under a 'Living Arch' formed by companions. This arch involves physical prostration and difficulty in passage. Candidates then kneel at the altar for their seventh obligation, a solemn oath covering secrecy, protection of companions, non-revelation of ineffable characters or the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word (except under specific conditions), avoidance of clandestine chapters, and strict adherence to procedural requirements such as exalting exactly three candidates at a time and requiring nine Royal Arch Masons to open a Chapter.

This section details the extensive obligation of a Royal Arch Mason, covering secrecy, prerequisites for candidates (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master), rules for exaltation (three candidates at a time, nine Masons for opening a Chapter), conduct towards fellow Companions (no speaking evil, apprising of danger, preference in employment, assistance in difficulty, keeping secrets, aiding the indigent), and adherence to constitutional laws and by-laws. The obligation concludes with a severe penalty for violation. Following the obligation, the ritual describes the candidate kissing the book seven times and the Principal Sojourner's address, emphasizing the importance of the Royal Arch secrets and the necessity of trials through 'rough and rugged ways' to test fidelity. The candidates are then led around the Chapter and kneel for a prayer to the 'Supreme and inscrutable Architect of universal Nature'.

This section describes a prayer offered during the Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony, emphasizing gratitude for human reason, capacity for improvement, and the institution of Freemasonry. It highlights new obligations of virtue and holiness, reverence for God's name, and the diligent search for divine authority in nature. The prayer concludes with a plea for devotion, love, and charity, and for heavenly grace to assist in rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' after the 'first temple of purity and innocence' was destroyed by sin. Following the prayer, the Principal Sojourner leads candidates around the Chapter, reading the first six verses of Exodus chapter 3, which describe Moses encountering the burning bush on Mount Horeb. The candidates are then brought before a representation of the burning bush, and their blindfolds are removed.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->

Duegard of an Entered Apprentice: Left hand supporting the Bible, right hand resting thereon.
Sign of an Entered Apprentice: Draw the right hand rapidly across the neck and drop the arm to the side.
Duegard of a Fellow Craft: Left arm held horizontally to the elbow, rest of the arm vertically, forming a square; right hand detached from the stomach, fingers extending outward.
Sign of a Fellow Craft is given after its duegard.
Duegard of a Master Mason: Both hands resting on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
Duegards and signs are made with right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, with slight, marked pauses.
For Fellow Craft duegard and sign, the left arm must be dropped suddenly and with spirit.
Candidate interrogated on motivations, desire for knowledge, service, and conformity to Order's usages.

Putting off the covering of the feet as a sign of reverence, paralleling Eastern customs.
Candidate avows belief and trust in God as the sole basis for admission; refusal leads to rejection.
Chief officer expresses satisfaction upon a candidate's declaration of trust in God.
If more than one candidate is initiated at once, they are required to take hold of each other's arms.
Up to five candidates can be initiated as Entered Apprentices at once.
Up to five candidates can be 'passed' to the Second Degree at once.
Only one candidate at a time can be 'raised' to the Master's Degree.
Small Lodges may only manage one candidate at a time conveniently.
In some Lodges, at the words 'throat cut across,' a member or conductor draws their hand across the candidate's throat, or sometimes draws a sword.
The Master, assisted by the Senior Warden, lays the chief corner-stone of a fabric.
Lodge labors are conducted to produce an exciting spirit of emulation.
The Worshipful Master personally requests each individual to give an opinion on a specific doctrine or ceremony from the Chair.
A ritualistic element where a conductor or brother draws a cold sword across the candidate's naked belly, causing a shocking effect.
Description of Master's Lodge jewels: suspended from blue velvet collars, bordered and embroidered with silver, with a blue rosette and silver five-pointed star.
Description of Master's Lodge apron: white, lined and bordered with blue, with an eye on the flap and selections from the Master's carpet on the area.
Description of Master's Lodge scarf: blue silk, trimmed with silver, with a blue rosette at the shoulder and hip, worn from left to right.

Specific jewels for officers: Worshipful Master (square), Past Master (compass opened on a quarter-circle), Senior Warden (level), Junior Warden (plumb), Secretary (cross pens), Treasurer (cross keys), Senior Deacon (square and compass with sun), Junior Deacon (square and compass with quarter moon), Stewards (cornucopia), Masters of Ceremonies (cross swords), Tyler (sabre).

Evolution of 'fatal weapons' in the Third Degree: from 'setting-tool, setting maul, and setting-beetle' (1717) to 'twenty-four-inch gauge, square, and gavel', then 'setting-tool, square, and rule', and currently 'plumb-rule, square, and strong or heavy maul'.
The Hebrew covenant ceremony involved cutting a beast in twain and passing between the parts thereof.
The throat of the animal was cut across with one single blow, dividing the windpipe, arteries, and veins without touching any bone.
The breast was torn open and the heart plucked out; any imperfection rendered the body unclean.
The animal was divided into two parts, placed north and south, for parties to the covenant to pass between them from east to west.
The carcass was then left as a prey to voracious animals.
Hiram Abiff's supposed practice of retiring to the Most Holy Place at high twelve to draw plans and offer orisons.
Hiram Abiff's alleged division of operatives into Apprentices, Fellow Crafts, and Masters.
Payment of Apprentices' wages at the pillar of Jachin.
Payment of Masters' wages in the Middle Chamber.
The High Priest's unique privilege of entering the Holy of Holies only once a year, with a string or belt around his waist.

The ritual description of "the three principal entrances to the Temple" has undergone historical alterations, from "east, west and south doors" to "north, south, and east entrances" by the Union in 1813.

Variations in the sequence of attacks/entrances exist across jurisdictions: United States (south, west, east), Scotland (east, south, west), and France (originally south, north, east; now west, south, east).
Adonhiramite Masonry, used in France around 1787, did not mention the doors in its ritual.

The Master Mason lecture is structured into three sections: the first illustrates the ancient and proper mode of raising a candidate, the second introduces historical traditions and exemplifies Masonic virtue, and the third explains emblems and describes Solomon's Temple construction.

The closing of a Mason's Lodge involves a report, inspection of avenues by the meridian officer, rehearsal of duties for leaders of craftsmen, the Senior Warden ensuring instruction and improvement, an impressive address on duties, and a fervent petition to the Deity.
A Grand Lecturer is elected annually by the Grand Lodge to teach the Ritual of Freemasonry to subordinate Lodges, typically compensated by salary or per lecture.
Singing the 'Mark Master's Song' to the tune of 'America'.

The Junior Deacon attends the alarm and returns with a letter for the Right Worshipful Master.
The letter requests a loan of twenty-five dollars, offering the sender's mark as security.
The pass-grip of a Mark Master Mason is associated with the password JOPPA, referencing the steep ascent from Joppa while conveying timber from Lebanon.

The real grip of a Mark Master Mason is named SIROC or MARK WELL, formed by locking the little fingers of the right hands, turning their backs together, and placing the ends of the thumbs against each other.
The Right Worshipful Master engages the candidate in a dialogue to transition from the pass-grip to the true grip.
The Right Worshipful Master admonishes the candidate never to reveal the words of the grip in any way other than how they were received.

The Overseers report rejecting a stone because it was 'neither oblong nor square,' lacked a craft mark, and was deemed unfit, subsequently thrown among the rubbish.
The Right Worshipful Master orders an immediate search for the keystone, stating the Temple cannot be finished without it.

Ensure the Bible, Square, and Compasses are in proper position when the Lodge is open: both points of compasses beneath the square for EA degree; one point elevated above the square for FC degree; both points elevated above the square for Master's degree.
Use of the gavel: one blow to call the Lodge to order and seat members; two blows to call up subordinate officers; three blows to call up the whole Lodge.

Master's duties include opening, instructing, and closing Lodges; initiating, crafting, and raising Masons; presiding at consecrations, dedications, and installations; laying corner stones of public edifices; and presiding at funeral obsequies.
Seven distinct knocks are given at the Chapter door.

The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken only in the presence of three Royal Arch Masons (including the speaker), by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.

The Principal Sojourner reads the first six verses of the third chapter of Exodus.
A representation of the burning bush is placed in a corner of the Chapter.
Candidates are directed to halt in front of the burning bush representation.
Bandages (blindfolds) are removed from the candidates' eyes by the Principal Sojourner.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The ode likely conveys the duties, responsibilities, and symbolic importance of the Senior Warden's office within the lodge.

The Lodge is symbolically prepared in a room adjacent to a legally constituted Lodge, representing the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple. The Junior Warden's station in the south symbolizes the sun at high meridian, representing the beauty and glory of the day, and his role in observing time and managing periods of labor and refreshment. The Worshipful Master's station in the east symbolizes the rising sun, indicating his role in opening the Lodge. Harmony is presented as the strength of all institutions, especially Freemasonry.

The Junior Warden's station in the south symbolizes the sun at high meridian, representing the beauty and glory of the day, and his duty to regulate the balance between labor and refreshment. The Worshipful Master's station in the east symbolizes the rising sun, signifying his role in opening and governing the Lodge, setting the craft to work, and providing instruction. The signs of each degree (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason) allude to the penalty of their respective obligations, emphasizing the solemnity and consequences of their vows.

The physical preparation, including partial nudity, hoodwink, slipper, and cable-tow, symbolizes the candidate's vulnerable state, humility, and journey from darkness (ignorance) to light (knowledge). The hoodwink specifically represents the darkness from which the candidate seeks enlightenment. The cable-tow signifies the candidate's bond and obligation to the Craft. The presentation of the point of the compasses to the naked left breast serves as a powerful symbol and a physical reminder of the severe consequences and internal 'torture' to one's conscience should Masonic secrets be unlawfully revealed. The invocation of the Deity and the candidate's declaration of trust in God underscore the spiritual foundation of Freemasonry and the necessity of faith in a Supreme Being.

The act of kneeling for prayer signifies humility and a plea for divine guidance and aid in the candidate's journey into Masonry. The declaration of trust in God establishes a foundational principle of faith and reliance on a higher power. The perambulation around the Lodge room symbolizes the candidate's initial journey and introduction to the sacred space and its officers, while the accompanying passage emphasizes the core Masonic value of unity and brotherly love. The gavel raps serve as a ritualistic acknowledgment of the candidate's passage and the officers' presence.

The Mosaic pavement is accounted pure and immaculate, symbolizing that no pollution can be tolerated on that sacred floor. The word 'Hail' (from Saxon 'Hael') is used as a salutation, inquiring 'Whence do you hail?' The word 'Hale' (from Saxon 'helan') signifies to conceal, referring to the 'tye.' Light is the first demand of a candidate at initiation, with material light succeeded by intellectual illumination. The palate and throat are considered the chief seat of irregular appetites, and the first sign instructs Masons to avoid temptation through proper restraint on passions, promoting temperance, decency, and decorum.

The text notes that an unspecified element denotes 'worldly possession.' It also connects Tubal Cain as an instructor of artificers in brass and iron, relating him to Vulcan, and Jubal as the inventor of music, relating him to Apollo. The sprig of acacia is mentioned as a mark left by either the murderers or recanters in the Hiramic legend.

The Hebrew covenant ceremony, involving the division of an animal, symbolized a solemn binding to a promise, with the implied curse: "Thus let it be done to him, and thus let his body be cut in two, who shall break this covenant." The acacia, used in Jewish law to mark graves for priests to avoid ritual impurity, is symbolically significant in Freemasonry. The Hiramic legend, including the exhumation of the body, is presented as an allegory rather than a literal historical fact, serving to inculcate the doctrine of resurrection and a future state. The Master Mason represents a man saved from iniquity and raised to faith in salvation, embodying the doctrine of love.

The Master Mason represents a man under the doctrine of love, saved from the grave of iniquity, and raised to the faith of salvation. The degree testifies faith in the resurrection of the body and inculcates a practical lesson of prudence and unshrinking fidelity, inspiring hope for a final reward. The legend serves to impress upon the candidate's mind the doctrine of a resurrection and a future state.

The Third Degree is interpreted as an allegory for the Christian dispensation, symbolizing the revelation of salvation, atonement for sin, and the resurrection from the dead, confirmed by Christ's resurrection. The degeneration of Jewish law and the burial of piety and reverence are symbolized by "filth and rubbish" from the Temple. The inability of ancient wisdom to restore religion is represented by those who sought to raise her in vain. The sprig of acacia waving over religion's monument symbolizes hope and immortality, anticipating the Saviour's arrival. Hiram Abiff's death is a symbol for the Messiah's death. The three blows given to Hiram at the three gates of the Temple allude to the three points of condemnation against Christ before Caiaphas, Herod, and Pilate. The three tools (gauge, square, gavel) used for the blows symbolize the blow on Christ's cheek, the flagellation, and the spear (or crown of thorns). Brethren assembled around Hiram's grave represent disciples lamenting Christ's death. The "lost Word" symbolizes the word pronounced on the cross, which the Jews could not comprehend. False brethren are represented by Judas. The sprig of cassia (acacia) represents the cross, said to be made of its wood.

The ritual symbolizes the importance of honest labor and the proper acquisition of wages, emphasizing that one must be duly qualified and recognized to receive the rewards of their craft. The 'impostor' scenario highlights the consequences of attempting to claim benefits or status without legitimate entitlement or skill, representing a test of integrity and knowledge. The threat to the right hand signifies the potential loss of one's ability to work and contribute, a severe penalty for deception in the craft. The intercession and subsequent instruction symbolize the mercy, guidance, and educational role of the fraternity in correcting errors and teaching proper conduct and skill. The keystone mentioned in the song represents a crucial element of the work and a symbol of recognition and fame for those who truly understand its significance.

The reprimand symbolizes the importance of diligence, skill, and integrity in one's work and the consequences of attempting to deceive or impose upon the Craft. The chisel and mallet, used by operative masons to shape stone, are symbolically employed by Free and Accepted Masons to 'hew, cut, carve, and indent the mind,' signifying the moral and intellectual refinement of the candidate. The reception upon the chisel and mallet represents the enduring impression of the Mark Master's lessons. The scripture passages about 'the stone which the builders refused' becoming 'the head stone of the corner' allude to the value of that which is initially rejected or overlooked, and its ultimate importance, a central theme of the Mark Master degree.

The four upright, regular steps forming a square symbolize precision, rectitude, and the candidate's commitment to moral and Masonic principles in their approach to sacred duties. The repeated questioning at various posts signifies the importance of consistency, truthfulness, and the candidate's unwavering affirmation of their commitment. The 'mark' itself is a profound symbol of a personal pledge, a bond of trust, and a form of security or credit within the fraternity, representing a brother's honor and commitment. Its specified value (half a shekel of silver or quarter of a dollar) gives it a tangible, albeit symbolic, economic weight. The severe penalties of having the right ear smitten off and the right hand chopped off symbolize the complete forfeiture of the ability to hear the sacred word and to perform Masonic work, representing the ultimate consequence for an impostor or oath-breaker.

The keystone, initially rejected by the builders for its unconventional shape and lack of a recognizable mark, ultimately becomes the 'head stone of the corner,' symbolizing that true value and importance may be overlooked or misunderstood at first. The scriptural passages reinforce this theme of vindication for the rejected. The 'new name' and the 'mark' on the keystone, along with the reference to the 'white stone' from Revelation, symbolize a unique identity, recognition, and a deeper, personal understanding or achievement within the Mark Master degree, signifying a special reward for those who overcome.

The apparent confusion and the candidate's embarrassment during the mock lodge proceedings are designed to convey a striking lesson about the importance of not soliciting or accepting any office or station for which one is not amply qualified.

The duegard sign alludes to the penalty of the obligation, signifying a preference for the tongue to cleave to the roof of the mouth rather than unlawfully divulging secrets. 'The sign' alludes to an additional penalty, indicating a willingness to suffer former penalties rather than unlawfully divulging secrets. The word 'Stone Squarer' signifies the role. The jewel of office, a square, symbolizes the hope that the recipient would prepare a 'square stone in the Temple of Masonry.' The Holy Bible within the three great lights is presented as necessary for counsel and guidance. The varying positions of the compasses relative to the square symbolize the different degrees: both points beneath the square for EA, one point elevated for FC, and both points elevated for Master Mason.

The Bible, Square, and Compasses are presented as the three great lights, providing counsel and guidance. The gavel is described as an emblem of power. The Master's chair is symbolically linked to Grand Master Solomon, with the hope that a portion of his wisdom will rest upon the Master.

The 'Living Arch' symbolizes the difficult and humbling path to exaltation, requiring perseverance and submission. The physical prostration and kneading represent the trials and purification necessary for advancement. Kneeling for the seventh time at the altar signifies the increasing solemnity and binding nature of the obligations as one progresses through Masonic degrees. The phrase 'he that humbleth himself shall be exalted' directly links the physical act of stooping low under the arch to the spiritual lesson of humility leading to higher understanding. The journey through darkness and crooked paths made straight ('I will bring the blind by a way they know not...') suggests divine guidance and enlightenment provided through the Masonic path.

The 'rough and rugged ways' and 'many trials' symbolize the challenges and tests of fidelity a candidate must undergo to prove their worthiness for the deeper secrets of the Royal Arch Degree. The 'stupendous arch of heaven' mentioned in the prayer symbolizes the divine architectural power of the Supreme Architect of universal Nature.

The prayer symbolizes a deep adoration for divine perfection, gratitude for the gifts of reason and improvement, and a commitment to virtue and holiness. The 'second temple of reformation' represents the spiritual rebuilding of the individual's character after the loss of original purity. The burning bush, where God appeared to Moses, symbolizes divine presence, revelation, and the sacred nature of the encounter, signifying a moment of profound spiritual awakening and instruction for the candidate.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate learns that their obligation is what makes them an Entered Apprentice Mason. They are taught the importance of temperance and avoiding excess during periods of refreshment. The concept of harmony as the strength of the institution is also emphasized.

Candidates are taught the importance of temperance and moderation during refreshment, the value of harmony and satisfaction among brethren, and the necessity of proper conduct within the Lodge. The performance of the duegards and signs, which allude to the penalties of the obligations, serves as a constant reminder of the seriousness of their vows and the duties they have undertaken.

The candidate is taught the importance of sincere and honorable motivations for seeking admission, including a desire for knowledge and a commitment to serving fellow creatures. The ritualistic reception, particularly the point of the compasses, impresses upon the candidate the solemnity of the obligation and the grave consequences of violating Masonic trust and secrecy. The prayer and the declaration of trust in God instill the fundamental lesson of reliance on a divine power and the moral principles of brotherly love, relief, and truth that are central to the Masonic way of life.

The candidate learns the importance of seeking divine aid and dedicating their life to service. They are taught that their faith in God is a well-founded trust. The perambulation and accompanying passage instill the lesson of unity and brotherly love as essential tenets of Freemasonry, encouraging them to become a true and faithful brother.

Admission into the fraternity is based on a sincere belief and trust in God, and where God's name is invoked, no danger can ensue. It is unwise to assert more than can be proven or argue against probability regarding historical claims. The story of Hipparchus serves as a severe caution against violating engagements, emphasizing the importance of truth and fidelity, and the dire consequences of their breach. Candidates are taught that the pursuit of 'Light' extends beyond the material to intellectual understanding. The first sign teaches the importance of temperance in all indulgences, self-restraint, and never exceeding the boundary of decency and decorum, under penalty of violating Masonic engagements.

To be a 'bright Mason,' one must have knowledge of the alterations made in Masonry since Morgan's exposure. The critical notes on the Third Degree's legendary nature (hyperbolical, apocryphal, fabulous) encourage a deeper, allegorical understanding rather than a literal interpretation of its traditions and history. The mention of 'worldly possession' as a symbolic denotation may serve as a lesson against materialism.

The self-imposed penalties within Freemasonry may be understood in the context of ancient Hebrew covenant practices, emphasizing the profound solemnity and consequences of breaking sacred promises. The Master Mason degree teaches the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, encouraging candidates to contemplate mortality, strive for ethical maturity, and embrace faith in salvation. The narrative of the Temple builders highlights the importance of fidelity and the severe repercussions of treachery.

The Master Mason degree teaches the doctrine of a resurrection and a future state, emphasizing prudence and unshrinking fidelity. It inspires hope for a final reward for those who are 'just made perfect' and symbolizes being saved from iniquity and raised to the faith of salvation.

Candidates are taught the ancient and proper mode of being raised to the Master Mason Degree, understanding its historical traditions and the exemplification of Masonic virtue. They learn the meaning of the degree's emblems and the description of Solomon's Temple. The solemnity of lodge closing reinforces the "chief care of a Mason" and the importance of continuous Masonic instruction and improvement. Masons are reminded of their duties as men and Masons in their daily avocations and the importance of piety. The allegorical interpretation of the Hiramic legend teaches profound lessons about spiritual death and resurrection, the restoration of truth and morality, and fidelity in the face of adversity, drawing parallels to Christian themes of salvation and immortality. The distinction between literal history and allegorical truth is emphasized, guiding the candidate to seek deeper symbolic meaning.

The candidate learns the critical importance of integrity, honesty, and proper qualification in all endeavors, especially concerning work and reward. It teaches that one must earn their wages through approved work and legitimate means, and that attempting to deceive or claim what is not due will lead to severe consequences. The lesson also emphasizes the value of humility, acknowledging one's inexperience, and being open to instruction from more experienced brethren to improve one's craft and conduct.

The candidate is taught the critical importance of performing work with skill and industry, and the ethical principle of not claiming rewards or wages that are not rightfully earned. The reprimand serves as a 'striking lesson' against imposition upon the Craft. The symbolic use of the chisel and mallet emphasizes the continuous process of self-improvement and the shaping of one's character and mind. The scripture readings impart a lesson on the potential for the rejected to become essential, encouraging perseverance and recognizing inherent worth.

The candidate learns the paramount importance of strict secrecy regarding the Mark Master Mason's secrets, disclosing them only to duly qualified brethren after rigorous examination. They are taught the duty to uphold the Constitutions of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter and State Grand Royal Arch Chapter, as well as the by-laws of any Mark Master Lodge. The obligation instills obedience to all regular signs and summonses from fellow Mark Master Masons or Lodges, within the bounds of their cable-tow. A strong ethical lesson is imparted regarding not wronging a brother or the Lodge, even to the smallest value, and preventing such wrongs if possible. The unique rules surrounding the 'mark' teach about personal integrity, the sanctity of a pledge, and the responsibility to honor commitments related to one's mark, including its redemption. The lesson of mutual aid is emphasized through the promise to receive a brother's mark when a favor is requested and to grant it if possible, or to return its specified value if the favor cannot be granted.

The candidate is taught the importance of discerning true worth and recognizing value in things that may initially appear unconventional or rejected. The narrative of the keystone emphasizes that what is deemed unfit by some may be essential and invaluable. The investment with a 'new name' signifies a personal transformation and a unique identity within the Craft, highlighting individual merit and the reward for perseverance. The admonition regarding the grips instills the importance of fidelity and the proper transmission of Masonic knowledge.

The primary lesson for the candidate is the necessity of at all times abstaining from soliciting or accepting any office or station that one does not know oneself amply qualified to fill.

The candidate learns the solemn obligation to keep and conceal the secrets belonging to the chair, understanding the severe penalties for unlawful disclosure. They are taught the importance of the Holy Bible for counsel and guidance and are encouraged to prepare a 'square stone in the Temple of Masonry.' The distinction between 'Actual' and 'Virtual' Past Masters teaches the specific rights and limitations associated with each status, particularly regarding privileges within a symbolic Lodge.

The Master is entrusted with significant authority and a wide array of responsibilities, including upholding the foundational documents of the Lodge, maintaining accurate records, ensuring proper ritualistic display of the Great Lights, and presiding over all essential Masonic work and ceremonies. The role demands wisdom, diligence, and adherence to Masonic principles, echoing the wisdom of Grand Master Solomon.

Candidates learn the importance of humility and perseverance in their Masonic journey, symbolized by the 'Living Arch.' They are taught the paramount importance of secrecy regarding the Royal Arch degree's secrets, including its ineffable characters and the Grand Omnific Word. The obligation reinforces loyalty to the Chapter and fellow companions, prohibiting wronging them or speaking ill of them, and requiring warning of danger. Candidates are instructed on the strict procedural requirements for the Royal Arch degree, including prerequisites, the number of candidates for exaltation, and the quorum for opening a Chapter. The repeated obligations emphasize the cumulative and increasingly binding nature of Masonic commitments, always subordinate to duties to country and Maker.

The candidate is taught the profound importance of fidelity, secrecy, and mutual support within the Royal Arch. They learn the strict procedural requirements for the degree and the extensive moral and fraternal duties expected of a Royal Arch Mason, including charity, assistance, and adherence to Masonic law. The journey through 'rough and rugged ways' emphasizes that true understanding and higher secrets are earned through perseverance and proven commitment.

Candidates are taught the importance of gratitude, humility, and adherence to new and stronger obligations of virtue and holiness. They are encouraged to seek divine wisdom in the 'book of nature' and to allow the solemnity of the ceremonies to profoundly impact their lives. The narrative of Moses and the burning bush instructs them in reverence for God's 'great, mighty, and terrible Name,' inspiring devotion, love, and charity, and emphasizing the continuous process of personal reformation and spiritual rebuilding.
tradition_notes: Installation odes are a traditional component of officer installation ceremonies in Freemasonry.
The number of raps given by the Worshipful Master varies by degree: one for Entered Apprentice, two for Fellow Craft, and three for Master Mason.
The Lodge is symbolically represented as the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple.
The traditional response to 'Are you an Entered Apprentice Mason?' is 'I am so taken and accepted among all brothers and fellows.'
The stations of the officers in the Lodge (East, West, South) are symbolically aligned with the course of the sun.
The Master always leads in giving the sign or signs during the opening ceremony.
The duegard represents the position of the hands when taking the oath of a degree.
The text notes that the Worshipful Master sometimes addresses the candidate as 'Stranger!' instead of by name during the declaration of trust in God.
The Junior Deacon 'always responds for the candidate' during the initial questioning at the door.
The phrase 'as all brothers and fellows have done before' emphasizes the continuity of Masonic tradition.
Masters sometimes use 'Stranger!' instead of the candidate's name when asking about trust.
The passage 'Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!' is noted as being from 'opening, or Monitor's.'

The reverence indicated by putting off the covering of the feet is still prevalent in the East among Christians, Moslems, or pagans, where it is considered highly irreverent to enter a sacred place with feet covered.
Masonic traditions do not support the belief that figures like Adam, Nimrod, Moses, Joshua, David, Solomon, Hiram, St. John the Evangelist, or St. John the Baptist belonged to the Masonic Order.
The 'work' and lectures taught by Barney are prevalent in the Northwestern States (Michigan, Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, Iowa) and were adopted by the Baltimore Convention.
Brother John Barney obtained the Preston Lectures from Gleason in Boston, which were approved by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts in 1817.
Barney adapted his lectures to different systems prevailing in the Western and Southwestern States, gaining sanction from respective Grand Masters.
The 'Webb Work,' inculcated by Robert Morris, is generally accepted as being of ancient origin and is expected to be adopted by Grand Lodges throughout the United States.

The 'fatal weapons' in the Third Degree have undergone several changes: from 'setting-tool, setting maul, and setting-beetle' (1717) to 'twenty-four-inch gauge, square, and gavel', then 'setting-tool, square, and rule', and currently 'plumb-rule, square, and strong or heavy maul'.
There are variations in the Hiramic legend regarding the number of Fellow Crafts who went in search (fifteen vs. twelve) and whether the sprig of acacia was left by the murderers or recanters.
There are variations in the reported location where the lost body was found: near the seaside (York Masons), Mount Moriah (Americans), Monet Lebanon (French), or near Jerusalem (others).
Accounts differ regarding the brethren's dispersal during the search: some say they dispersed widely (east, west, and south), while others state they kept within hail of each other.

Alternative names for the ruffians are preserved in subsidiary degrees, including GRAVELOT, QUIBO, and AKIROP, and in the Degree of Elect of Fifteen, they are named JUBELA-KURMAVIL (a corruption of Cromwell), JUBELO-GRAVELOT, and JUBELUM-AKIROP.
There are differing traditions on who advised the change to the substitute word: some say King Solomon, while others affirm the three Fellow Crafts adopted it without consulting him.
The Hiramic legend is described as a 'fabulous narrative' used to promote the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, similar to 'spurious initiations'.
Traditions mention instances of treachery, violation of sacred pledges, and crime among Apprentices and Craftsmen during the Temple's construction.
The connection of the 'widow's son' (Hiram Abiff) with the building of the Temple was endeared to the two monarchs, leading to a new arrangement of discipline and a legend to preserve his memory.
It was customary for Hiram Abiff to retire to the Most Holy Place at high twelve to draw plans and offer orisons.
Some rituals taught that Hiram Abiff divided operatives into Apprentices, Fellow Crafts, and Masters, paying them at the pillar of Jachin and in the Middle Chamber.
Traditions state that the Ark of the Covenant had not been removed to the Holy of Holies at the time Hiram supposedly entered it, as this occurred at the dedication.
The description of "the three principal entrances to the Temple" has evolved over time, from "east, west and south doors" to "north, south, and east entrances" by the Union in 1813.
Different Masonic jurisdictions (United States, Scotland, France) have varying orders for the "attacks" or "entrances" in the ritual.
Adonhiramite Masonry, used in France around 1787, did not mention the doors at all.

The Christian allegorical interpretation of the Hiramic legend is explicitly stated to be "borne out in the higher Degrees of sublime Masonry," specifically the 32nd Degree (Prince of the Royal Secret) in Continental nomenclature.

The historical accuracy of Hiram Abiff's death during the Temple's construction is challenged by scriptural references (1 Kings vii. 40, 2 Chron. iv. 11) which indicate he was alive at the Temple's completion.
The severe reprimand ordered to be given to the candidate is omitted in most Lodges at the present day, but is inserted in this text for the satisfaction of young Masons and the curious.
The severe reprimand ordered to be given to the candidate is omitted in most Lodges at the present day.
The Lodge is erected to Almighty God and dedicated to Hiram the Builder.
The obligation is stated to be 'in addition to my former obligations,' indicating a progressive system of degrees.

The obligation explicitly mentions supporting the 'Constitution of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America' and the 'Grand Royal Arch Chapter of this State,' placing the Mark Master degree within the American Royal Arch system.
The 'mark' chosen by a brother must be recorded in a 'book of marks.'
The value of a brother's mark, when a favor cannot be granted, is specified as 'half a shekel of silver, or quarter of a dollar,' linking ancient and modern monetary equivalents.
Some Lodges do not require all brethren to rise after the Right Worshipful Master gives four raps, choosing instead to keep their seats.
The ceremonies for conferring the Past Master Degree are frequently shortened by omitting parts, such as presenting implements and their explanations, and often the charge.
Historically, Royal Arch Masonry was governed by symbolic Lodges, and a regulation required candidates to have presided as Master of a Lodge to receive the Royal Arch Degree.

To extend the Royal Arch Degree, Grand Masters often granted dispensations to Master Masons to 'pass the chair,' a brief ceremony investing them with Past Master mysteries, allowing them to advance to Royal Arch.
When control of the Royal Arch shifted to Chapters, the regulation continued to be observed.
The method of initiation for the Past Master Degree has become much abridged, often involving a ruse within a Mark Masters Lodge.
The candidate is usually relieved from embarrassment in good season by the retired Master, rather than being treated as badly as some representations suggest.
The law historically required the Royal Arch Degree to be restricted to Past Masters, leading to the practice of candidates passing the chair as a preparation.

A distinction is made between 'Actual Past Masters' (those elected and installed to preside over a symbolic Lodge) and 'Virtual Past Masters' (those who pass through the ceremony in a Chapter as a prerequisite for the Royal Arch).
The tradition of the Master being covered while the brethren remain uncovered.
The specific traditional positions of the Bible, Square, and Compasses for each of the three degrees (EA, FC, MM).
The traditional use of the gavel with specific numbers of blows for different commands in the Lodge.
The traditional association of the Master's chair with Grand Master Solomon.
The Royal Arch degree is referred to as the 'august sublime Degree of the Holy Royal Arch'.
Candidates must have received the degrees of Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master as prerequisites.
The Chapter is dedicated to Zerubbabel.
The obligation taken is the seventh in the candidate's Masonic journey.
The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken by three companions, by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.
Exaltations must involve exactly three candidates at a time.
A minimum of nine Royal Arch Masons are required to form or open a Chapter.
The Royal Arch secrets are considered 'infinitely more importance than any that precede it'.
The obligation is taken 'under a Living Arch, and at low breath'.
The General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America and State Grand Chapters are recognized authorities.
The concept of 'cable-tow' is applied to the Royal Arch degree for answering summonses.
The reading from Exodus chapter 3, verses 1-6, is a specific part of the ritual, timed to coincide with the candidates' arrival at the burning bush representation.
The concept of rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' is a significant allegorical theme within this degree, contrasting with a 'first temple of purity and innocence' lost through sin.
caution_notes: The Tyler's duty is to keep off all cowans and eavesdroppers.
The Junior Warden must ensure that the means of refreshment are not converted into intemperance or excess.
Means of refreshment should not be converted into intemperance or excess.
Private committees and improper, unmasonic conduct are strictly forbidden during Lodge business, under penalty determined by a majority of brethren acting under by-laws.
Duegards and signs must be made with right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, with very slight, but marked pauses between each motion or part of the sign.
When making the duegard and sign of the Fellow Craft, the left arm must be dropped suddenly and with spirit as soon as the two motions are accomplished.

The point of a sharp instrument pressing the naked left breast is to teach the candidate that 'as it is a torture to your flesh, so should the recollection of it ever be to your mind and conscience, should you attempt to reveal the secrets of Masonry unlawfully.'
It is unwise to assert more than can be proven and to argue against probability regarding historical figures being Freemasons.

The story of Hipparchus illustrates the terrible consequences of violating engagements, leading to expulsion, social death, and denial of burial rites, serving as a warning against breaches of truth and fidelity.
Disobedience or violation of Masonic engagements, particularly regarding temperance and decorum, carries a penalty.

The 'work' in Minnesota and New York is 'strongly impregnated with what is called, among Masons, "Morganry"--very similar to the disclosures of Morgan and Richardson,' implying a caution regarding unauthorized disclosures.
The Third Degree's traditions are 'unfortunately hyperbolical; its history apocryphal, and its legends fabulous,' cautioning against literal interpretations.
Professor Stuart endeavored to show that the legend of the Third Degree is an 'imposture' due to the Latin origin of the criminals' names, not Hebrew.
The text notes that the uniformity of Freemasonry on certain subjects is a 'delicate point' and difficult to clarify for brethren, implying a need for discretion regarding ritual variations.
CASSIA is sometimes improperly and ignorantly used for acacia.
The interpolated word (substitute for the lost word) is not a real word in any language, but merely a jumble of letters forming a sound without meaning.

Masons should not literally believe Dr. Anderson's explanation of the exhumation of H.A.B. as a real historical fact, as Hiram Abiff was known to be alive years after the Temple's completion, indicating the story is allegorical.

The historical veracity of the Hiramic legend is questioned, with references to Dr. Anderson and Josephus stating Hiram Abiff lived to complete the Temple and died in Tyre, contradicting the ritual narrative.

Inconsistencies in ritual narratives are highlighted, such as Hiram Abiff's supposed entry into the Holy of Holies before its completion or accessibility, and the division of operatives and payment of wages before the relevant architectural features (pillars, Middle Chamber) were built.
A divergence from uniformity is noted in describing the places where the instruments of Hiram's murder were supposedly used.

The author explicitly states that the tradition of Hiram's death during the Temple's construction is "merely allegorical" and not historically factual, citing scriptural evidence (1 Kings vii. 40, 2 Chron. iv. 11) that Hiram was alive at the Temple's completion. This serves as a caution against a literal interpretation of the legend.
Caution against attempting to receive wages without being able to give the token.
Warning that such conduct requires severe punishment, with the penalty being to have the right hand struck off.
The ritual includes a dramatic threat of striking off the hand and even life, emphasizing the seriousness of the offense.

I will not give the secrets of a Mark Master Mason to any one of an inferior degree, nor to any other person in the known world, except it be a true and lawful brother, or brethren, of this degree; and not unto him nor unto them whom I shall hear so to be, but unto him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Lodge, or a brother of this Degree, to the value of his wages (or one penny), myself, knowingly, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.

I will not sell, swap, barter, or exchange my mark, which I shall hereafter choose, after it has been recorded in the book of marks, for any other one, unless it be a dead mark, or one of an older date, nor will I pledge it a second time until it is lawfully redeemed from the first pledge.

Binding myself under no less penalty than to have my right ear smitten off, that I may forever be unable to hear the word, and my right hand chopped off, as the penalty of an impostor, if I should ever prove wilfully guilty of violating any part of this my solemn oath, or obligation, of a Mark Master Mason.
The candidate is admonished never to give the words of the grip in any way but that in which he received them.
The lesson explicitly cautions against accepting any office or station without being amply qualified to fill it.
The ceremony of passing the chair in a Chapter (making one a 'Virtual Past Master') does not confer any official rank outside of the Chapter, nor can it claim peculiar privileges in a symbolic Lodge.
The penalties of the obligation emphasize the severe consequences of unlawfully divulging any secrets belonging to the chair.
The Master must ensure the constitution is not infringed.
The Master must ensure the by-laws are strictly enforced.
The Master must ensure records are carefully kept so that nothing improper is transmitted to paper.

I will not reveal the secrets of this Degree to any of an inferior Degree, nor to any being in the known world, except it be to a true and lawful Companion Royal Arch Mason, or within the body of a just and legally constituted Chapter of such and never unto him, or them, whom I shall hear so to be, but to him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Chapter of Royal Arch Masons, or a companion of this Degree, out of the value of any thing, myself, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.
I will not reveal the key to the ineffable characters of this Degree, nor retain it in my possession, but will destroy it whenever it comes to my sight.

I will not speak the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word, which I shall hereafter receive, in any manner, except in that in which I shall receive it, which will be in the presence of three Companions Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of the number; and then by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.

I will not be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter, nor converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or with one who has been expelled or suspended, while under that sentence.

I will not assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate to this Degree, who has not received the Degrees of: Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master.
I will not be at the exaltation of more nor less than three candidates at one and the same time.
I will not be at the forming or opening of a Chapter of Royal Arch Masons unless there be present nine Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of that number.
I will not speak evil of a Companion Royal Arch Mason, behind his back nor before his face, but will apprise him of all approaching danger, if in my power.
Penalty for violating the obligation: 'skull smote off, and my brains exposed to the scorching rays of the meridian sun'.
Not to be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter.
Not to converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or one who has been expelled or suspended.
Not to assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate who has not received the prerequisite degrees.
Not to strike a Companion Royal Arch Mason so as to draw his blood, in anger.
