entries.title: Chaplain
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the sacred duties and responsibilities of the Chaplain within a Masonic Lodge, emphasizing their role in leading devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master. It clarifies that Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be seen as a substitute for the Church, yet its tenets are founded on the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, recognizing the need for Divine guidance.
entries.full_summary:
This section outlines the sacred duties and responsibilities of the Chaplain within a Masonic Lodge, emphasizing their role in leading devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master. It clarifies that Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be seen as a substitute for the Church, yet its tenets are founded on the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, recognizing the need for Divine guidance.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0025 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: First Section.

This section presents a catechism for an Entered Apprentice Mason, covering the candidate's origin, purpose in Masonry (to subdue passions and improve oneself), and methods of recognition. It details how a Mason is known by signs, tokens, a word, and the perfect points of entrance, including specific descriptions of signs as right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, and tokens as friendly grips. The symbolic meaning of the common gavel is explained as a tool for divesting the mind of vices to become a 'living stone' for a 'spiritual building'. The structure of the degree's lecture is outlined, consisting of three sections: the manner of initiation, the reasons for it, and the form, furniture, and lights of the Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0025 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Degree: Historical Notes

This section provides historical notes and explanations for various elements of the Entered Apprentice Degree. It discusses the reverence associated with removing footwear in the East, linking it to the purity of the Mosaic pavement. It emphasizes that a candidate's admission is solely based on their avowal of belief and trust in God. Practical details are given regarding the number of candidates that can be initiated, passed, or raised in each degree. The text debunks claims that biblical figures like Adam, Moses, or Solomon were Freemasons. It clarifies the dual meaning of the word 'Hail' or 'Hale' in Masonic context. Specific ritual actions, such as drawing a hand or sword across the candidate's throat, are mentioned. The story of Hipparchus, a Pythagorean, is recounted as a cautionary tale about the severe consequences of violating societal engagements. The concept of 'Light' in initiation is explained as progressing from material to intellectual illumination. Finally, the symbolic meaning of the first sign, related to the palate and throat, is detailed as a lesson in temperance and restraint of passions, and the role of the Master and Senior Warden in laying the corner-stone is noted, along with the practice of fostering emulation and discussion in Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->
practical_elements: The Chaplain's duty is to lead the Lodge in its devotions.
The Chaplain leads devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master.
An appropriate musical selection may be sung during the installation.
An address may be made after the musical selection.
A charge is rehearsed by the Installing Officer to the Chaplain.
Catechism format (Q&A)
Giving the sign of an Entered Apprentice
Giving the token of an Entered Apprentice
Verbal exchange: 'I hail.' 'I conceal.'
Recognition by signs, a token, a word, and the perfect points of entrance
Description of signs as right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars
Description of tokens as certain friendly or brotherly grips
Putting off the covering of the feet as a sign of reverence, paralleling Eastern customs.
Candidate avows belief and trust in God as the sole basis for admission; refusal leads to rejection.
Chief officer expresses satisfaction upon a candidate's declaration of trust in God.
If more than one candidate is initiated at once, they are required to take hold of each other's arms.
Up to five candidates can be initiated as Entered Apprentices at once.
Up to five candidates can be 'passed' to the Second Degree at once.
Only one candidate at a time can be 'raised' to the Master's Degree.
Small Lodges may only manage one candidate at a time conveniently.
In some Lodges, at the words 'throat cut across,' a member or conductor draws their hand across the candidate's throat, or sometimes draws a sword.
The Master, assisted by the Senior Warden, lays the chief corner-stone of a fabric.
Lodge labors are conducted to produce an exciting spirit of emulation.
The Worshipful Master personally requests each individual to give an opinion on a specific doctrine or ceremony from the Chair.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The Chaplain symbolizes the spiritual dimension within the Lodge, representing the recognition of a Supreme Being and the need for Divine guidance among its members. The 'sacred trust' highlights the profound responsibility of fostering a reverent atmosphere while maintaining the distinct non-religious nature of Freemasonry.

The common gavel, typically used by operative Masons to remove superfluous corners from rough stones, is symbolically used by Free and Accepted Masons to divest their minds and consciences of all vices and superfluities of life. This process prepares them as 'living stones' for a 'spiritual building, that house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens'. The sign of an Entered Apprentice alludes to the penalty of the obligation.

The Mosaic pavement is accounted pure and immaculate, symbolizing that no pollution can be tolerated on that sacred floor. The word 'Hail' (from Saxon 'Hael') is used as a salutation, inquiring 'Whence do you hail?' The word 'Hale' (from Saxon 'helan') signifies to conceal, referring to the 'tye.' Light is the first demand of a candidate at initiation, with material light succeeded by intellectual illumination. The palate and throat are considered the chief seat of irregular appetites, and the first sign instructs Masons to avoid temptation through proper restraint on passions, promoting temperance, decency, and decorum.
entries.candidate_lesson:
Candidates learn that while Freemasonry is not a religion, it is founded on moral and ethical principles derived from sacred texts like the Holy Bible, and it encourages a belief in a Supreme Being and the pursuit of spiritual and moral improvement. They are taught that the Lodge is not a substitute for their personal religious affiliations.

The primary lesson for the candidate is to learn to subdue their passions and improve themselves in Masonry. This is achieved by symbolically using the common gavel to cleanse the mind and conscience of vices, thereby becoming a suitable 'living stone' for a spiritual edifice.

Admission into the fraternity is based on a sincere belief and trust in God, and where God's name is invoked, no danger can ensue. It is unwise to assert more than can be proven or argue against probability regarding historical claims. The story of Hipparchus serves as a severe caution against violating engagements, emphasizing the importance of truth and fidelity, and the dire consequences of their breach. Candidates are taught that the pursuit of 'Light' extends beyond the material to intellectual understanding. The first sign teaches the importance of temperance in all indulgences, self-restraint, and never exceeding the boundary of decency and decorum, under penalty of violating Masonic engagements.
tradition_notes: The principle tenets of Freemasonry are based upon the teachings found in the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible.
Freemasons, as religious men, recognize their need for Divine guidance.
The origin of the Lodge is stated as 'From a Lodge of the Sts. John of Jerusalem', a traditional Masonic reference.

The reverence indicated by putting off the covering of the feet is still prevalent in the East among Christians, Moslems, or pagans, where it is considered highly irreverent to enter a sacred place with feet covered.
Masonic traditions do not support the belief that figures like Adam, Nimrod, Moses, Joshua, David, Solomon, Hiram, St. John the Evangelist, or St. John the Baptist belonged to the Masonic Order.
caution_notes: Freemasonry never has been, is not now, nor will it ever be, a religion.
Members should never accept the Lodge as a substitute for the Church.
It is unwise to assert more than can be proven and to argue against probability regarding historical figures being Freemasons.

The story of Hipparchus illustrates the terrible consequences of violating engagements, leading to expulsion, social death, and denial of burial rites, serving as a warning against breaches of truth and fidelity.
Disobedience or violation of Masonic engagements, particularly regarding temperance and decorum, carries a penalty.

entries.title: Worshipful Master
entries.short_summary: The Worshipful Master is charged with the superintendence and government of the Lodge's brethren, emphasizing the significant obligations and responsibilities inherent in their role as the head of the Lodge.
entries.full_summary:
The Worshipful Master is charged with the superintendence and government of the Lodge's brethren, emphasizing the significant obligations and responsibilities inherent in their role as the head of the Lodge.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0007 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Most Worshipful Master in the East

This section outlines the detailed procedures for brethren entering a Masonic Lodge, including the alarm system, the roles of the Junior Deacon and Tyler in admitting visitors, and the mandatory salutation of the Worshipful Master with the duegard and sign of the degree. It also describes the process for visitors to be vouched for or examined, particularly in cities with multiple lodges. Furthermore, it details the preparation for opening a Lodge, specifying the Master's attire and command, and the precise stations, attire, and tools of various officers within the Lodge.

This section details the ritualistic procedure for ensuring the Lodge is properly tyled before opening. It describes the interaction between the Worshipful Master, the Tyler, and the Junior Deacon, outlining the Tyler's station, duties, and the implement of his office (the sword), as well as the Junior Deacon's role in confirming the Lodge's security. The process ensures that only qualified Masons are present and that the Lodge is prepared for the specific degree (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, or Master Mason).
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0007 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0008 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Opening the Lodge: Officer Stations and Duties

This section details the ritualistic opening of a Masonic Lodge, focusing on the stations and duties of various officers, including the Tyler, Junior Deacon, Senior Warden, Senior Deacon, Secretary, Treasurer, Junior Warden, and Worshipful Master. It outlines the procedure for ensuring the Lodge is properly tyled, confirming the qualification of members, and the symbolic significance of officer placements and responsibilities, particularly for an Entered Apprentice Lodge.

This section details the stations and duties of the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Worshipful Master during the opening of a Masonic Lodge. It explains their symbolic positions relative to the sun and their responsibilities, such as calling the craft to refreshment, paying wages, and governing the Lodge. The text also outlines the procedure for opening the Lodge on the First, Second, or Third Degree, including the communication of orders and the performance of the duegards and signs for the Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, and Master Mason degrees, with specific instructions on their execution and symbolic allusions to their respective obligations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0008 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0013 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Form of Petition.

This section outlines the formal process for submitting a petition for membership into a Masonic lodge. It details the required information on the petition, the need for recommendations from existing Brothers, the accompanying fee, and the procedural steps taken by the Worshipful Master and Brethren, including a motion to receive petitions, a vote by the usual sign, and the appointment of a committee of investigation for each applicant.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0013 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0014 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Third Order of Business

This section details the 'Third Order of Business' in a Masonic lodge, focusing on the process of receiving and acting upon committee reports concerning candidate applications. It illustrates the roles of the Worshipful Master, Secretary, and committee chairmen in presenting findings on a candidate's character. The text also highlights potential discussions and delays that can arise if further inquiry into a candidate's background is deemed necessary, concluding with the motion and vote to proceed with balloting.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0014 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0015 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fourth Order of Business

This section outlines the Fourth Order of Business in a Masonic lodge, which focuses on balloting for candidates seeking admission to the First Degree. It details the roles of the Worshipful Master and Secretary in initiating the process, the opportunity for brethren to voice opinions on a candidate, and the Senior Deacon's duty to prepare and place the ballot-box on the altar for the vote.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0015 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0016 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Balloting and Petitions

This section details the precise procedure for balloting on a candidate's petition for the First Degree in Masonry. It outlines the sequence of voting, starting with the Master, followed by the Secretary calling names, and each Mason casting a white or black ball. The ballot box is then systematically examined by the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and finally the Worshipful Master, who declares the outcome of the election.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0016 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0017 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fifth Order of Business

The Fifth Order of Business outlines the procedure for handling candidates for degrees. The Worshipful Master directs the Junior Deacon to ascertain if there are candidates waiting and for which degree. Upon report, the Worshipful Master decides on conferring the degree, potentially deferring candidates to a special communication due to time constraints. The process includes informing candidates of the decision and a ballot for election to the First Degree.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0017 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0019 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Preparation of the Candidate

This section details the initial preparation and entry of a candidate for the First Degree in Masonry. It begins with an interrogation to confirm the candidate's motivations and willingness to conform to Masonic usages. Following assent, the candidate undergoes specific physical preparation, including partial disrobing and the application of a hoodwink, slipper, and cable-tow. The narrative then describes the ritualistic knocking at the Lodge door, the dialogue between the Deacons regarding the candidate's qualifications and desire for light, and the formal reception into the Lodge. This reception involves the symbolic presentation of the compasses to the candidate's naked breast, followed by a prayer led by the Worshipful Master and the candidate's declaration of trust in God.

This section details the final preparatory steps for an Entered Apprentice candidate. It includes the candidate kneeling for a prayer recited by the Worshipful Master, followed by the Master asking the candidate in whom they put their trust. Upon declaring trust in God, the candidate is assisted to rise and begins a perambulation around the Lodge room, guided by the Senior Deacon. During this circuit, the Worshipful Master recites a passage on unity, and the Master, Junior Warden, and Senior Warden each give a single gavel rap as the candidate passes their stations. The perambulation concludes at the Junior Warden's station, where a challenge is issued.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0019 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0021 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Obligation.

This section details the Entered Apprentice obligation, emphasizing secrecy and the severe penalties for its violation. It describes the ritualistic process of the candidate receiving 'light,' which involves the removal of the hoodwink and cable-tow, and the explanation of the three great lights (Holy Bible, square, and compasses) and three lesser lights (sun, moon, and Master of the Lodge). The section concludes with the Master presenting the grip and word of an Entered Apprentice, reinforcing the commitment to Masonic principles.

This section details the ritualistic process for identifying an Entered Apprentice Mason through a specific grip and the word "Boaz," which is revealed through a letter-by-letter exchange. Following this, the newly obligated candidate is assisted to rise from the altar and presented to the Junior and Senior Wardens. The text describes variations in lodge procedures for this presentation, either a single circuit around the room with signs or a more formal halt at the Wardens' stations involving a dialogue about the nature of Masonic signs (right angles, horizontals, perpendiculars) and tokens (brotherly grips for recognition).
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0021 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0022 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Bringing to Light: Entered Apprentice Sign and Word

This section details the Entered Apprentice signs, token, and the word 'Boaz,' explaining their allusions to the obligation and secrecy. It describes the ritualistic examination by the Junior and Senior Wardens. The Worshipful Master then presents the candidate with a lambskin apron, an emblem of innocence and a badge of a Mason, instructing how it should be worn. The section concludes with the ancient custom of requesting a metallic deposit from the candidate as a memento for the Lodge archives.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0022 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0026 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Lecture: First Section

This section presents the First Section of the Entered Apprentice Lecture in a question-and-answer format, detailing the candidate's journey through the initiation ritual. It covers the secrecy of Masonic knowledge, the preparation of the candidate, the process of admission including knocks and questions, the solemn obligation, the discovery and meaning of the three great and three lesser lights in Masonry, the presentation of the lambskin apron as an emblem of innocence, and the ancient custom of depositing a metallic memorial.

This section details the Entered Apprentice's return to the Lodge after examination, his placement in the northeast corner, and the presentation of the working tools: the twenty-four-inch gauge and common gavel. It explains the symbolic use of the twenty-four-inch gauge for dividing one's time into portions for the service of God and relief of a distressed brother, usual avocations, and refreshment and sleep.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0026 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0029 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Second Section.

This section explains the symbolic and practical reasons behind various elements of the Entered Apprentice initiation ritual. It covers the divestment of metals, the state of being neither naked nor clothed, the removal of shoes, being hoodwinked with a cable-tow, the three knocks, reception on a sharp instrument, kneeling for prayer, the inquiry about trust in Deity, being led by the right hand, circumambulation, encountering obstructions, kneeling on the left knee, and resting the hand on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses. These actions are connected to ancient customs, moral lessons, and the construction of King Solomon's Temple.

This section explains the symbolic significance of key elements in the Entered Apprentice degree: the lambskin apron, the metallic deposit, and the candidate's placement in the northeast corner of the Lodge. It highlights lessons of innocence, purity, charity, and the foundational principles for building a moral and Masonic life.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0029 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0030 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Third Section.

This section defines a Masonic Lodge, detailing its form, covering, and symbolic supports (Wisdom, Strength, Beauty represented by the Master and Wardens). It describes the Lodge's furniture (Holy Bible, square, compasses), ornaments (mosaic pavement, indented tessel, blazing star), and lights (three, none in the north). It also explains the six jewels (rough ashler, perfect ashler, trestle-board, square, level, plumb) and their moral teachings. Finally, it discusses the traditional dedication of Lodges to King Solomon and, in modern times, to St. John the Baptist and St. John the Evangelist, introducing the emblem of the point within a circle.

This section concludes the lecture on the Entered Apprentice Degree, emphasizing that a Mason who adheres to its precepts will not err. It also notes that very few Masons fully master these lectures, with only a small percentage, often those seeking office, achieving perfect knowledge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0030 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0032 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Charge at Initiation Into the First Degree

This section presents the Charge given to a newly initiated Entered Apprentice, congratulating them on joining an ancient and honorable order. It outlines three fundamental duties: to God (reverence, imploring aid), to one's neighbor (acting upon the square, reciprocity), and to oneself (avoiding intemperance, maintaining dignity). The charge also details civic responsibilities, proper outward demeanor, the balance between Masonic duties and personal avocations, the importance of secrecy, and the careful recommendation of new candidates. A special instruction is included for clergymen, emphasizing Freemasonry's alignment with universal benevolence and charity. Footnotes provide historical context on the Master's hat in foreign lodges and the etymology of 'cowan'.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0032 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0033 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Rough Ashler

This section introduces the concepts of the Rough Ashler and Perfect Ashler, fundamental symbols in Freemasonry. It is accompanied by footnotes that elaborate on specific Masonic traditions and terminology. These include the practice of the Master wearing a hat in certain lodges, a detailed etymological and symbolic explanation of the term 'cowan' as an unqualified or profane intruder, and a description of the solemn Lodge Opening ceremony, emphasizing duties, obligations, and precautions against the profane.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0033 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0034 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Rough Ashler

This section discusses fundamental aspects of Masonic practice, including the concept of Masonry as a cohesive whole where each Mason plays a part. It details the solemn procedure of opening a Lodge, emphasizing officer duties, and the protocol for a Mason entering an opened Lodge, which involves approaching the altar, facing the Worshipful Master, and giving the duegard and sign of the degree. The text also highlights a mandatory emblematical feature that every initiated Mason must undergo as an indispensable pledge of fidelity, regardless of personal objections, noting its ancient and universal nature.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0034 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Degree: Historical Notes

This section provides historical notes and explanations for various elements of the Entered Apprentice Degree. It discusses the reverence associated with removing footwear in the East, linking it to the purity of the Mosaic pavement. It emphasizes that a candidate's admission is solely based on their avowal of belief and trust in God. Practical details are given regarding the number of candidates that can be initiated, passed, or raised in each degree. The text debunks claims that biblical figures like Adam, Moses, or Solomon were Freemasons. It clarifies the dual meaning of the word 'Hail' or 'Hale' in Masonic context. Specific ritual actions, such as drawing a hand or sword across the candidate's throat, are mentioned. The story of Hipparchus, a Pythagorean, is recounted as a cautionary tale about the severe consequences of violating societal engagements. The concept of 'Light' in initiation is explained as progressing from material to intellectual illumination. Finally, the symbolic meaning of the first sign, related to the palate and throat, is detailed as a lesson in temperance and restraint of passions, and the role of the Master and Senior Warden in laying the corner-stone is noted, along with the practice of fostering emulation and discussion in Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0055 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Hiram Abiff: Legend, Murder, Burial, and Discovery

This section details the ritualistic instruction for a candidate to approach the East, the symbolic 'place of further light in Masonry'. The Worshipful Master directs the Senior Deacon to have the Senior Warden teach the candidate how to perform three specific upright, regular steps to position themselves erect at the altar before the Master.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0055 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0059 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Trowel.

This section details the procedure for calling the Lodge from labor to refreshment during an initiation ceremony. The Worshipful Master instructs the Junior Warden to declare 'High twelve' and call the craft to refreshment for a specified period. The Junior Warden then erects his column, gives three raps with his gavel, and announces the refreshment. It also describes the contrasting positions of the Junior and Senior Wardens' columns, indicating whether the Lodge is at labor or refreshment. During this time, the candidate is prepared in the ante-room, and the Lodge is made ready for the subsequent part of the ceremony, including the preparation of a large canvas.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0059 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0060 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Preparation for the Third Section Drama

This section details the elaborate preparation for the dramatic climax of the Master Mason degree. It involves specific props, clearing the lodge, dressing the candidate with a Senior Warden's jewel, and a deliberate deception to make him believe the ceremony is complete. The Worshipful Master then reveals that the candidate is not yet a Master Mason and must undergo severe trials to prove his fidelity, emphasizing self-reliance and trust in a higher power. The candidate is hoodwinked, prays, and then embarks on a symbolic journey around the darkened lodge, guided by the Conductor who introduces the legend of Hiram Abiff and his daily ritual at King Solomon's Temple.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0060 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0082 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Charge at Closing a Lodge

This section presents the 'Charge at Closing a Lodge,' which reminds brethren of their duties to uphold friendship, virtue, diligence, prudence, temperance, and discretion in the world. It emphasizes mutual support among Masons, correcting errors, and extending benevolence to all humanity, especially the 'household of the faithful,' concluding with a blessing for peace and unity. The procedural steps for closing the lodge are detailed, including the Worshipful Master's declaration, instruction to the Junior Deacon to inform the Tyler, a single gavel rap, and the brethren divesting themselves of regalia. Additionally, the text notes the significant variations in Masonic 'work' and lectures across different states, particularly within the 'Blue Degrees' or 'Blue Lodge,' explaining that each Grand Lodge has its own 'work' taught annually by its Grand Lecturer.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0082 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0083 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Critical Notes on the Third Degree

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing the historical variations in Masonic 'work' and lectures across different U.S. states and Europe. It highlights the influence of Brother John Barney's lectures, derived from the Preston Lectures, and their adoption in the Northwestern states, noting how Barney adapted his teachings to different systems in the Western and Southwestern states. The text also mentions the impact of 'Morganry' in Minnesota and New York, where rituals closely resemble Morgan's disclosures. It asserts that while minor differences exist, the essential points of Masonry are universal. Footnotes offer critical commentary on the Third Degree's nature, describing it as traditionary, historical, and legendary, but also hyperbolical, apocryphal, and fabulous. They include practical details of a ritualistic element involving a sword, discuss the symbolism of worldly possession and the historical connections of Tubal Cain, Jubal, and Vulcan. Detailed descriptions of Master's Lodge regalia and officer jewels are provided, alongside the historical evolution of the 'fatal weapons' used in the Third Degree. The section also references Professor Stuart's critique regarding the linguistic origins of names in the legend and notes variations in the narrative concerning the number of Fellow Crafts searching for Hiram and the placement of the sprig of acacia.

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing variations in the Hiramic legend, including the disputed location of the lost body and the names of the ruffians. It clarifies the symbolic use of acacia, distinguishing it from cassia, and explains its significance in Jewish law for marking graves. The text also connects Masonic penalties to ancient Hebrew covenant ceremonies, describing their ritualistic elements. Furthermore, it discusses the allegorical nature of the Master Mason degree, particularly regarding the lost word and the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, cautioning against literal historical interpretations of the legend.

This section critically examines the historical and internal consistency of the Hiramic legend within the Third Degree. It challenges the historical accuracy of Hiram Abiff's death and burial, citing Dr. Anderson and Josephus, who assert Hiram lived to complete the Temple and returned to Tyre. The text also highlights inconsistencies in ritual narratives, such as Hiram drawing plans in the Holy of Holies before its completion or accessibility, and the division of operatives and payment of wages before the pillars and Middle Chamber were built. Despite these critiques, the section reaffirms the symbolic importance of the legend in teaching the doctrine of resurrection, fidelity, and a future state, representing a Master Mason as one saved from iniquity and raised to faith.

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing historical variations in ritual descriptions of the "three principal entrances" to the Temple across different jurisdictions and time periods. It outlines the structure of the Master Mason lecture, covering the mode of raising, historical traditions, and emblem explanations. A significant portion is dedicated to an extensive Christian allegorical interpretation of the Third Degree, linking Hiram Abiff's death and resurrection to the Christian dispensation, the state of religion before Christ, and its restoration by the Saviour. This allegory is further elaborated with specific parallels drawn from the 32nd Degree, connecting Hiram's fate to Christ's condemnation, suffering, and the symbolism of the lost Word, false brethren, and the sprig of cassia. The text also includes a description of the solemn closing ceremony of a Mason's Lodge and an author's note emphasizing the allegorical nature of the Hiramic legend over its literal historical accuracy, citing scriptural evidence. Finally, it briefly defines the role of a Grand Lecturer.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0083 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0086 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master.

This section details the opening of a Mark Master Masons Lodge, including the specific gavel raps and declaration by the Right Worshipful Master. It clarifies that the Mark Master Lodge's sole business is candidate initiation, with other administrative tasks reserved for the Royal Arch Degree. The process of preparing a candidate for advancement is described, involving specific attire adjustments and the carrying of symbolic blocks (square, plumb, and a keystone with initials H. T. W. S. S. T. K. S.). The candidate, along with two officers, enters the Lodge as "Workmen from the quarries" and undergoes a ritualistic challenge at the Junior Overseer's station, presenting their work for inspection.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0086 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0088 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master's Song.

This section details the 'Mark Master's Song' and a significant ritualistic drama involving a candidate attempting to receive wages. The song calls Mark Masters to present their work for inspection by the Chief Overseer and Chief Architect, emphasizing the importance of approved work and the right to wages. It references Hiram, the widow's son, and the keystone. The ritual reenacts a scenario where a candidate, after the second verse of the song, attempts to receive a penny from the Senior Warden through a lattice-window, using a specific hand gesture. The candidate is accused of being an 'impostor' for not being able to give the proper token, leading to a dramatic threat of having his hand struck off. The Senior Deacon intercedes, vouching for the candidate as a craftsman and promising to take him to the Right Worshipful Master. The candidate then proves himself a Fellow Craft Mason. The Right Worshipful Master sternly reprimands the candidate for attempting to receive wages without the token, but remits the penalty due to youth and inexperience, ordering the Senior Deacon to teach him how to bring up a regularly wrought stone in the quarries. The reprimand itself is often omitted in modern lodges but is included for historical context. The section concludes with the preparation of the candidate for the degree, including divesting of apparel, money, and valuables, baring the breast, a cable-tow four times around the body, and blindfolding with a hoodwink, followed by four distinct knocks at the door.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0088 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0089 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master Reception and Obligation

This section details the reception and obligation ceremony for the Mark Master Degree. It begins with a severe reprimand given to the candidate for presenting unfit work and claiming undeserved wages, a practice noted as often omitted in modern lodges. The candidate is then prepared by being divested of valuables, blindfolded, and having a cable-tow placed around his body. After an alarm at the door, the candidate, identified as a Master Mason seeking further light, is admitted using the pass-word JOPPA. The Senior Deacon then symbolically receives the candidate upon the edge of an indenting chisel and under the pressure of a mallet, explaining their use for 'indenting the mind.' The ceremony concludes with a perambulation around the Lodge, during which the Master reads scripture passages concerning 'the stone which the builders refused/rejected' becoming 'the head stone of the corner.'

This section details the reception and obligation of a Mark Master Mason. The candidate undergoes a ritualistic perambulation, answering questions at the posts of the Junior and Senior Wardens, and the Right Worshipful Master. The core of the section is the extensive obligation, which includes promises of secrecy, adherence to Masonic law, obedience to summonses, and specific rules regarding the use, exchange, and redemption of a personal 'mark.' It also outlines severe penalties for violating the oath and concludes with a practical demonstration of how a brother's mark can be used to request financial aid.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0089 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0090 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: John Jay.

This section describes two methods of instructing a candidate in the Mark Master Mason degree regarding the duty associated with a 'mark' and a request for a loan. The older method involved the Right Worshipful Master asking the candidate to accommodate a Brother Jay with a loan, and the candidate, having no money, would be reminded of his oath to return the mark with its price (half a Jewish shekel or a quarter-dollar). The candidate would then 'discover' a quarter-dollar previously placed in his pocket, leading to a lesson on diligent search before denying a brother's request. The newer, more common method involves the Right Worshipful Master presenting a metal mark and requesting a loan, which the candidate cannot provide. A brother then supplies the candidate with the money, and the Right Worshipful Master administers the same caution. The section concludes with the instruction for the pass-grip and word, and the real grip and word of a Mark Master Mason, noting the pass-grip's origin from assisting someone up a steep ascent.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0090 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0091 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master: Grips and the Wages Parable

This section details the grips and passwords of a Mark Master Mason, including the pass-grip (JOPPA) and the real grip (SIROC or MARK WELL). It then describes a ritualistic parable concerning a keystone ordered by Hiram Abiff, which was initially rejected by the Overseers but later found in the rubbish. The Right Worshipful Master presents this keystone, reads several scriptural passages about the 'stone which the builders refused,' and invests the candidate with a new name, represented by a circle of letters on the keystone.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0091 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0099 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Past Master: Investiture and Lecture

This section details the investiture and lecture for a Past Master, outlining the specific signs, duegard, and grip of the degree. It describes the ritualistic seating of the candidate in the 'oriental chair of King Solomon,' referencing Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff. The ceremony includes the presentation and explanation of various Masonic implements such as the Holy Writings, Square, Compasses, Rule, Line, Book of Constitutions, By-laws, and Mallet, each with its symbolic meaning for conduct in life. The process concludes with the brethren saluting the new Master and the transfer of officer duties and jewels.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0099 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0100 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Page 342

This section describes a ritualistic scenario where a candidate, acting as the Worshipful Master, is tasked with nominating a Treasurer. The brethren intentionally create a scene of confusion and disorder, making various disruptive proposals and challenging the candidate's authority and adherence to Masonic law, particularly regarding the established price of Chapter Degrees.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0100 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0102 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Page 344

This section details the ceremonies of the Past Master Degree, which often involves a mock lodge closing and business session designed to test the candidate. The candidate, placed in the Master's chair, is expected to close the Lodge but is reminded of the obligation to deliver a lecture. The experience is intended to teach the necessity of being qualified for any office accepted. The lecture for this degree covers constituting a Lodge, installation ceremonies, laying foundation stones, dedicating Masonic Halls, and funeral ceremonies. The text also provides historical context for the Past Master Degree, explaining its origin as a prerequisite for Royal Arch Masonry when it was governed by symbolic Lodges, and the practice of granting dispensations to 'pass the chair' for Master Masons not actually elected to preside.

This section explains the Past Master's Degree, distinguishing between 'Actual Past Masters' who are elected to preside over a symbolic Lodge and 'Virtual Past Masters' who receive the degree in a Chapter as a prerequisite for the Royal Arch. It clarifies that the Virtual Past Master status does not confer official rank or privileges in a symbolic Lodge. The text includes a closing prayer for the Lodge and a detailed Q&A lecture on the Past Master's Degree, covering the obligation, specific signs (the duegard and 'the sign'), the grip, the word 'Stone Squarer,' the jewel of office (a square), the three great lights (Holy Bible, square, and compasses) and their varying positions across the Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, and Master Mason degrees, and the charter or warrant.

This section details the various items presented to a Master, likely during an installation, and the extensive duties associated with the office. These include the constitution, by-laws, records, the privilege of being covered, the Great Lights (Bible, Square, and Compasses) with their specific positions for each degree, the charter or warrant, and the gavel. The Master's responsibilities encompass opening, instructing, and closing Lodges, initiating, crafting, and raising Masons, and presiding over numerous Masonic ceremonies.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0102 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0107 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Most Excellent Master: Ceremony of Completion

This section details the 'Ceremony of Completion' for the Most Excellent Master degree, celebrating the symbolic completion of the Temple. It includes the singing of an 'Excellent Master's Song' which marks the end of labor and the laying of the cope-stone and keystone. The ritual involves placing the keystone into an arch supported by pillars Jachin and Boaz, secured with six gavel raps. Brethren divest themselves of working tools and regalia, hanging them on the arch, signifying the completion of their work. The Ark is brought forward and placed on the altar with a pot of incense. The ceremony culminates in the acceptance and investiture of worthy candidates as Most Excellent Masters, a sign of admiration or astonishment, a prayer for divine presence, and a reading from 2 Chronicles vii. 1, 4, followed by a ritual involving fire and repeated declarations of God's mercy. The Master then delivers a charge to the candidate, emphasizing the duty to dispense light and truth and the importance of acquiring knowledge.

This section details the completion and closing ceremonies of the Most Excellent Master degree, including specific ritualistic actions, a charge to the newly admitted candidate, and readings from Psalms. The charge emphasizes the candidate's duty to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons and to diligently acquire knowledge. The closing ritual involves brethren assembling around the altar, kneeling, joining hands, and performing balancing movements, followed by a lecture explaining the significance of the cap stone and the six distinct knocks for admission.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0107 -->
practical_elements: Superintendence of the brethren
Government of the brethren
Faithful discharge of important duties
The Junior Deacon attends to alarms from the outside, inquires of the Tyler, and reports the cause to the Master.
The Tyler reports the names of brethren seeking admission.
The Master grants permission for brethren to be admitted.
Brethren advance to the center of the Lodge, at the altar, make the duegard and sign of the degree on which the Lodge is opened.
No brother is allowed to take his seat until he has saluted the Worshipful Master upon entering a Lodge.
The Tyler generally cautions brethren about giving the sign before they enter the Lodge.
The Junior Deacon cautions brethren about giving the sign as soon as they are inside the Lodge.
The Junior Deacon's station is at the inner door, and it is his duty to attend to all alarms, report to the Master, and get permission before admitting anyone.
Visitors not personally known to the Master or brethren must be vouched for or pass an examination.
Upon admission, the Tyler informs visitors of the degree the Lodge is opened on and furnishes them with aprons.
Brethren generally gather at an early hour at the Lodge-room, which has been opened and cleaned by the Tyler.
Upon the Master's arrival and the hour of meeting, the Master repairs to his seat in the East, puts on his hat, sash, yoke, and apron, with gavel in hand.
The Master commands: "Brethren will be properly clothed and in order; officers repair to their stations for the purpose of opening."
Brethren put on their aprons and seat themselves around the Lodge-room.
Officers invest themselves with their yokes and aprons and take their specific stations.

Officer stations include: Senior Warden in the West; Junior Warden in the South; Senior Deacon in front of the Worshipful Master in the East, a little to his right, with a long rod; Junior Deacon at the right hand of the Senior Warden in the West, guarding the inner door, with rod; Secretary at the left of the Worshipful Master; Treasurer at the right; two Stewards on the right and left of the Junior Warden in the South, with rods.
The Worshipful Master calls the Tyler to approach the East.
The Tyler approaches the Worshipful Master's seat with yoke and apron on.
The Tyler states his place: 'Without the inner door.'
The Tyler states his duty: 'To keep off all cowans and eavesdroppers, and not to pass or repass any but such as are duly qualified and have the Worshipful Master's permission.'
The Worshipful Master hands the Tyler a sword as the implement of his office.
The Tyler retires to the inside of the outer door of the ante-room.
All Lodge-doors are closed after the Tyler retires.
The Worshipful Master gives one rap with his gavel.
The Junior Deacon rises upon the gavel rap.
The Junior Deacon states the first and constant care of Masons: 'To see that the Lodge is duly tyled.'
The Junior Deacon opens the door to communicate with the Tyler.
The Junior Deacon informs the Tyler to tyle the Lodge as an Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, or Master Mason, as appropriate.
The Junior Deacon closes the door after giving instructions to the Tyler.
One rap for an Entered Apprentice Lodge, two for a Fellow Craft Lodge, and three for a Master Mason's Lodge.
The Tyler responds to the raps.
The Junior Deacon confirms the Lodge is tyled.
The Tyler is stationed without the inner door, invested with a sword.
The Senior Warden confirms all present are of the correct degree.
The Senior Warden's station is in the west.
The Junior Deacon's station is at the right hand of the Senior Warden in the west.
The Senior Deacon's station is at the right hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Secretary's station is at the left hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Treasurer's station is at the right hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Junior Warden's station is in the south.
The Worshipful Master's station is in the east.
The Junior Deacon makes the sign of an Entered Apprentice Mason.
The Worshipful Master uses two raps with his gavel to prompt all officers to rise.
The Secretary records Lodge proceedings, transmits copies to the Grand Lodge if required, receives money from brethren, and passes it to the Treasurer, taking a receipt.
The Treasurer receives money from the Secretary, keeps accounts, and pays out funds by order of the Worshipful Master and consent of the Lodge.
The Senior Deacon carries orders from the Worshipful Master to the Senior Warden and elsewhere, introduces and clothes visiting brethren, and receives and conducts candidates.
The Junior Deacon carries orders from the Senior Warden to the Junior Warden and elsewhere, and ensures the Lodge is tyled.
Junior Warden's station is in the south.
Senior Warden's station is in the west.
Worshipful Master's station is in the east.
Worshipful Master gives three raps with his gavel to signal brethren to rise.
Orders for opening the Lodge are communicated from Worshipful Master to Senior Warden, then to Junior Warden, and finally to the brethren.
Brethren perform signs together, led by the Master.
Duegard of an Entered Apprentice: Left hand supporting the Bible, right hand resting thereon.
Sign of an Entered Apprentice: Draw the right hand rapidly across the neck and drop the arm to the side.
Duegard of a Fellow Craft: Left arm held horizontally to the elbow, rest of the arm vertically, forming a square; right hand detached from the stomach, fingers extending outward.
Sign of a Fellow Craft is given after its duegard.
Duegard of a Master Mason: Both hands resting on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
Duegards and signs are made with right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, with slight, marked pauses.
For Fellow Craft duegard and sign, the left arm must be dropped suddenly and with spirit.
A petition for membership must include the applicant's residence (e.g., New York City), age (e.g., thirty-eight years), and occupation (e.g., bookseller).
Petitions require recommendations from existing Brothers (e.g., Brothers Jones, Carson, Fox, Derby, Jackson).
Petitions must be accompanied by a fee (e.g., ten dollars).
The Worshipful Master presents petitions to the Wardens and Brethren for their consideration.
A motion is made and seconded to receive petitions and appoint a committee of investigation.
Brethren vote on motions using the 'usual sign'.
The Worshipful Master appoints specific Brothers to serve on committees of investigation for each petitioner.
Worshipful Master asks the Secretary for committee reports.
Secretary announces pending reports on candidate applications.
Worshipful Master asks if committee chairmen are present.
Committee chairmen present their reports on candidate character, stating whether they are favorable or unfavorable.
Lodge members may engage in discussion regarding a candidate's character or history.
A motion is made to receive the report, discharge the committee, and ballot for the candidate.
The motion is seconded.
Worshipful Master calls for a vote using the 'usual sign'.
The Worshipful Master initiates the balloting process for candidates.
The Secretary identifies candidates to be balloted for.
Brethren are given an opportunity to speak for or against a candidate before balloting.
The Senior Deacon is instructed to prepare the ballot-box.
The ballot-box is described as a small box, five or six inches square, with two drawers and a small hopper on top.
The hopper's hole leads to the first drawer, which is empty and shoved in.
The lower drawer is drawn out and contains both black and white balls.
The Senior Deacon places the ballot-box on the altar in the middle of the Lodge.
The Worshipful Master instructs the brethren to proceed with balloting.
The Master initiates the balloting process.
The Secretary calls the names of Masons, from the Senior Warden down to the Tyler.
Each Mason approaches the altar, makes the sign of a Master Mason to the Master.
Each Mason takes a ball (white or black) from the lower drawer of the ballot-box.
Each Mason deposits the ball in the hopper above and returns to their seat.
The Senior Deacon closes the ballot drawer after all have voted.
The Senior Deacon carries the ballot box to the Junior Warden in the south.
The Junior Warden examines the top drawer to determine if the ballot is 'clear' or 'not clear' and reports to the Worshipful Master.
The Deacon then carries the ballot box to the Senior Warden in the west for examination.
The Senior Warden examines the ballot and reports its status ('clear' or 'not clear') to the Worshipful Master.
The Deacon delivers the ballot box to the Worshipful Master's station in the east.
The Worshipful Master examines the box and declares whether the candidate (e.g., Mr. Joseph Locker) has been elected or not to the First Degree in Masonry.
The same balloting process is followed for any other candidates.
The Worshipful Master (W. M.) instructs the Junior Deacon to ascertain candidates in waiting and for what Degree, and to report at once.
The Junior Deacon inquires of the Tyler and brethren generally.
The Junior Deacon reports candidates, e.g., Mr. Peter Gabe and Mr. John Milke, waiting for the First Degree.
The W. M. decides to confer the Degree upon one candidate (e.g., Mr. Gabe) and defer another (e.g., Mr. Milke) to a special communication due to business engagements.
The Junior Deacon is instructed to inform the deferred candidate (Mr. Milke) of the decision and not keep him waiting.
The Junior Deacon is also instructed to inform the chosen candidate (Mr. Gabe) that the First Degree will be conferred after the Lodge's regular business.
A Senior Warden (S. W.) declares 'Clear (or not) in the west, Worshipful,' making a sign.
The Junior Deacon arrives at the Worshipful Master's station in the east, looks in the box.
The W. M. declares 'And clear (or not clear) in the east.'
The W. M. announces the election (or not) of a candidate (e.g., Mr. Joseph Locker) to the First Degree in Masonry.
Other candidates are balloted for in the same manner.
The Junior Deacon performs his duty of informing candidates.
Candidate interrogated on motivations, desire for knowledge, service, and conformity to Order's usages.
Candidate requested to strip: coat, shoes, stockings, vest, cravat, pantaloons.
Candidate provided with a pair of drawers.
Left arm slipped out of shirt-sleeve and put through bosom, baring arm and breast.
Handkerchief or hoodwink tied over candidate's eyes.
Slipper placed on candidate's right foot.
Rope, called a cable-tow, placed once around candidate's neck, dragging behind.
Junior Deacon leads candidate to Lodge door and gives three distinct knocks.
Senior Deacon makes the sign of an Entered Apprentice to the Master.
Dialogue at the door between Senior Deacon and Junior Deacon (responding for candidate).
Senior Deacon takes compasses from the altar.
Senior Deacon presents one point of the compasses to the candidate's naked left breast.
Junior Deacon leaves candidate with Senior Deacon and takes his seat.
Senior Deacon, followed by two Stewards, travels once regularly around the Lodge-room with the candidate.
Worshipful Master gives one rap to stop the procession.
Candidate conducted to the center of the Lodge and kneels for prayer.
Worshipful Master leaves his seat, kneels by candidate, and recites a prayer.
All respond with "So mote it be."
Worshipful Master rises, takes candidate by the right hand, places left hand on candidate's head.
Candidate prompted to declare trust in God.
Candidate kneels for prayer.
Worshipful Master leaves his seat, kneels by the candidate, and recites a prayer.
Worshipful Master takes the candidate by the right hand and places his left on the candidate's head.
Candidate declares trust in God.
Worshipful Master assists the candidate to rise.
Senior Deacon conducts the candidate once around the Lodge room.
Worshipful Master recites the passage 'Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!' during the perambulation.
The Master, Junior Warden, and Senior Warden each give one gavel rap as the candidate passes their respective stations.
The candidate halts at the Junior Warden's station in the south.
Junior Warden gives one rap, followed by the conductor giving one rap.
Junior Warden asks, 'Who comes here?'
Candidate places hands on the Holy Bible.
Master presses gavel on candidate's knuckles during the obligation.
Candidate promises to hail, conceal, and never reveal Masonic secrets.
Candidate promises not to print, paint, stamp, stain, cut, carve, mark, or engrave Masonic secrets on any movable or immovable object.
Candidate binds himself under specific penalties: throat cut across, tongue torn out by its roots, and body buried in the rough sands of the sea at low-water mark.
Candidate detaches hands and kisses the Holy Bible.
Candidate is prompted to state 'Light' as their most desired condition.
Brethren surrounding the altar stretch forth their hands in the form of the duegard of an Entered Apprentice Mason.
Worshipful Master recites Genesis 1:1-3 ('Let there be light, and there was light').
Worshipful Master gives one rap, signaling all to be seated except himself.
Conductor strips off the hoodwink from the candidate's eyes while the candidate remains kneeling at the altar.
Senior Deacon removes the cable-tow from the candidate's neck.
Worshipful Master explains the symbolism of the compasses, square, and Holy Bible.
Worshipful Master explains the symbolism of the three lesser lights (three burning tapers).
Worshipful Master approaches the candidate from the east, performing the duegard, sign, and step of an Entered Apprentice Mason.
Worshipful Master takes the candidate by the right hand and presents the grip and word of an Entered Apprentice.
Dialogue between the Worshipful Master and Senior Deacon (answering for the candidate) regarding 'hail,' 'conceal,' and the token.
Dialogue for identifying the grip of an Entered Apprentice Mason.
Ritualistic exchange of letters (A, B, O, Z, Bo, Az) to reveal the word "Boaz."
Worshipful Master assists the candidate to rise from the altar by the right hand.
Candidate salutes the Junior and Senior Wardens as an obligated Entered Apprentice.
Lodge procedural variation: candidate passes once around the room, giving the duegard and sign at officers' stations.
Lodge procedural variation: candidate halts at Wardens' stations for a specific ceremony.
Deacon guides the candidate by the right arm around the altar to the Junior Warden's station.
Deacon gives one rap with his rod on the floor, responded to by the Junior Warden with one gavel rap.
Dialogue between Junior Warden and Senior Deacon regarding the identity of the candidate.
Senior Deacon defines signs as "Right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars."
Senior Deacon defines tokens as "Certain friendly or brotherly grips, by which one Mason may know another, in the dark as well as in the light."
Senior Deacon gives the duegard and directs the candidate.
Candidate performs a duegard, alluding to the hand placement during the Entered Apprentice obligation.
Candidate makes the sign of an Entered Apprentice, alluding to the penalty of the obligation.
Senior Deacon guides the candidate to take the Junior Warden by the right hand for the token/grip.
The word 'Boaz' is lettered and then pronounced during the examination.
Candidate undergoes examination by the Junior Warden and Senior Warden.
Worshipful Master presents a white linen or lambskin apron to the candidate.
Senior Warden ties the apron on the candidate with the flap turned up, explaining how Entered Apprentices wore them at King Solomon's Temple.
Worshipful Master requests a metallic deposit from the candidate as a memento for the Lodge archives.
Concealing secrets of Masons, alluded to by pressing the thumb-nail on the first joint.
Being divested of all metals, neither naked nor clothed, barefoot nor shod, hoodwinked, with a cable-tow around the neck during preparation.
Gaining admission by three distinct knocks.
Being received on the point of a sharp instrument pressing the naked left breast.
Being conducted to the center of the Lodge and caused to kneel for prayer.
Being taken by the right hand and ordered to arise, follow the conductor, and fear no danger.
Following the conductor once around the Lodge to the Junior Warden's station in the south, then to the Senior Warden in the west, and finally to the Worshipful Master in the east.
Approaching the east by one upright, regular step, with feet forming an angle of an oblong square and body erect, at the altar.
Kneeling on the naked left knee, with the right forming a square, left hand supporting the Holy Bible, square, and compasses, and right hand resting thereon, to take the solemn oath.
Receiving light by order of the Worshipful Master and assistance of the brethren.
Worshipful Master approaching under the duegard and sign of an Entered Apprentice, presenting the right hand, grip, and word.
Saluting the Junior and Senior Wardens as an Entered Apprentice.
Receiving a lambskin or white linen apron and being taught to wear it with the flap turned up.
Being requested to deposit something of a metallic kind as a memorial.
Candidate is returned to the place from whence he came, reinvested, and returned to the Lodge for further instructions.
Candidate is placed in the northeast corner, with feet forming a right angle and body erect, at the right hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Worshipful Master presents the working-tools of an Entered Apprentice Mason: the twenty-four-inch gauge and common gavel.
Operative masons use the twenty-four-inch gauge to measure and lay out their work.
Divesting of all metals.
Being neither naked nor clothed.
Being neither barefoot nor shod.
Being hoodwinked.
Having a cable-tow about the neck.
Giving three distinct knocks.
Kneeling and attending at prayer.
Being asked in whom trust is placed.
Being taken by the right hand.
Being ordered to arise, follow the conductor, and fear no danger.
Being conducted once around the Lodge.
Meeting with several obstructions on passage.
Kneeling on the naked left knee.
Resting the right hand on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
Presentation of a lambskin or white linen apron to the candidate.
Request for the candidate to deposit something of a metallic kind.
Conduction of the candidate to the northeast corner of the Lodge.
Candidate standing upright with feet forming a square in the northeast corner.
Masons are duly assembled with the Holy Bible, square, compasses, and charter or warrant.
The Worshipful Master has the duty to open the Lodge, set the craft at work, and give proper instructions.
The Senior Warden's duty is to assist the Worshipful Master, pay the craft their wages, and ensure harmony.
The Junior Warden's duty is to observe the sun at high meridian.
Operative workmen erect temporal buildings according to plans on a trestle-board.
Speculative Masons endeavor to erect their spiritual building according to the rules and designs laid down by the Supreme Architect in the Holy Bible.
A Lodge should be situated due east and west.
Masons are expected to keep themselves circumscribed within the precepts of the degree.
Masons should be able to answer inquiries regarding the lectures of the Entered Apprentice Degree.
Strictly observe and inculcate duties to God, your neighbor, and yourself.
Never mention God's name but with reverential awe, implore His aid, and esteem Him as your chief good.
Act upon the square with your neighbor, doing unto him as you would have him do unto you.
Avoid all irregularity and intemperance that may impair faculties or debase your profession's dignity.
Be a quiet and peaceable citizen, true to your government, and just to your country.
Do not countenance disloyalty or rebellion; patiently submit to legal authority and conform to the government.
Be particularly careful in outward demeanor to avoid censure or reproach.
Do not let interest, favor, or prejudice bias your integrity or lead to dishonorable actions.
Ensure Masonry does not interfere with necessary avocations, as these are not to be neglected.
Do not let zeal for the institution lead to arguments with those who ridicule it through ignorance.
At leisure hours, converse with well-informed brethren to improve in Masonic knowledge.
Keep sacred and inviolable the mysteries of the Order.
Be particularly careful not to recommend a person for initiation unless convinced they will conform to rules.
If the candidate is a clergyman, add the specific charge emphasizing benevolence and charity.
In most foreign Lodges, the Master wears his hat while the rest of the brethren remain uncovered, a practice followed by MacKenzie Beverly Esq., D. P. G. M. for the East Riding of York.
The ceremony of OPENING THE LODGE is solemn and impressive, reminding every brother of his duties and obligations.
Necessary precautions are employed during the Lodge Opening to avoid the intrusion of the profane.
Every member is compelled to assume a share of responsibility during the Lodge Opening ceremony.
Opening the Lodge is a solemn and imposing rite.
Every officer is made acquainted with his duty and impressed with its importance.
When a Mason enters an opened Lodge, he proceeds to the center of the Lodge, at the altar.
The Mason must face the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Mason must give the duegard and sign of the degree in which the Lodge is working.
The duegard is never omitted when the Master is addressed.
Every initiated person is bound to pass through an emblematical feature as an unmistakable pledge of fidelity.
This emblematical feature is an indispensable condition of Masonic tenure and cannot be avoided.
Putting off the covering of the feet as a sign of reverence, paralleling Eastern customs.
Candidate avows belief and trust in God as the sole basis for admission; refusal leads to rejection.
Chief officer expresses satisfaction upon a candidate's declaration of trust in God.
If more than one candidate is initiated at once, they are required to take hold of each other's arms.
Up to five candidates can be initiated as Entered Apprentices at once.
Up to five candidates can be 'passed' to the Second Degree at once.
Only one candidate at a time can be 'raised' to the Master's Degree.
Small Lodges may only manage one candidate at a time conveniently.
In some Lodges, at the words 'throat cut across,' a member or conductor draws their hand across the candidate's throat, or sometimes draws a sword.
The Master, assisted by the Senior Warden, lays the chief corner-stone of a fabric.
Lodge labors are conducted to produce an exciting spirit of emulation.
The Worshipful Master personally requests each individual to give an opinion on a specific doctrine or ceremony from the Chair.
The Worshipful Master orders the Senior Deacon to reconduct the candidate to the Senior Warden.
The Senior Deacon reports the Worshipful Master's orders to the Senior Warden.
The Senior Warden instructs the candidate to face the East (towards the Master).

The Senior Warden teaches the candidate to take three specific steps: 1) step off with the left foot, bringing the heel of the right foot into the hollow of the left; 2) step off with the right foot, bringing the heel of the left foot into the hollow of the right; 3) step off with the left foot, bringing both heels together.
The candidate is to maintain an erect body at the altar before the Worshipful Master in the East.
Conductor and candidate exit the Lodge.
Worshipful Master gives three gavel sounds (• • •).
Worshipful Master asks Junior Warden the hour.
Junior Warden responds 'High twelve'.
Worshipful Master orders Junior Warden to erect his column and call the craft from labor to refreshment for thirty or fifteen minutes.
Junior Warden takes an eighteen-inch wooden column from his desk and sets it upright at his right hand.
Junior Warden gives three raps (• • •) with his gavel.
Junior Warden announces, 'Brethren, you are accordingly at refreshment.'
Junior Warden's column is upright when at refreshment, and horizontal when the Lodge is opened (at labor).
Senior Warden's column is horizontal when the Junior Warden's is up, and upright when the Lodge is opened (at labor).
Candidate is prepared in the ante-room.
Lodge is prepared for the remaining portion of the initiation ceremony, including a canvas seven feet long and six feet wide.
Production of six strong loops and a buckskin bag stuffed with hair.
Removal of altar, lights, and Second Degree pillars.
Candidate dressed with apron (right-hand corner tucked up) and a yoke with a Senior Warden's jewel.
Friends congratulate the candidate upon his Masonic appearance, asking if he is 'through' to mislead him.
Worshipful Master gives one rap with gavel.
Junior Warden gives one rap, steps to desk, and turns small column down.
Senior Warden steps to stand and turns column up.
Brethren and candidate take seats.
Worshipful Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, and Secretary engage in a deceptive dialogue about closing the Lodge, making the Master Mason sign.
Candidate is called to approach the East.
Conductor (Senior Deacon) leads candidate to the Master's seat in the East.
Conductor takes position behind candidate, holding or concealing a hoodwink.
Worshipful Master questions candidate about his status as a Master Mason, then reveals he is not yet one and must face trials, instructing him to pray for himself.
Candidate kneels, and Conductor ties a hoodwink closely over his eyes.
Candidate prays orally or mentally, signifying completion by saying 'Amen'.
Lodge-room darkened by turning down gaslights or lamps.
Conductor assists candidate to arise and leads him three times around the room, traveling with the sun.
Conductor narrates Hiram Abiff's daily custom of entering the Sanctum Sanctorum of King Solomon's Temple at high twelve for designs and devotions, then retiring at the south gate.
The Worshipful Master formally declares the Lodge duly closed.
The Worshipful Master instructs the Junior Deacon to inform the Tyler of the Lodge's closing.
The Worshipful Master gives one rap with his gavel to signify the closing of the Lodge.
Brethren divest themselves of their regalia after the Lodge is closed.
Each Grand Lodge has a specific 'work' that is taught annually to its subordinate Lodges by a Grand Lecturer.
A ritualistic element where a conductor or brother draws a cold sword across the candidate's naked belly, causing a shocking effect.
Description of Master's Lodge jewels: suspended from blue velvet collars, bordered and embroidered with silver, with a blue rosette and silver five-pointed star.
Description of Master's Lodge apron: white, lined and bordered with blue, with an eye on the flap and selections from the Master's carpet on the area.
Description of Master's Lodge scarf: blue silk, trimmed with silver, with a blue rosette at the shoulder and hip, worn from left to right.

Specific jewels for officers: Worshipful Master (square), Past Master (compass opened on a quarter-circle), Senior Warden (level), Junior Warden (plumb), Secretary (cross pens), Treasurer (cross keys), Senior Deacon (square and compass with sun), Junior Deacon (square and compass with quarter moon), Stewards (cornucopia), Masters of Ceremonies (cross swords), Tyler (sabre).

Evolution of 'fatal weapons' in the Third Degree: from 'setting-tool, setting maul, and setting-beetle' (1717) to 'twenty-four-inch gauge, square, and gavel', then 'setting-tool, square, and rule', and currently 'plumb-rule, square, and strong or heavy maul'.
The Hebrew covenant ceremony involved cutting a beast in twain and passing between the parts thereof.
The throat of the animal was cut across with one single blow, dividing the windpipe, arteries, and veins without touching any bone.
The breast was torn open and the heart plucked out; any imperfection rendered the body unclean.
The animal was divided into two parts, placed north and south, for parties to the covenant to pass between them from east to west.
The carcass was then left as a prey to voracious animals.
Hiram Abiff's supposed practice of retiring to the Most Holy Place at high twelve to draw plans and offer orisons.
Hiram Abiff's alleged division of operatives into Apprentices, Fellow Crafts, and Masters.
Payment of Apprentices' wages at the pillar of Jachin.
Payment of Masters' wages in the Middle Chamber.
The High Priest's unique privilege of entering the Holy of Holies only once a year, with a string or belt around his waist.

The ritual description of "the three principal entrances to the Temple" has undergone historical alterations, from "east, west and south doors" to "north, south, and east entrances" by the Union in 1813.

Variations in the sequence of attacks/entrances exist across jurisdictions: United States (south, west, east), Scotland (east, south, west), and France (originally south, north, east; now west, south, east).
Adonhiramite Masonry, used in France around 1787, did not mention the doors in its ritual.

The Master Mason lecture is structured into three sections: the first illustrates the ancient and proper mode of raising a candidate, the second introduces historical traditions and exemplifies Masonic virtue, and the third explains emblems and describes Solomon's Temple construction.

The closing of a Mason's Lodge involves a report, inspection of avenues by the meridian officer, rehearsal of duties for leaders of craftsmen, the Senior Warden ensuring instruction and improvement, an impressive address on duties, and a fervent petition to the Deity.
A Grand Lecturer is elected annually by the Grand Lodge to teach the Ritual of Freemasonry to subordinate Lodges, typically compensated by salary or per lecture.
The Right Worshipful Master gives two raps with his gavel.
The Senior Grand Warden gives two raps.
The Junior Grand Warden gives two raps.
The raps are then repeated.
The Junior Deacon informs the Tyler that the Lodge is open.
The candidate divests himself of his coat and rolls up his shirt-sleeves to the shoulder.
The Senior Deacon and his associate also divest their coats and roll up their shirt-sleeves.
The Deacon takes a small block of marble or painted wood, about the size of a brick and weighing five or six pounds, with a square engraved upon it.
The Deacon's associate takes a similar block with a plumb engraved upon it.

The candidate is furnished with a block representing a keystone, which he carries between the thumb and two first fingers of the right hand, with other fingers clinched and the arm extended down perpendicularly at the side.
The three "Workmen from the quarries" form in a line about three feet distant from each other, with the candidate being last.
The door is opened without ceremony.
The Junior Deacon, as conductor, together with his associate and the candidate, enter the Lodge.
They march four times around the room.
They halt the last time at the Junior Overseer's station, at the south gate.
The conductor gives four raps (in couplets) on the floor with his heel.
The Senior Deacon presents his stone to the Junior Overseer.
The Junior Overseer applies his small trying square to the different angles of the stone.
Singing the 'Mark Master's Song' to the tune of 'America'.
Brothers walking up to the Senior Warden after the second verse.
Senior Warden standing behind a lattice-window.
Candidate thrusting right hand, with thumb and two first fingers open, third and little fingers clinched, palm up, through a hole in the window.
Receiving a penny as wages.
Senior Grand Warden seizing the candidate's hand and drawing his arm through to the shoulder.
Exclaiming 'An impostor! an impostor!'
Another person running up with a drawn sword, exclaiming 'Strike off his hand! strike off his hand!'
Senior Deacon interceding for the candidate, stating 'Spare him! spare him! he is not an impostor; I know him to be a craftsman; I have wrought with him in the quarries.'
Candidate withdrawing arm and giving the sign of a Fellow Craft Mason.
Senior Deacon taking candidate to the Master and stating the case.
Right Worshipful Master questioning the candidate about being a Fellow Craft Mason and asking for the sign.
Right Worshipful Master reprimanding the candidate.
Senior Deacon taking candidate by the collar and delivering a severe reprimand (often omitted).
Senior Deacon shaking the candidate severely and hurrying him to the preparation-room.
