Junior Deacon divesting candidate of outward apparel, money, and valuables.
Baring the candidate's breast.
Placing a cable-tow four times around the candidate's body.
Blindfolding the candidate with a hoodwink.
Junior Deacon conducting the candidate to the door and giving four distinct knocks (• • • •).
Candidate receives a severe reprimand for presenting unfit work and claiming undeserved wages.
Candidate is taken to the quarries to learn how to bring up a regularly wrought stone.
Candidate is divested of outward apparel, money, and valuables.
Candidate's breast is made bare.
A cable-tow is placed four times around the candidate's body.
Candidate is securely blindfolded with a hoodwink.
Junior Deacon conducts candidate to the door and gives four distinct knocks (• • • •).
Senior Deacon answers the alarm with four knocks, responded to by one knock from outside, then returned by Senior Deacon.
Junior Deacon whispers the pass-word JOPPA to the Senior Deacon.
Candidate enters the Lodge.
Senior Deacon approaches candidate with a mallet and engraving chisel.
Senior Deacon places the edge of the chisel against the candidate's breast.
Senior Deacon raises mallet, makes false motions, and gives a violent blow upon the head of the chisel.
Senior Deacon throws down mallet and chisel, takes candidate's left arm, and instructs him to 'Follow me.'
Candidate and Senior Deacon walk four times around the Lodge.
Master, Senior, and Junior Grand Wardens each give one loud rap with their mallet as the candidate passes their stations.
Master reads scripture passages from Psalm cxviii. 22, Matthew xxi. 42, Mark xii. 10, and Luke xx. 17.
Candidate arrives at the Junior Warden's post, where four knocks are given, and questions are asked and answered.
Candidate proceeds to the Senior Warden, who gives four raps, and the same questions and answers are exchanged.
Candidate is directed to the Right Worshipful Master in the East, where the same questions are asked and answered.
The Master orders the candidate to be conducted back to the Senior Warden in the West.
The candidate is taught to approach the East by four upright, regular steps, with feet forming a square and body erect at the altar.
The candidate kneels to receive the obligation.
After the obligation, the Right Worshipful Master instructs the candidate to detach their hand and kiss the book four times.
An alarm is made on the outside of the door immediately after the obligation.
The Junior Deacon attends the alarm and returns with a letter for the Right Worshipful Master.
The letter requests a loan of twenty-five dollars, offering the sender's mark as security.
Right Worshipful Master (R.W.M.) hands the candidate a mark.
Candidate states he has no money and cannot grant the loan request.
Senior Grand Warden (S.G.W.) offers to accommodate with twenty-five dollars if the mark is left as a pledge.
R.W.M. asks candidate to return the mark.
Candidate returns the mark without the price.
R.W.M. questions the candidate about violating his oath regarding the mark's price (half a Jewish shekel of silver or a fourth of a dollar).
Candidate searches pockets and finds a quarter-dollar (slyly placed by a brother).
Candidate hands the quarter-dollar and the mark to the Master.
R.W.M. administers a caution to the candidate.
In the newer method, R.W.M. presents a metal mark and requests a loan.
Candidate attempts to return the mark, but R.W.M. refuses, citing the oath.
R.W.M. calls attention to the obligation.
A brother provides the candidate with twenty-five cents (the price of the Mark).
Candidate hands the money and mark to the R.W.M.
R.W.M. administers the caution.
R.W.M. takes the candidate by the hand and invests him with the pass-grip and word, and the real grip and word of a Mark Master Mason.
Description of the pass-grip: extending right arms and clasping fingers, as if assisting up a steep ascent.
The pass-grip of a Mark Master Mason is associated with the password JOPPA, referencing the steep ascent from Joppa while conveying timber from Lebanon.

The real grip of a Mark Master Mason is named SIROC or MARK WELL, formed by locking the little fingers of the right hands, turning their backs together, and placing the ends of the thumbs against each other.
The Right Worshipful Master engages the candidate in a dialogue to transition from the pass-grip to the true grip.
The Right Worshipful Master admonishes the candidate never to reveal the words of the grip in any way other than how they were received.
Brethren shuffle their feet to signify a disturbance among the workmen.
The Right Worshipful Master gives two raps with his gavel to summon the three Overseers.
The Right Worshipful Master exhibits the figure of a keystone for inspection.
The Overseers report rejecting a stone because it was 'neither oblong nor square,' lacked a craft mark, and was deemed unfit, subsequently thrown among the rubbish.
The Right Worshipful Master orders an immediate search for the keystone, stating the Temple cannot be finished without it.
Brethren shuffle about the Lodge to find the keystone and bring it to the East.
The Senior Warden takes the keystone and reports its discovery in the Temple's rubbish to the Right Worshipful Master.
Two or three brethren carry the keystone to the Right Worshipful Master.
The Right Worshipful Master receives the keystone and places it upright and plumb on his desk, with its initials facing the Lodge and the candidate.
The Right Worshipful Master gives four raps with the gavel, prompting all to rise (though some Lodges remain seated).

The Right Worshipful Master reads specific scriptural passages (Ps. cxviii. 22, Matt. xxi. 42, Mark xii. 10, Luke xx. 17) about the rejected stone becoming the head of the corner, striking the keystone once, twice, thrice, and four times after each passage.
The Right Worshipful Master reads from Revelation xi. 17 about receiving a white stone and a new name.
The candidate steps forward to receive the new name.
The Right Worshipful Master invests the candidate with a new name, described as a circle of letters and the general mark of the Degree.
The Right Worshipful Master points out the initials on the keystone to the candidate.
The Master approaches under the step, sign, and duegard of a Past Master Mason.
The Master steps off with the left foot, placing the heel of the right foot at the toe of the left, forming a right angle (square).
First sign: thumb of the right hand (fingers clinched) placed upon the lips.
Second sign: right hand placed upon the left side of the neck, drawing it edgewise downward toward the right side.
The candidate is conducted into the 'oriental chair of King Solomon'.
A large cocked hat is placed on the candidate's head.
The candidate is seated in a chair in front of the Master's chair.
The Past Master's grip is used to raise and seat the candidate multiple times.
The Master and candidate take each other by the Master Mason's grip.
Insides of their feet are placed together.
The Master whispers 'GIBLEM' in the candidate's ear.
Right hands slip to catch just above the wrist of the left arm.
Left hands are raised, catching each other's right elbow.
The Master says, and candidate repeats: 'From a grip to a span, from a span to a grip'.
Left hand slips up the right arm to the back of each other.
The Master says: 'A threefold cord is strong', and the candidate replies: 'A fourfold cord is not easily broken'.
The Holy Writings, Square, Compasses, Rule, Line, Book of Constitutions, By-laws, and Mallet are presented and explained.
One stroke of the Mallet calls up the Junior and Senior Deacons.
Two strokes of the Mallet call up all subordinate officers.
Three strokes of the Mallet call up the whole Lodge.
Brethren salute the new Master by walking in front of the chair and giving the sign of an Entered Apprentice, repeated for each degree up to Past Master.
The Senior Warden and other officers deliver their jewels and gavels to the new Master.
The new Master requests the Wardens and other officers to resume their jewels and seats.
Candidate acts as Master to nominate a Treasurer.

Brethren engage in disruptive behavior: soliciting nominations, decrying others, crying 'Order, Worshipful!', proposing to dance/sing, whistling, singing, jumping, scuffling, knocking down chairs/benches.
A brother proposes to call from labor to refreshment.
A brother makes a speech advocating for the reduction of Chapter Degree prices and payment in produce.
The candidate is pressed to put the question regarding the price reduction.
Brethren vote against the proposal if put, then accuse the Master of breaking his oath to support the Constitution of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter.
The Master attempts to use the gavel to restore order, which often has the contrary effect.
Candidate is placed in the Oriental chair (Master's chair).
Brethren engage in a mock, noisy session, proposing to close the Lodge.
Candidate attempts to close the Lodge by rapping with a gavel.
Retired Master stops the candidate, reminding him of the obligation to give a lecture before closing.
A brother whispers to the candidate, suggesting he resign the chair to the old Master.
Candidate resigns the chair.
Master delivers a lecture divided into five sections.
The lecture is usually read from a Monitor.
Abridged ceremonies for the Past Master Degree are described, involving a ruse of unfinished business and committee reports in a Mark Masters Lodge.
Mock debate ensues regarding legality of funds disposition in a Chapter.
Right Worshipful Master is accused of corruption and indignantly resigns.
Candidate is nominated, elected, and placed in the Oriental chair.
Retired Master reads a charge to the candidate.
Candidates are made to pass the chair as preparation for the Royal Arch.
The ceremony of passing the chair.
Kneeling at the altar in due form to take a solemn oath or obligation.
The due form for obligation involves kneeling upon both knees, both hands covering the Holy Bible, square and compasses, with the body erect.
Giving the duegard sign (thumb to mouth).
Giving 'the sign' (dropping hand from mouth in a circular manner down over the breast to the right side).
Communicating the Past Master's grip.
Giving the Past Master's word.
Presentation of the jewel of office (a square).
Presentation of the three great lights in Masonry (Holy Bible, square and compasses).
Presentation of the charter or warrant.
Lodge closed with a specific prayer.
The lecture is delivered by the Right Worshipful Master.
Presentation of the constitution, by-laws, records, the privilege of being covered, the Bible, Square, and Compasses, the charter or warrant, and the gavel to the Master.
Master to carefully search the constitution to ensure it is not infringed.
Master to carefully search the by-laws and ensure they are strictly enforced.
Master to ensure records are carefully kept to prevent improper transmissions to paper, and to have general supervision over the secretary's duties.
Master to be covered while the rest of the brethren remain uncovered.

Ensure the Bible, Square, and Compasses are in proper position when the Lodge is open: both points of compasses beneath the square for EA degree; one point elevated above the square for FC degree; both points elevated above the square for Master's degree.
Use of the gavel: one blow to call the Lodge to order and seat members; two blows to call up subordinate officers; three blows to call up the whole Lodge.

Master's duties include opening, instructing, and closing Lodges; initiating, crafting, and raising Masons; presiding at consecrations, dedications, and installations; laying corner stones of public edifices; and presiding at funeral obsequies.
Singing of the 'Excellent Master's Song' celebrating the Temple's completion.
Placement of the keystone into an arch supported by two pillars (Jachin and Boaz), secured with six raps of a gavel by the Most Excellent Master.
Brethren moving around the arch and divesting themselves of jewels, aprons, and sashes, hanging them on the arch.
The Ark, carried by four brethren, is placed on the altar, with a pot of incense placed on top of it.
Brethren halting, facing inward to the altar, and beckoning the candidate to join the ceremonies.
Brethren throwing up hands and rolling eyes upward in a 'sign of admiration or astonishment'.
Brothers joining hands as in opening, while the Right Worshipful Master reads Scripture (2 Chron. vii. 1, 4).
The Master (high priest of the Chapter) kneeling and joining hands with the brethren during the Scripture reading.
Brethren repeating 'For he is good, for his mercy endureth forever' six times in concert, bowing their heads low.
A piece of blazing gum-camphor or other combustible matter thrown into the pot of incense on the altar by a brother.
Members balancing six times, rising, and balancing six more times, then disengaging and taking seats.
One of the brethren throws a piece of blazing gum-camphor or other combustible matter into the pot of incense on the altar.
Brethren kneel around the altar, bowing their heads low toward the floor while repeating 'for his mercy endureth forever' six times.
Members balance six times as in opening, rise, and balance six times more.
Brethren assemble around the altar in a circle, kneeling on the right knee, putting their left arms over, and joining hands.
The Master reads verses from the one hundred and thirty-fourth Psalm.
The Master closes the circle as in opening, followed by balancing six times, rising, balancing six times more, disengaging hands, and giving signs from this Degree downward.
The Lodge is closed as in preceding Degrees.
Psalm twenty-third is read at closing.
Admission to the Most Excellent Master degree is gained by six distinct knocks (• • • • • •).
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The role of the Worshipful Master symbolizes leadership, authority, and the profound responsibility of governing a Masonic Lodge.

The strict adherence to protocol, including the duegard and sign, and the mandatory salutation of the Worshipful Master upon entering, symbolizes respect for authority, order, and the sanctity of the Masonic space. The structured placement of officers and their specific attire and tools during the opening ceremony further emphasizes the organized and purposeful nature of the Lodge's work.

The act of tyling symbolizes the protection of Masonic secrets and the sanctity of the Lodge from unqualified intrusion. The Tyler's sword represents vigilance and defense against external threats, while the Junior Deacon's role emphasizes internal security and order, ensuring the integrity of the Craft.

The Lodge is symbolically prepared in a room adjacent to a legally constituted Lodge, representing the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple. The Junior Warden's station in the south symbolizes the sun at high meridian, representing the beauty and glory of the day, and his role in observing time and managing periods of labor and refreshment. The Worshipful Master's station in the east symbolizes the rising sun, indicating his role in opening the Lodge. Harmony is presented as the strength of all institutions, especially Freemasonry.

The Junior Warden's station in the south symbolizes the sun at high meridian, representing the beauty and glory of the day, and his duty to regulate the balance between labor and refreshment. The Worshipful Master's station in the east symbolizes the rising sun, signifying his role in opening and governing the Lodge, setting the craft to work, and providing instruction. The signs of each degree (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason) allude to the penalty of their respective obligations, emphasizing the solemnity and consequences of their vows.

The physical preparation, including partial nudity, hoodwink, slipper, and cable-tow, symbolizes the candidate's vulnerable state, humility, and journey from darkness (ignorance) to light (knowledge). The hoodwink specifically represents the darkness from which the candidate seeks enlightenment. The cable-tow signifies the candidate's bond and obligation to the Craft. The presentation of the point of the compasses to the naked left breast serves as a powerful symbol and a physical reminder of the severe consequences and internal 'torture' to one's conscience should Masonic secrets be unlawfully revealed. The invocation of the Deity and the candidate's declaration of trust in God underscore the spiritual foundation of Freemasonry and the necessity of faith in a Supreme Being.

The act of kneeling for prayer signifies humility and a plea for divine guidance and aid in the candidate's journey into Masonry. The declaration of trust in God establishes a foundational principle of faith and reliance on a higher power. The perambulation around the Lodge room symbolizes the candidate's initial journey and introduction to the sacred space and its officers, while the accompanying passage emphasizes the core Masonic value of unity and brotherly love. The gavel raps serve as a ritualistic acknowledgment of the candidate's passage and the officers' presence.

The compasses being hid by the square signifies the candidate's initial darkness or lack of full understanding in Masonry, having only received the Entered Apprentice degree. The three great lights (Holy Bible, square, and compasses) symbolize the rule and guide of faith and practice, the squaring of actions, and the circumscribing of passions to keep within moral bounds. The three lesser lights (sun, moon, and Master of the Lodge) represent the regular governance of the Lodge, mirroring the celestial bodies' rule over day and night. The candidate's desire for 'Light' symbolizes the pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment within Freemasonry. The removal of the hoodwink signifies the transition from ignorance to understanding, or darkness to light.

The exchange of the grip and the word "Boaz" serves as a fundamental means of recognition and confirmation of an Entered Apprentice's obligated status. The definition of signs as "right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars" symbolically represents principles of rectitude, balance, and uprightness in conduct. Tokens, as "friendly or brotherly grips," symbolize the fraternal bond and the ability to recognize fellow Masons under any circumstances.

The duegard and the sign of an Entered Apprentice symbolize the candidate's obligation and its associated penalty. The token and grip signify the commitment to conceal Masonic secrets. The word 'Boaz' serves as a password and a means to detect impostors. The lambskin or white apron is presented as an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason, described as more ancient and honorable than other prestigious orders. The metallic deposit, though of little intrinsic value, symbolizes a personal memento of the candidate's initiation into the Lodge.

The lambskin or white linen apron is an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason, signifying purity and honor. The Holy Bible is presented as the rule and guide to faith and practice, the square to square actions, and the compasses to circumscribe and keep Masons within bounds with all mankind, especially brother Masons. The three lesser lights (sun, moon, and Master of the Lodge) represent the principle of regularity in governance, as the sun rules the day and the moon governs the night, so the Worshipful Master should rule and govern the Lodge with equal regularity. Being brought from darkness to light symbolizes the candidate's transition from ignorance to Masonic knowledge.

The placement in the northeast corner symbolizes the candidate becoming an upright man and Mason. The twenty-four-inch gauge, divided into twenty-four equal parts, is emblematic of the twenty-four hours of the day. Its symbolic use teaches the division of time into three parts: for the service of God and relief of a distressed worthy brother, for usual avocations, and for refreshment and sleep, representing a balanced life of duty, labor, and rest.

The divestment of metals symbolizes not carrying offensive or defensive items into the Lodge and echoes the silent construction of King Solomon's Temple. Being neither naked nor clothed signifies that Masonry values internal, not external, qualifications. Being neither barefoot nor shod conforms to an ancient Israelitish custom, serving as a pledge of fidelity and obedience. Being hoodwinked represents the candidate's state of darkness and the need to keep Masonic secrets from the world, also acting as a safeguard. The three knocks alarm the Lodge, inform the Worshipful Master of preparedness, and symbolize asking, seeking, and knocking for admission. The sharp instrument serves as a reminder to the conscience against revealing secrets. Kneeling for prayer acknowledges the necessity of imploring Deity's blessings before important undertakings. Trust in Deity is essential for an oath to be binding, as Atheists cannot be Masons. Being taken by the right hand assures the candidate of being in the hands of a true and trusty friend, signifying fidelity and safety. Circumambulation allows brethren to see the candidate is duly prepared. Obstructions symbolize guards at King Solomon's Temple gates, ensuring proper examination and preparation. Kneeling on the left knee represents the left side as the weakest part of man, signifying entry into the foundational, 'weaker' part of Masonry (Entered Apprentice). Resting the right hand on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses signifies fidelity, echoing the ancient worship of Fides.

The lambskin apron symbolizes innocence and purity, reminding the Mason of the conduct necessary for admission into the celestial Lodge. The metallic deposit signifies the candidate's initial poor and penniless state, prompting a commitment to liberal charity towards distressed brethren. The placement in the northeast corner, where the first stone of a building is typically laid, symbolizes the beginning of the candidate's Masonic and moral edifice, where they receive their foundational instructions.

The oblong square form of a Lodge signifies the universality of Masonry and the extensive nature of a Mason's charity. The three great pillars (Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty) represent the necessary elements for all great undertakings. The Worshipful Master embodies Wisdom, the Senior Warden Strength (through harmony), and the Junior Warden Beauty (observing the sun at high meridian). The clouded canopy or starry-decked heavens symbolize the celestial destination for good Masons. The Holy Bible is dedicated to God as His inestimable gift to man. The mosaic pavement represents the world, checkered with good and evil, where brethren can walk together without stumbling. The three lights (East, West, South) and the absence of light in the North symbolize the Lodge's representation of King Solomon's Temple, situated north of the ecliptic, making the North a place of darkness. The rough ashler reminds us of our rude and imperfect natural state, while the perfect ashler signifies the state of perfection achievable through virtuous education, personal effort, and divine blessing. The trestle-board serves as a guide for both operative and speculative Masons, with the Holy Bible being the spiritual trestle-board. The square teaches morality, the level teaches equality, and the plumb teaches rectitude of life. The point within a circle, with parallel lines representing St. John the Baptist and St. John the Evangelist and the Holy Scriptures on top, symbolizes an individual brother's conduct within moral boundaries, guided by the teachings of the Saints and the Scriptures.

The concept of being 'circumscribed within their precepts' symbolizes the moral and ethical boundaries within which a Mason is expected to conduct himself, preventing deviation from Masonic principles.

The phrase 'acting upon the square' symbolizes fairness, honesty, and integrity in dealings with others. Freemasonry is presented as a 'centre of union' for mankind, symbolizing its power to bring together individuals who might otherwise remain distant. Virtue is described as 'luminous as the meridian sun,' shining refulgent on the mind, symbolizing its illuminating and enlivening effect on the human spirit and its capacity to convert mere approval into deep affection.

The Rough Ashler symbolizes man in his rude and unpolished state, while the Perfect Ashler represents man in a state of moral and intellectual refinement achieved through education and discipline. The term 'cowan' is symbolically linked to 'dogs' (κυνες) or 'evil workers' in biblical contexts, representing unqualified or profane listeners and intruders in a Masonic setting, emphasizing the need for vigilance and purity within the Lodge.

Masonry is presented as a whole, with each Mason forming an integral part. The 'emblematical feature' serves as a profound pledge of fidelity, drawing an analogy to the customs of primitive nations and suggesting an ancient, unfathomable origin.

The Mosaic pavement is accounted pure and immaculate, symbolizing that no pollution can be tolerated on that sacred floor. The word 'Hail' (from Saxon 'Hael') is used as a salutation, inquiring 'Whence do you hail?' The word 'Hale' (from Saxon 'helan') signifies to conceal, referring to the 'tye.' Light is the first demand of a candidate at initiation, with material light succeeded by intellectual illumination. The palate and throat are considered the chief seat of irregular appetites, and the first sign instructs Masons to avoid temptation through proper restraint on passions, promoting temperance, decency, and decorum.

The act of approaching the East by three upright, regular steps symbolizes the candidate's progression towards greater Masonic knowledge and enlightenment, as the East is considered the 'place of further light'. The upright posture and regular steps represent rectitude, discipline, and a deliberate, structured advancement in Masonic principles.

The deception regarding the completion of the degree symbolizes the unexpected challenges and deeper truths yet to be revealed in the Masonic journey. The Senior Warden's jewel, worn by the candidate, represents a temporary elevation or a test of his understanding of authority and responsibility. The 'rough and rugged road beset with thieves, robbers, and murderers' symbolizes the moral and spiritual dangers and trials one faces in life, requiring unwavering fidelity and trust. The act of praying for oneself emphasizes self-reliance and direct communion with the Deity. The journey around the darkened lodge, guided by the Conductor, represents a transition into the profound narrative of Hiram Abiff, symbolizing the search for light and knowledge amidst obscurity, and the emulation of a revered Grand Master's devotion and diligence.

The Lodge is referred to as a 'sacred retreat of friendship and virtue,' symbolizing a sanctuary for moral and ethical development. The 'household of the faithful' symbolically represents the wider community of Masons.

The text notes that an unspecified element denotes 'worldly possession.' It also connects Tubal Cain as an instructor of artificers in brass and iron, relating him to Vulcan, and Jubal as the inventor of music, relating him to Apollo. The sprig of acacia is mentioned as a mark left by either the murderers or recanters in the Hiramic legend.

The Hebrew covenant ceremony, involving the division of an animal, symbolized a solemn binding to a promise, with the implied curse: "Thus let it be done to him, and thus let his body be cut in two, who shall break this covenant." The acacia, used in Jewish law to mark graves for priests to avoid ritual impurity, is symbolically significant in Freemasonry. The Hiramic legend, including the exhumation of the body, is presented as an allegory rather than a literal historical fact, serving to inculcate the doctrine of resurrection and a future state. The Master Mason represents a man saved from iniquity and raised to faith in salvation, embodying the doctrine of love.

The Master Mason represents a man under the doctrine of love, saved from the grave of iniquity, and raised to the faith of salvation. The degree testifies faith in the resurrection of the body and inculcates a practical lesson of prudence and unshrinking fidelity, inspiring hope for a final reward. The legend serves to impress upon the candidate's mind the doctrine of a resurrection and a future state.

The Third Degree is interpreted as an allegory for the Christian dispensation, symbolizing the revelation of salvation, atonement for sin, and the resurrection from the dead, confirmed by Christ's resurrection. The degeneration of Jewish law and the burial of piety and reverence are symbolized by "filth and rubbish" from the Temple. The inability of ancient wisdom to restore religion is represented by those who sought to raise her in vain. The sprig of acacia waving over religion's monument symbolizes hope and immortality, anticipating the Saviour's arrival. Hiram Abiff's death is a symbol for the Messiah's death. The three blows given to Hiram at the three gates of the Temple allude to the three points of condemnation against Christ before Caiaphas, Herod, and Pilate. The three tools (gauge, square, gavel) used for the blows symbolize the blow on Christ's cheek, the flagellation, and the spear (or crown of thorns). Brethren assembled around Hiram's grave represent disciples lamenting Christ's death. The "lost Word" symbolizes the word pronounced on the cross, which the Jews could not comprehend. False brethren are represented by Judas. The sprig of cassia (acacia) represents the cross, said to be made of its wood.

The biblical quote emphasizes the candidate as a "living stone" to be built into a "spiritual house" and an "holy priesthood," highlighting the transformation from raw material to a refined, useful element in a sacred structure. The keystone, carried by the candidate, symbolizes the essential, crowning element of an arch, representing perfection and completion in the work. The square and plumb carried by the deacons represent accuracy, rectitude, and uprightness in their work.

The ritual symbolizes the importance of honest labor and the proper acquisition of wages, emphasizing that one must be duly qualified and recognized to receive the rewards of their craft. The 'impostor' scenario highlights the consequences of attempting to claim benefits or status without legitimate entitlement or skill, representing a test of integrity and knowledge. The threat to the right hand signifies the potential loss of one's ability to work and contribute, a severe penalty for deception in the craft. The intercession and subsequent instruction symbolize the mercy, guidance, and educational role of the fraternity in correcting errors and teaching proper conduct and skill. The keystone mentioned in the song represents a crucial element of the work and a symbol of recognition and fame for those who truly understand its significance.

The reprimand symbolizes the importance of diligence, skill, and integrity in one's work and the consequences of attempting to deceive or impose upon the Craft. The chisel and mallet, used by operative masons to shape stone, are symbolically employed by Free and Accepted Masons to 'hew, cut, carve, and indent the mind,' signifying the moral and intellectual refinement of the candidate. The reception upon the chisel and mallet represents the enduring impression of the Mark Master's lessons. The scripture passages about 'the stone which the builders refused' becoming 'the head stone of the corner' allude to the value of that which is initially rejected or overlooked, and its ultimate importance, a central theme of the Mark Master degree.

The four upright, regular steps forming a square symbolize precision, rectitude, and the candidate's commitment to moral and Masonic principles in their approach to sacred duties. The repeated questioning at various posts signifies the importance of consistency, truthfulness, and the candidate's unwavering affirmation of their commitment. The 'mark' itself is a profound symbol of a personal pledge, a bond of trust, and a form of security or credit within the fraternity, representing a brother's honor and commitment. Its specified value (half a shekel of silver or quarter of a dollar) gives it a tangible, albeit symbolic, economic weight. The severe penalties of having the right ear smitten off and the right hand chopped off symbolize the complete forfeiture of the ability to hear the sacred word and to perform Masonic work, representing the ultimate consequence for an impostor or oath-breaker.

The 'mark' symbolizes a pledge or a request for aid from a brother. The act of the candidate finding money unexpectedly, or being provided with it, symbolizes that resources or assistance may be available even when one believes they are destitute, and that a brother should make a diligent search before denying aid. The price of the mark (half a Jewish shekel or a quarter-dollar) represents the value of the pledge and the commitment to the obligation. The pass-grip symbolizes mutual assistance and support, particularly in overcoming difficulties or 'steep ascents'.

The keystone, initially rejected by the builders for its unconventional shape and lack of a recognizable mark, ultimately becomes the 'head stone of the corner,' symbolizing that true value and importance may be overlooked or misunderstood at first. The scriptural passages reinforce this theme of vindication for the rejected. The 'new name' and the 'mark' on the keystone, along with the reference to the 'white stone' from Revelation, symbolize a unique identity, recognition, and a deeper, personal understanding or achievement within the Mark Master degree, signifying a special reward for those who overcome.

The first sign, with the thumb on the lips, alludes to the penalty of having the tongue split from tip to root. The second sign, drawing the hand across the neck, signifies crossing former penalties. The act of raising King Solomon by Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff using the Past Master's grip symbolizes support and assistance in leadership. The Holy Writings are presented as a guide to truth, happiness, and the whole duty of man. The Square teaches to regulate actions by morality and virtue. The Compasses instruct to limit desires, leading to eminence by merit and a respected life and death. The Rule directs punctual observance of duty, pursuit of virtue, and having eternity in view. The Line teaches moral rectitude, avoiding dissimulation, and directing steps towards immortality. The Mallet is an emblem of power, used to maintain order and call the Lodge to attention. The phrases 'A threefold cord is strong' and 'A fourfold cord is not easily broken' symbolize strength, unity, and resilience.

The chaotic scene symbolizes the challenges and tests a leader, particularly a Master, must face in maintaining order, upholding principles, and exercising authority within the Lodge. It tests the candidate's composure, knowledge of Masonic law, and ability to govern amidst disorder.

The apparent confusion and the candidate's embarrassment during the mock lodge proceedings are designed to convey a striking lesson about the importance of not soliciting or accepting any office or station for which one is not amply qualified.

The duegard sign alludes to the penalty of the obligation, signifying a preference for the tongue to cleave to the roof of the mouth rather than unlawfully divulging secrets. 'The sign' alludes to an additional penalty, indicating a willingness to suffer former penalties rather than unlawfully divulging secrets. The word 'Stone Squarer' signifies the role. The jewel of office, a square, symbolizes the hope that the recipient would prepare a 'square stone in the Temple of Masonry.' The Holy Bible within the three great lights is presented as necessary for counsel and guidance. The varying positions of the compasses relative to the square symbolize the different degrees: both points beneath the square for EA, one point elevated for FC, and both points elevated for Master Mason.

The Bible, Square, and Compasses are presented as the three great lights, providing counsel and guidance. The gavel is described as an emblem of power. The Master's chair is symbolically linked to Grand Master Solomon, with the hope that a portion of his wisdom will rest upon the Master.

The completion of the Temple signifies the culmination of labor and the achievement of a significant spiritual or architectural goal. The cope-stone and keystone represent the final, essential elements that bind the structure together, symbolizing perfection and stability. The divestment of working tools indicates that the physical labor is complete, and the focus shifts to the spiritual and intellectual. The Ark being safely seated on the altar with incense symbolizes the divine presence and acceptance of the completed work. The sign of admiration and the prayer for divine glory emphasize the sacredness of the institution and the aspiration for divine guidance and inspiration. The fire from heaven and the glory of the Lord filling the house signify divine approval and the sacredness of the consecrated space.

The cap stone symbolizes the completion of King Solomon's Temple, upon the dedication ceremonies of which this degree is founded. The six distinct knocks allude to the Sixth Degree of Masonry.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The text highlights the high standards and significant responsibilities expected of the Worshipful Master, implicitly teaching all members about the importance of duty, leadership, and the proper governance within the fraternity.

A candidate learns the critical importance of proper decorum, respect for the Master and the Lodge, and strict adherence to established ritualistic procedures, including the correct use of signs and duegards. The anecdote about the Master Mason's error highlights the need for attentiveness to the degree on which the Lodge is opened. The process of admitting visitors underscores the fraternal bonds and the system of recognition and trust within Masonry.

The importance of secrecy, vigilance, and the proper order and security within a Masonic Lodge. It teaches that the integrity of the Craft depends on preventing unauthorized access and maintaining the sanctity of its proceedings.

The candidate learns that their obligation is what makes them an Entered Apprentice Mason. They are taught the importance of temperance and avoiding excess during periods of refreshment. The concept of harmony as the strength of the institution is also emphasized.

Candidates are taught the importance of temperance and moderation during refreshment, the value of harmony and satisfaction among brethren, and the necessity of proper conduct within the Lodge. The performance of the duegards and signs, which allude to the penalties of the obligations, serves as a constant reminder of the seriousness of their vows and the duties they have undertaken.

The process emphasizes the importance of a candidate's good character and reputation within society, as well as the thoroughness with which a lodge investigates potential members. It demonstrates that entry into Freemasonry is not taken lightly and requires the approval of the brethren based on a careful assessment of one's moral standing.

The candidate is taught the importance of sincere and honorable motivations for seeking admission, including a desire for knowledge and a commitment to serving fellow creatures. The ritualistic reception, particularly the point of the compasses, impresses upon the candidate the solemnity of the obligation and the grave consequences of violating Masonic trust and secrecy. The prayer and the declaration of trust in God instill the fundamental lesson of reliance on a divine power and the moral principles of brotherly love, relief, and truth that are central to the Masonic way of life.

The candidate learns the importance of seeking divine aid and dedicating their life to service. They are taught that their faith in God is a well-founded trust. The perambulation and accompanying passage instill the lesson of unity and brotherly love as essential tenets of Freemasonry, encouraging them to become a true and faithful brother.

The candidate learns the profound importance of secrecy and fidelity to the Masonic obligation, understanding the severe consequences of its violation. They are introduced to fundamental Masonic symbols, such as the great and lesser lights and working tools, and their moral applications. The ritual emphasizes the pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment, symbolized by the request for 'Light,' and the concept of being bound by a 'stronger tie' than physical restraints, referring to the moral commitment of the obligation. The candidate also learns about brotherly love and favor through the Master's actions.

The candidate learns the specific grip, the method of revealing the word "Boaz," the duegard, and the definitions of Masonic signs and tokens. These elements are crucial for demonstrating their obligated status and for mutual recognition within the fraternity.

The candidate learns the solemnity of the Entered Apprentice obligation and the importance of maintaining secrecy regarding Masonic knowledge. They are taught the significance of the lambskin apron as a symbol of purity and honor within the fraternity. The ritual also imparts the tradition of contributing a personal item as a lasting reminder of their initiation.

The candidate is taught the importance of secrecy regarding Masonic knowledge and the solemnity of the Entered Apprentice obligation. Trust in God is emphasized as a well-founded faith. The candidate learns the moral application of the working tools: to square their actions and to circumscribe their passions and desires, keeping them within due bounds. The apron teaches the value of innocence and purity, and the overall ritual emphasizes the importance of free will, worthiness, and good repute for admission into the fraternity.

The candidate is strictly charged to ever walk and act as an upright man and Mason. He is taught the importance of wisely dividing his time to fulfill his duties to God and his brethren, attend to his daily work, and allow for necessary rest.

Candidates learn that internal qualifications are paramount over worldly wealth or honors. They pledge fidelity and obedience to the ancient institution and are taught the importance of secrecy in Free-Masonry. The principle of 'Ask, seek, knock' is applied to their journey into Masonry. A solemn warning is given against revealing Masonic secrets. The necessity of invoking divine blessings for all significant endeavors and the fundamental requirement of belief in a Deity for Masonic obligations to be binding are emphasized. Candidates are assured of trust in brethren and the guidance of a true friend. The Entered Apprentice degree is presented as the foundational, 'weaker' part of Masonry, requiring humility and learning, with the right hand symbolizing fidelity and commitment.

Candidates are taught to maintain purity of life and conduct, to practice liberal charity towards those in need, and to build their future Masonic and moral character upon upright principles, acting honorably before God and man.

A Mason's charity should be as extensive as the vast dimensions of the Lodge. Candidates are reminded of their imperfect natural state and encouraged to strive for perfection through virtuous education, personal endeavors, and divine blessing. They should endeavor to erect their spiritual building according to the divine rules found in the Holy Scriptures. Masons are taught to embody morality, equality, and rectitude of life. A brother must learn to control his prejudices and passions, keeping his conduct within the moral boundary represented by the circle, guided by the examples of St. John the Baptist and St. John the Evangelist and the Holy Scriptures. Brethren are taught to navigate the world's challenges (good and evil) together without stumbling.

The primary lesson is the importance of adhering to Masonic precepts to maintain moral rectitude and avoid error. It also highlights the depth and challenge of fully understanding and memorizing the degree lectures, suggesting a commitment to continuous learning.

The candidate is taught the foundational moral and ethical principles of Freemasonry, encompassing duties to God, humanity, and self. They learn the importance of reverence, integrity, temperance, and civic responsibility. The lesson emphasizes balancing Masonic commitments with personal and professional life, maintaining the secrecy of the Order, and exercising discernment when recommending new members. For clergymen, it highlights the profound alignment between Masonic tenets and the virtues of universal benevolence and charity, encouraging a life of virtue and continuous self-improvement.

Candidates are taught the importance of continuous moral and intellectual self-improvement, striving to transform from a 'Rough Ashler' to a 'Perfect Ashler'. They learn the necessity of upholding their duties and obligations within the Lodge and the critical importance of guarding the sanctity and privacy of Masonic proceedings from the profane and unqualified.

Candidates are taught that Masonry is a serious and cohesive institution requiring adherence to its forms and procedures. They must understand the importance of officer duties and the solemnity of Lodge operations. Furthermore, they learn that certain 'emblematical features' are mandatory and indispensable conditions for being a Mason, serving as a pledge of fidelity, and must be endured with patience, regardless of personal feelings.

Admission into the fraternity is based on a sincere belief and trust in God, and where God's name is invoked, no danger can ensue. It is unwise to assert more than can be proven or argue against probability regarding historical claims. The story of Hipparchus serves as a severe caution against violating engagements, emphasizing the importance of truth and fidelity, and the dire consequences of their breach. Candidates are taught that the pursuit of 'Light' extends beyond the material to intellectual understanding. The first sign teaches the importance of temperance in all indulgences, self-restraint, and never exceeding the boundary of decency and decorum, under penalty of violating Masonic engagements.

The candidate learns the precise ritualistic method of approaching the East, which signifies their journey towards understanding and their commitment to the established forms and traditions of Freemasonry. It emphasizes the importance of order, precision, and a respectful demeanor in the pursuit of Masonic light.

The candidate learns that the Masonic journey is not easily completed and requires profound fidelity and endurance. He is taught to trust in a higher power and to rely on his own spiritual strength, as he must pray for himself in the face of impending trials. The introduction to Hiram Abiff's daily devotions and diligent work instills the importance of consistent dedication, moral rectitude, and reverence for the Deity, setting an example for the candidate's own conduct. The experience also teaches humility, as his presumed status as a Master Mason is challenged, and he is reminded of the 'more valuable secrets' yet to be entrusted.

Masons are charged to carry the virtues and principles learned within the Lodge—such as diligence, prudence, temperance, discretion, friendship, and benevolence—into their daily lives and interactions with the world. They are reminded of their promise to befriend and relieve fellow brethren, to gently correct their errors, and to extend kindness to all human beings, especially those within the Masonic fraternity.

To be a 'bright Mason,' one must have knowledge of the alterations made in Masonry since Morgan's exposure. The critical notes on the Third Degree's legendary nature (hyperbolical, apocryphal, fabulous) encourage a deeper, allegorical understanding rather than a literal interpretation of its traditions and history. The mention of 'worldly possession' as a symbolic denotation may serve as a lesson against materialism.

The self-imposed penalties within Freemasonry may be understood in the context of ancient Hebrew covenant practices, emphasizing the profound solemnity and consequences of breaking sacred promises. The Master Mason degree teaches the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, encouraging candidates to contemplate mortality, strive for ethical maturity, and embrace faith in salvation. The narrative of the Temple builders highlights the importance of fidelity and the severe repercussions of treachery.

The Master Mason degree teaches the doctrine of a resurrection and a future state, emphasizing prudence and unshrinking fidelity. It inspires hope for a final reward for those who are 'just made perfect' and symbolizes being saved from iniquity and raised to the faith of salvation.

Candidates are taught the ancient and proper mode of being raised to the Master Mason Degree, understanding its historical traditions and the exemplification of Masonic virtue. They learn the meaning of the degree's emblems and the description of Solomon's Temple. The solemnity of lodge closing reinforces the "chief care of a Mason" and the importance of continuous Masonic instruction and improvement. Masons are reminded of their duties as men and Masons in their daily avocations and the importance of piety. The allegorical interpretation of the Hiramic legend teaches profound lessons about spiritual death and resurrection, the restoration of truth and morality, and fidelity in the face of adversity, drawing parallels to Christian themes of salvation and immortality. The distinction between literal history and allegorical truth is emphasized, guiding the candidate to seek deeper symbolic meaning.

The candidate is taught to be a "living stone," chosen and precious, contributing to a "spiritual house" through "well-doing." They are encouraged to act as free servants of God, honoring all men, loving the brotherhood, and fearing God, putting to silence the ignorance of foolish men. The act of carrying the keystone and presenting work for inspection teaches the importance of diligent, accurate, and upright labor, and the necessity of having one's work proven worthy.

The candidate learns the critical importance of integrity, honesty, and proper qualification in all endeavors, especially concerning work and reward. It teaches that one must earn their wages through approved work and legitimate means, and that attempting to deceive or claim what is not due will lead to severe consequences. The lesson also emphasizes the value of humility, acknowledging one's inexperience, and being open to instruction from more experienced brethren to improve one's craft and conduct.

The candidate is taught the critical importance of performing work with skill and industry, and the ethical principle of not claiming rewards or wages that are not rightfully earned. The reprimand serves as a 'striking lesson' against imposition upon the Craft. The symbolic use of the chisel and mallet emphasizes the continuous process of self-improvement and the shaping of one's character and mind. The scripture readings impart a lesson on the potential for the rejected to become essential, encouraging perseverance and recognizing inherent worth.

The candidate learns the paramount importance of strict secrecy regarding the Mark Master Mason's secrets, disclosing them only to duly qualified brethren after rigorous examination. They are taught the duty to uphold the Constitutions of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter and State Grand Royal Arch Chapter, as well as the by-laws of any Mark Master Lodge. The obligation instills obedience to all regular signs and summonses from fellow Mark Master Masons or Lodges, within the bounds of their cable-tow. A strong ethical lesson is imparted regarding not wronging a brother or the Lodge, even to the smallest value, and preventing such wrongs if possible. The unique rules surrounding the 'mark' teach about personal integrity, the sanctity of a pledge, and the responsibility to honor commitments related to one's mark, including its redemption. The lesson of mutual aid is emphasized through the promise to receive a brother's mark when a favor is requested and to grant it if possible, or to return its specified value if the favor cannot be granted.

The primary lesson for the candidate is the importance of diligently searching for means to assist a worthy brother before denying a request. It teaches that one should not hastily refuse aid, as unforeseen resources or assistance from others might be available. It also reinforces the solemnity of the obligation taken, particularly concerning the duties associated with a brother's mark.

The candidate is taught the importance of discerning true worth and recognizing value in things that may initially appear unconventional or rejected. The narrative of the keystone emphasizes that what is deemed unfit by some may be essential and invaluable. The investment with a 'new name' signifies a personal transformation and a unique identity within the Craft, highlighting individual merit and the reward for perseverance. The admonition regarding the grips instills the importance of fidelity and the proper transmission of Masonic knowledge.

The candidate learns the specific signs, duegard, and grip of a Past Master Mason, including their associated penalties and meanings. They are instructed in the duties and responsibilities of a Master, symbolized by the various Masonic implements. The candidate is charged to be guided by the Holy Writings, regulate actions by the Square, limit desires by the Compasses, observe duty by the Rule, maintain moral rectitude by the Line, and diligently enforce the Book of Constitutions and Lodge By-laws. They are also taught the use of the Mallet as an emblem of power for maintaining order within the Lodge and are entrusted with the government of the Lodge.

The candidate learns the importance of maintaining order, exercising authority with discretion, and strictly adhering to the established laws and Constitutions of Freemasonry, even when faced with pressure and deliberate disruption. It teaches the Master's responsibility to uphold the Craft's regulations and manage the brethren effectively.

The primary lesson for the candidate is the necessity of at all times abstaining from soliciting or accepting any office or station that one does not know oneself amply qualified to fill.

The candidate learns the solemn obligation to keep and conceal the secrets belonging to the chair, understanding the severe penalties for unlawful disclosure. They are taught the importance of the Holy Bible for counsel and guidance and are encouraged to prepare a 'square stone in the Temple of Masonry.' The distinction between 'Actual' and 'Virtual' Past Masters teaches the specific rights and limitations associated with each status, particularly regarding privileges within a symbolic Lodge.

The Master is entrusted with significant authority and a wide array of responsibilities, including upholding the foundational documents of the Lodge, maintaining accurate records, ensuring proper ritualistic display of the Great Lights, and presiding over all essential Masonic work and ceremonies. The role demands wisdom, diligence, and adherence to Masonic principles, echoing the wisdom of Grand Master Solomon.

The candidate's admission to the Most Excellent Master degree is a recognition of their Masonic abilities and an encouragement to maintain that esteem through good conduct and adherence to rules. A primary duty of a Most Excellent Master is to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons, which necessitates an accurate and comprehensive acquaintance with the lectures of all degrees. The candidate is urged to apply double diligence to acquire knowledge and information to properly discharge their duties and preserve the unsullied title of Most Excellent Master.

The candidate's admission is a testament to the brethren's good opinion of their Masonic abilities, which should not be forfeited by misconduct. As a Most Excellent Master, the candidate has a great duty to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons, requiring an accurate acquaintance with the lectures of each degree. Diligence in acquiring knowledge is essential to properly discharge duties and preserve the title of Most Excellent Master.
tradition_notes: This describes the traditional and fundamental role and responsibilities of the Worshipful Master within a Masonic Lodge.
In large cities, the presence of numerous lodges leads to frequent visiting among brethren.
The tradition of visitors not personally known to the Master or brethren being vouched for or undergoing an examination.
The established practice of officers investing themselves with specific attire (yokes, aprons) and taking their designated stations for the formal opening of the Lodge.

The dialogue and actions described are part of the traditional opening ceremony of a Masonic Lodge, emphasizing the historical roles of the Tyler and Junior Deacon in maintaining the integrity and privacy of the Craft.
The number of raps given by the Worshipful Master varies by degree: one for Entered Apprentice, two for Fellow Craft, and three for Master Mason.
The Lodge is symbolically represented as the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple.
The traditional response to 'Are you an Entered Apprentice Mason?' is 'I am so taken and accepted among all brothers and fellows.'
The stations of the officers in the Lodge (East, West, South) are symbolically aligned with the course of the sun.
The Master always leads in giving the sign or signs during the opening ceremony.
The duegard represents the position of the hands when taking the oath of a degree.
The institution is referred to as an 'ancient institution'.
It is not uncommon for discussions to lead to a delay in balloting for a candidate if more thorough inquiry is deemed necessary.
The process of balloting for candidates, including the opportunity for discussion and the use of a specific ballot-box, is a traditional Masonic procedure for admission to the First Degree.
The specific sequence of officers examining the ballot box (Junior Warden, Senior Warden, Worshipful Master) is a traditional procedural element.
The use of white and black balls for voting is a traditional method of secret ballot in Freemasonry.
The requirement for each Mason to make the sign of a Master Mason to the Master before voting is a traditional ritualistic detail.
This section outlines a traditional procedural order for handling candidates and conferring degrees within a Masonic lodge.
The text notes that the Worshipful Master sometimes addresses the candidate as 'Stranger!' instead of by name during the declaration of trust in God.
The Junior Deacon 'always responds for the candidate' during the initial questioning at the door.
The phrase 'as all brothers and fellows have done before' emphasizes the continuity of Masonic tradition.
Masters sometimes use 'Stranger!' instead of the candidate's name when asking about trust.
The passage 'Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!' is noted as being from 'opening, or Monitor's.'
Some Masters use the phrase 'As we now hold the brother by a stronger tie' when the cable-tow is removed, indicating a symbolic shift from physical restraint to moral commitment.
The text notes that the old way of spelling the word "Boaz," as represented by Morgan, Craft, Allyn, Richardson, and Barnard, was by syllabling it.
Lodges differ in their post-obligation procedure: some pass the candidate once around the room, while others require a halt at the Wardens' stations for a specific ceremony.
The lambskin apron is described as 'more ancient than the Golden Fleece or Roman Eagle' and 'more honorable than the Star and Garter'.
Entered Apprentices wore their aprons with the flap turned up 'at the building of King Solomon's Temple'.
The request for a metallic deposit is an 'ancient custom, adopted among Masons'.
The lambskin or white linen apron is described as 'more ancient than the Golden Fleece or Roman Eagle; more honorable than the Star or Garter, or any other order'.
The request to deposit something of a metallic kind is noted as 'agreeably to an ancient custom, adopted in every regulated and well-governed Lodge'.
The construction of King Solomon's Temple without the sound of iron tools, using pre-prepared stones and wood, and wooden mauls.
The ancient Israelitish custom of changing and redeeming, as read in the Book of Ruth, involving the plucking off of a shoe as testimony.
The concept of guards placed at the south, west, and east gates of the courts of King Solomon's Temple to control passage.
The ancient brethren's belief that the right hand was the seat of fidelity, and their worship of Deity under the name of Fides, represented by joined right hands or two human figures holding hands.
The lamb has been universally regarded as an emblem of innocence throughout history.
The first stone of a building is traditionally laid in the northeast corner.
Ancient brethren usually met on a high hill or in a low valley.
Lodges were dedicated to King Solomon in ancient times, as he was considered the most ancient Grand Master or founder of the present system.
In modern times, Lodges are dedicated to St. John the Baptist and St. John the Evangelist, who were eminent Christian patrons of Masonry.
The situation of a Lodge due east and west commemorates Moses' erection of a tabernacle to God after crossing the Red Sea, which was an exact model of Solomon's Temple.
The existence of detailed lectures for the Entered Apprentice Degree is a traditional element of Masonic instruction.
There is an implied expectation for Masons to be knowledgeable in these lectures, though the text notes this is rarely achieved perfectly.
Freemasonry is described as an 'ancient and honorable order,' existing from time immemorial.
In most foreign Lodges, the Master wears his hat while brethren remain uncovered, a practice exemplified by MacKenzie Beverly Esq. as D. P. G. M. for the East Riding of York.

The term 'cowan' is historically linked to Jephthah and Ephraimites, and etymologically to the Egyptian 'cohen' (priest/prince/dog), with biblical references from St. John and St. Paul equating 'dogs' with 'cowans' or 'listeners' (profane intruders).
In most foreign Lodges, the Master wears his hat while the rest of the brethren remain uncovered, a practice historically followed by figures like MacKenzie Beverly Esq.
The term 'cowan' originated from the affair of Jephthah, where an Ephraimite was termed a cowan.
Historically, 'cohen' in Egypt was a title of honor for a priest or prince, but also meant 'dog,' leading to symbolic associations with 'dogs of Jove' and later, unqualified intruders in Masonry.
Opening the Lodge is described as a 'solemn and imposing rite.'
The 'emblematical feature' bears a 'beautiful analogy to the customs of all primitive nations' and its origin 'may be reasonably ascribed to some unfathomable antiquity.'

The reverence indicated by putting off the covering of the feet is still prevalent in the East among Christians, Moslems, or pagans, where it is considered highly irreverent to enter a sacred place with feet covered.
Masonic traditions do not support the belief that figures like Adam, Nimrod, Moses, Joshua, David, Solomon, Hiram, St. John the Evangelist, or St. John the Baptist belonged to the Masonic Order.
The practice of calling the craft from labor to refreshment at 'High twelve' is a traditional Masonic procedure.
The use of columns by the Junior and Senior Wardens to signify the Lodge's state (labor or refreshment) is a traditional visual cue.
The custom of dressing the candidate with a rich apron and yoke in some Lodges.
The specific tying of the apron with the right-hand corner tucked up for a Master Mason.
The use of columns by the Wardens to signify refreshment or labor.

The Conductor's narrative introduces Hiram Abiff's daily custom of entering the Sanctum Sanctorum at high twelve for drawing designs and offering devotions, then retiring at the south gate of the Temple. This is explicitly noted as the first time the candidate hears about Hiram Abiff.
The 'Charge at Closing a Lodge' is a traditional ritualistic element.
The terms 'Blue Degrees' and 'Blue Lodge' are traditional designations for the first three degrees of Masonry.
It is a tradition for each Grand Lodge to have its own specific 'work' which is taught to subordinate Lodges annually by its Grand Lecturer.
The 'work' and lectures taught by Barney are prevalent in the Northwestern States (Michigan, Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, Iowa) and were adopted by the Baltimore Convention.
Brother John Barney obtained the Preston Lectures from Gleason in Boston, which were approved by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts in 1817.
Barney adapted his lectures to different systems prevailing in the Western and Southwestern States, gaining sanction from respective Grand Masters.
The 'Webb Work,' inculcated by Robert Morris, is generally accepted as being of ancient origin and is expected to be adopted by Grand Lodges throughout the United States.

The 'fatal weapons' in the Third Degree have undergone several changes: from 'setting-tool, setting maul, and setting-beetle' (1717) to 'twenty-four-inch gauge, square, and gavel', then 'setting-tool, square, and rule', and currently 'plumb-rule, square, and strong or heavy maul'.
There are variations in the Hiramic legend regarding the number of Fellow Crafts who went in search (fifteen vs. twelve) and whether the sprig of acacia was left by the murderers or recanters.
There are variations in the reported location where the lost body was found: near the seaside (York Masons), Mount Moriah (Americans), Monet Lebanon (French), or near Jerusalem (others).
Accounts differ regarding the brethren's dispersal during the search: some say they dispersed widely (east, west, and south), while others state they kept within hail of each other.

Alternative names for the ruffians are preserved in subsidiary degrees, including GRAVELOT, QUIBO, and AKIROP, and in the Degree of Elect of Fifteen, they are named JUBELA-KURMAVIL (a corruption of Cromwell), JUBELO-GRAVELOT, and JUBELUM-AKIROP.
There are differing traditions on who advised the change to the substitute word: some say King Solomon, while others affirm the three Fellow Crafts adopted it without consulting him.
The Hiramic legend is described as a 'fabulous narrative' used to promote the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, similar to 'spurious initiations'.
Traditions mention instances of treachery, violation of sacred pledges, and crime among Apprentices and Craftsmen during the Temple's construction.
The connection of the 'widow's son' (Hiram Abiff) with the building of the Temple was endeared to the two monarchs, leading to a new arrangement of discipline and a legend to preserve his memory.
It was customary for Hiram Abiff to retire to the Most Holy Place at high twelve to draw plans and offer orisons.
Some rituals taught that Hiram Abiff divided operatives into Apprentices, Fellow Crafts, and Masters, paying them at the pillar of Jachin and in the Middle Chamber.
Traditions state that the Ark of the Covenant had not been removed to the Holy of Holies at the time Hiram supposedly entered it, as this occurred at the dedication.
The description of "the three principal entrances to the Temple" has evolved over time, from "east, west and south doors" to "north, south, and east entrances" by the Union in 1813.
Different Masonic jurisdictions (United States, Scotland, France) have varying orders for the "attacks" or "entrances" in the ritual.
Adonhiramite Masonry, used in France around 1787, did not mention the doors at all.

The Christian allegorical interpretation of the Hiramic legend is explicitly stated to be "borne out in the higher Degrees of sublime Masonry," specifically the 32nd Degree (Prince of the Royal Secret) in Continental nomenclature.

The historical accuracy of Hiram Abiff's death during the Temple's construction is challenged by scriptural references (1 Kings vii. 40, 2 Chron. iv. 11) which indicate he was alive at the Temple's completion.
No business other than candidate initiation is done in a Lodge of Mark Master Masons.
All other business, such as balloting for candidates and committee reports, is done in the Seventh, or Royal Arch Degree.
The Mark Master Degree is under the sanction of the Royal Arch Chapter.
The Lodge is declared opened in "due and ancient form."
The severe reprimand ordered to be given to the candidate is omitted in most Lodges at the present day, but is inserted in this text for the satisfaction of young Masons and the curious.
The severe reprimand ordered to be given to the candidate is omitted in most Lodges at the present day.
The Lodge is erected to Almighty God and dedicated to Hiram the Builder.
The obligation is stated to be 'in addition to my former obligations,' indicating a progressive system of degrees.

The obligation explicitly mentions supporting the 'Constitution of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America' and the 'Grand Royal Arch Chapter of this State,' placing the Mark Master degree within the American Royal Arch system.
The 'mark' chosen by a brother must be recorded in a 'book of marks.'
The value of a brother's mark, when a favor cannot be granted, is specified as 'half a shekel of silver, or quarter of a dollar,' linking ancient and modern monetary equivalents.

The text notes a change in ritual practice: 'The above is a true description of the manner in which the candidate was formerly taught his duty as a Mark Master Mason. In these latter days, however, very few Masters countenance this method of instruction, and it is therefore almost entirely discarded. The plan now generally adopted is as follows:--'
The pass-grip is said to have originated from the fact that the banks of the river at a certain location required assistance to ascend.
Some Lodges do not require all brethren to rise after the Right Worshipful Master gives four raps, choosing instead to keep their seats.

The ritual references King Solomon, when old and decrepit, being attended by his two friends, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff, who raised and seated him in his chair by means of the Past Master's grip.
The candidate is conducted into the 'oriental chair of King Solomon'.
The nomination and election of a Treasurer by members present is described as customary.
The 'scene of confusion' is a traditional test designed to challenge the newly installed Worshipful Master.
The ceremonies for conferring the Past Master Degree are frequently shortened by omitting parts, such as presenting implements and their explanations, and often the charge.
Historically, Royal Arch Masonry was governed by symbolic Lodges, and a regulation required candidates to have presided as Master of a Lodge to receive the Royal Arch Degree.

To extend the Royal Arch Degree, Grand Masters often granted dispensations to Master Masons to 'pass the chair,' a brief ceremony investing them with Past Master mysteries, allowing them to advance to Royal Arch.
When control of the Royal Arch shifted to Chapters, the regulation continued to be observed.
The method of initiation for the Past Master Degree has become much abridged, often involving a ruse within a Mark Masters Lodge.
The candidate is usually relieved from embarrassment in good season by the retired Master, rather than being treated as badly as some representations suggest.
The law historically required the Royal Arch Degree to be restricted to Past Masters, leading to the practice of candidates passing the chair as a preparation.

A distinction is made between 'Actual Past Masters' (those elected and installed to preside over a symbolic Lodge) and 'Virtual Past Masters' (those who pass through the ceremony in a Chapter as a prerequisite for the Royal Arch).
The tradition of the Master being covered while the brethren remain uncovered.
The specific traditional positions of the Bible, Square, and Compasses for each of the three degrees (EA, FC, MM).
The traditional use of the gavel with specific numbers of blows for different commands in the Lodge.
The traditional association of the Master's chair with Grand Master Solomon.
The pillars Jachin and Boaz are referenced (see pp. 71 and 83).
The sign of admiration or astonishment is compared to that expressed by the Queen of Sheba upon first viewing Solomon's Temple (see p. 203).
A passage of Scripture from 2 Chronicles vii. 1, 4 is read by the Right Worshipful Master, describing the fire from heaven and the glory of the Lord filling the Temple after Solomon's prayer.
The Master is identified as the 'high priest of the Chapter'.
The ceremony involves specific readings from Psalms 134 and 23.
The degree is founded upon the ceremonies of the dedication of King Solomon's Temple.
caution_notes: No brother is allowed to take his seat until he has saluted the Worshipful Master on entering a Lodge; omission results in immediate reminder.
The Tyler and Junior Deacon caution brethren about giving the correct sign before/upon entering the Lodge.
It is a common error among Masons to carelessly give the duegard and sign of a higher degree when the Lodge is actually open on a lower one.
The Tyler's duty is to keep off all cowans and eavesdroppers.
The Junior Warden must ensure that the means of refreshment are not converted into intemperance or excess.
Means of refreshment should not be converted into intemperance or excess.
Private committees and improper, unmasonic conduct are strictly forbidden during Lodge business, under penalty determined by a majority of brethren acting under by-laws.
Duegards and signs must be made with right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars, with very slight, but marked pauses between each motion or part of the sign.
When making the duegard and sign of the Fellow Craft, the left arm must be dropped suddenly and with spirit as soon as the two motions are accomplished.
Lengthy discussions can arise regarding a candidate's early history or character, potentially delaying the ballot until a subsequent meeting.

The point of a sharp instrument pressing the naked left breast is to teach the candidate that 'as it is a torture to your flesh, so should the recollection of it ever be to your mind and conscience, should you attempt to reveal the secrets of Masonry unlawfully.'

The practice of brethren stamping their feet and clapping their hands once at the word 'light' is noted as 'nearly done away with now-a-days' due to being 'Too much "Morganry" about it, as it is styled by Masons.'
The spelling of 'Boaz' (always beginning with 'A') is noted as a way Masons detect impostors, such as 'Morgan or book Masons'.
The candidate is obligated to conceal 'All the secrets of Masons, in Masons'.
The cable-tow signifies that the candidate might be led out of the Lodge if they do not submit to the initiation manner and mode.
The sharp instrument serves as a reminder of torture to the conscience should secrets of Free-Masonry ever be revealed.
No Atheist could be made a Mason, as trust in Deity is necessary for an oath to be binding among Masons.
The need to observe the approach of cowans or eaves-droppers.
A brother is cautioned never to suffer his prejudices or passions to betray him beyond the boundary-line of his conduct.
Never mention God's name but with reverential awe.
Avoid all irregularity and intemperance.
Do not countenance disloyalty or rebellion.
Be particularly careful to avoid censure or reproach in outward demeanor.
Do not let interest, favor, or prejudice bias your integrity or lead to dishonorable actions.
Masonry should not interfere with necessary avocations; these are on no account to be neglected.
Do not suffer zeal for the institution to lead into arguments with those who ridicule it through ignorance.
Keep sacred and inviolable the mysteries of the Order.
Be particularly careful not to recommend a person for initiation unless convinced they will conform to our rules.
St. John cautions Christian brethren that 'without are dogs' (κυνες), referring to cowans or listeners (Rev. xxii. 15).
St. Paul exhorts Christians to 'beware of dogs, because they are evil workers' (Phil. W. 2).
Necessary precautions are employed during the Lodge Opening to avoid the intrusion of the profane.
The text implies a caution against resisting the 'emblematical feature,' stating that a Mason 'has no option' and 'cannot avoid it,' even if they 'may not like it' or 'think it degrading.'
It is unwise to assert more than can be proven and to argue against probability regarding historical figures being Freemasons.

The story of Hipparchus illustrates the terrible consequences of violating engagements, leading to expulsion, social death, and denial of burial rites, serving as a warning against breaches of truth and fidelity.
Disobedience or violation of Masonic engagements, particularly regarding temperance and decorum, carries a penalty.

The Worshipful Master explicitly warns the candidate of 'amazing trials and dangers', a 'rough and rugged road to travel, beset with thieves, robbers, and murderers', and the possibility of losing his life, stating it 'will not be the first instance of the kind'.
Brethren are cautioned not to forget the duties inculcated in the Lodge amidst the 'concerns and temptations' of the world.

It is noted that the 'work' and lectures in the several degrees of Masonry, especially the first three (Blue Degrees), differ significantly in each State, making it difficult to get exact proper responses to Masonic interrogatories.

The 'work' in Minnesota and New York is 'strongly impregnated with what is called, among Masons, "Morganry"--very similar to the disclosures of Morgan and Richardson,' implying a caution regarding unauthorized disclosures.
The Third Degree's traditions are 'unfortunately hyperbolical; its history apocryphal, and its legends fabulous,' cautioning against literal interpretations.
Professor Stuart endeavored to show that the legend of the Third Degree is an 'imposture' due to the Latin origin of the criminals' names, not Hebrew.
The text notes that the uniformity of Freemasonry on certain subjects is a 'delicate point' and difficult to clarify for brethren, implying a need for discretion regarding ritual variations.
CASSIA is sometimes improperly and ignorantly used for acacia.
The interpolated word (substitute for the lost word) is not a real word in any language, but merely a jumble of letters forming a sound without meaning.

Masons should not literally believe Dr. Anderson's explanation of the exhumation of H.A.B. as a real historical fact, as Hiram Abiff was known to be alive years after the Temple's completion, indicating the story is allegorical.

The historical veracity of the Hiramic legend is questioned, with references to Dr. Anderson and Josephus stating Hiram Abiff lived to complete the Temple and died in Tyre, contradicting the ritual narrative.

Inconsistencies in ritual narratives are highlighted, such as Hiram Abiff's supposed entry into the Holy of Holies before its completion or accessibility, and the division of operatives and payment of wages before the relevant architectural features (pillars, Middle Chamber) were built.
