A divergence from uniformity is noted in describing the places where the instruments of Hiram's murder were supposedly used.

The author explicitly states that the tradition of Hiram's death during the Temple's construction is "merely allegorical" and not historically factual, citing scriptural evidence (1 Kings vii. 40, 2 Chron. iv. 11) that Hiram was alive at the Temple's completion. This serves as a caution against a literal interpretation of the legend.
The Right Worshipful Master forbids "all improper conduct whereby this Lodge may be disturbed, under no less penalty than the by-laws of a majority of the Lodge may see fit to inflict."
Caution against attempting to receive wages without being able to give the token.
Warning that such conduct requires severe punishment, with the penalty being to have the right hand struck off.
The ritual includes a dramatic threat of striking off the hand and even life, emphasizing the seriousness of the offense.

I will not give the secrets of a Mark Master Mason to any one of an inferior degree, nor to any other person in the known world, except it be a true and lawful brother, or brethren, of this degree; and not unto him nor unto them whom I shall hear so to be, but unto him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Lodge, or a brother of this Degree, to the value of his wages (or one penny), myself, knowingly, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.

I will not sell, swap, barter, or exchange my mark, which I shall hereafter choose, after it has been recorded in the book of marks, for any other one, unless it be a dead mark, or one of an older date, nor will I pledge it a second time until it is lawfully redeemed from the first pledge.

Binding myself under no less penalty than to have my right ear smitten off, that I may forever be unable to hear the word, and my right hand chopped off, as the penalty of an impostor, if I should ever prove wilfully guilty of violating any part of this my solemn oath, or obligation, of a Mark Master Mason.

The Right Worshipful Master cautions the candidate: 'should you ever hereafter have a mark presented you by a worthy brother, asking a favor, before you deny him make diligent search, and be quite sure of your inability to serve him; perhaps you will then find, as in the present instance, that some unknown person has befriended you, and you are really in a better situation than you thought yourself.'
The candidate is admonished never to give the words of the grip in any way but that in which he received them.
The first sign, placing the thumb of the right hand upon the lips, alludes to the penalty of having the tongue split from tip to root.
The candidate is cautioned against putting a question that violates the Constitution of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter, as doing so would be seen as breaking his oath.
The lesson explicitly cautions against accepting any office or station without being amply qualified to fill it.
The ceremony of passing the chair in a Chapter (making one a 'Virtual Past Master') does not confer any official rank outside of the Chapter, nor can it claim peculiar privileges in a symbolic Lodge.
The penalties of the obligation emphasize the severe consequences of unlawfully divulging any secrets belonging to the chair.
The Master must ensure the constitution is not infringed.
The Master must ensure the by-laws are strictly enforced.
The Master must ensure records are carefully kept so that nothing improper is transmitted to paper.
Candidates are cautioned to be careful not to forfeit the esteem of the brethren through misconduct and inattention to the rules of the Lodge.
The candidate is cautioned against forfeiting the brethren's esteem by misconduct and inattention to the rules.

entries.title: Chaplain
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the role of the Chaplain, who is responsible for leading the Lodge in devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master. It emphasizes that Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be a substitute for the Church, yet its core principles are rooted in the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible, acknowledging the need for Divine guidance.
entries.full_summary:
This section outlines the role of the Chaplain, who is responsible for leading the Lodge in devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master. It emphasizes that Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be a substitute for the Church, yet its core principles are rooted in the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible, acknowledging the need for Divine guidance.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Degree: Historical Notes

This section provides historical notes and explanations for various elements of the Entered Apprentice Degree. It discusses the reverence associated with removing footwear in the East, linking it to the purity of the Mosaic pavement. It emphasizes that a candidate's admission is solely based on their avowal of belief and trust in God. Practical details are given regarding the number of candidates that can be initiated, passed, or raised in each degree. The text debunks claims that biblical figures like Adam, Moses, or Solomon were Freemasons. It clarifies the dual meaning of the word 'Hail' or 'Hale' in Masonic context. Specific ritual actions, such as drawing a hand or sword across the candidate's throat, are mentioned. The story of Hipparchus, a Pythagorean, is recounted as a cautionary tale about the severe consequences of violating societal engagements. The concept of 'Light' in initiation is explained as progressing from material to intellectual illumination. Finally, the symbolic meaning of the first sign, related to the palate and throat, is detailed as a lesson in temperance and restraint of passions, and the role of the Master and Senior Warden in laying the corner-stone is noted, along with the practice of fostering emulation and discussion in Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->
practical_elements: The Chaplain's duty is to lead the Lodge in its devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master.
An appropriate musical selection may be sung after the installation charge.
An address may be made after the musical selection.
A charge is rehearsed by the Installing Officer after the address.
Putting off the covering of the feet as a sign of reverence, paralleling Eastern customs.
Candidate avows belief and trust in God as the sole basis for admission; refusal leads to rejection.
Chief officer expresses satisfaction upon a candidate's declaration of trust in God.
If more than one candidate is initiated at once, they are required to take hold of each other's arms.
Up to five candidates can be initiated as Entered Apprentices at once.
Up to five candidates can be 'passed' to the Second Degree at once.
Only one candidate at a time can be 'raised' to the Master's Degree.
Small Lodges may only manage one candidate at a time conveniently.
In some Lodges, at the words 'throat cut across,' a member or conductor draws their hand across the candidate's throat, or sometimes draws a sword.
The Master, assisted by the Senior Warden, lays the chief corner-stone of a fabric.
Lodge labors are conducted to produce an exciting spirit of emulation.
The Worshipful Master personally requests each individual to give an opinion on a specific doctrine or ceremony from the Chair.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The Chaplain symbolizes the spiritual aspect acknowledged within Freemasonry, guiding members in recognizing the need for Divine guidance, even while maintaining that the Institution itself is not a religion. The Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible, serves as the foundation for its moral and ethical tenets.

The Mosaic pavement is accounted pure and immaculate, symbolizing that no pollution can be tolerated on that sacred floor. The word 'Hail' (from Saxon 'Hael') is used as a salutation, inquiring 'Whence do you hail?' The word 'Hale' (from Saxon 'helan') signifies to conceal, referring to the 'tye.' Light is the first demand of a candidate at initiation, with material light succeeded by intellectual illumination. The palate and throat are considered the chief seat of irregular appetites, and the first sign instructs Masons to avoid temptation through proper restraint on passions, promoting temperance, decency, and decorum.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The Chaplain is entrusted with a sacred position to provide spiritual leadership. All members are taught that Freemasonry is not a religion and should not replace their individual religious affiliations, but it encourages a recognition of Divine guidance and bases its principles on the teachings of the Holy Bible.

Admission into the fraternity is based on a sincere belief and trust in God, and where God's name is invoked, no danger can ensue. It is unwise to assert more than can be proven or argue against probability regarding historical claims. The story of Hipparchus serves as a severe caution against violating engagements, emphasizing the importance of truth and fidelity, and the dire consequences of their breach. Candidates are taught that the pursuit of 'Light' extends beyond the material to intellectual understanding. The first sign teaches the importance of temperance in all indulgences, self-restraint, and never exceeding the boundary of decency and decorum, under penalty of violating Masonic engagements.
tradition_notes: The principle tenets of Freemasonry are based upon the teachings found in the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible.
As religious men, Masons recognize their need for Divine guidance.

The reverence indicated by putting off the covering of the feet is still prevalent in the East among Christians, Moslems, or pagans, where it is considered highly irreverent to enter a sacred place with feet covered.
Masonic traditions do not support the belief that figures like Adam, Nimrod, Moses, Joshua, David, Solomon, Hiram, St. John the Evangelist, or St. John the Baptist belonged to the Masonic Order.
caution_notes: Freemasonry never has been, is not now, nor will it ever be, a religion.
Its members should never accept the Lodge as a substitute for the Church.
It is unwise to assert more than can be proven and to argue against probability regarding historical figures being Freemasons.

The story of Hipparchus illustrates the terrible consequences of violating engagements, leading to expulsion, social death, and denial of burial rites, serving as a warning against breaches of truth and fidelity.
Disobedience or violation of Masonic engagements, particularly regarding temperance and decorum, carries a penalty.

entries.title: Charge to the Grand Deacons
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the charge given to the newly appointed Grand Deacons of the Grand Lodge. It details their investment with the jewel of their office and rods as ensigns of authority. Their primary duties include attending upon the Grand Master and Grand Wardens, acting as their proxies in Grand Lodge duties, and performing their functions with vigilance and attention.
entries.full_summary:
This section outlines the charge given to the newly appointed Grand Deacons of the Grand Lodge. It details their investment with the jewel of their office and rods as ensigns of authority. Their primary duties include attending upon the Grand Master and Grand Wardens, acting as their proxies in Grand Lodge duties, and performing their functions with vigilance and attention.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0013 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Form of Petition.

This section outlines the formal process for submitting a petition for membership into a Masonic lodge. It details the required information on the petition, the need for recommendations from existing Brothers, the accompanying fee, and the procedural steps taken by the Worshipful Master and Brethren, including a motion to receive petitions, a vote by the usual sign, and the appointment of a committee of investigation for each applicant.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0013 -->
practical_elements: Appointment as Grand Deacons
Investment with the jewel of office
Investment with rods as ensigns of authority
Attending upon the Grand Master and Grand Wardens
Acting as proxies for the Grand Master and Grand Wardens in active Grand Lodge duties
A petition for membership must include the applicant's residence (e.g., New York City), age (e.g., thirty-eight years), and occupation (e.g., bookseller).
Petitions require recommendations from existing Brothers (e.g., Brothers Jones, Carson, Fox, Derby, Jackson).
Petitions must be accompanied by a fee (e.g., ten dollars).
The Worshipful Master presents petitions to the Wardens and Brethren for their consideration.
A motion is made and seconded to receive petitions and appoint a committee of investigation.
Brethren vote on motions using the 'usual sign'.
The Worshipful Master appoints specific Brothers to serve on committees of investigation for each petitioner.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The jewel of office and the rods serve as symbolic ensigns of the Grand Deacons' authority and the importance of their role within the Grand Lodge structure.
entries.candidate_lesson: The charge emphasizes the importance of diligence, vigilance, and attention in fulfilling one's duties, particularly in positions of authority and responsibility within the Masonic fraternity. It also highlights the structured hierarchy and the specific roles of officers within the Grand Lodge.
tradition_notes: The investment with a jewel and rods is a traditional ceremonial act for Grand Lodge officers, signifying their assumption of office and authority.
The institution is referred to as an 'ancient institution'.
caution_notes: The instruction to 'Let vigilance and attention actuate you' serves as a caution against negligence or inattentiveness in the discharge of their important functions.

entries.title: Charge to the Grand Pursuivant
entries.short_summary: This charge outlines the duties of the Grand Pursuivant, a Grand Lodge officer. Key responsibilities include receiving reports from the Grand Tiler, announcing the names and Masonic ranks of those seeking admission, ensuring all entrants wear appropriate decorations, and maintaining punctual attendance at all Grand Lodge Communications. The officer's station is near the door, and they are invested with the jewel of their office.
entries.full_summary:
This charge outlines the duties of the Grand Pursuivant, a Grand Lodge officer. Key responsibilities include receiving reports from the Grand Tiler, announcing the names and Masonic ranks of those seeking admission, ensuring all entrants wear appropriate decorations, and maintaining punctual attendance at all Grand Lodge Communications. The officer's station is near the door, and they are invested with the jewel of their office.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0015 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fourth Order of Business

This section outlines the Fourth Order of Business in a Masonic lodge, which focuses on balloting for candidates seeking admission to the First Degree. It details the roles of the Worshipful Master and Secretary in initiating the process, the opportunity for brethren to voice opinions on a candidate, and the Senior Deacon's duty to prepare and place the ballot-box on the altar for the vote.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0015 -->
practical_elements: Receive all reports from the Grand Tiler.
Announce the name and Masonic rank of all who desire admission.
Ensure none enter without wearing their appropriate decorations.
Maintain punctual attendance at all Communications of the Grand Lodge.
Stationed near the door.
Investiture with the jewel of office.
The Worshipful Master initiates the balloting process for candidates.
The Secretary identifies candidates to be balloted for.
Brethren are given an opportunity to speak for or against a candidate before balloting.
The Senior Deacon is instructed to prepare the ballot-box.
The ballot-box is described as a small box, five or six inches square, with two drawers and a small hopper on top.
The hopper's hole leads to the first drawer, which is empty and shoved in.
The lower drawer is drawn out and contains both black and white balls.
The Senior Deacon places the ballot-box on the altar in the middle of the Lodge.
The Worshipful Master instructs the brethren to proceed with balloting.
tradition_notes: The officer is invested with the jewel of their office.
The station of the Grand Pursuivant is traditionally near the door.
The process of balloting for candidates, including the opportunity for discussion and the use of a specific ballot-box, is a traditional Masonic procedure for admission to the First Degree.
caution_notes: Ensure that none enter without wearing their appropriate decorations.

entries.title: Iv. Qualifications of Admission
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the fundamental qualifications required for admission into Freemasonry, which include being a man, of free birth, of lawful age, having a good reputation, and applying of one's own free will and consent.
entries.full_summary:
This section outlines the fundamental qualifications required for admission into Freemasonry, which include being a man, of free birth, of lawful age, having a good reputation, and applying of one's own free will and consent.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0015 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fourth Order of Business

This section outlines the Fourth Order of Business in a Masonic lodge, which focuses on balloting for candidates seeking admission to the First Degree. It details the roles of the Worshipful Master and Secretary in initiating the process, the opportunity for brethren to voice opinions on a candidate, and the Senior Deacon's duty to prepare and place the ballot-box on the altar for the vote.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0015 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0019 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Preparation of the Candidate

This section details the initial preparation and entry of a candidate for the First Degree in Masonry. It begins with an interrogation to confirm the candidate's motivations and willingness to conform to Masonic usages. Following assent, the candidate undergoes specific physical preparation, including partial disrobing and the application of a hoodwink, slipper, and cable-tow. The narrative then describes the ritualistic knocking at the Lodge door, the dialogue between the Deacons regarding the candidate's qualifications and desire for light, and the formal reception into the Lodge. This reception involves the symbolic presentation of the compasses to the candidate's naked breast, followed by a prayer led by the Worshipful Master and the candidate's declaration of trust in God.

This section details the final preparatory steps for an Entered Apprentice candidate. It includes the candidate kneeling for a prayer recited by the Worshipful Master, followed by the Master asking the candidate in whom they put their trust. Upon declaring trust in God, the candidate is assisted to rise and begins a perambulation around the Lodge room, guided by the Senior Deacon. During this circuit, the Worshipful Master recites a passage on unity, and the Master, Junior Warden, and Senior Warden each give a single gavel rap as the candidate passes their stations. The perambulation concludes at the Junior Warden's station, where a challenge is issued.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0019 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0020 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Reception and Perambulation

This section details the reception and perambulation of an Entered Apprentice candidate, Mr. Peter Gabe, within the Lodge. It covers the initial questioning by the Junior Warden regarding his free will, qualifications, and right of admission, followed by similar inquiries from the Senior Warden and Worshipful Master. The candidate's journey from west to east in search of light is emphasized. The ritual then proceeds to instruct the candidate on the proper approach to the east and the altar, culminating in the preparation for taking the solemn oath or obligation of an Entered Apprentice Mason, including the specific 'due form' posture involving kneeling on the naked left knee with the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0020 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0021 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Obligation.

This section details the Entered Apprentice obligation, emphasizing secrecy and the severe penalties for its violation. It describes the ritualistic process of the candidate receiving 'light,' which involves the removal of the hoodwink and cable-tow, and the explanation of the three great lights (Holy Bible, square, and compasses) and three lesser lights (sun, moon, and Master of the Lodge). The section concludes with the Master presenting the grip and word of an Entered Apprentice, reinforcing the commitment to Masonic principles.

This section details the ritualistic process for identifying an Entered Apprentice Mason through a specific grip and the word "Boaz," which is revealed through a letter-by-letter exchange. Following this, the newly obligated candidate is assisted to rise from the altar and presented to the Junior and Senior Wardens. The text describes variations in lodge procedures for this presentation, either a single circuit around the room with signs or a more formal halt at the Wardens' stations involving a dialogue about the nature of Masonic signs (right angles, horizontals, perpendiculars) and tokens (brotherly grips for recognition).
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0021 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0026 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Lecture: First Section

This section presents the First Section of the Entered Apprentice Lecture in a question-and-answer format, detailing the candidate's journey through the initiation ritual. It covers the secrecy of Masonic knowledge, the preparation of the candidate, the process of admission including knocks and questions, the solemn obligation, the discovery and meaning of the three great and three lesser lights in Masonry, the presentation of the lambskin apron as an emblem of innocence, and the ancient custom of depositing a metallic memorial.

This section details the Entered Apprentice's return to the Lodge after examination, his placement in the northeast corner, and the presentation of the working tools: the twenty-four-inch gauge and common gavel. It explains the symbolic use of the twenty-four-inch gauge for dividing one's time into portions for the service of God and relief of a distressed brother, usual avocations, and refreshment and sleep.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0026 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0029 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Second Section.

This section explains the symbolic and practical reasons behind various elements of the Entered Apprentice initiation ritual. It covers the divestment of metals, the state of being neither naked nor clothed, the removal of shoes, being hoodwinked with a cable-tow, the three knocks, reception on a sharp instrument, kneeling for prayer, the inquiry about trust in Deity, being led by the right hand, circumambulation, encountering obstructions, kneeling on the left knee, and resting the hand on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses. These actions are connected to ancient customs, moral lessons, and the construction of King Solomon's Temple.

This section explains the symbolic significance of key elements in the Entered Apprentice degree: the lambskin apron, the metallic deposit, and the candidate's placement in the northeast corner of the Lodge. It highlights lessons of innocence, purity, charity, and the foundational principles for building a moral and Masonic life.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0029 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Royal Arch: Exaltation Ceremony

The Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony details the admission process for candidates seeking further light in Masonry. After an alarm at the Chapter door, candidates, who have previously attained the degrees of Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master, confirm their free will, preparation, and proficiency. They present a pass word, RABBONI, before being admitted under a 'Living Arch' formed by companions. This arch involves physical prostration and difficulty in passage. Candidates then kneel at the altar for their seventh obligation, a solemn oath covering secrecy, protection of companions, non-revelation of ineffable characters or the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word (except under specific conditions), avoidance of clandestine chapters, and strict adherence to procedural requirements such as exalting exactly three candidates at a time and requiring nine Royal Arch Masons to open a Chapter.

This section details the extensive obligation of a Royal Arch Mason, covering secrecy, prerequisites for candidates (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master), rules for exaltation (three candidates at a time, nine Masons for opening a Chapter), conduct towards fellow Companions (no speaking evil, apprising of danger, preference in employment, assistance in difficulty, keeping secrets, aiding the indigent), and adherence to constitutional laws and by-laws. The obligation concludes with a severe penalty for violation. Following the obligation, the ritual describes the candidate kissing the book seven times and the Principal Sojourner's address, emphasizing the importance of the Royal Arch secrets and the necessity of trials through 'rough and rugged ways' to test fidelity. The candidates are then led around the Chapter and kneel for a prayer to the 'Supreme and inscrutable Architect of universal Nature'.

This section describes a prayer offered during the Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony, emphasizing gratitude for human reason, capacity for improvement, and the institution of Freemasonry. It highlights new obligations of virtue and holiness, reverence for God's name, and the diligent search for divine authority in nature. The prayer concludes with a plea for devotion, love, and charity, and for heavenly grace to assist in rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' after the 'first temple of purity and innocence' was destroyed by sin. Following the prayer, the Principal Sojourner leads candidates around the Chapter, reading the first six verses of Exodus chapter 3, which describe Moses encountering the burning bush on Mount Horeb. The candidates are then brought before a representation of the burning bush, and their blindfolds are removed.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0122 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: First.

This section provides a Q&A overview of the Royal Arch Mason degree, detailing how a Royal Arch Mason is recognized, the constitution and officers of a Chapter, and its historical setting near the ruins of King Solomon's temple. It explains the significance of the four veils (blue, purple, scarlet, white), their symbolic meanings related to friendship, connection to Craft Masonry, fervency, zeal, purity, and rectitude, and their allusion to the four tribes of Israel. The text also describes the placement of guards at the veils to ensure only duly qualified individuals enter the presence of the Most Excellent High Priest, Excellent King, and Scribe.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0122 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0123 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Second Section.

This section details the ritualistic preparation and reception of a candidate for the Most Sublime Degree of a Royal Arch Mason. It describes the candidate's attire, the seven distinct knocks at the Chapter door, the required qualifications including previous degrees, and the significance of the pass word 'Rabboni'. The ritual proceeds with reception under a living arch, kneeling at the altar for an obligation, and symbolic representations such as the burning bush and the destruction of the Temple by Nebuchadnezzar, followed by the captivity and deliverance by Cyrus. The journey through 'rough and rugged paths' symbolizing the sojourning of the children of Israel is also described, leading to the first obstruction at the first veil.

This section details the ritualistic progression of "worthy sojourners" through three veils and then to the fourth veil or sanctuary. The narrative identifies the participants as Giblemites, descendants of those who completed the First Temple, endured Babylonian captivity, and are now liberated by Cyrus to assist in rebuilding the house of the Lord without expectation of reward. Admission through each veil is granted by specific words, signs, and explanations, which are derived from biblical accounts of Moses receiving divine signs (rod to serpent, hand to leprosy, water to blood). The journey culminates in an encounter with the Royal Arch Captain at the sanctuary.

This section details a candidate's journey to the Grand Council, where they are examined on their lineage and proficiency. They are tasked with removing rubbish from ancient ruins, leading to a series of significant discoveries. First, a keystone believed to be from King Solomon's Temple is found. Subsequently, after a perilous descent into an arch using a cable tow, three squares identified as masters' jewels of Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff are recovered. A second descent reveals a gold-overlaid trunk with mysterious characters, all promising further important revelations for the Craft.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0123 -->
practical_elements: Manhood
Free birth
Lawful age of maturity
Under the tongue of good report (good reputation)
Free will and consent
The Worshipful Master initiates the balloting process for candidates.
The Secretary identifies candidates to be balloted for.
Brethren are given an opportunity to speak for or against a candidate before balloting.
The Senior Deacon is instructed to prepare the ballot-box.
The ballot-box is described as a small box, five or six inches square, with two drawers and a small hopper on top.
The hopper's hole leads to the first drawer, which is empty and shoved in.
The lower drawer is drawn out and contains both black and white balls.
The Senior Deacon places the ballot-box on the altar in the middle of the Lodge.
The Worshipful Master instructs the brethren to proceed with balloting.
Candidate interrogated on motivations, desire for knowledge, service, and conformity to Order's usages.
Candidate requested to strip: coat, shoes, stockings, vest, cravat, pantaloons.
Candidate provided with a pair of drawers.
Left arm slipped out of shirt-sleeve and put through bosom, baring arm and breast.
Handkerchief or hoodwink tied over candidate's eyes.
Slipper placed on candidate's right foot.
Rope, called a cable-tow, placed once around candidate's neck, dragging behind.
Junior Deacon leads candidate to Lodge door and gives three distinct knocks.
Senior Deacon makes the sign of an Entered Apprentice to the Master.
Dialogue at the door between Senior Deacon and Junior Deacon (responding for candidate).
Senior Deacon takes compasses from the altar.
Senior Deacon presents one point of the compasses to the candidate's naked left breast.
Junior Deacon leaves candidate with Senior Deacon and takes his seat.
Senior Deacon, followed by two Stewards, travels once regularly around the Lodge-room with the candidate.
Worshipful Master gives one rap to stop the procession.
Candidate conducted to the center of the Lodge and kneels for prayer.
Worshipful Master leaves his seat, kneels by candidate, and recites a prayer.
All respond with "So mote it be."
Worshipful Master rises, takes candidate by the right hand, places left hand on candidate's head.
Candidate prompted to declare trust in God.
Candidate kneels for prayer.
Worshipful Master leaves his seat, kneels by the candidate, and recites a prayer.
Worshipful Master takes the candidate by the right hand and places his left on the candidate's head.
Candidate declares trust in God.
Worshipful Master assists the candidate to rise.
Senior Deacon conducts the candidate once around the Lodge room.
Worshipful Master recites the passage 'Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!' during the perambulation.
The Master, Junior Warden, and Senior Warden each give one gavel rap as the candidate passes their respective stations.
The candidate halts at the Junior Warden's station in the south.
Junior Warden gives one rap, followed by the conductor giving one rap.
Junior Warden asks, 'Who comes here?'
The Conductor (Senior Deacon) introduces the candidate, Mr. Peter Gabe, stating he has been in darkness and seeks light and rights in the Lodge, which is erected to God and dedicated to St. John.
The Junior Warden questions the candidate on his free will, worthiness, preparation, lawful age, and vouching.
The Senior Deacon confirms the candidate's qualifications, including being a man, free born, of good repute, and well recommended.
The candidate is passed from the Junior Warden to the Senior Warden, and then to the Worshipful Master, with each officer making similar inquiries.

The Worshipful Master asks the candidate about his origin ('From whence come you') and destination ('whither are you travelling'), to which the Senior Deacon replies, 'From the west, and travelling toward the east.'
The Worshipful Master asks the reason for traveling from west to east, and the Senior Deacon replies, 'In search of light.'
The candidate is instructed to approach the east by advancing with one upright, regular step, placing the heel of his right foot in the hollow of his left, with his body erect at the altar.
The Senior Warden teaches the candidate this specific step, leading him within one pace of the altar.
The Worshipful Master informs the candidate about the solemn oath or obligation, assuring it will not interfere with duties to God, self, family, country, or neighbor.
The candidate affirms his willingness to take the oath.

The Senior Warden places the candidate in 'due form' for the obligation: kneeling on his naked left knee, his right knee forming the angle of a square, his left hand supporting the Holy Bible, square, and compasses, and his right hand resting thereon.
The Master gives one rap with his gavel, signaling all present to assemble around the altar for the Entered Apprentice Obligation.
Candidate places hands on the Holy Bible.
Master presses gavel on candidate's knuckles during the obligation.
Candidate promises to hail, conceal, and never reveal Masonic secrets.
Candidate promises not to print, paint, stamp, stain, cut, carve, mark, or engrave Masonic secrets on any movable or immovable object.
Candidate binds himself under specific penalties: throat cut across, tongue torn out by its roots, and body buried in the rough sands of the sea at low-water mark.
Candidate detaches hands and kisses the Holy Bible.
Candidate is prompted to state 'Light' as their most desired condition.
Brethren surrounding the altar stretch forth their hands in the form of the duegard of an Entered Apprentice Mason.
Worshipful Master recites Genesis 1:1-3 ('Let there be light, and there was light').
Worshipful Master gives one rap, signaling all to be seated except himself.
Conductor strips off the hoodwink from the candidate's eyes while the candidate remains kneeling at the altar.
Senior Deacon removes the cable-tow from the candidate's neck.
Worshipful Master explains the symbolism of the compasses, square, and Holy Bible.
Worshipful Master explains the symbolism of the three lesser lights (three burning tapers).
Worshipful Master approaches the candidate from the east, performing the duegard, sign, and step of an Entered Apprentice Mason.
Worshipful Master takes the candidate by the right hand and presents the grip and word of an Entered Apprentice.
Dialogue between the Worshipful Master and Senior Deacon (answering for the candidate) regarding 'hail,' 'conceal,' and the token.
Dialogue for identifying the grip of an Entered Apprentice Mason.
Ritualistic exchange of letters (A, B, O, Z, Bo, Az) to reveal the word "Boaz."
Worshipful Master assists the candidate to rise from the altar by the right hand.
Candidate salutes the Junior and Senior Wardens as an obligated Entered Apprentice.
Lodge procedural variation: candidate passes once around the room, giving the duegard and sign at officers' stations.
Lodge procedural variation: candidate halts at Wardens' stations for a specific ceremony.
Deacon guides the candidate by the right arm around the altar to the Junior Warden's station.
Deacon gives one rap with his rod on the floor, responded to by the Junior Warden with one gavel rap.
Dialogue between Junior Warden and Senior Deacon regarding the identity of the candidate.
Senior Deacon defines signs as "Right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars."
Senior Deacon defines tokens as "Certain friendly or brotherly grips, by which one Mason may know another, in the dark as well as in the light."
Senior Deacon gives the duegard and directs the candidate.
Concealing secrets of Masons, alluded to by pressing the thumb-nail on the first joint.
Being divested of all metals, neither naked nor clothed, barefoot nor shod, hoodwinked, with a cable-tow around the neck during preparation.
Gaining admission by three distinct knocks.
Being received on the point of a sharp instrument pressing the naked left breast.
Being conducted to the center of the Lodge and caused to kneel for prayer.
Being taken by the right hand and ordered to arise, follow the conductor, and fear no danger.
Following the conductor once around the Lodge to the Junior Warden's station in the south, then to the Senior Warden in the west, and finally to the Worshipful Master in the east.
Approaching the east by one upright, regular step, with feet forming an angle of an oblong square and body erect, at the altar.
Kneeling on the naked left knee, with the right forming a square, left hand supporting the Holy Bible, square, and compasses, and right hand resting thereon, to take the solemn oath.
Receiving light by order of the Worshipful Master and assistance of the brethren.
Worshipful Master approaching under the duegard and sign of an Entered Apprentice, presenting the right hand, grip, and word.
Saluting the Junior and Senior Wardens as an Entered Apprentice.
Receiving a lambskin or white linen apron and being taught to wear it with the flap turned up.
Being requested to deposit something of a metallic kind as a memorial.
Candidate is returned to the place from whence he came, reinvested, and returned to the Lodge for further instructions.
Candidate is placed in the northeast corner, with feet forming a right angle and body erect, at the right hand of the Worshipful Master in the east.
The Worshipful Master presents the working-tools of an Entered Apprentice Mason: the twenty-four-inch gauge and common gavel.
Operative masons use the twenty-four-inch gauge to measure and lay out their work.
Divesting of all metals.
Being neither naked nor clothed.
Being neither barefoot nor shod.
Being hoodwinked.
Having a cable-tow about the neck.
Giving three distinct knocks.
Kneeling and attending at prayer.
Being asked in whom trust is placed.
Being taken by the right hand.
Being ordered to arise, follow the conductor, and fear no danger.
Being conducted once around the Lodge.
Meeting with several obstructions on passage.
Kneeling on the naked left knee.
Resting the right hand on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
Presentation of a lambskin or white linen apron to the candidate.
Request for the candidate to deposit something of a metallic kind.
Conduction of the candidate to the northeast corner of the Lodge.
Candidate standing upright with feet forming a square in the northeast corner.
Seven distinct knocks are given at the Chapter door.
The Captain of the Host raps nine times, answered by the Principal Sojourner with three times three, then one rap, which the Captain answers with one.
The Principal Sojourner whispers the word RABBONI as a pass.
Candidates are led by a rope upon entry.
Companions form a 'Living Arch' by creating two lines facing each other and locking fingers.
As candidates pass under the Living Arch, companions place knuckles on their necks and backs, kneading them and prostrating candidates on the floor.
Candidates kneel at the altar to receive the obligation.
The obligation is the seventh taken by the candidate in their Masonic journey.
The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken only in the presence of three Royal Arch Masons (including the speaker), by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.
Exaltation of candidates must be exactly three at one time.
A Chapter of Royal Arch Masons requires nine Royal Arch Masons to form or open.
Obligation taken in the presence of three Companions Royal Arch Masons, candidate making one.
Secrets received by 'three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath'.
Candidate must answer and obey all due signs and summonses within the length of their cable-tow.
Candidate kisses the book seven times.
Candidates are conducted once around the Chapter.
Candidates kneel for a prayer read by the Principal Sojourner.
A prayer is recited during the ceremony.
Candidates are led once around the Chapter by the Principal Sojourner.
The Principal Sojourner reads the first six verses of the third chapter of Exodus.
A representation of the burning bush is placed in a corner of the Chapter.
Candidates are directed to halt in front of the burning bush representation.
Bandages (blindfolds) are removed from the candidates' eyes by the Principal Sojourner.
Recognition of a Royal Arch Mason is by 'three times three'.
A Chapter of Royal Arch Masons is constituted by nine Regular Royal Arch Masons.
Officers include Most Excellent High Priest, Excellent King, Scribe, Captain of the Host, Principal Sojourner, Royal Arch Captain, and Three Masters of the veils.
Meetings are held in a place representing a Tabernacle, erected near the ruins of King Solomon's temple.
There are four veils: blue, purple, scarlet, and white.
The veils were placed at the outer courts of the tabernacle to serve as a covering and stations for guards.
Guards were stationed to ensure only duly qualified individuals could pass or repass.
The children of Israel made themselves known to guards by words and signs given by God to Moses.
Candidate divested of outward apparel, in a working posture, hoodwinked, and a cable tow seven times around the body.
Accompanied by two brethren.
Seven distinct knocks at the Chapter door.
Pass word: Rabboni.
Reception under a living arch.
Conducted once around the outer courts of the tabernacle.
Kneeling at the altar to invoke a blessing from Deity.
Kneeling upon both knees, both hands covering the Holy Bible, square and compasses for the solemn oath or obligation of a Royal Arch Mason.
Exhibition of the symbol of the burning bush.
Representation of the destruction of the temple.
Journey through rough and rugged paths.
Meeting an obstruction at the first veil.
Identification as "children of the captivity," "descendants of those noble Giblemites," and "Most Excellent Masters."
Stating the purpose: "help, aid, and assist in rebuilding the house of the Lord, without the hope of fee or reward."
Using the pass: "I am that I am, I am hath sent me unto you."
Giving the word of the Master of the first veil: "I am that I am, I am hath sent you unto us, Shem, Ham, and Japheth."
Performing the sign of the first veil, imitating God's command to Moses to cast his rod, which became a serpent.
Explaining the first sign by reciting the biblical passage from Exodus.
Using the word and sign from the first veil to gain admission to the second veil.
Performing the sign of the second veil, imitating God's command to Moses to put his hand into his bosom, which became leprous.
Explaining the second sign by reciting the biblical passage from Exodus.
Using the word and sign from the first and second veils to gain admission to the third veil.
Performing the sign of the third veil, imitating God's command to Moses to take water from the river and pour it on dry land, where it became blood.
Explaining the third sign by reciting the biblical passage from Exodus.
Encountering the Royal Arch Captain at the fourth veil or sanctuary.
Responding to the Royal Arch Captain's challenge: "Three worthy sojourners, who have come up to help, aid, and assist in the rebuilding of the house of the Lord, without the hope of fee or reward."
Being informed of a Grand Council decree restricting entry to true descendants of the twelve tribes of Israel.
Identifying oneself as 'children of the captivity' and 'Most Excellent Masters' to gain admission.
Gaining admission by 'words and signs' from masters of the first, second, and third veils, along with a signet.
Being received by the Captain of the Host and examined for proficiency in the preceding degree.
Undertaking the task of removing rubbish from the northeast part of the ruins.
Being furnished with working tools for the task.
Discovering a keystone resembling the top of an arch with effaced characters.
Descending into the arch using a cable tow wrapped seven times around a companion, with specific signals (swing right to ascend, left to descend).
Discovering three squares identified as masters' jewels during the first descent.
Performing a second descent into the arch, enabled by 'redoubled splendor' of the sun.
Discovering a gold-overlaid trunk with mysterious characters in the eastern-most part of the arch.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The physical preparation, including partial nudity, hoodwink, slipper, and cable-tow, symbolizes the candidate's vulnerable state, humility, and journey from darkness (ignorance) to light (knowledge). The hoodwink specifically represents the darkness from which the candidate seeks enlightenment. The cable-tow signifies the candidate's bond and obligation to the Craft. The presentation of the point of the compasses to the naked left breast serves as a powerful symbol and a physical reminder of the severe consequences and internal 'torture' to one's conscience should Masonic secrets be unlawfully revealed. The invocation of the Deity and the candidate's declaration of trust in God underscore the spiritual foundation of Freemasonry and the necessity of faith in a Supreme Being.

The act of kneeling for prayer signifies humility and a plea for divine guidance and aid in the candidate's journey into Masonry. The declaration of trust in God establishes a foundational principle of faith and reliance on a higher power. The perambulation around the Lodge room symbolizes the candidate's initial journey and introduction to the sacred space and its officers, while the accompanying passage emphasizes the core Masonic value of unity and brotherly love. The gavel raps serve as a ritualistic acknowledgment of the candidate's passage and the officers' presence.

The journey from the west to the east symbolizes the candidate's transition from a state of darkness or ignorance to a search for light, knowledge, and enlightenment within Masonry. The 'due form' for the obligation, involving kneeling on the naked left knee and forming a square with the right, signifies humility, readiness to receive instruction, and adherence to moral principles. The presence of the Holy Bible, square, and compasses during the obligation represents the Volume of Sacred Law as a guide for faith and morals, and the tools for circumscribing passions and squaring actions.

The compasses being hid by the square signifies the candidate's initial darkness or lack of full understanding in Masonry, having only received the Entered Apprentice degree. The three great lights (Holy Bible, square, and compasses) symbolize the rule and guide of faith and practice, the squaring of actions, and the circumscribing of passions to keep within moral bounds. The three lesser lights (sun, moon, and Master of the Lodge) represent the regular governance of the Lodge, mirroring the celestial bodies' rule over day and night. The candidate's desire for 'Light' symbolizes the pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment within Freemasonry. The removal of the hoodwink signifies the transition from ignorance to understanding, or darkness to light.

The exchange of the grip and the word "Boaz" serves as a fundamental means of recognition and confirmation of an Entered Apprentice's obligated status. The definition of signs as "right angles, horizontals, and perpendiculars" symbolically represents principles of rectitude, balance, and uprightness in conduct. Tokens, as "friendly or brotherly grips," symbolize the fraternal bond and the ability to recognize fellow Masons under any circumstances.

The lambskin or white linen apron is an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason, signifying purity and honor. The Holy Bible is presented as the rule and guide to faith and practice, the square to square actions, and the compasses to circumscribe and keep Masons within bounds with all mankind, especially brother Masons. The three lesser lights (sun, moon, and Master of the Lodge) represent the principle of regularity in governance, as the sun rules the day and the moon governs the night, so the Worshipful Master should rule and govern the Lodge with equal regularity. Being brought from darkness to light symbolizes the candidate's transition from ignorance to Masonic knowledge.

The placement in the northeast corner symbolizes the candidate becoming an upright man and Mason. The twenty-four-inch gauge, divided into twenty-four equal parts, is emblematic of the twenty-four hours of the day. Its symbolic use teaches the division of time into three parts: for the service of God and relief of a distressed worthy brother, for usual avocations, and for refreshment and sleep, representing a balanced life of duty, labor, and rest.

The divestment of metals symbolizes not carrying offensive or defensive items into the Lodge and echoes the silent construction of King Solomon's Temple. Being neither naked nor clothed signifies that Masonry values internal, not external, qualifications. Being neither barefoot nor shod conforms to an ancient Israelitish custom, serving as a pledge of fidelity and obedience. Being hoodwinked represents the candidate's state of darkness and the need to keep Masonic secrets from the world, also acting as a safeguard. The three knocks alarm the Lodge, inform the Worshipful Master of preparedness, and symbolize asking, seeking, and knocking for admission. The sharp instrument serves as a reminder to the conscience against revealing secrets. Kneeling for prayer acknowledges the necessity of imploring Deity's blessings before important undertakings. Trust in Deity is essential for an oath to be binding, as Atheists cannot be Masons. Being taken by the right hand assures the candidate of being in the hands of a true and trusty friend, signifying fidelity and safety. Circumambulation allows brethren to see the candidate is duly prepared. Obstructions symbolize guards at King Solomon's Temple gates, ensuring proper examination and preparation. Kneeling on the left knee represents the left side as the weakest part of man, signifying entry into the foundational, 'weaker' part of Masonry (Entered Apprentice). Resting the right hand on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses signifies fidelity, echoing the ancient worship of Fides.

The lambskin apron symbolizes innocence and purity, reminding the Mason of the conduct necessary for admission into the celestial Lodge. The metallic deposit signifies the candidate's initial poor and penniless state, prompting a commitment to liberal charity towards distressed brethren. The placement in the northeast corner, where the first stone of a building is typically laid, symbolizes the beginning of the candidate's Masonic and moral edifice, where they receive their foundational instructions.

The 'Living Arch' symbolizes the difficult and humbling path to exaltation, requiring perseverance and submission. The physical prostration and kneading represent the trials and purification necessary for advancement. Kneeling for the seventh time at the altar signifies the increasing solemnity and binding nature of the obligations as one progresses through Masonic degrees. The phrase 'he that humbleth himself shall be exalted' directly links the physical act of stooping low under the arch to the spiritual lesson of humility leading to higher understanding. The journey through darkness and crooked paths made straight ('I will bring the blind by a way they know not...') suggests divine guidance and enlightenment provided through the Masonic path.

The 'rough and rugged ways' and 'many trials' symbolize the challenges and tests of fidelity a candidate must undergo to prove their worthiness for the deeper secrets of the Royal Arch Degree. The 'stupendous arch of heaven' mentioned in the prayer symbolizes the divine architectural power of the Supreme Architect of universal Nature.

The prayer symbolizes a deep adoration for divine perfection, gratitude for the gifts of reason and improvement, and a commitment to virtue and holiness. The 'second temple of reformation' represents the spiritual rebuilding of the individual's character after the loss of original purity. The burning bush, where God appeared to Moses, symbolizes divine presence, revelation, and the sacred nature of the encounter, signifying a moment of profound spiritual awakening and instruction for the candidate.

Blue denotes friendship and is the principal color of a Master Mason. Purple, being a mixture of blue and scarlet, signifies the intimate connection between the Royal Arch degree and ancient Craft Masonry. Scarlet denotes the fervency and zeal that should actuate all Royal Arch Masons and is the peculiar color of this degree. White denotes purity of life and rectitude of conduct, which should govern those seeking admission to the Sanctum Sanctorum or Holy of Holies. The four veils allude to the four tribes of the children of Israel who bore banners through the wilderness: Judah (strength of the Lion), Reuben (intelligence of the Man), Ephraim (patience of the Ox), and Dan (swiftness of the Eagle).

The seven distinct knocks allude to the seventh Degree of Masonry. The reception under a living arch signifies that the principal secrets of the Royal Arch Degree should be communicated only under a living arch. The symbol of the burning bush impresses that the words and signs are of divine origin and sacred. The rough and rugged paths denote the sojourning of the children of Israel through the wilderness. The pass word 'Rabboni' signifies 'Good Master' or 'Most Excellent Master'.

The progression through the veils symbolizes a journey of testing, purification, and the acquisition of deeper knowledge and understanding. The biblical signs given to Moses (rod to serpent, hand to leprosy, water to blood) represent divine power, authentication of a divine messenger, and the ability to perform miracles to convince unbelievers, signifying God's ultimate authority. The phrase "I am that I am" symbolizes God's self-existence, eternal nature, and supreme authority. The narrative of the Giblemites, captivity, and rebuilding symbolizes resilience, dedication to a sacred task, and the continuity of spiritual and moral construction despite adversity.

The removal of rubbish symbolizes the clearing away of ignorance or obstacles to uncover hidden truths and ancient wisdom. The discovery of the keystone from King Solomon's Temple represents a crucial, foundational element of lost knowledge. The three squares, identified as masters' jewels of ancient Grand Masters, symbolize authority, perfection, and the foundational principles of the Craft. These discoveries collectively signify the ongoing pursuit of knowledge, the recovery of lost secrets, and the importance of perseverance in the search for truth.
entries.candidate_lesson:
A candidate learns the foundational requirements for joining the fraternity, emphasizing personal character, autonomy, and societal standing.

The candidate is taught the importance of sincere and honorable motivations for seeking admission, including a desire for knowledge and a commitment to serving fellow creatures. The ritualistic reception, particularly the point of the compasses, impresses upon the candidate the solemnity of the obligation and the grave consequences of violating Masonic trust and secrecy. The prayer and the declaration of trust in God instill the fundamental lesson of reliance on a divine power and the moral principles of brotherly love, relief, and truth that are central to the Masonic way of life.

The candidate learns the importance of seeking divine aid and dedicating their life to service. They are taught that their faith in God is a well-founded trust. The perambulation and accompanying passage instill the lesson of unity and brotherly love as essential tenets of Freemasonry, encouraging them to become a true and faithful brother.

The candidate learns that entry into Masonry requires free will and a sincere desire for moral and intellectual improvement, symbolized by the search for light. They are taught the importance of the solemn obligation, which is presented as a commitment that aligns with, rather than conflicts with, their existing duties to God, family, country, and neighbor. The ritualistic posture for the oath emphasizes humility, reverence, and the foundational principles of morality and rectitude.

The candidate learns the profound importance of secrecy and fidelity to the Masonic obligation, understanding the severe consequences of its violation. They are introduced to fundamental Masonic symbols, such as the great and lesser lights and working tools, and their moral applications. The ritual emphasizes the pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment, symbolized by the request for 'Light,' and the concept of being bound by a 'stronger tie' than physical restraints, referring to the moral commitment of the obligation. The candidate also learns about brotherly love and favor through the Master's actions.

The candidate learns the specific grip, the method of revealing the word "Boaz," the duegard, and the definitions of Masonic signs and tokens. These elements are crucial for demonstrating their obligated status and for mutual recognition within the fraternity.

The candidate is taught the importance of secrecy regarding Masonic knowledge and the solemnity of the Entered Apprentice obligation. Trust in God is emphasized as a well-founded faith. The candidate learns the moral application of the working tools: to square their actions and to circumscribe their passions and desires, keeping them within due bounds. The apron teaches the value of innocence and purity, and the overall ritual emphasizes the importance of free will, worthiness, and good repute for admission into the fraternity.

The candidate is strictly charged to ever walk and act as an upright man and Mason. He is taught the importance of wisely dividing his time to fulfill his duties to God and his brethren, attend to his daily work, and allow for necessary rest.

Candidates learn that internal qualifications are paramount over worldly wealth or honors. They pledge fidelity and obedience to the ancient institution and are taught the importance of secrecy in Free-Masonry. The principle of 'Ask, seek, knock' is applied to their journey into Masonry. A solemn warning is given against revealing Masonic secrets. The necessity of invoking divine blessings for all significant endeavors and the fundamental requirement of belief in a Deity for Masonic obligations to be binding are emphasized. Candidates are assured of trust in brethren and the guidance of a true friend. The Entered Apprentice degree is presented as the foundational, 'weaker' part of Masonry, requiring humility and learning, with the right hand symbolizing fidelity and commitment.

Candidates are taught to maintain purity of life and conduct, to practice liberal charity towards those in need, and to build their future Masonic and moral character upon upright principles, acting honorably before God and man.

Candidates learn the importance of humility and perseverance in their Masonic journey, symbolized by the 'Living Arch.' They are taught the paramount importance of secrecy regarding the Royal Arch degree's secrets, including its ineffable characters and the Grand Omnific Word. The obligation reinforces loyalty to the Chapter and fellow companions, prohibiting wronging them or speaking ill of them, and requiring warning of danger. Candidates are instructed on the strict procedural requirements for the Royal Arch degree, including prerequisites, the number of candidates for exaltation, and the quorum for opening a Chapter. The repeated obligations emphasize the cumulative and increasingly binding nature of Masonic commitments, always subordinate to duties to country and Maker.

The candidate is taught the profound importance of fidelity, secrecy, and mutual support within the Royal Arch. They learn the strict procedural requirements for the degree and the extensive moral and fraternal duties expected of a Royal Arch Mason, including charity, assistance, and adherence to Masonic law. The journey through 'rough and rugged ways' emphasizes that true understanding and higher secrets are earned through perseverance and proven commitment.

Candidates are taught the importance of gratitude, humility, and adherence to new and stronger obligations of virtue and holiness. They are encouraged to seek divine wisdom in the 'book of nature' and to allow the solemnity of the ceremonies to profoundly impact their lives. The narrative of Moses and the burning bush instructs them in reverence for God's 'great, mighty, and terrible Name,' inspiring devotion, love, and charity, and emphasizing the continuous process of personal reformation and spiritual rebuilding.

Candidates are taught the importance of friendship, fervency, zeal, purity of life, and rectitude of conduct. The emphasis on guards and qualifications underscores the necessity of being duly prepared and worthy to advance within the Masonic fraternity and to enter sacred spaces.

The candidate is taught the solemnity and divine origin of the Royal Arch secrets, the importance of their obligation, and the historical narrative of the destruction of the Temple, the Babylonian captivity, and the subsequent deliverance, which serves as an allegory for perseverance through trials. The journey through rough and rugged paths emphasizes the challenges and spiritual journey of the children of Israel.

Candidates are taught the importance of perseverance, unwavering faith, and commitment to noble undertakings, even when no material reward is expected. The lessons emphasize the divine origin of authority and the power of God, as demonstrated through the signs given to Moses. The historical narrative of the Giblemites teaches about resilience in the face of adversity, the continuity of sacred work, and the dedication required for the spiritual rebuilding of the 'house of the Lord.' The ritual progression signifies that deeper truths and privileges are earned through demonstrated worthiness, knowledge, and adherence to ritual.

The candidate demonstrates humility, dedication, and courage by expressing willingness to undertake any task, even the most servile or dangerous, to promote a noble and glorious undertaking. This emphasizes the importance of perseverance, self-sacrifice, and unwavering commitment in the pursuit of Masonic ideals and the recovery of lost knowledge.
tradition_notes: These qualifications are traditional requirements for admission into a Blue Lodge.
The process of balloting for candidates, including the opportunity for discussion and the use of a specific ballot-box, is a traditional Masonic procedure for admission to the First Degree.
The text notes that the Worshipful Master sometimes addresses the candidate as 'Stranger!' instead of by name during the declaration of trust in God.
The Junior Deacon 'always responds for the candidate' during the initial questioning at the door.
The phrase 'as all brothers and fellows have done before' emphasizes the continuity of Masonic tradition.
Masters sometimes use 'Stranger!' instead of the candidate's name when asking about trust.
The passage 'Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!' is noted as being from 'opening, or Monitor's.'
The Lodge is described as 'erected to God, and dedicated to the holy St. John,' reflecting a traditional Masonic dedication.
The phrase 'as all brothers and fellows have done before' emphasizes the continuity and tradition of the Masonic initiation process.
Some Masters use the phrase 'As we now hold the brother by a stronger tie' when the cable-tow is removed, indicating a symbolic shift from physical restraint to moral commitment.
The text notes that the old way of spelling the word "Boaz," as represented by Morgan, Craft, Allyn, Richardson, and Barnard, was by syllabling it.
Lodges differ in their post-obligation procedure: some pass the candidate once around the room, while others require a halt at the Wardens' stations for a specific ceremony.
The lambskin or white linen apron is described as 'more ancient than the Golden Fleece or Roman Eagle; more honorable than the Star or Garter, or any other order'.
The request to deposit something of a metallic kind is noted as 'agreeably to an ancient custom, adopted in every regulated and well-governed Lodge'.
The construction of King Solomon's Temple without the sound of iron tools, using pre-prepared stones and wood, and wooden mauls.
The ancient Israelitish custom of changing and redeeming, as read in the Book of Ruth, involving the plucking off of a shoe as testimony.
The concept of guards placed at the south, west, and east gates of the courts of King Solomon's Temple to control passage.
The ancient brethren's belief that the right hand was the seat of fidelity, and their worship of Deity under the name of Fides, represented by joined right hands or two human figures holding hands.
The lamb has been universally regarded as an emblem of innocence throughout history.
The first stone of a building is traditionally laid in the northeast corner.
The Royal Arch degree is referred to as the 'august sublime Degree of the Holy Royal Arch'.
Candidates must have received the degrees of Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master as prerequisites.
The Chapter is dedicated to Zerubbabel.
The obligation taken is the seventh in the candidate's Masonic journey.
The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken by three companions, by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.
Exaltations must involve exactly three candidates at a time.
A minimum of nine Royal Arch Masons are required to form or open a Chapter.
The Royal Arch secrets are considered 'infinitely more importance than any that precede it'.
The obligation is taken 'under a Living Arch, and at low breath'.
The General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America and State Grand Chapters are recognized authorities.
The concept of 'cable-tow' is applied to the Royal Arch degree for answering summonses.
The reading from Exodus chapter 3, verses 1-6, is a specific part of the ritual, timed to coincide with the candidates' arrival at the burning bush representation.
The concept of rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' is a significant allegorical theme within this degree, contrasting with a 'first temple of purity and innocence' lost through sin.
The Chapter represents a Tabernacle erected by ancient brethren near the ruins of King Solomon's temple.
The three former officers represent ancient brethren who formed the first Most Grand Council at Jerusalem.
The three latter officers represent ancient brethren who brought to light principal secrets buried since the death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff until the erection of the second temple.
The veils allude to the four tribes of the children of Israel who bore banners through the wilderness.
The method of identification for the children of Israel involved words and signs given by God to Moses.

The ritual references the progression through previous degrees: Duly initiated (Entered Apprentice), Passed to the Degree of Fellow Craft, Raised to the Sublime Degree of Master Mason, Advanced to the Degree of Mark Master Mason, Regularly passed the Chair (Past Master), Received and acknowledged as Most Excellent Masters.

The historical narrative includes the destruction of the Temple by Nebuchadnezzar, King of Babylon, in the eleventh year of Zedekiah, King of Jerusalem, the seventy-year captivity, and the deliverance by Cyrus, King of Persia.
The narrative connects the participants to the Giblemites, who were skilled stonemasons involved in the construction of King Solomon's Temple.
It references the historical events of the destruction of the First Temple by Nebuchadnezzar, the Babylonian captivity, and the liberation by Cyrus, King of Persia, for the purpose of rebuilding.
The ritual incorporates specific biblical accounts from Exodus regarding Moses' divine commission and the signs given to him.
The explicit mention of "Most Excellent Masters" and "Royal Arch Captain" places this ritual firmly within the Royal Arch degree tradition.

The narrative references a Grand Council decree and the 'children of the captivity,' which are characteristic elements of Royal Arch Masonry, often associated with the rebuilding of the Temple after the Babylonian captivity.
The mention of 'Most Excellent High Priest, Excellent King, and Scribe' and 'Most Excellent Masters' indicates a degree beyond the Master Mason, specifically within the Royal Arch system.
The use of 'masters of the first, second and third veils' and a 'signet' are specific ritualistic elements found in Royal Arch Masonry.

The identification of the three squares as 'masters' jewels' worn by Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff directly links this narrative to the core figures and themes of the Hiramic legend.
caution_notes: The point of a sharp instrument pressing the naked left breast is to teach the candidate that 'as it is a torture to your flesh, so should the recollection of it ever be to your mind and conscience, should you attempt to reveal the secrets of Masonry unlawfully.'

The practice of brethren stamping their feet and clapping their hands once at the word 'light' is noted as 'nearly done away with now-a-days' due to being 'Too much "Morganry" about it, as it is styled by Masons.'
The candidate is obligated to conceal 'All the secrets of Masons, in Masons'.
The cable-tow signifies that the candidate might be led out of the Lodge if they do not submit to the initiation manner and mode.
The sharp instrument serves as a reminder of torture to the conscience should secrets of Free-Masonry ever be revealed.
No Atheist could be made a Mason, as trust in Deity is necessary for an oath to be binding among Masons.

I will not reveal the secrets of this Degree to any of an inferior Degree, nor to any being in the known world, except it be to a true and lawful Companion Royal Arch Mason, or within the body of a just and legally constituted Chapter of such and never unto him, or them, whom I shall hear so to be, but to him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Chapter of Royal Arch Masons, or a companion of this Degree, out of the value of any thing, myself, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.
I will not reveal the key to the ineffable characters of this Degree, nor retain it in my possession, but will destroy it whenever it comes to my sight.

I will not speak the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word, which I shall hereafter receive, in any manner, except in that in which I shall receive it, which will be in the presence of three Companions Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of the number; and then by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.

I will not be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter, nor converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or with one who has been expelled or suspended, while under that sentence.

I will not assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate to this Degree, who has not received the Degrees of: Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master.
I will not be at the exaltation of more nor less than three candidates at one and the same time.
I will not be at the forming or opening of a Chapter of Royal Arch Masons unless there be present nine Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of that number.
I will not speak evil of a Companion Royal Arch Mason, behind his back nor before his face, but will apprise him of all approaching danger, if in my power.
Penalty for violating the obligation: 'skull smote off, and my brains exposed to the scorching rays of the meridian sun'.
Not to be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter.
Not to converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or one who has been expelled or suspended.
Not to assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate who has not received the prerequisite degrees.
Not to strike a Companion Royal Arch Mason so as to draw his blood, in anger.

Guards were stationed to take 'special pains that none pass or repass, except such as were duly qualified,' indicating a strict requirement for proper authorization and qualification to enter sacred spaces or the presence of principal officers.
The principal secrets of this Degree should be communicated only under a living arch.
