entries.title: Vi. Government of the Craft
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the fundamental principles governing the Craft, detailing the requirements for regularly constituted Lodges, the hierarchy of appeals from Lodge decisions, and the specific prerogatives of a Grand Master. It also covers rules regarding inter-Lodge relations, entry into a Lodge, the indispensable secrecy of the ballot box, and the structure of Grand Lodges and General Assemblies.
entries.full_summary:
This section outlines the fundamental principles governing the Craft, detailing the requirements for regularly constituted Lodges, the hierarchy of appeals from Lodge decisions, and the specific prerogatives of a Grand Master. It also covers rules regarding inter-Lodge relations, entry into a Lodge, the indispensable secrecy of the ballot box, and the structure of Grand Lodges and General Assemblies.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0013 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Form of Petition.

This section outlines the formal process for submitting a petition for membership into a Masonic lodge. It details the required information on the petition, the need for recommendations from existing Brothers, the accompanying fee, and the procedural steps taken by the Worshipful Master and Brethren, including a motion to receive petitions, a vote by the usual sign, and the appointment of a committee of investigation for each applicant.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0013 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0099 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Past Master: Investiture and Lecture

This section details the investiture and lecture for a Past Master, outlining the specific signs, duegard, and grip of the degree. It describes the ritualistic seating of the candidate in the 'oriental chair of King Solomon,' referencing Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff. The ceremony includes the presentation and explanation of various Masonic implements such as the Holy Writings, Square, Compasses, Rule, Line, Book of Constitutions, By-laws, and Mallet, each with its symbolic meaning for conduct in life. The process concludes with the brethren saluting the new Master and the transfer of officer duties and jewels.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0099 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0102 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Page 344

This section details the ceremonies of the Past Master Degree, which often involves a mock lodge closing and business session designed to test the candidate. The candidate, placed in the Master's chair, is expected to close the Lodge but is reminded of the obligation to deliver a lecture. The experience is intended to teach the necessity of being qualified for any office accepted. The lecture for this degree covers constituting a Lodge, installation ceremonies, laying foundation stones, dedicating Masonic Halls, and funeral ceremonies. The text also provides historical context for the Past Master Degree, explaining its origin as a prerequisite for Royal Arch Masonry when it was governed by symbolic Lodges, and the practice of granting dispensations to 'pass the chair' for Master Masons not actually elected to preside.

This section explains the Past Master's Degree, distinguishing between 'Actual Past Masters' who are elected to preside over a symbolic Lodge and 'Virtual Past Masters' who receive the degree in a Chapter as a prerequisite for the Royal Arch. It clarifies that the Virtual Past Master status does not confer official rank or privileges in a symbolic Lodge. The text includes a closing prayer for the Lodge and a detailed Q&A lecture on the Past Master's Degree, covering the obligation, specific signs (the duegard and 'the sign'), the grip, the word 'Stone Squarer,' the jewel of office (a square), the three great lights (Holy Bible, square, and compasses) and their varying positions across the Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, and Master Mason degrees, and the charter or warrant.

This section details the various items presented to a Master, likely during an installation, and the extensive duties associated with the office. These include the constitution, by-laws, records, the privilege of being covered, the Great Lights (Bible, Square, and Compasses) with their specific positions for each degree, the charter or warrant, and the gavel. The Master's responsibilities encompass opening, instructing, and closing Lodges, initiating, crafting, and raising Masons, and presiding over numerous Masonic ceremonies.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0102 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0130 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Appendix: Notes and Authorities

This section provides an appendix of notes and authorities, covering various aspects of the Master Mason degree. It details the strong grip of a Master Mason, the three Grand Masonic Pillars (Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty) and their representation by Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff. It also describes the construction of the Temple, including the number of columns, pilasters, and workers by degree. The text explains the symbolism of the three steps on the Master's carpet and the nine classes of Master's emblems, specifically focusing on the setting maul, spade, coffin, and sprig of acacia as emblems of morality and immortality. Finally, it includes a historical note on the transmission and abridgment of Masonic lectures by figures like Webb, Preston, Gleason, and J. Browne.

This section, an address by M. W. P. Al. Tucker, Grand Master of Vermont, defends the authenticity and lineage of Masonic lectures, specifically those transmitted through T. S. Webb and Benjamin Gleason, asserting their connection to Preston's original lectures. Tucker addresses criticisms regarding his previous statements, clarifying that Webb abridged and rearranged Preston's lectures but retained their substance. He cites his possession of 'key' copies of both Webb's abridgment and Preston's full lectures, believed to be true manuscripts as taught by Gleason. Tucker also defends the claim that Gleason's lectures were approved by the Grand Lodge of England, referencing Gleason's own published letter from 1843, where Gleason states he acquired complete knowledge of the three degrees' lectures from Webb and successfully communicated them across the United States and Europe, 'according to the ancient landmarks,' without objection. He further supports Gleason's knowledge of Preston by quoting Gleason's 1812 oration, which praises Preston's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and his 'estimable system of improvements'. The discussion also mentions J. Browne's 1802 'Master Key' as a presumed source for Prestonian lectures.

This section discusses the historical presence and influence of Preston's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and the 'Prestonian system of work and lectures' in the United States, particularly in New England. It highlights Benjamin Gleason's crucial role, alongside Thomas Smith Webb, in introducing and promulgating this system. The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts adopted the Preston ritual as its standard in 1804, employing Gleason to communicate it to lodges under its jurisdiction. Gleason continued as a dedicated lecturer until his death in 1847, remembered as a master teacher of ancient Craft Masonry. The author defends the existence of Prestonian lectures in the US against a critic, emphasizing the importance of factual investigation over mere negation.

This section discusses the history and transmission of the Prestonian system of Masonic ritual. It notes that the system, as revived by Preston and sanctioned by the Grand Lodge of England, underwent a revision in 1813 for reconciliation purposes, which led to some incongruities. The original Prestonian ritual was brought to the United States around 1803 by two English brethren, one of whom was a pupil of Preston. It was communicated to Thomas Smith Webb and subsequently imparted to Brother Gleason, who then taught it to lodges in Massachusetts. The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts approved and adopted this system, which has since been the recognized Masonic work in the Commonwealth. Despite some modifications and innovations, the ritual has been largely preserved in its original purity, especially in older jurisdictions. The text also highlights the ongoing struggle against corrupting influences from ignorant itinerant lecturers and spurious publications, emphasizing the importance of preserving the ritual's integrity.

This section discusses the nature and importance of Masonic lectures, specifically comparing Preston's lengthy lectures with Webb's abridgment. The author, through the Grand Lecturer's opinion, suggests that Webb's version retains practical value and is more manageable. The text criticizes those who undervalue lectures, labeling proponents of their preservation as 'parrot Masons' and advocating for a 'new era' focused solely on the philosophy, history, and symbolism of Masonry, arguing that these elements are already embedded within the traditional lectures.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0130 -->
practical_elements: Lodges must be regularly constituted, duly tiled, properly furnished, and governed by a Master with the assistance of two Wardens.
Appeals from a Lodge Master's decision are made to the Grand Master, Grand Lodge, or General Assembly.
No Lodge can try its Master.
Every Mason has the right to appeal actions of his Lodge to the Grand Lodge or General Assembly.
No Lodge can interfere with the work of any other Lodge.
Entry into a Lodge requires personal knowledge, strict trial, due examination, or legal information.
The secrecy of the ballot box is indispensable.
The Grand Master presides over every meeting of the Craft within his jurisdiction.

The Grand Master has the prerogative to grant dispensation to confer degrees at irregular times, to temporarily set aside by-laws, to open and hold Lodges (dissolvable at his pleasure), and to make Masons at sight with the consent and assistance of Masons he assembles.
The Grand Master can assemble the Grand Lodge at his pleasure.
Grand Lodges, General Assemblies, or General Meetings govern Masons, and all Masons have a right to be present if the meeting is opened in the degree they have attained.
Members of Lodges are represented at Grand Lodge by their Master and Wardens, or their representatives, with the power and privilege to instruct them.
A petition for membership must include the applicant's residence (e.g., New York City), age (e.g., thirty-eight years), and occupation (e.g., bookseller).
Petitions require recommendations from existing Brothers (e.g., Brothers Jones, Carson, Fox, Derby, Jackson).
Petitions must be accompanied by a fee (e.g., ten dollars).
The Worshipful Master presents petitions to the Wardens and Brethren for their consideration.
A motion is made and seconded to receive petitions and appoint a committee of investigation.
Brethren vote on motions using the 'usual sign'.
The Worshipful Master appoints specific Brothers to serve on committees of investigation for each petitioner.
The Master approaches under the step, sign, and duegard of a Past Master Mason.
The Master steps off with the left foot, placing the heel of the right foot at the toe of the left, forming a right angle (square).
First sign: thumb of the right hand (fingers clinched) placed upon the lips.
Second sign: right hand placed upon the left side of the neck, drawing it edgewise downward toward the right side.
The candidate is conducted into the 'oriental chair of King Solomon'.
A large cocked hat is placed on the candidate's head.
The candidate is seated in a chair in front of the Master's chair.
The Past Master's grip is used to raise and seat the candidate multiple times.
The Master and candidate take each other by the Master Mason's grip.
Insides of their feet are placed together.
The Master whispers 'GIBLEM' in the candidate's ear.
Right hands slip to catch just above the wrist of the left arm.
Left hands are raised, catching each other's right elbow.
The Master says, and candidate repeats: 'From a grip to a span, from a span to a grip'.
Left hand slips up the right arm to the back of each other.
The Master says: 'A threefold cord is strong', and the candidate replies: 'A fourfold cord is not easily broken'.
The Holy Writings, Square, Compasses, Rule, Line, Book of Constitutions, By-laws, and Mallet are presented and explained.
One stroke of the Mallet calls up the Junior and Senior Deacons.
Two strokes of the Mallet call up all subordinate officers.
Three strokes of the Mallet call up the whole Lodge.
Brethren salute the new Master by walking in front of the chair and giving the sign of an Entered Apprentice, repeated for each degree up to Past Master.
The Senior Warden and other officers deliver their jewels and gavels to the new Master.
The new Master requests the Wardens and other officers to resume their jewels and seats.
Candidate is placed in the Oriental chair (Master's chair).
Brethren engage in a mock, noisy session, proposing to close the Lodge.
Candidate attempts to close the Lodge by rapping with a gavel.
Retired Master stops the candidate, reminding him of the obligation to give a lecture before closing.
A brother whispers to the candidate, suggesting he resign the chair to the old Master.
Candidate resigns the chair.
Master delivers a lecture divided into five sections.
The lecture is usually read from a Monitor.
Abridged ceremonies for the Past Master Degree are described, involving a ruse of unfinished business and committee reports in a Mark Masters Lodge.
Mock debate ensues regarding legality of funds disposition in a Chapter.
Right Worshipful Master is accused of corruption and indignantly resigns.
Candidate is nominated, elected, and placed in the Oriental chair.
Retired Master reads a charge to the candidate.
Candidates are made to pass the chair as preparation for the Royal Arch.
The ceremony of passing the chair.
Kneeling at the altar in due form to take a solemn oath or obligation.
The due form for obligation involves kneeling upon both knees, both hands covering the Holy Bible, square and compasses, with the body erect.
Giving the duegard sign (thumb to mouth).
Giving 'the sign' (dropping hand from mouth in a circular manner down over the breast to the right side).
Communicating the Past Master's grip.
Giving the Past Master's word.
Presentation of the jewel of office (a square).
Presentation of the three great lights in Masonry (Holy Bible, square and compasses).
Presentation of the charter or warrant.
Lodge closed with a specific prayer.
The lecture is delivered by the Right Worshipful Master.
Presentation of the constitution, by-laws, records, the privilege of being covered, the Bible, Square, and Compasses, the charter or warrant, and the gavel to the Master.
Master to carefully search the constitution to ensure it is not infringed.
Master to carefully search the by-laws and ensure they are strictly enforced.
Master to ensure records are carefully kept to prevent improper transmissions to paper, and to have general supervision over the secretary's duties.
Master to be covered while the rest of the brethren remain uncovered.

Ensure the Bible, Square, and Compasses are in proper position when the Lodge is open: both points of compasses beneath the square for EA degree; one point elevated above the square for FC degree; both points elevated above the square for Master's degree.
Use of the gavel: one blow to call the Lodge to order and seat members; two blows to call up subordinate officers; three blows to call up the whole Lodge.

Master's duties include opening, instructing, and closing Lodges; initiating, crafting, and raising Masons; presiding at consecrations, dedications, and installations; laying corner stones of public edifices; and presiding at funeral obsequies.
The strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, is mentioned.
The name associated with the strong grip cannot be given except on the five (5) points of fellowship and heard in a low breath.
The instruction to 'Advance and give it' refers to the word associated with the grip.
The word is 'right'.
Promulgating the Prestonian system of work and lectures.
Communicating the Preston ritual to lodges under Grand Lodge jurisdiction.
Teaching Masonic ritual as a lecturer.
Adoption of a ritual standard by a Grand Lodge.
The Prestonian system of ritual was brought to the United States around 1803.
The ritual was communicated to Webb and then imparted to Gleason.
Brother Gleason was employed to teach the ritual to lodges in Massachusetts.
The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts approved and adopted this system.
The ritual has been preserved in a remarkable degree of purity in Massachusetts lodges, particularly in the lodge where Gleason was Master in 1809.
The system underwent modifications in its general arrangement and adaptation after its introduction to the US.
Grand Lecturer compared Preston and Gleason Lectures
Preston Lectures are lengthy, estimated to cover nearly one hundred pages of foolscap paper if written out in full
Requirement for Masons to commit lectures in extenso is considered a difficult task
Webb's abridgment and new arrangement of lectures are considered to preserve substantial practical value and use preferable language
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The first sign, with the thumb on the lips, alludes to the penalty of having the tongue split from tip to root. The second sign, drawing the hand across the neck, signifies crossing former penalties. The act of raising King Solomon by Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff using the Past Master's grip symbolizes support and assistance in leadership. The Holy Writings are presented as a guide to truth, happiness, and the whole duty of man. The Square teaches to regulate actions by morality and virtue. The Compasses instruct to limit desires, leading to eminence by merit and a respected life and death. The Rule directs punctual observance of duty, pursuit of virtue, and having eternity in view. The Line teaches moral rectitude, avoiding dissimulation, and directing steps towards immortality. The Mallet is an emblem of power, used to maintain order and call the Lodge to attention. The phrases 'A threefold cord is strong' and 'A fourfold cord is not easily broken' symbolize strength, unity, and resilience.

The apparent confusion and the candidate's embarrassment during the mock lodge proceedings are designed to convey a striking lesson about the importance of not soliciting or accepting any office or station for which one is not amply qualified.

The duegard sign alludes to the penalty of the obligation, signifying a preference for the tongue to cleave to the roof of the mouth rather than unlawfully divulging secrets. 'The sign' alludes to an additional penalty, indicating a willingness to suffer former penalties rather than unlawfully divulging secrets. The word 'Stone Squarer' signifies the role. The jewel of office, a square, symbolizes the hope that the recipient would prepare a 'square stone in the Temple of Masonry.' The Holy Bible within the three great lights is presented as necessary for counsel and guidance. The varying positions of the compasses relative to the square symbolize the different degrees: both points beneath the square for EA, one point elevated for FC, and both points elevated for Master Mason.

The Bible, Square, and Compasses are presented as the three great lights, providing counsel and guidance. The gavel is described as an emblem of power. The Master's chair is symbolically linked to Grand Master Solomon, with the hope that a portion of his wisdom will rest upon the Master.

The three grand masonic pillars, Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty, are represented by Solomon, King of Israel (wisdom to contrive), Hiram, King of Tyre (strength to support), and Hiram Abiff (beauty to adorn). The three steps on the Master's carpet are emblematic of the three principal stages of human life: youth, manhood, and age. The setting maul, spade, and coffin are striking emblems of mortality. The sprig of acacia symbolizes the imperishable part of man that survives the grave, bearing affinity to the Supreme Intelligence that pervades all nature and never dies.

The text implies that Masonic lectures are a primary vehicle for conveying Masonic symbolism, arguing against the idea that symbolism exists independently or is better understood by dismissing the lectures.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate learns the specific signs, duegard, and grip of a Past Master Mason, including their associated penalties and meanings. They are instructed in the duties and responsibilities of a Master, symbolized by the various Masonic implements. The candidate is charged to be guided by the Holy Writings, regulate actions by the Square, limit desires by the Compasses, observe duty by the Rule, maintain moral rectitude by the Line, and diligently enforce the Book of Constitutions and Lodge By-laws. They are also taught the use of the Mallet as an emblem of power for maintaining order within the Lodge and are entrusted with the government of the Lodge.

The primary lesson for the candidate is the necessity of at all times abstaining from soliciting or accepting any office or station that one does not know oneself amply qualified to fill.

The candidate learns the solemn obligation to keep and conceal the secrets belonging to the chair, understanding the severe penalties for unlawful disclosure. They are taught the importance of the Holy Bible for counsel and guidance and are encouraged to prepare a 'square stone in the Temple of Masonry.' The distinction between 'Actual' and 'Virtual' Past Masters teaches the specific rights and limitations associated with each status, particularly regarding privileges within a symbolic Lodge.

The Master is entrusted with significant authority and a wide array of responsibilities, including upholding the foundational documents of the Lodge, maintaining accurate records, ensuring proper ritualistic display of the Great Lights, and presiding over all essential Masonic work and ceremonies. The role demands wisdom, diligence, and adherence to Masonic principles, echoing the wisdom of Grand Master Solomon.

Candidates are encouraged to imitate Grand Master Hiram Abiff in his virtuous conduct, unfeigned piety to God, and inflexible fidelity to his trust. This imitation prepares them to welcome death as a kind messenger from the Supreme Grand Master, translating them from an imperfect lodge to a perfect, glorious, and celestial lodge above, presided over by the Supreme Architect of the universe.

The candidate should understand that Masonic lectures are a vital source for learning the history, philosophy, and symbolism of Freemasonry, and that their preservation and study are crucial for a complete understanding of the Craft, rather than being dismissed as mere rote memorization.
tradition_notes: Though elected by Grand Lodges, the office of Grand Master was not created by them, suggesting a traditional or inherent authority.
The institution is referred to as an 'ancient institution'.

The ritual references King Solomon, when old and decrepit, being attended by his two friends, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff, who raised and seated him in his chair by means of the Past Master's grip.
The candidate is conducted into the 'oriental chair of King Solomon'.
The ceremonies for conferring the Past Master Degree are frequently shortened by omitting parts, such as presenting implements and their explanations, and often the charge.
Historically, Royal Arch Masonry was governed by symbolic Lodges, and a regulation required candidates to have presided as Master of a Lodge to receive the Royal Arch Degree.

To extend the Royal Arch Degree, Grand Masters often granted dispensations to Master Masons to 'pass the chair,' a brief ceremony investing them with Past Master mysteries, allowing them to advance to Royal Arch.
When control of the Royal Arch shifted to Chapters, the regulation continued to be observed.
The method of initiation for the Past Master Degree has become much abridged, often involving a ruse within a Mark Masters Lodge.
The candidate is usually relieved from embarrassment in good season by the retired Master, rather than being treated as badly as some representations suggest.
The law historically required the Royal Arch Degree to be restricted to Past Masters, leading to the practice of candidates passing the chair as a preparation.

A distinction is made between 'Actual Past Masters' (those elected and installed to preside over a symbolic Lodge) and 'Virtual Past Masters' (those who pass through the ceremony in a Chapter as a prerequisite for the Royal Arch).
The tradition of the Master being covered while the brethren remain uncovered.
The specific traditional positions of the Bible, Square, and Compasses for each of the three degrees (EA, FC, MM).
The traditional use of the gavel with specific numbers of blows for different commands in the Lodge.
The traditional association of the Master's chair with Grand Master Solomon.

The text includes an extract from an Annual Address by M. W. P. Al. Tucker, G. M. of Vermont, discussing the history and transmission of Masonic lectures, specifically noting Webb's abridgment and rearrangement of Preston's lectures.
It mentions that Webb learned and taught Preston's lectures in full, as well as his own abridgment.
Reference is made to a 'copy in key' of both Webb's abridgment and Preston's full lectures, believed to be true manuscripts as taught by Gleason.
A 'very rare' book by J. Browne, published in London in 1802, called the 'Master Key,' containing lectures in an 'abstruse cypher,' is also noted.
The text discusses the historical transmission and authenticity of Masonic lectures, specifically the lineage from Preston to Webb and Gleason.
It highlights the practice of Grand Lecturers, such as Benjamin Gleason, being commissioned to instruct lodges.
The importance of 'ancient landmarks' in the communication of lectures is emphasized.
Reference is made to historical Masonic publications like 'Free Mason's Monitor' by T. S. Webb, 'Masonic Trestleboard', and J. Browne's 'Master Key'.
The role of Grand Lodges (England, Massachusetts and Maine) in overseeing and potentially authenticating lecture content is noted.
Preston of England's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and his 'estimable system of improvements' are noted for defining the 'immovable landmarks' of the ancient Society.
Brother Benjamin Gleason, in association with Thomas Smith Webb, introduced the Prestonian system of work and lectures into the Lodges of New England and other sections of the country.
In 1804, the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts adopted the Preston ritual as its standard of work.
Brother Gleason was exclusively employed by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts during 1804 to communicate the ritual to lodges under its jurisdiction, including what is now the State of Maine.
Benjamin Gleason continued his labors as a lecturer until his death in 1847.
Charles W. Moore, Editor of the Freemasons' Monthly Magazine, acknowledged Benjamin Gleason as a master and teacher in the purest and most perfect Masonic ritual of ancient Craft Masonry.
The 'work' of Masonry, as revived by Preston, was approved and sanctioned by the Grand Lodge of England near the close of the last century.
The 'union' in 1813 led to a revision of the ritual for reconciliation purposes.
The verbal ritual, as revised by Preston, was introduced to the US around 1803.
The system was received and preserved, especially in older jurisdictions, essentially as it came from the original source of Craft Masonry.
Discussion of the historical lectures by Preston and their abridgment by Webb, highlighting their role in transmitting Masonic knowledge.
Emphasis on the importance of preserving Masonic lectures intact as a repository of history, philosophy, and symbolism.
Reference to the role of a Grand Lecturer in comparing and evaluating different versions of Masonic lectures.
caution_notes: No Lodge can try its Master.
No Lodge can interfere with the work of any other Lodge.
None can enter a Lodge unless personally known, without a strict trial, due examination, or legal information.
Secrecy of the ballot box is indispensable.
The first sign, placing the thumb of the right hand upon the lips, alludes to the penalty of having the tongue split from tip to root.
The lesson explicitly cautions against accepting any office or station without being amply qualified to fill it.
The ceremony of passing the chair in a Chapter (making one a 'Virtual Past Master') does not confer any official rank outside of the Chapter, nor can it claim peculiar privileges in a symbolic Lodge.
The penalties of the obligation emphasize the severe consequences of unlawfully divulging any secrets belonging to the chair.
The Master must ensure the constitution is not infringed.
The Master must ensure the by-laws are strictly enforced.
The Master must ensure records are carefully kept so that nothing improper is transmitted to paper.
The name associated with the strong grip cannot be given except on the five points of fellowship and heard in a low breath, emphasizing secrecy and proper ritualistic context.
Do not 'pin their faith' upon the mere negations of any writer, whatever may be the strength of his masonic reputation, but rather examine and investigate matters of fact and principle.
The revised Prestonian system after the 1813 union had many incongruities and departures from the original.
Many unwarrantable liberties have been taken with the ritual, and innovations have crept in.
The ritual has had to contend against the corrupting influences of ignorant itinerant lecturers and spurious publications.
Caution against undervaluing Masonic lectures and dismissing them as mere rote memorization.
Warning against attempts to inaugurate a 'new era' that seeks to change or disregard established Masonic history, philosophy, and symbolism as contained in the lectures.

entries.title: The Chaplain
entries.short_summary: The Chaplain holds a sacred, appointed position within the lodge, serving as its spiritual leader at the discretion of the Worshipful Master. While Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be a substitute for the Church, its core tenets are founded upon the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible. The Chaplain's duties include leading lodge devotions when requested and attending to the spiritual needs of the lodge and its members, acknowledging the need for Divine guidance.
entries.full_summary: The Chaplain holds a sacred, appointed position within the lodge, serving as its spiritual leader at the discretion of the Worshipful Master. While Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be a substitute for the Church, its core tenets are founded upon the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible. The Chaplain's duties include leading lodge devotions when requested and attending to the spiritual needs of the lodge and its members, acknowledging the need for Divine guidance.
practical_elements: The Chaplain is appointed at the will and pleasure of the Worshipful Master.
The Chaplain is installed as the spiritual leader of the lodge.
The Chaplain may be asked to lead the lodge in its devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master.
The Chaplain should serve the spiritual needs of the Lodge and its membership.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The Chaplain symbolizes the lodge's recognition of the need for Divine guidance and the spiritual foundation of Masonic tenets, even though Freemasonry itself is not a religion. The role represents the integration of moral and spiritual principles derived from sacred texts within the fraternity.
entries.candidate_lesson: Candidates learn that Freemasonry, while not a religion, acknowledges and emphasizes the importance of spiritual guidance and moral principles, often derived from sacred texts like the Holy Bible. They are taught that the Lodge is not a substitute for religious institutions, but rather complements a member's personal faith through the office of the Chaplain.
tradition_notes: The position of Chaplain is a sacred one within the lodge structure.
The Chaplain is appointed by the Worshipful Master, indicating a traditional method of selecting this officer.
caution_notes: Freemasonry has never been, is not now, nor will it ever be, a religion.
Members should never accept the Lodge as a substitute for the Church.

entries.title: Lodge Procedure and Officers
entries.short_summary: Ceremonial procedure, officer duties, installations, charges, and the operational order of the lodge.
entries.full_summary:
<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0081 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Closing the Lodge.

This section details the ritualistic closing of a Masonic Lodge, noting its similarity across all three degrees. It outlines a sequence of questions and responses among the Worshipful Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Secretary, and Junior Deacon, covering topics such as the state of the Lodge, approval of minutes, the process of tyling, and the stations and duties of officers. The ceremony concludes with a prayer and an optional closing song, emphasizing brotherly love and moral conduct.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0081 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0086 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master.

This section details the opening of a Mark Master Masons Lodge, including the specific gavel raps and declaration by the Right Worshipful Master. It clarifies that the Mark Master Lodge's sole business is candidate initiation, with other administrative tasks reserved for the Royal Arch Degree. The process of preparing a candidate for advancement is described, involving specific attire adjustments and the carrying of symbolic blocks (square, plumb, and a keystone with initials H. T. W. S. S. T. K. S.). The candidate, along with two officers, enters the Lodge as "Workmen from the quarries" and undergoes a ritualistic challenge at the Junior Overseer's station, presenting their work for inspection.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0086 -->
practical_elements: The Worshipful Master initiates the closing by asking the Senior Warden if there is anything further before closing the Lodge.
The Senior Warden rises, gives the sign of a Master Mason (if on that degree), and responds.
The Junior Warden makes the same sign and responds when questioned by the Worshipful Master.
The Secretary confirms there is nothing on the desk.
Minutes of the communication are read by the Secretary and approved if no objections, indicated by one rap from the Worshipful Master.
The Junior Deacon rises upon the Worshipful Master's rap and is questioned about the Lodge being duly tyled.
The Junior Deacon makes the sign, confirms the Lodge is tyled, and is instructed to inform the Tyler to tyle accordingly.

The Junior Deacon reports the Lodge is tyled by a brother of the degree within the outer door, whose duty is to keep off cowans and eavesdroppers and allow passage only to the qualified with the Worshipful Master's permission.
The Worshipful Master gives one rap, and the Senior Warden rises to answer questions about becoming a Master Mason, including traveling for wages and relieving distressed Masons.
Optional questions and answers are used by some Lodges regarding traveling from west to east in search of 'that which was lost' (the Master's word) and finding a substitute.
The Worshipful Master gives two raps, and all officers rise to confirm their stations and duties, often referring to the opening ceremony.
The Worshipful Master gives three raps, and all in the Lodge rise, with the Master rising first, as the Senior Warden explains the Master's duty to open and govern the Lodge.
The Worshipful Master orders the Lodge closed until the next communication, instructing the Senior Warden to communicate this to the Junior Warden, who then informs the brethren.
Brethren perform the signs, which are the same as at opening.
The Master gives one rap, responded to by the Senior Warden, then the Junior Warden, and repeated three times each.
The Worshipful Master questions how Masons should meet, act, and part, with responses 'On the Level,' 'On the Plumb,' and 'On the Square.'

The Master removes his hat and recites a closing prayer: 'May the blessing of Heaven rest upon us, and all regular Masons; may brotherly love prevail, and every moral and social virtue cement us. Amen.'
The brethren respond: 'So mote it be.'
Some Lodges sing a closing song, such as 'Adieu! a heart-warm, fond adieu,' to the air of 'Bonny Doon.'
The Right Worshipful Master gives two raps with his gavel.
The Senior Grand Warden gives two raps.
The Junior Grand Warden gives two raps.
The raps are then repeated.
The Junior Deacon informs the Tyler that the Lodge is open.
The candidate divests himself of his coat and rolls up his shirt-sleeves to the shoulder.
The Senior Deacon and his associate also divest their coats and roll up their shirt-sleeves.
The Deacon takes a small block of marble or painted wood, about the size of a brick and weighing five or six pounds, with a square engraved upon it.
The Deacon's associate takes a similar block with a plumb engraved upon it.

The candidate is furnished with a block representing a keystone, which he carries between the thumb and two first fingers of the right hand, with other fingers clinched and the arm extended down perpendicularly at the side.
The three "Workmen from the quarries" form in a line about three feet distant from each other, with the candidate being last.
The door is opened without ceremony.
The Junior Deacon, as conductor, together with his associate and the candidate, enter the Lodge.
They march four times around the room.
They halt the last time at the Junior Overseer's station, at the south gate.
The conductor gives four raps (in couplets) on the floor with his heel.
The Senior Deacon presents his stone to the Junior Overseer.
The Junior Overseer applies his small trying square to the different angles of the stone.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The ritual of closing the Lodge includes symbolic elements such as the Senior Warden's journey from west to east in search of 'that which was lost,' referring to the Master's word, and finding a substitute. The principles of meeting 'On the Level,' acting 'On the Plumb,' and parting 'On the Square' symbolize equality, uprightness, and rectitude in Masonic conduct. The Worshipful Master's station in the East, like the rising sun, symbolizes his role in governing and instructing the Craft.

The biblical quote emphasizes the candidate as a "living stone" to be built into a "spiritual house" and an "holy priesthood," highlighting the transformation from raw material to a refined, useful element in a sacred structure. The keystone, carried by the candidate, symbolizes the essential, crowning element of an arch, representing perfection and completion in the work. The square and plumb carried by the deacons represent accuracy, rectitude, and uprightness in their work.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The closing ceremony reinforces the importance of brotherly love, moral and social virtues, and the expectation for every brother to conduct himself as a man and a Mason. It also highlights the duty to support oneself and family, and to contribute to the relief of worthy distressed Master Masons, their widows, and orphans, emphasizing the practical application of Masonic principles in daily life.

The candidate is taught to be a "living stone," chosen and precious, contributing to a "spiritual house" through "well-doing." They are encouraged to act as free servants of God, honoring all men, loving the brotherhood, and fearing God, putting to silence the ignorance of foolish men. The act of carrying the keystone and presenting work for inspection teaches the importance of diligent, accurate, and upright labor, and the necessity of having one's work proven worthy.
tradition_notes: Some Lodges use specific variations in the Senior Warden's response, such as adding 'in the west' after 'Nothing, Worshipful'.
Some Lodges use specific questions and answers regarding the search for 'that which was lost' (the Master's word) both at opening and closing.
The questions and answers about the Master's word are also occasionally used by some Masters in the lecture.
Some Lodges sing a specific closing song, 'Adieu! a heart-warm, fond adieu,' to the air of 'Bonny Doon'.
No business other than candidate initiation is done in a Lodge of Mark Master Masons.
All other business, such as balloting for candidates and committee reports, is done in the Seventh, or Royal Arch Degree.
The Mark Master Degree is under the sanction of the Royal Arch Chapter.
The Lodge is declared opened in "due and ancient form."
caution_notes: The Right Worshipful Master forbids "all improper conduct whereby this Lodge may be disturbed, under no less penalty than the by-laws of a majority of the Lodge may see fit to inflict."

entries.title: Preparing the Candidate
entries.short_summary: This section details the physical preparation of a candidate for initiation. It describes the specific attire adjustments, including baring the legs and breast, and the application of a cable-tow around the body and a hoodwink over the eyes. The role of the Deacon in taking the prepared candidate is also mentioned, along with a note on the placement of compasses in a Master Mason's Lodge.
entries.full_summary: This section details the physical preparation of a candidate for initiation. It describes the specific attire adjustments, including baring the legs and breast, and the application of a cable-tow around the body and a hoodwink over the eyes. The role of the Deacon in taking the prepared candidate is also mentioned, along with a note on the placement of compasses in a Master Mason's Lodge.
practical_elements: Candidate is divested of all wearing apparel except shirt and drawers.
If no drawers, a pair is furnished by brethren.
Drawers are rolled up just above the candidate's knees.
Both arms are taken out of shirt-sleeves, leaving legs and breast bare.
A rope, called a cable-tow, is wound around the candidate's body three times.
A bandage, or hoodwink, is tied very closely over the candidate's eyes.
The Deacon takes the candidate after preparation.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The text describes the practical steps of preparation, but does not explicitly detail the symbolic meaning of the cable-tow, hoodwink, or baring of the body parts within this section. It does note the specific placement of compasses in a Master Mason's Lodge, with both points elevated above the square, which carries symbolic weight.
tradition_notes: The rope used is technically called a 'cable-tow' by Masons.
Compasses are placed in a Lodge of Master Masons with 'both points elevated above the square'.
